cases.title: Burning Chest Discomfort After Meals
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent burning behind the breastbone that worsens after large meals and at night.
cases.history: A 42-year-old man presents to his primary care clinic with a 6-month history of retrosternal burning that occurs 30-60 minutes after meals, especially heavy or spicy ones, and when he lies down at night. He often tastes something sour in the back of his throat and has occasional regurgitation. Over-the-counter calcium carbonate antacids give him quick partial relief. He denies dysphagia, odynophagia, weight loss, vomiting, hematemesis, or melena. He has no early satiety. PMH is notable only for obesity (BMI 32). He takes no prescription medications and has no allergies. Social history: he drinks 2-3 beers most evenings, drinks 4 cups of coffee daily, and eats late dinners; he quit smoking 5 years ago after a 10-pack-year history. He works as a long-haul accountant and is sedentary. Family history is unremarkable for gastrointestinal cancer. He has not lost weight and his appetite is normal. He is worried because his symptoms now occur 4-5 times per week.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and in no distress. Oropharynx is moist without erythema or thrush. Neck is supple with no lymphadenopathy and no thyromegaly. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal with regular rhythm and clear lungs. Abdomen is soft, obese, non-tender, with normal bowel sounds and no epigastric mass, hepatomegaly, or splenomegaly. There is no palpable supraclavicular node. Rectal exam reveals brown stool that is negative for occult blood. There is no peripheral edema and no pallor of the conjunctivae.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given his classic symptoms and the absence of alarm features, what is the most appropriate initial management step?
options: Refer immediately for upper endoscopy (EGD) as the first step today
Order an esophageal manometry study as the first step today
Begin an 8-week trial of a once-daily proton pump inhibitor plus lifestyle modification
Start an H2 receptor antagonist only and reassess in 6 months as the first step today
Order a barium swallow esophagram as the first step today
stages.explanation: In a patient under 60 with typical GERD symptoms and no alarm features, an empiric 8-week trial of a once-daily PPI combined with lifestyle changes (weight loss, smaller meals, avoiding late meals, reducing alcohol/caffeine, head-of-bed elevation) is the guideline-recommended first step. Immediate EGD is reserved for alarm features or older new-onset symptoms, so referring to endoscopy now is premature. Manometry evaluates motility disorders such as achalasia, not uncomplicated reflux. A barium swallow is insensitive for reflux and not first-line. An H2RA alone is less effective than a PPI for moderate-frequency symptoms and a 6-month delay before reassessment is too long; a structured 8-week trial allows earlier evaluation of response.

stages.label: ALARM FEATURES
stages.question: Which of the following findings, if present, would have prompted prompt upper endoscopy rather than an empiric PPI trial?
options: Sour taste in the mouth in this setting
Postprandial timing of symptoms
Nocturnal symptoms before discharge planning
Progressive dysphagia
Symptom relief with antacids in this setting
stages.explanation: Alarm features that mandate endoscopy include dysphagia, odynophagia, unintentional weight loss, GI bleeding/anemia, recurrent vomiting, a palpable mass or adenopathy, and new-onset symptoms in patients over 60. Progressive dysphagia with weight loss raises concern for stricture, esophageal cancer, or eosinophilic esophagitis, warranting direct visualization. A sour taste, antacid responsiveness, nocturnal symptoms, and postprandial timing are all typical of uncomplicated GERD and do not by themselves require endoscopy. The presence of true dysphagia changes the pretest probability of a structural lesion enough to justify scoping before any empiric therapy.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP VISIT
stages.content:
Return visit at 8 weeks
Symptoms: ~80% improvement on once-daily omeprazole 20 mg before breakfast
Lost 4 kg with lifestyle changes; cut alcohol to weekends
No dysphagia, no weight loss, no GI bleeding
Residual mild symptoms 1-2 times/week, mostly after late dinners

stages.label: OPTIMIZATION
stages.question: He has substantial but incomplete relief after 8 weeks of once-daily PPI. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Add long-term metoclopramide for prokinetic effect as the immediate next step
Refer for Nissen fundoplication now as the immediate next step
Reinforce timing/adherence
Stop the PPI and switch to as-needed antacids only as the immediate next step
Order an H. pylori stool antigen test and treat empirically as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Partial response is often due to improper PPI timing; PPIs work best taken 30-60 minutes before the first meal. The correct step is to reinforce adherence and correct timing, and if still inadequate, escalate to twice-daily dosing. Stopping the PPI abandons an effective therapy and risks rebound. Anti-reflux surgery is reserved for refractory disease after objective testing (pH study, manometry) or for patients wanting to stop lifelong medication, not as the immediate next step. H. pylori testing is relevant for ulcer disease, not typical GERD without dyspepsia. Chronic metoclopramide carries tardive dyskinesia risk and is not appropriate maintenance therapy.

stages.label: LONG-TERM PLAN
stages.question: After symptom control is achieved, when would endoscopy with the goal of screening for Barrett esophagus be most appropriate in this patient?
options: Routinely in all GERD patients after 8 weeks of therapy in this setting
Never, because he responded to a PPI as the immediate next step
Only if he develops new constipation as the immediate next step
If he has chronic GERD plus multiple risk factors
Annually regardless of symptoms as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Screening EGD for Barrett esophagus is recommended for patients with chronic GERD and multiple risk factors: age 50 or older, male sex, white race, central obesity, tobacco history, and family history of Barrett or esophageal adenocarcinoma. This patient is male, obese, has chronic reflux, and has a prior smoking history, so a one-time screening endoscopy is reasonable. Routine EGD in all GERD patients is not indicated and is low-yield. Symptom response to a PPI does not exclude Barrett, so 'never' is wrong. Constipation is unrelated. Annual endoscopy is not warranted without established Barrett or dysplasia.

pearls: Typical GERD symptoms in a patient under 60 without alarm features warrant an empiric 8-week PPI trial plus lifestyle modification before any endoscopy.
Alarm features (dysphagia, odynophagia, weight loss, GI bleeding/anemia, recurrent vomiting, mass, age over 60 new-onset) mandate prompt EGD.
PPIs are most effective taken 30-60 minutes before the first meal; partial response is frequently a timing/adherence issue before escalating the dose.
Consider one-time Barrett esophagus screening in chronic GERD with multiple risk factors (older age, male, obese, smoker).

cases.title: Gnawing Upper Belly Pain That Eases With Eating
cases.chiefComplaint: Gnawing epigastric pain that improves when he eats and wakes him at night.
cases.history: A 38-year-old man presents to clinic with a 2-month history of gnawing, burning epigastric pain. The pain is worst when his stomach is empty, classically 2-3 hours after eating and in the early morning hours, and it reliably improves within minutes of eating or drinking milk. He has been taking ibuprofen 400 mg a few times weekly for recurrent tension headaches. He denies vomiting, hematemesis, melena, weight loss, dysphagia, or early satiety, though he notes occasional nausea. PMH is otherwise unremarkable. He takes no other medications and has no allergies. Social history: he drinks alcohol socially (2-3 drinks per week), does not smoke, and works as a chef. He emigrated from a region with high Helicobacter pylori prevalence in childhood. Family history is negative for gastric cancer and inflammatory bowel disease. He has not had any prior endoscopy. His appetite is preserved and his weight is stable. He has not tried any acid-suppressing medication and is concerned the pain is becoming more frequent.
cases.exam: The patient appears comfortable and well-nourished. Conjunctivae are pink without pallor. The abdomen is soft with mild epigastric tenderness to deep palpation but no rebound or guarding. There is no palpable mass, no hepatosplenomegaly, and bowel sounds are normal. There is no Murphy sign and no costovertebral angle tenderness. Rectal examination shows brown stool that is negative for occult blood. There is no jaundice, no peripheral lymphadenopathy, and no abdominal distension.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: His pain that improves with food is most characteristic of which condition, and what is the best initial diagnostic step in this patient under 60 without alarm features?
options: Biliary colic; obtain right upper quadrant ultrasound
Acute pancreatitis; check serum lipase as the first step today
Gastric ulcer; obtain urgent EGD as the first step today
Duodenal ulcer; perform non-invasive H. pylori testing
Gastroesophageal reflux; start a PPI without testing in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Pain that improves with food (and recurs when the stomach empties) is classic for a duodenal ulcer, whereas gastric ulcer pain typically worsens with eating. In a patient under 60 with dyspepsia and no alarm features, the recommended strategy is non-invasive 'test-and-treat' for H. pylori using a urea breath test or stool antigen assay. Urgent EGD is reserved for alarm features or older patients. Lipase and biliary ultrasound address pancreatitis and gallstone disease, which his food-relieved, empty-stomach pattern does not fit. Empiric PPI without H. pylori testing misses a curable, ulcer-causing infection that also requires test-of-cure.

stages.label: TESTING CAVEAT
stages.question: Before performing a urea breath test or stool antigen test for H. pylori, which instruction is most important to ensure an accurate result?
options: Administer a PPI loading dose just before the test as the immediate next step
Stop proton pump inhibitors for 1-2 weeks
Perform the test immediately after a course of amoxicillin in this setting
Check serum IgG antibody instead, as it is unaffected by medications
Have the patient fast for 12 hours but continue all medications in this setting
stages.explanation: PPIs suppress H. pylori and can cause false-negative urea breath and stool antigen tests, so they should be held for 1-2 weeks before testing; antibiotics and bismuth should be stopped for 4 weeks. Fasting alone without stopping PPIs does not solve the false-negative problem. Testing right after antibiotics would also yield false negatives. Serum IgG serology is unaffected by acid suppression but cannot distinguish active from past infection and is no longer preferred where active-infection tests are available, so it is not the best answer. Giving a PPI before the test would worsen accuracy.

stages.label: DIAGNOSTIC RESULTS
stages.content:
Urea breath test: POSITIVE for H. pylori
CBC: Hemoglobin 14.6 g/dL, normal indices
No prior macrolide exposure reported
No penicillin allergy

stages.label: TREATMENT
stages.question: His urea breath test is positive. Which is the most appropriate first-line eradication regimen, assuming no penicillin allergy and low macrolide resistance is not assured locally?
options: Bismuth quadruple therapy for 14 days
Ciprofloxacin plus an H2 blocker for 10 days in this setting
Amoxicillin monotherapy for 7 days as the immediate next step
A PPI alone for 8 weeks with no antibiotics in this setting
A PPI plus a single course of metronidazole for 5 days
stages.explanation: With rising clarithromycin resistance, bismuth quadruple therapy (PPI + bismuth + tetracycline + metronidazole) for 14 days is a preferred first-line regimen, especially when local macrolide resistance is high or unknown or there is prior macrolide exposure. Amoxicillin monotherapy and single-agent metronidazole do not reliably eradicate H. pylori and promote resistance. A PPI alone heals ulcers symptomatically but does not eradicate the organism, leading to recurrence. Ciprofloxacin-based regimens are reserved for salvage, not first-line. Combination therapy targeting the organism is essential to cure the ulcer diathesis and prevent recurrence.

stages.label: TEST OF CURE
stages.question: After completing eradication therapy, how should successful cure be confirmed in this patient?
options: Check serum gastrin level as the immediate next step
Repeat EGD with biopsy in 1 week as the immediate next step
No confirmation is needed if symptoms resolve as the immediate next step
Repeat serum H. pylori IgG antibody in 4 weeks before discharge planning
Urea breath test or stool antigen at least 4 weeks after antibiotics
stages.explanation: Test-of-cure is recommended for all treated H. pylori patients and is best done with a urea breath test or stool antigen assay performed at least 4 weeks after completing antibiotics and after holding PPIs for 1-2 weeks, to avoid false negatives. Symptom resolution alone is unreliable because symptoms can improve without eradication. Serum IgG remains positive for months to years after cure and cannot confirm eradication. Immediate repeat endoscopy is invasive and unnecessary for a duodenal ulcer without alarm features. Serum gastrin assesses for gastrinoma, not eradication success.

pearls: Duodenal ulcer pain classically improves with food and recurs 2-3 hours later or at night; gastric ulcer pain typically worsens with eating.
In dyspeptic patients under 60 without alarm features, use non-invasive H. pylori test-and-treat.
Hold PPIs 1-2 weeks and antibiotics/bismuth 4 weeks before urea breath or stool antigen testing to avoid false negatives.
Always confirm eradication with a test-of-cure (breath or stool antigen) at least 4 weeks after therapy; serology cannot confirm cure.

cases.title: Belly Pain That Moved to the Right Lower Side
cases.chiefComplaint: Pain that started near the navel and shifted to the right lower abdomen with loss of appetite.
cases.history: A 19-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with 18 hours of abdominal pain. The pain began as a dull, poorly localized ache around her umbilicus, and over the past 8 hours it has migrated to the right lower quadrant where it is now sharp and constant. She reports anorexia, nausea, and one episode of non-bilious vomiting. Movement and the car ride in worsened the pain. She denies diarrhea, dysuria, or vaginal discharge. Her last menstrual period was 1 week ago and she uses condoms; she is not on hormonal contraception. PMH is unremarkable, she takes no medications, and she has no allergies. Social history: she is a college student, drinks alcohol occasionally on weekends, and does not smoke. Family history is non-contributory. She has had no prior abdominal surgery. She notes the pain is worse when she coughs and when the car went over bumps. She has not eaten since the pain began because food is unappealing. She has had no fevers measured at home but feels warm.
cases.exam: The patient lies still and is reluctant to move. Abdomen has focal tenderness at McBurney point with voluntary guarding and rebound tenderness in the right lower quadrant. Rovsing sign is positive (palpation of the left lower quadrant elicits right lower quadrant pain). Psoas sign is positive. Bowel sounds are present but reduced. There is no flank tenderness and no suprapubic fullness. Pelvic examination shows no cervical motion tenderness, no adnexal mass, and no discharge. There is no rebound in other quadrants and no hernia is palpable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Beyond a focused exam, which single test must be obtained in every woman of reproductive age presenting with this picture before further workup?
options: Hepatitis panel in this clinical context
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate
Serum lipase as the first step today
Urine or serum beta-hCG
Plain abdominal radiograph
stages.explanation: A pregnancy test (urine or serum beta-hCG) is mandatory in any reproductive-age woman with abdominal pain. It is essential to exclude ectopic pregnancy, which can mimic appendicitis and is life-threatening, and the result alters imaging choice and management. Serum lipase evaluates pancreatitis, which does not fit RLQ migratory pain. ESR is non-specific and does not change acute management. A plain radiograph is low-yield for appendicitis. A hepatitis panel is irrelevant to this acute presentation. The pregnancy test is the gatekeeper test that must precede CT and guides whether ultrasound or MRI is preferred.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Urine beta-hCG: NEGATIVE
WBC 14.6 ×10³/µL with 82% neutrophils
Hemoglobin 13.4 g/dL
Urinalysis: trace leukocytes, no nitrites, no blood
C-reactive protein 48 mg/L (elevated)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Her pregnancy test is negative and she has leukocytosis with neutrophilia. What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: Barium enema as the primary diagnosis
MRI brain as the primary diagnosis
Discharge with oral analgesia and 24-hour recheck
Diagnostic laparoscopy without imaging as the primary diagnosis
CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast
stages.explanation: With a classic history, peritoneal signs, leukocytosis, and a negative pregnancy test, imaging confirms appendicitis and assesses for perforation or alternative diagnoses. CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast has high sensitivity and specificity; graded-compression ultrasound is a reasonable radiation-sparing first test in young or thin patients and in pregnancy, escalating to MRI or CT if non-diagnostic. Discharge is unsafe given peritoneal signs and risk of perforation. Proceeding straight to laparoscopy is acceptable when the diagnosis is clinically unequivocal, but imaging is standard to confirm and reduce negative appendectomy rates. A barium enema and a brain MRI are not relevant.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast:
Dilated appendix measuring 11 mm with wall thickening and enhancement
Periappendiceal fat stranding
A small appendicolith at the base
No free air, no abscess, no free fluid

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: CT confirms acute uncomplicated appendicitis with an appendicolith. What is the most appropriate definitive management?
options: Observation with serial exams for 72 hours
Oral antibiotics as an outpatient only as monotherapy
IV antibiotics alone with planned discharge in 24 hours
Laparoscopic appendectomy with perioperative antibiotics
Percutaneous drain placement as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Laparoscopic appendectomy with perioperative IV antibiotics is the standard definitive treatment for acute appendicitis and is curative. While non-operative antibiotic management is an option in selected uncomplicated cases, the presence of an appendicolith predicts higher failure and recurrence rates, favoring surgery. Antibiotics alone or oral outpatient antibiotics are therefore not the best choice here. Percutaneous drainage is reserved for a contained abscess (complicated appendicitis), which she does not have. Prolonged observation delays definitive care and risks perforation. Early appendectomy minimizes morbidity in this otherwise healthy young patient.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If her appendectomy had been delayed and she developed diffuse abdominal rigidity with free air on imaging, this would most likely indicate which complication?
options: Perforation with generalized peritonitis
Meckel diverticulitis
Appendiceal mucocele
Mesenteric adenitis in this presentation
Resolved appendicitis
stages.explanation: Diffuse rigidity (a board-like abdomen) with free intraperitoneal air signifies perforation with spillage of luminal contents and generalized peritonitis, a surgical emergency requiring urgent operation and broad-spectrum antibiotics. An appendiceal mucocele is typically an indolent finding, not an acute peritonitis. Resolved appendicitis would show clinical improvement, not worsening. Meckel diverticulitis and mesenteric adenitis are alternative causes of right lower quadrant pain but do not explain free air in the context of a CT-confirmed appendicitis; free air points to a ruptured viscus, most consistent with perforated appendicitis here.

pearls: Periumbilical pain migrating to the right lower quadrant (McBurney point) with anorexia, nausea, and low-grade fever is the classic appendicitis sequence.
Always check beta-hCG in reproductive-age women with abdominal pain to exclude ectopic pregnancy before CT.
CT with contrast confirms the diagnosis; graded-compression ultrasound or MRI spares radiation in young or pregnant patients.
Laparoscopic appendectomy is definitive; an appendicolith predicts failure of antibiotic-only therapy.

cases.title: Right Upper Belly Pain After a Greasy Dinner
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe, constant right upper abdominal pain that began after a fatty meal and has lasted several hours.
cases.history: A 48-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with 8 hours of severe, constant right upper quadrant pain that started about an hour after a fried chicken dinner. The pain radiates to her right shoulder blade and is associated with nausea and two episodes of vomiting. She has had several similar but milder episodes over the past few months, usually after fatty meals, that resolved within an hour; this episode is more intense and has not abated. She denies fever at home but now feels feverish. She denies jaundice, dark urine, pale stools, chest pain, or dysuria. PMH includes obesity (BMI 31) and two prior pregnancies. Medications: a combined oral contraceptive pill and a daily multivitamin. She has no allergies. Social history: she does not smoke, drinks wine occasionally, and works as a teacher. Family history: her mother had her gallbladder removed. She has not had prior abdominal surgery. She reports that deep breaths make the pain sharper. Her last meal was the dinner that preceded symptoms; she has been unable to eat since due to nausea.
cases.exam: The patient appears uncomfortable and prefers to lie still. Sclerae are anicteric. Abdomen is soft but markedly tender in the right upper quadrant with voluntary guarding. Murphy sign is positive: deep palpation under the right costal margin during inspiration causes the patient to abruptly halt inhalation due to pain. There is no rebound elsewhere, no palpable mass, and no hepatomegaly. Bowel sounds are present. There is no costovertebral angle tenderness. There is no jaundice, and the lungs are clear bilaterally. No abdominal distension is noted.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Based on the history, fever, and a positive Murphy sign, which imaging study should be obtained first?
options: MRCP as the first step today
Hepatobiliary iminodiacetic acid (HIDA) scan
Right upper quadrant abdominal ultrasound
Abdominal radiograph series
CT abdomen without contrast
stages.explanation: Right upper quadrant ultrasound is the first-line imaging test for suspected acute cholecystitis: it is sensitive for gallstones, gallbladder wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, and a sonographic Murphy sign, and it is fast, radiation-free, and bedside-available. CT is less sensitive for stones and is reserved for ruling out other pathology or complications. A HIDA scan is the most specific test and is used when ultrasound is equivocal but cholecystitis is still suspected (non-visualization of the gallbladder confirms cystic duct obstruction). MRCP excels at ductal stones, not acute cholecystitis. Plain radiographs are low-yield since most gallstones are radiolucent.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 13.8 ×10³/µL with left shift
Total bilirubin 1.2 mg/dL (normal)
AST 34 U/L, ALT 38 U/L (normal)
Alkaline phosphatase 96 U/L (normal)
Lipase 42 U/L (normal)
RUQ ultrasound: multiple gallstones, gallbladder wall thickened to 5 mm, pericholecystic fluid, positive sonographic Murphy sign; common bile duct 4 mm (normal caliber)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Her labs show leukocytosis with normal bilirubin and liver enzymes, and ultrasound shows wall thickening with pericholecystic fluid and a normal-caliber CBD. Which diagnosis best fits?
options: Acute cholecystitis
Uncomplicated biliary colic
Choledocholithiasis
Ascending cholangitis
Acute pancreatitis
stages.explanation: The combination of persistent RUQ pain, fever, leukocytosis, a positive sonographic Murphy sign, gallbladder wall thickening, and pericholecystic fluid with a normal-caliber CBD and normal bilirubin/liver enzymes defines acute cholecystitis from cystic duct obstruction. Choledocholithiasis would show a dilated CBD and elevated bilirubin/alkaline phosphatase. Cholangitis requires evidence of biliary obstruction with systemic infection (Charcot triad: fever, jaundice, RUQ pain) and typically elevated bilirubin. Acute pancreatitis would show markedly elevated lipase. Biliary colic is transient pain without fever, leukocytosis, or inflammatory imaging findings, which this sustained, inflamed presentation exceeds.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate definitive management for this patient with acute cholecystitis?
options: Discharge on oral antibiotics with outpatient follow-up as initial therapy
Urgent ERCP as initial therapy
IV fluids, analgesia, IV antibiotics,
Ursodeoxycholic acid for stone dissolution as initial therapy
Percutaneous cholecystostomy as first-line in this stable patient
stages.explanation: Acute cholecystitis is managed with IV fluids, analgesia, and IV antibiotics, followed by early laparoscopic cholecystectomy during the same admission (ideally within 72 hours), which reduces complications and length of stay versus delayed surgery. Discharge on oral antibiotics is inappropriate for an acutely inflamed gallbladder with systemic signs. ERCP targets common bile duct stones, which she does not have (normal CBD, normal bilirubin). Percutaneous cholecystostomy is reserved for poor surgical candidates who are critically ill or have prohibitive operative risk, not a stable patient who can undergo surgery. Ursodeoxycholic acid is too slow and not appropriate for acute cholecystitis.

stages.label: RISK FACTORS
stages.question: Which set of features in this patient best reflects classic risk factors for gallstone disease?
options: Vegetarian diet and high fiber intake as the immediate next step
Chronic NSAID use and H. pylori infection as the immediate next step
Smoking and chronic alcohol use as the immediate next step
Male sex, young age, low BMI as the immediate next step
Female sex, age around 40, obesity, multiparity,
stages.explanation: The classic gallstone risk factors are summarized as the 'F's: Female, Forty, Fertile (multiparous), and Fat (obesity), with estrogen exposure (pregnancy, oral contraceptives, hormone therapy) promoting cholesterol stone formation. This patient is a 48-year-old obese woman with two pregnancies on a combined oral contraceptive, fitting the profile. Male sex, youth, and low BMI are not classic risks. Smoking and alcohol are not primary drivers of cholesterol gallstones. A high-fiber vegetarian diet is protective, not a risk. NSAID use and H. pylori relate to peptic ulcer disease, not gallstones.

pearls: A positive Murphy sign with RUQ pain, fever, and leukocytosis suggests acute cholecystitis; ultrasound is the first-line imaging test.
Ultrasound findings of gallstones, wall thickening (>3 mm), pericholecystic fluid, and a sonographic Murphy sign support the diagnosis.
Normal bilirubin and a normal-caliber CBD distinguish cholecystitis from choledocholithiasis and cholangitis.
Treat with IV fluids, antibiotics, and early laparoscopic cholecystectomy during the same admission; HIDA scan is the confirmatory test if ultrasound is equivocal.

cases.title: Yellowing Eyes With Right-Sided Belly Pain
cases.chiefComplaint: Right upper abdominal pain with yellowing of the eyes and dark urine.
cases.history: A 55-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with 2 days of intermittent right upper quadrant and epigastric pain accompanied by new yellowing of her eyes, dark 'tea-colored' urine, and pale stools. The pain is colicky and partly relieved between episodes. She reports nausea but no vomiting and no fever or rigors. She has a known history of gallstones diagnosed on a prior ultrasound but declined surgery. PMH includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily and atorvastatin 20 mg nightly. She has no allergies. Social history: she does not smoke, drinks alcohol rarely, and is retired. Family history is notable for gallstones in a sister. She has not had prior biliary surgery or ERCP. She denies pruritus initially but on questioning notes mild itching over the last day. Her appetite is reduced but she is tolerating sips of water. She is concerned because she has never been jaundiced before and the discoloration of her eyes appeared suddenly.
cases.exam: The patient is alert and oriented, mildly uncomfortable. Scleral icterus is present and the skin has a faint jaundiced tint. The abdomen is soft with mild right upper quadrant and epigastric tenderness but no rebound, no guarding, and a negative Murphy sign. There is no palpable gallbladder and no hepatosplenomegaly. Bowel sounds are normal. There is no asterixis and no spider angiomata. Lungs are clear and the cardiac exam is unremarkable. There are excoriations on the forearms consistent with mild pruritus. No costovertebral angle tenderness is noted.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given jaundice, dark urine, and pale stools without fever, which laboratory pattern would you most expect, and what is the best initial confirmatory imaging?
options: Conjugated hyperbilirubinemia with elevated alkaline phosphatase
Unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia; obtain a peripheral smear as the first step today
Elevated lipase with normal bilirubin; obtain a CT pancreas protocol in this clinical context
Isolated transaminase elevation in the thousands; obtain a viral hepatitis panel only
Normal liver tests; obtain a HIDA scan as the first step today
stages.explanation: Obstruction of the common bile duct by a stone produces a cholestatic (obstructive) pattern: conjugated (direct) hyperbilirubinemia with elevated alkaline phosphatase and GGT, dark urine (bilirubinuria), and pale stools. RUQ ultrasound is the best initial test to confirm gallstones and look for CBD dilation. Unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia with a smear suggests hemolysis, which does not cause pale stools or bilirubinuria. Transaminases in the thousands suggest acute hepatocellular injury (viral/ischemic/toxic), not stone obstruction. Markedly elevated lipase points to pancreatitis. Normal liver tests would not explain jaundice; a HIDA scan evaluates cystic duct obstruction, not choledocholithiasis.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Total bilirubin 5.6 mg/dL, direct bilirubin 4.3 mg/dL
Alkaline phosphatase 312 U/L (elevated)
GGT 410 U/L (elevated)
AST 120 U/L, ALT 145 U/L (mildly elevated)
Lipase 50 U/L (normal)
WBC 8.4 ×10³/µL (normal), no fever
RUQ ultrasound: cholelithiasis; common bile duct dilated to 9 mm; a stone is suspected in the distal CBD; intrahepatic ducts mildly dilated

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS & NEXT STEP
stages.question: Ultrasound shows a dilated 9 mm CBD with a suspected distal stone and a cholestatic lab pattern. According to risk stratification, she has a HIGH probability of choledocholithiasis. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Proceed directly to laparoscopic cholecystectomy without addressing the duct
Discharge with outpatient MRCP in 6 weeks before discharge planning
Start broad-spectrum antibiotics and observe indefinitely
Begin ursodeoxycholic acid and recheck labs in 3 months
Proceed to ERCP for stone extraction and biliary clearance
stages.explanation: She meets high-probability criteria for choledocholithiasis (CBD stone visualized/suspected on ultrasound, CBD >6 mm, and total bilirubin >4 mg/dL). The recommended step is ERCP, which is both diagnostic and therapeutic, allowing sphincterotomy and stone extraction to clear the duct, typically followed by cholecystectomy. Cholecystectomy alone leaves the obstructing ductal stone behind. A 6-week outpatient MRCP delays treatment of active obstruction; MRCP/EUS is reserved for intermediate-probability cases to decide whether ERCP is needed. Antibiotics with indefinite observation does not relieve obstruction and risks cholangitis. Ursodeoxycholic acid does not clear an obstructing duct stone.

stages.label: PROCEDURE TIMING
stages.question: She is hemodynamically stable, afebrile, and without signs of infection. What is the most appropriate timing of ERCP?
options: No ERCP; lifelong antibiotics instead as the immediate next step
ERCP only after the gallbladder is removed as the immediate next step
Semi-elective ERCP during this admission, before the planned cholecystectomy
Emergent ERCP within 2 hours regardless of stability in this setting
Defer ERCP for 3 months to allow spontaneous passage in this setting
stages.explanation: In a stable patient with choledocholithiasis but no cholangitis, ERCP is performed semi-electively during the same admission to clear the duct, followed by (often same-admission) laparoscopic cholecystectomy to prevent recurrent biliary events. Emergent within-hours ERCP is reserved for cholangitis or severe obstruction with sepsis, which she does not have. Performing ERCP only after cholecystectomy risks leaving the duct obstructed during and after surgery. Lifelong antibiotics do not treat mechanical obstruction. Deferring for 3 months risks progression to cholangitis or pancreatitis; while small stones can pass, a 9 mm dilated duct with a visualized stone warrants intervention.

stages.label: DEFINITIVE PLAN
stages.question: After successful ERCP with sphincterotomy and stone extraction, what is the recommended definitive management of her gallbladder?
options: Lifelong ursodeoxycholic acid only as the immediate next step
Laparoscopic cholecystectomy, ideally during the same admission
Percutaneous cholecystostomy as the immediate next step
Repeat ERCP in 6 months as the immediate next step
Leave the gallbladder in place indefinitely since the duct is now clear
stages.explanation: After ERCP clears the common bile duct, the gallbladder remains the source of stones, so laparoscopic cholecystectomy is recommended, ideally during the same hospitalization, to prevent recurrent choledocholithiasis, biliary colic, cholecystitis, and gallstone pancreatitis. Leaving the gallbladder in place carries a high rate of recurrent biliary events. Repeat ERCP is unnecessary once the duct is cleared unless symptoms recur. Ursodeoxycholic acid does not reliably prevent recurrent symptomatic stones and is not a substitute for cholecystectomy in a symptomatic patient. Percutaneous cholecystostomy is a temporizing drainage procedure for nonsurgical candidates, not definitive therapy here.

pearls: Choledocholithiasis causes obstructive jaundice: conjugated hyperbilirubinemia, elevated alkaline phosphatase/GGT, dark urine, and pale stools.
High-probability predictors include a CBD stone on ultrasound, CBD >6 mm, and total bilirubin >4 mg/dL — these warrant ERCP directly.
Intermediate-probability cases are best clarified with MRCP or endoscopic ultrasound before committing to ERCP.
ERCP with sphincterotomy clears the duct; follow with laparoscopic cholecystectomy (ideally same admission) to prevent recurrence.

cases.title: Fever, Jaundice, and Confusion in an Older Man
cases.chiefComplaint: High fevers with shaking chills, yellow eyes, and right upper abdominal pain, now with confusion.
cases.history: A 70-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by his daughter for 1 day of high fevers with rigors, right upper quadrant pain, and yellowing of the eyes. Over the last few hours he has become increasingly confused and lethargic, which prompted the visit. He had an episode of biliary colic 2 weeks ago and a prior ultrasound showed gallstones. PMH includes type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension. Medications: metformin 1000 mg twice daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily, and aspirin 81 mg daily. He has no known drug allergies. Social history: he is a retired electrician, drinks alcohol rarely, and does not smoke. Family history is non-contributory. His daughter reports his urine has been dark and his appetite poor. He has not had prior biliary surgery or stent placement. He was previously fully oriented and independent; the confusion is new today. He has had decreasing oral intake and one episode of vomiting. There is no recent travel, no sick contacts, and no recent antibiotic use.
cases.exam: The patient is lethargic but arousable, oriented only to person. He is icteric with visibly jaundiced skin. He is warm and diaphoretic. The abdomen is soft with marked right upper quadrant tenderness; Murphy sign is equivocal due to guarding. There is no rebound or rigidity and no palpable mass. Bowel sounds are present but reduced. Capillary refill is delayed at 3 seconds and the extremities are cool. There is no asterixis appreciated. Lungs are clear; heart is tachycardic and regular. There is no flank tenderness. Mucous membranes are dry.

stages.label: RECOGNITION
stages.question: He has fever, jaundice, and RUQ pain (Charcot triad) plus hypotension and altered mental status. What clinical syndrome does the full constellation represent?
options: Charcot triad only, indicating uncomplicated cholangitis in this setting
Reynolds pentad, indicating suppurative ascending cholangitis with septic shock
Acute viral hepatitis with encephalopathy as the immediate next step
Acute cholecystitis with sepsis as the immediate next step
Hepatic abscess with bacteremia as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Charcot triad (fever, jaundice, RUQ pain) plus hypotension and altered mental status constitutes Reynolds pentad, which signifies severe (suppurative) ascending cholangitis with septic shock — a biliary emergency. Charcot triad alone would describe cholangitis without the additional shock and confusion that he clearly has, so 'uncomplicated' is incorrect. Acute viral hepatitis typically presents with very high transaminases and a prodrome, not rigors with obstructive jaundice from a stone. Acute cholecystitis usually has a normal-caliber duct and normal bilirubin. A hepatic abscess can cause fever but does not typically produce the obstructive pentad with ductal dilation; the picture here is decompensating cholangitis.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 19.4 ×10³/µL with bandemia
Total bilirubin 6.8 mg/dL, direct 5.1 mg/dL
Alkaline phosphatase 388 U/L, GGT 460 U/L (elevated)
AST 110 U/L, ALT 130 U/L
Lactate 3.6 mmol/L (elevated)
Creatinine 1.8 mg/dL (baseline 1.0)
Blood cultures drawn ×2
RUQ ultrasound: dilated CBD to 12 mm with an obstructing distal stone; intrahepatic biliary dilation

stages.label: INITIAL MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He is hypotensive with a lactate of 3.6 and acute kidney injury. What is the most appropriate immediate management before definitive biliary intervention?
options: Aggressive IV crystalloid resuscitation, blood cultures,
Immediate ERCP without resuscitation as the immediate next step
Urgent laparoscopic cholecystectomy as the first intervention in this setting
Vasopressors alone without fluids or antibiotics as the immediate next step
Oral ciprofloxacin and discharge planning as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Severe cholangitis with septic shock requires immediate sepsis resuscitation: IV crystalloid fluids, blood cultures, and prompt empiric broad-spectrum IV antibiotics covering enteric Gram-negatives and anaerobes (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam), alongside hemodynamic support. Stabilization should precede the procedure when feasible, though biliary decompression must not be unduly delayed. Going to ERCP without any resuscitation is unsafe in a hypotensive patient. Oral antibiotics and discharge are dangerous in septic shock. Cholecystectomy is not the emergent intervention; the duct must be decompressed first. Vasopressors without fluids or source-directed antibiotics fail to treat the underlying sepsis.

stages.label: DEFINITIVE INTERVENTION
stages.question: After initial resuscitation and antibiotics, his cholangitis is severe and not improving. What is the definitive treatment to control the source?
options: Urgent biliary decompression, preferably by ERCP with stone extraction or stent placement
Continue antibiotics alone and reassess in 48 hours as the immediate next step
Immediate open common bile duct exploration in all patients as the immediate next step
Elective MRCP in the morning as the immediate next step
Percutaneous liver biopsy as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: The cornerstone of treatment for ascending cholangitis is urgent biliary decompression, preferably via ERCP with stone extraction and/or stent placement, especially when the patient is not responding to antibiotics or has severe disease. Antibiotics alone do not relieve the obstruction driving the sepsis and will fail without drainage. MRCP is purely diagnostic and would delay therapy. Open CBD exploration is reserved for cases where endoscopic drainage fails or is unavailable; percutaneous transhepatic drainage is the alternative if ERCP cannot be performed. Liver biopsy plays no role in acute cholangitis and would be hazardous in this septic, coagulopathy-prone patient.

stages.label: ANTIBIOTIC TARGET
stages.question: Empiric antibiotics for ascending cholangitis should reliably cover which organisms most commonly responsible?
options: Candida species as the primary pathogens before discharge planning
Pseudomonas only as the immediate next step
Atypical organisms such as Mycoplasma as the immediate next step
Staphylococcus aureus and Streptococcus pyogenes in this setting
Enteric Gram-negative bacilli
stages.explanation: Ascending cholangitis is most commonly caused by enteric organisms ascending from the duodenum: Escherichia coli and Klebsiella species are the leading Gram-negative pathogens, with Enterococcus and anaerobes (including Bacteroides) also implicated, so empiric therapy must cover these (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam or a third-generation cephalosporin plus metronidazole). Skin flora like S. aureus and S. pyogenes are not typical biliary pathogens. Pseudomonas coverage is considered in healthcare-associated or post-instrumentation cases but is not the sole target. Atypicals such as Mycoplasma cause respiratory infections, not cholangitis. Candida is an uncommon biliary pathogen, usually in immunocompromised or chronically instrumented patients, not first-line empiric coverage.

pearls: Charcot triad (fever, jaundice, RUQ pain) suggests cholangitis; adding hypotension and altered mental status makes Reynolds pentad, indicating septic shock.
Management priorities are sepsis resuscitation with fluids plus empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics, then urgent biliary decompression.
ERCP with stone extraction/stenting is the preferred decompression; percutaneous transhepatic drainage or surgery is used if ERCP fails.
The usual pathogens are enteric Gram-negatives (E. coli, Klebsiella) plus anaerobes and enterococci ascending from the gut.

cases.title: Epigastric Pain Boring Through to the Back
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe constant upper abdominal pain radiating to the back with vomiting.
cases.history: A 60-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with 12 hours of severe, constant epigastric pain that bores straight through to her back and is partially relieved by leaning forward. She has had multiple episodes of vomiting and cannot keep anything down. She reports no recent alcohol use and rarely drinks. She has a known history of gallstones on a prior ultrasound and intermittent biliary colic after fatty meals. PMH includes hyperlipidemia and obesity (BMI 33). Medications: atorvastatin 40 mg nightly and a daily multivitamin; she takes no thiazide diuretics. She has no allergies. Social history: she does not smoke and drinks alcohol fewer than 2 times per month. Family history includes gallstones in her mother. She has not had abdominal surgery. She denies hematemesis or melena. The pain is the worst she has experienced and is unrelieved by over-the-counter analgesics. She last ate a fried meal about 14 hours ago. There is no chest pain and no shortness of breath, though she feels lightheaded after vomiting.
cases.exam: The patient appears in significant distress, lying still and leaning forward when able. The abdomen is tender in the epigastrium with voluntary guarding but no rebound and no rigidity. Bowel sounds are diminished. There is no scleral icterus appreciated yet. There is no Cullen or Grey Turner sign. There is no palpable mass and no costovertebral angle tenderness. Mucous membranes are dry and skin turgor is reduced, consistent with volume depletion. The lungs are clear with mildly decreased breath sounds at the left base. Heart is tachycardic and regular.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which combination of findings is required to establish a diagnosis of acute pancreatitis (needing 2 of 3 criteria)?
options: Characteristic epigastric pain radiating to the back, lipase or amylase greater than 3 times the upper limit of normal, and/or characteristic imaging findings
Hematemesis, melena, and a drop in hemoglobin as the primary diagnosis
Diarrhea, weight loss, and steatorrhea as the primary diagnosis
Jaundice, elevated alkaline phosphatase, and dilated CBD as the primary diagnosis
Fever, leukocytosis, and a positive Murphy sign as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Acute pancreatitis is diagnosed when at least 2 of 3 criteria are met: (1) characteristic abdominal pain (epigastric, radiating to the back), (2) serum lipase or amylase greater than 3 times the upper limit of normal, and (3) characteristic findings on cross-sectional imaging (CT/MRI/ultrasound). Fever, leukocytosis, and Murphy sign describe cholecystitis. Jaundice, elevated alkaline phosphatase, and a dilated duct describe biliary obstruction/choledocholithiasis. Hematemesis and melena with anemia indicate GI bleeding. Diarrhea, weight loss, and steatorrhea suggest chronic pancreatic insufficiency, not acute pancreatitis. Lipase is more specific and remains elevated longer than amylase.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Lipase 1240 U/L (>3× upper limit of normal)
Amylase 680 U/L (elevated)
WBC 15.6 ×10³/µL
Total bilirubin 2.4 mg/dL, ALT 220 U/L (>3× normal), AST 180 U/L
Alkaline phosphatase 240 U/L (elevated)
Triglycerides 180 mg/dL (normal)
Calcium 9.2 mg/dL (normal)
BUN 28 mg/dL, creatinine 1.3 mg/dL
RUQ ultrasound: cholelithiasis; CBD 7 mm; no definite ductal stone visualized

stages.label: ETIOLOGY
stages.question: Her lipase is markedly elevated and ALT is more than 3 times normal with gallstones on ultrasound. What is the most likely etiology of her pancreatitis?
options: Alcohol-induced pancreatitis in this setting
Hypercalcemia-induced pancreatitis
Hypertriglyceridemia-induced pancreatitis
Medication-induced (thiazide) pancreatitis
Gallstone (biliary) pancreatitis
stages.explanation: Gallstones and alcohol cause most acute pancreatitis. This patient drinks minimally, but has documented cholelithiasis, and an ALT greater than 3 times normal is a strong predictor of a biliary etiology, making gallstone pancreatitis most likely. Alcohol is unlikely given her minimal intake. Her triglycerides are normal (hypertriglyceridemic pancreatitis typically requires levels above ~1000 mg/dL). Her calcium is normal, excluding hypercalcemia. She takes no thiazide diuretics, so medication-induced pancreatitis is not supported. The transient transaminase elevation reflects passage of a stone through the ampulla, the hallmark of biliary pancreatitis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most important initial therapeutic intervention for her acute pancreatitis in the first 24 hours?
options: Goal-directed IV fluid resuscitation with isotonic crystalloid plus analgesia
Prophylactic broad-spectrum antibiotics as initial therapy
Immediate ERCP for all patients with gallstone pancreatitis as initial therapy
Urgent cholecystectomy within the first hour as initial therapy
Strict prolonged bowel rest with total parenteral nutrition from admission
stages.explanation: Early goal-directed IV fluid resuscitation with isotonic crystalloid (e.g., lactated Ringer's), along with pain control and antiemetics, is the cornerstone of early management and improves outcomes by maintaining pancreatic perfusion. Routine early ERCP is not indicated unless there is concurrent cholangitis or persistent biliary obstruction; her duct is only 7 mm without a visualized stone. Prophylactic antibiotics are not recommended in acute pancreatitis without infected necrosis or cholangitis. Early feeding is now favored once tolerated rather than prolonged bowel rest with TPN. Cholecystectomy is indicated, but during the same admission after the pancreatitis improves, not within the first hour.

stages.label: SEVERITY & DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which finding would most strongly indicate severe disease warranting ICU-level monitoring in acute pancreatitis?
options: ALT elevation greater than 3 times normal as the immediate next step
Presence of gallstones on ultrasound as the immediate next step
Persistent organ failure such as hypotension, hypoxemia, or rising creatinine ,
Lipase value of 1240 U/L as the immediate next step
Mild epigastric tenderness on exam as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Severity in acute pancreatitis is defined by persistent organ failure (cardiovascular, respiratory, or renal lasting more than 48 hours), not by the absolute enzyme level. Markers such as a rising BUN/creatinine, hypotension, hypoxemia, hemoconcentration, and a high BISAP score (BUN >25, impaired mental status, SIRS, age >60, pleural effusion) predict severe disease and the need for ICU monitoring. The magnitude of lipase elevation does not correlate with severity or prognosis. The mere presence of gallstones identifies etiology, not severity. An ALT >3× normal points to a biliary cause, not severity. Mild tenderness alone does not denote severe disease.

pearls: Diagnose acute pancreatitis with 2 of 3: typical pain, lipase/amylase >3× upper limit, or characteristic imaging.
An ALT >3× normal with gallstones strongly suggests a biliary etiology; lipase magnitude does NOT predict severity.
Early goal-directed isotonic crystalloid resuscitation plus analgesia is the mainstay; avoid routine prophylactic antibiotics.
Reserve urgent ERCP for concurrent cholangitis or persistent obstruction; perform cholecystectomy during the same admission once recovered.

cases.title: Recurrent Upper Belly Pain in a Heavy Drinker
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe epigastric pain and vomiting after a weekend of heavy drinking.
cases.history: A 45-year-old man is admitted to the medical ward with 1 day of severe epigastric pain radiating to the back, nausea, and vomiting following a weekend binge of heavy alcohol consumption. He has had two prior similar admissions over the past 3 years, each after binge drinking. He reports drinking 8-12 standard drinks daily for over a decade with weekend binges. PMH includes a prior episode of acute pancreatitis and hypertension. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily; he is not on chronic opioids. He has no allergies. Social history: he smokes half a pack per day and is unemployed; he denies recreational drug use. Family history is non-contributory. He has had no recent gallstone diagnosis and no abdominal surgery. He has not been able to tolerate oral intake since the pain began. He denies hematemesis. On admission he is started on IV fluids, analgesia, and antiemetics. By hospital day 4 his pain persists and he develops a low-grade fever, raising concern for a complication. He has been NPO since admission and is asking when he can eat.
cases.exam: The patient is uncomfortable and mildly diaphoretic. The abdomen is distended with epigastric tenderness and voluntary guarding; bowel sounds are hypoactive. There is no rebound or rigidity and no palpable mass. There is no scleral icterus. No Cullen or Grey Turner sign is present. The lungs have decreased breath sounds at both bases. Heart is mildly tachycardic and regular. There is no peripheral edema. Mucous membranes are slightly dry. He has a fine tremor in the hands and is mildly anxious but oriented, without asterixis. No spider angiomata are noted.

stages.label: ETIOLOGY & WORKUP
stages.question: Given his drinking history and prior episodes, alcohol is the likely cause. Which laboratory pattern is most characteristic of alcohol-related pancreatitis as opposed to gallstone pancreatitis?
options: Eosinophilia with elevated IgG4 as the immediate next step
ALT and alkaline phosphatase markedly elevated with a dilated CBD in this setting
Elevated serum calcium with a low PTH as the immediate next step
Lipase elevated with a relatively higher AST:ALT ratio
Markedly elevated triglycerides above 1000 mg/dL as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: In alcohol-related pancreatitis, lipase is elevated and the liver tests often show an AST:ALT ratio greater than 2 (reflecting alcohol effect) without the biliary obstruction pattern; ultrasound typically shows no ductal dilation or stones. A markedly elevated ALT and alkaline phosphatase with a dilated CBD points to gallstone pancreatitis. Triglycerides above 1000 indicate hypertriglyceridemic pancreatitis. High calcium with low PTH suggests hypercalcemia-induced pancreatitis. Eosinophilia with elevated IgG4 suggests autoimmune pancreatitis. The clinical context (chronic heavy alcohol use, recurrent episodes, no gallstones) plus a high AST:ALT ratio supports an alcoholic etiology here.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING (HOSPITAL DAY 4)
stages.content:
Lipase 900 U/L (still elevated)
WBC 16.8 ×10³/µL with left shift
AST 160 U/L, ALT 60 U/L (AST:ALT ratio ~2.7)
Total bilirubin 1.1 mg/dL (normal), alkaline phosphatase 110 U/L (normal)
Triglycerides 210 mg/dL, calcium 9.0 mg/dL (both normal)
CT abdomen with contrast: diffuse pancreatic enlargement with peripancreatic fluid and a focal area of non-enhancing pancreatic parenchyma (~30%) consistent with necrosis; no gas in the collection
Blood cultures pending

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: CT on day 4 shows a non-enhancing area of pancreas (~30%) without gas, and he now has fever and leukocytosis. What is the most appropriate next step regarding the necrosis?
options: Immediate open surgical necrosectomy as the leading concern
Place a percutaneous drain in all necrotic collections on day 4 as the leading concern
Begin empiric antibiotics for all sterile necrosis indefinitely as the leading concern
Continue supportive care and obtain blood cultures
Start prophylactic antifungals immediately as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Most pancreatic necrosis early on is sterile; antibiotics are not indicated for sterile necrosis. The appropriate approach is supportive care, cultures, and close monitoring; if infected necrosis is documented (e.g., gas in the collection, positive cultures, clinical deterioration), start carbapenem-class antibiotics and pursue a delayed, minimally invasive 'step-up' approach (percutaneous/endoscopic drainage first, necrosectomy only if needed), ideally after ~4 weeks when the collection walls off. Immediate open necrosectomy carries high mortality and is avoided early. Prophylactic antifungals and indefinite empiric antibiotics for sterile necrosis are not recommended. Routine early drainage of all necrosis is not indicated; intervention is reserved for infection or symptomatic walled-off collections.

stages.label: NUTRITION
stages.question: He has been NPO for several days and asks about eating. What is the most appropriate nutrition strategy?
options: Maintain strict prolonged NPO with total parenteral nutrition as first choice in this setting
Initiate enteral nutrition early as tolerated, preferring the gut over parenteral nutrition
Withhold all nutrition until lipase fully normalizes as the immediate next step
Use parenteral nutrition routinely to 'rest the pancreas' as the immediate next step
Begin a high-fat diet immediately to restore calories as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Current evidence favors early enteral nutrition in acute pancreatitis: oral feeding as tolerated in mild disease, and nasogastric or nasojejunal enteral feeding in severe disease, because enteral nutrition maintains gut mucosal integrity, reduces infectious complications, and lowers mortality compared with parenteral nutrition. The old practice of prolonged NPO and routine TPN to 'rest the pancreas' is no longer recommended and increases infection risk. A high-fat diet is inappropriate during recovery. Waiting for complete lipase normalization is unnecessary; feeding is guided by tolerance and clinical status, not by enzyme values alone. Parenteral nutrition is reserved for those who cannot tolerate enteral feeding.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: As he recovers, which intervention is most important to prevent recurrent episodes and long-term complications?
options: Prophylactic cholecystectomy with appropriate follow-up
Routine annual ERCP with appropriate follow-up
Chronic high-dose opioid therapy based on current risk
Lifelong prophylactic antibiotics based on current risk
Complete alcohol cessation with counseling/treatment
stages.explanation: For alcohol-related pancreatitis, the single most important intervention is complete alcohol cessation, supported by counseling and addiction treatment, which dramatically reduces recurrent attacks and progression to chronic pancreatitis; smoking cessation is also critical because tobacco independently accelerates pancreatic damage. Lifelong prophylactic antibiotics have no role. Routine annual ERCP is unnecessary and carries procedural risk. Chronic high-dose opioids risk dependence and do not address the cause. Prophylactic cholecystectomy is indicated in gallstone pancreatitis, not in an alcoholic etiology without gallstones. Addressing the modifiable behavioral risk factors is the key to preventing recurrence and chronic disease.

pearls: Alcohol and gallstones cause most acute pancreatitis; an AST:ALT ratio >2 without biliary dilation supports an alcoholic etiology.
Sterile necrosis does not need antibiotics; reserve carbapenem antibiotics and a delayed minimally invasive step-up approach for infected necrosis.
Favor early enteral nutrition over parenteral; prolonged NPO/TPN to 'rest the pancreas' is outdated and raises infection risk.
Alcohol and smoking cessation are the most important steps to prevent recurrence and progression to chronic pancreatitis.

cases.title: Chronic Belly Pain With Greasy, Floating Stools
cases.chiefComplaint: Months of recurring upper abdominal pain with weight loss and bulky, foul, greasy stools.
cases.history: A 52-year-old man presents to gastroenterology clinic with several months of recurrent dull epigastric pain radiating to the back, along with 7 kg of unintentional weight loss. He describes bulky, malodorous, greasy stools that float and are difficult to flush (steatorrhea). He has a long history of heavy alcohol use (about a pint of spirits daily for 20 years) and has had two prior hospitalizations for acute pancreatitis. PMH includes type 2 diabetes mellitus diagnosed last year. Medications: metformin 500 mg twice daily and an as-needed acetaminophen; he is not on enzyme supplements. He has no allergies. Social history: he continues to drink heavily and smokes one pack per day. Family history is non-contributory. He has not had pancreatic surgery. He reports the pain is chronic with intermittent flares and that the diarrhea worsens after fatty meals. His appetite is fair but he avoids eating because of pain. He has noticed his diabetes is hard to control. He has no fevers, no jaundice, and no hematemesis.
cases.exam: The patient is thin with temporal wasting and appears chronically ill but is in no acute distress. The abdomen is soft with mild epigastric tenderness and no rebound, guarding, or palpable mass. Bowel sounds are normal. There is no scleral icterus and no hepatosplenomegaly. There is no ascites or peripheral edema. Skin shows no jaundice. The conjunctivae are slightly pale. Neurologic exam reveals diminished vibration sense at the toes, consistent with a possible nutritional or diabetic neuropathy. There is no asterixis and no spider angiomata. Muscle bulk is reduced in the extremities.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given chronic epigastric pain, steatorrhea, weight loss, new diabetes, and a heavy alcohol history, which single imaging finding would be most specific for chronic pancreatitis?
options: A dilated common bile duct as the primary diagnosis
Pancreatic calcifications on CT
Free intraperitoneal air as the primary diagnosis
Gallstones in the gallbladder as the primary diagnosis
Hepatic steatosis as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Pancreatic calcifications on imaging are a hallmark of chronic pancreatitis and, together with main pancreatic duct dilation, ductal irregularity, and parenchymal atrophy, are highly specific. CT is a good initial test; MRCP or endoscopic ultrasound is used when CT is non-diagnostic. Gallstones suggest biliary disease, not chronic pancreatitis per se. A dilated common bile duct points to biliary obstruction. Hepatic steatosis reflects fatty liver, common in alcohol use but not specific for pancreatitis. Free intraperitoneal air indicates a perforated viscus, an acute surgical finding unrelated to the chronic, calcific glandular destruction seen here.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
CT abdomen: diffuse pancreatic calcifications, dilated and irregular main pancreatic duct, parenchymal atrophy
Fecal elastase-1: 80 µg/g stool (low; <100 indicates severe exocrine insufficiency)
Hemoglobin A1c 8.6%
Hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL (low)
Fat-soluble vitamins: vitamin D 18 ng/mL (low)
Fecal fat: elevated (confirms steatorrhea)

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His fecal elastase is low and CT confirms chronic pancreatitis with steatorrhea. What is the most appropriate treatment for his exocrine pancreatic insufficiency?
options: A low-fat, low-calorie diet alone as initial therapy
Pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy taken with meals
High-dose proton pump inhibitor as monotherapy
Long-term broad-spectrum antibiotics as initial therapy
Octreotide infusion as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Exocrine pancreatic insufficiency causing steatorrhea is treated with pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT) — lipase-containing enzyme capsules taken with every meal and snack — which corrects fat malabsorption, improves nutrition, and reduces steatorrhea; fat-soluble vitamin (A, D, E, K) supplementation addresses deficiencies. A low-fat, low-calorie diet alone worsens malnutrition and does not replace missing enzymes. Antibiotics treat bacterial overgrowth, not enzyme deficiency. A PPI may be added as an adjunct to optimize enzyme activity by reducing acid degradation but is not effective as monotherapy. Octreotide is used for select secretory or fistula problems, not routine exocrine insufficiency.

stages.label: PAIN & RISK
stages.question: Which intervention is most important to slow disease progression and address the underlying cause in this patient?
options: Routine surgical pancreatectomy for all patients
Strict alcohol and tobacco cessation
Continue current alcohol intake but add enzymes
High-dose vitamin C supplementation in this setting
Chronic daily opioids as the primary strategy
stages.explanation: Alcohol and tobacco cessation are the most important interventions in alcoholic chronic pancreatitis: continued alcohol use accelerates glandular destruction and pain, and smoking is an independent risk factor for progression and pancreatic cancer. Continuing to drink while adding enzymes does not halt the disease. Surgical pancreatectomy or drainage procedures are reserved for refractory pain or specific complications (obstruction, pseudocyst), not routine first-line therapy. Chronic opioids carry dependence risk and should be part of a multimodal plan, not the primary strategy. High-dose vitamin C has no established disease-modifying role. Lifestyle modification combined with enzyme replacement and nutritional support is the foundation of management.

stages.label: COMPLICATION SURVEILLANCE
stages.question: His new, difficult-to-control diabetes in the setting of chronic pancreatitis is best classified as which type, and what additional long-term risk requires vigilance?
options: Type 1 autoimmune diabetes; risk of thyroid disease as the immediate next step
Steroid-induced diabetes; risk of adrenal insufficiency as the immediate next step
Maturity-onset diabetes of the young; risk of renal cysts as the immediate next step
Gestational diabetes; risk of macrosomia as the immediate next step
Pancreatogenic diabetes from islet loss
stages.explanation: Diabetes arising from chronic pancreatitis results from progressive destruction of insulin-producing islet cells and is classified as pancreatogenic (type 3c) diabetes; it is often brittle because glucagon-producing cells are also lost, increasing hypoglycemia risk with treatment. Patients with chronic pancreatitis, especially with continued smoking and alcohol use, have a substantially elevated long-term risk of pancreatic adenocarcinoma, warranting vigilance for alarm symptoms. Type 1 autoimmune diabetes, gestational diabetes, steroid-induced diabetes, and MODY have different mechanisms and associations that do not fit this destructive, calcific pancreatitis picture. Recognizing type 3c diabetes guides careful glycemic management and reinforces cancer-risk counseling.

pearls: The classic triad of chronic pancreatitis is abdominal pain, steatorrhea (exocrine insufficiency), and diabetes (endocrine insufficiency).
Pancreatic calcifications with ductal dilation and atrophy on CT are highly specific; low fecal elastase confirms exocrine insufficiency.
Treat steatorrhea with pancreatic enzyme replacement at every meal/snack plus fat-soluble vitamin supplementation.
Alcohol and tobacco cessation slow progression; chronic pancreatitis raises the long-term risk of pancreatic adenocarcinoma and type 3c diabetes.

cases.title: Left Lower Belly Pain With Fever and Change in Bowels
cases.chiefComplaint: Aching pain in the left lower abdomen with fever and a change in bowel habits.
cases.history: A 63-year-old man presents to the emergency department with 2 days of steady, aching left lower quadrant pain, low-grade fevers, and a sensation of bloating. He reports mild constipation over the past few days and one episode of loose stool today. He denies hematochezia, vomiting, dysuria, or hematuria. He has had no prior episodes like this. PMH includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia; a screening colonoscopy 3 years ago showed scattered sigmoid diverticulosis and was otherwise normal. Medications: lisinopril 20 mg daily and atorvastatin 20 mg nightly. He has no allergies. Social history: he eats a low-fiber Western diet, does not smoke, drinks alcohol socially, and works as an accountant. Family history is non-contributory for inflammatory bowel disease and colon cancer. He has not had abdominal surgery. He is tolerating sips of liquids and his appetite is reduced. He reports no recent travel and no antibiotic use. He is otherwise functional and lives at home with his spouse, who can monitor him.
cases.exam: The patient appears mildly uncomfortable but well. The abdomen is soft with localized tenderness in the left lower quadrant and mild voluntary guarding; there is no rebound, no rigidity, and no diffuse peritonitis. No mass is palpable, though there is fullness in the left lower quadrant. Bowel sounds are present and normal. There is no costovertebral angle tenderness. Rectal exam shows brown stool that is negative for occult blood and no tenderness or mass. The remainder of the abdomen is non-tender. There is no inguinal hernia and no abdominal distension beyond mild bloating.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given localized LLQ pain, fever, and known diverticulosis, what is the most appropriate confirmatory imaging study?
options: Barium enema as the first step today
Colonoscopy during the acute episode
Plain abdominal radiograph alone
Upper endoscopy as the first step today
CT abdomen and pelvis with IV contrast
stages.explanation: CT of the abdomen and pelvis with contrast is the imaging test of choice for suspected acute diverticulitis: it confirms the diagnosis (sigmoid wall thickening, pericolic fat stranding, diverticula), assesses severity, and detects complications such as abscess, perforation, or fistula. Colonoscopy is contraindicated during acute diverticulitis because of perforation risk; it is performed several weeks after resolution to exclude malignancy. A barium enema is also avoided acutely due to perforation/leak risk. Upper endoscopy evaluates the foregut and is irrelevant to colonic disease. A plain radiograph is insensitive and only useful to detect free air, so CT is far superior for diagnosis and staging.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 13.2 ×10³/µL with left shift
Hemoglobin 14.0 g/dL
Lactate 1.4 mmol/L (normal)
Urinalysis: unremarkable
CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast: sigmoid diverticulosis with focal wall thickening and pericolic fat stranding; no abscess, no free air, no obstruction (Hinchey stage consistent with uncomplicated diverticulitis)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS & DISPOSITION
stages.question: CT confirms acute uncomplicated diverticulitis in a stable patient who tolerates oral intake and has good home support. What is the most appropriate disposition and treatment?
options: CT-guided percutaneous drainage now as the immediate next step
Emergent sigmoid colectomy as the immediate next step
Immediate colonoscopy to confirm the diagnosis as the immediate next step
Outpatient management with oral intake as tolerated, close follow-up,
Admit for IV antibiotics and bowel rest in all cases as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Selected patients with CT-confirmed acute uncomplicated diverticulitis who are clinically stable, can tolerate oral intake, have adequate support, and lack significant comorbidities can be managed as outpatients with close follow-up. Guidelines now support a selective (rather than routine) approach to antibiotics in mild uncomplicated disease, though oral antibiotics are commonly used for patients with comorbidities or more pronounced inflammation. Routine admission for IV antibiotics is reserved for complicated disease, intolerance of oral intake, immunosuppression, or failure of outpatient therapy. Emergent colectomy and percutaneous drainage address complicated disease (perforation, large abscess), which he does not have. Colonoscopy is deferred to after recovery.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which finding on presentation would have indicated COMPLICATED diverticulitis and changed management away from outpatient care?
options: A single episode of loose stool as the leading concern
Low-grade fever of 38.1 °C as the leading concern
An abscess, free perforation with peritonitis, obstruction, or fistula on CT
Localized left lower quadrant tenderness without rebound
Mild pericolic fat stranding only as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Complicated diverticulitis is defined by an abscess, free perforation with peritonitis, bowel obstruction, or fistula formation on imaging; any of these mandates inpatient care, IV antibiotics, and possible drainage or surgery rather than outpatient management. Mild pericolic fat stranding without these features is the hallmark of uncomplicated disease. A single loose stool and a low-grade fever are nonspecific and do not denote complication. Localized LLQ tenderness without rebound or peritoneal signs is consistent with uncomplicated diverticulitis. Recognizing complicated features is what triages a patient out of the outpatient pathway and toward admission and procedural intervention.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: After his acute episode resolves, what is the most appropriate follow-up recommendation if he has not had a recent quality colonoscopy?
options: Repeat CT every month indefinitely as the immediate next step
No further evaluation is ever needed as the immediate next step
Lifelong daily antibiotics as the immediate next step
Colonoscopy in roughly 6-8 weeks after resolution to exclude colorectal malignancy, plus a high-fiber diet for prevention
Immediate prophylactic colectomy after a single episode as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: After an episode of acute diverticulitis (particularly the first, or if no recent quality colonoscopy), a colonoscopy is recommended approximately 6-8 weeks after resolution to exclude an underlying colorectal malignancy that can mimic or coexist with diverticulitis; long-term prevention emphasizes a high-fiber diet and physical activity. Saying no evaluation is ever needed ignores the small but real cancer risk. Monthly CT scans expose the patient to unnecessary radiation. Lifelong daily antibiotics are not indicated and promote resistance. Elective colectomy is considered individually for recurrent or complicated disease, not reflexively after a single uncomplicated episode. His prior colonoscopy was 3 years ago, so confirmatory follow-up imaging of the colon is reasonable.

pearls: Left lower quadrant pain with fever and leukocytosis in an older adult suggests acute (sigmoid) diverticulitis; CT with contrast is the confirmatory test.
Avoid colonoscopy and barium enema during the acute episode because of perforation risk.
Stable, uncomplicated diverticulitis in reliable patients can be managed outpatient; antibiotics are increasingly selective in mild disease.
After recovery, perform colonoscopy in 6-8 weeks (if none recent) to exclude malignancy, and recommend a high-fiber diet for prevention.

cases.title: Yellow Newborn With Pale Stools
cases.chiefComplaint: Persistent yellow skin and pale-colored stools since shortly after birth.
cases.history: A 6-week-old term female infant is brought in by her parents for jaundice that began around day 3 of life and has not resolved. The mother initially attributed it to breastfeeding, but the baby's eyes and skin remain yellow. Over the past two weeks the parents noticed her stools have become pale, almost clay-colored, while her urine appears dark and stains the diaper. The infant feeds well, is exclusively breastfed, and has gained weight appropriately. There is no fever, vomiting, or lethargy. She was born at 39 weeks via uncomplicated vaginal delivery; birth weight 3.2 kg. Newborn screen was reportedly normal. Vitamin K was given at birth. No prior hospitalizations. Medications: vitamin D drops. No known allergies. Family history is unremarkable; parents are non-consanguineous. Social: lives with both parents, no sick contacts, immunizations up to date for age. The pregnancy was unremarkable with normal prenatal ultrasounds.
cases.exam: Alert, well-appearing infant in no distress. Scleral icterus and generalized jaundice extending below the umbilicus. Abdomen soft, non-distended; liver edge palpable 3 cm below the right costal margin with a firm consistency; spleen tip palpable. No ascites. No dysmorphic features. Anterior fontanelle soft and flat. Cardiac exam normal without murmur. Skin without petechiae or hemangiomas. Diaper contains a pale, acholic stool and dark urine. Neurologic exam age-appropriate with normal tone and a strong suck. No cataracts on red reflex testing.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most important first laboratory step in evaluating this jaundiced 6-week-old?
options: Obtain a total and direct (conjugated) bilirubin to characterize the hyperbilirubinemia
Order a peripheral smear and reticulocyte count to evaluate for hemolysis
Start phototherapy empirically for presumed unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia
Reassure the parents that this is physiologic breast-milk jaundice and recheck in 4 weeks
stages.explanation: Any infant jaundiced beyond 2 weeks of age requires fractionation of bilirubin into total and direct (conjugated) components — this is the pivotal branch point. A direct bilirubin >1 mg/dL or >20% of total defines cholestasis and mandates urgent workup. Reassurance (B) is dangerous: breast-milk jaundice is unconjugated and should not persist with acholic stools — assuming it delays diagnosis of biliary atresia, where outcomes are time-dependent. Phototherapy (C) treats unconjugated bilirubin and is contraindicated/ineffective in conjugated hyperbilirubinemia (risk of bronze-baby syndrome). Hemolysis workup (D) addresses unconjugated jaundice and ignores the acholic stools/dark urine that scream cholestasis. Fractionation comes first.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Before fractionation results return, which finding on history/exam would most increase your concern for a surgical biliary cause rather than a treatable medical cholestasis?
options: A history of vitamin K administration at birth
Exclusive breastfeeding in this setting
Mild physiologic weight loss in the first week
Persistently acholic (clay-colored) stools
stages.explanation: Persistently acholic stools indicate that little or no bile (and thus stercobilin pigment) is reaching the gut — the hallmark of an obstructed/atretic extrahepatic biliary tree, which is a surgical problem requiring urgent evaluation. Consistently pale stools should always trigger workup for biliary atresia. Early physiologic weight loss (B) is normal and nonspecific. Vitamin K at birth (C) is routine prophylaxis and, if anything, reassures that coagulopathy from fat-soluble vitamin malabsorption hasn't yet caused bleeding — it doesn't point toward or away from a surgical cause. Exclusive breastfeeding (D) might raise the question of breast-milk jaundice, but that is unconjugated and does not cause acholic stools. The stool color is the single most useful bedside discriminator.

stages.label: INITIAL LABS
stages.content:
Total bilirubin: 9.8 mg/dL
Direct (conjugated) bilirubin: 6.2 mg/dL
AST: 210 U/L
ALT: 180 U/L
GGT: 720 U/L
Alkaline phosphatase: 560 U/L
Albumin: 3.6 g/dL
INR: 1.1 (normal after vitamin K)
TSH/T4: normal
Urine and serum reducing substances: negative
Alpha-1-antitrypsin level and phenotype: normal (PiMM)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given conjugated hyperbilirubinemia with markedly elevated GGT, acholic stools, and a firm liver, which imaging study is the best next step?
options: MRI of the brain to evaluate for kernicterus as the primary diagnosis
CT abdomen with contrast to characterize the liver parenchyma
Abdominal ultrasound, ideally after a 4-hour fast, to assess the gallbladder
Plain abdominal radiograph to look for bowel obstruction in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Abdominal ultrasound is the first-line imaging study for neonatal cholestasis.
