stages.explanation: Prevention centers on trigger avoidance: avoid porphyrinogenic drugs (barbiturates, sulfonamides, many anticonvulsants, rifampin, certain progestins), avoid fasting/crash diets and excessive alcohol and smoking, maintain adequate carbohydrate/caloric intake, and always check medications against a porphyria-safe list. For patients with frequent recurrent attacks, givosiran (RNAi against hepatic ALA synthase) reduces attack frequency. Resuming the OCP and barbiturate (A) would directly precipitate further attacks. A ketogenic/very-low-carb diet (C) is exactly wrong — carbohydrate restriction induces ALA synthase and triggers attacks. Prophylactic barbiturates (D) are porphyrinogenic and contraindicated. Educating the patient (and screening at-risk relatives) is essential given the autosomal-dominant inheritance and her family history.

pearls: Think AIP with the pentad: severe abdominal pain out of proportion to exam, neuropsychiatric symptoms, peripheral neuropathy, autonomic instability, and dark/port-wine urine on standing.
Triggers include fasting/crash diets, alcohol, smoking, and CYP-inducing/porphyrinogenic drugs (barbiturates, sulfonamides, some progestins/estrogens).
Confirm with markedly elevated urinary PBG and ALA during the attack; hyponatremia (SIADH) is common.
Treat with IV hemin and dextrose, remove triggers, and give supportive care; barbiturates are contraindicated, and givosiran helps prevent recurrent attacks.

cases.title: Painless Bright Red Blood per Rectum
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden passage of a large amount of bright red blood from the rectum.
cases.history: A 74-year-old woman presents after two episodes of passing maroon-to-bright-red blood per rectum over the past three hours, filling the toilet bowl each time. There was no abdominal pain, straining, or preceding diarrhea, and no vomiting. She felt lightheaded standing up and nearly fainted, prompting her daughter to call EMS. She denies fever, recent NSAID use beyond occasional acetaminophen, weight loss, or change in bowel caliber. Her last colonoscopy 4 years ago showed numerous sigmoid diverticula and a single tubular adenoma that was removed. Past medical history includes hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and osteoarthritis. Medications: amlodipine 10 mg daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily, and aspirin 81 mg daily for primary prevention. No anticoagulants. Allergies: none. She does not smoke, drinks wine occasionally, and lives independently. Family history is notable for colon cancer in a brother at age 70. She has had no prior GI bleeding.
cases.exam: The patient appears pale but alert and oriented. Conjunctivae are pale. Cardiovascular exam reveals tachycardia with a regular rhythm and no murmurs. The abdomen is soft, non-distended, and non-tender, without guarding, rebound, or palpable masses; bowel sounds are normal. There is no hepatosplenomegaly. Digital rectal exam reveals gross bright red blood and clots in the vault with no palpable mass, no hemorrhoids visualized externally, and no anal fissure. Extremities are cool with delayed capillary refill. Skin shows no jaundice, spider angiomata, or petechiae. Mucous membranes are dry.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate priority in managing this patient?
options: Establish two large-bore IVs, send type
Administer an IV proton pump infusion and consult endoscopy for urgent EGD
Obtain an urgent CT abdomen with oral contrast to localize the bleed
Place a nasogastric tube and start an octreotide infusion as the first step today
stages.explanation: This patient has acute lower GI bleeding with hemodynamic compromise (tachycardia, near-syncope, borderline hypotension), so hemodynamic stabilization comes first: two large-bore IVs, type and crossmatch, and crystalloid resuscitation, with transfusion guided by a restrictive threshold (~7 g/dL). CT is useful for localization but only after stabilization, and oral contrast would obscure CT angiography. PPI infusion and EGD target upper GI sources; while a brisk upper bleed can rarely cause hematochezia, this is not the first step before resuscitation. Octreotide and NG tube are aimed at variceal bleeding, for which there is no clinical evidence here. Resuscitation always precedes diagnostic localization in an unstable bleeder.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 9.6 g/dL (baseline 13.1 six months ago)
Platelets 232 ×10³/µL
INR 1.0
BUN 18 mg/dL
Creatinine 1.1 mg/dL
Lactate 2.1 mmol/L
Type and screen: O positive, crossmatch in progress

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the presentation and a normal BUN/creatinine ratio, which feature most strongly favors a lower GI source over an upper GI source?
options: Painless hematochezia with a normal BUN-to-creatinine ratio
Coffee-ground emesis with epigastric tenderness
A markedly elevated BUN-to-creatinine ratio
The presence of melena on rectal exam in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Painless hematochezia with a normal BUN-to-creatinine ratio points to a lower GI source. In upper GI bleeding, digested blood raises the BUN out of proportion to creatinine (ratio often >30), whereas a normal ratio argues against a large upper bleed. Melena reflects digestion of blood in transit and suggests an upper or proximal source, not lower. A markedly elevated BUN/creatinine ratio would actually favor an upper source, making it the wrong distinguishing feature here. Coffee-ground emesis with epigastric tenderness describes an upper GI source. The combination of bright red blood, no pain, and a normal BUN/creatinine ratio in an elderly patient with known diverticulosis points strongly toward diverticular bleeding.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: After resuscitation, the patient stabilizes (HR 88, BP 118/70). What is the most appropriate next diagnostic and potentially therapeutic step?
options: Exploratory laparotomy with segmental colectomy
Repeat colonoscopy in 4 years as previously scheduled
Colonoscopy after a rapid bowel purge
Tagged red blood cell scintigraphy followed by observation
stages.explanation: In a hemodynamically stabilized patient with suspected diverticular bleeding, colonoscopy after a rapid bowel preparation (e.g., 4-6 L polyethylene glycol) is the preferred next step: it can localize the source and provide hemostasis with clips, epinephrine injection, or band ligation. Tagged RBC scan detects slow bleeding (0.1-0.5 mL/min) and can guide angiography but is not therapeutic and is reserved for ongoing bleeding when colonoscopy is unrevealing or impossible. Laparotomy with colectomy is a last resort for uncontrolled, life-threatening bleeding after endoscopic and angiographic measures fail. Deferring evaluation is unsafe given active, hemodynamically significant bleeding. Colonoscopy is both diagnostic and therapeutic and is first-line once the patient is stable enough to be prepped.

stages.label: COLONOSCOPY
stages.content:
Sigmoid colon: numerous diverticula
A single diverticulum with a visible vessel and adherent clot identified
No active spurting at time of exam after clot removal; oozing controlled
Through-the-scope clips applied across the bleeding diverticulum
No mass, no colitis, no angiodysplasia elsewhere

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Bleeding is controlled endoscopically. Regarding her long-term medications, what is the most appropriate recommendation?
options: Start lifelong therapeutic anticoagulation to prevent rebleeding as initial therapy
Reassess whether aspirin for primary prevention should be continued given bleeding risk
Begin a high-dose proton pump inhibitor indefinitely to prevent diverticular rebleeding
Permanently discontinue all antiplatelet and antihypertensive therapy as monotherapy
stages.explanation: Aspirin used for primary prevention in an elderly patient carries a bleeding risk that often outweighs cardiovascular benefit, especially after a significant GI bleed; current guidance favors reassessing and frequently stopping primary-prevention aspirin in adults over 70. (If aspirin were for secondary prevention of established cardiovascular disease, it would typically be resumed after hemostasis.) Discontinuing all antihypertensives is unwarranted and would worsen her hypertension. Anticoagulation is not indicated and would increase rebleeding. PPIs reduce upper GI ulcer bleeding but have no role in preventing diverticular bleeding, which originates in the colon. The key teaching point is individualized antiplatelet stewardship after lower GI hemorrhage.

pearls: Diverticular bleeding is the most common cause of acute lower GI bleeding and is typically painless and brisk; most cases stop spontaneously.
A normal BUN-to-creatinine ratio with hematochezia favors a lower GI source, whereas a ratio >30 suggests an upper source.
Resuscitate first: two large-bore IVs, type and crossmatch, restrictive transfusion to ~7 g/dL, then localize with colonoscopy after rapid prep.
Endoscopic hemostasis (clips, epinephrine, banding) is first-line; tagged RBC scan plus angiographic embolization or surgery is reserved for refractory bleeding.

cases.title: Vomiting Large Volumes of Blood in a Patient with Liver Disease
cases.chiefComplaint: Vomiting large volumes of bright red blood with lightheadedness.
cases.history: A 57-year-old man with known alcohol-associated cirrhosis is brought to the ICU after vomiting several cupfuls of bright red blood at home, followed by two black tarry stools. He reports progressive abdominal distension and lower-extremity swelling over months. He has had no prior variceal bleed but a surveillance EGD a year ago reportedly showed 'large veins' in the esophagus for which he was prescribed a beta-blocker that he stopped taking. He drinks roughly a pint of vodka daily and last drank yesterday. Past medical history includes cirrhosis with ascites, hepatic encephalopathy controlled on lactulose, and thrombocytopenia. Medications: lactulose 30 mL three times daily (poorly adherent), spironolactone 100 mg daily, furosemide 40 mg daily; he stopped nadolol months ago. Allergies: none. He smokes half a pack daily. Family history is non-contributory. He is disoriented to date but follows commands intermittently.
cases.exam: The patient is pale, diaphoretic, and somnolent but rousable. Scleral icterus is present. There is fresh blood at the lips. Cardiac exam shows tachycardia without murmurs. Lungs are clear. The abdomen is distended with a fluid wave and shifting dullness consistent with ascites; it is non-tender without rebound. Caput medusae and several spider angiomata are noted on the chest. There is palmar erythema and asterixis. Digital rectal exam reveals melena. Extremities show 2+ pitting edema. He is mildly confused with no focal neurologic deficit.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Beyond establishing IV access and fluid resuscitation, what is the most critical immediate intervention given his depressed mental status and active hematemesis?
options: Endotracheal intubation for airway protection before endoscopy
Large-volume paracentesis to relieve abdominal pressure
Oral activated charcoal to bind ingested blood
Immediate emergent EGD without airway assessment
stages.explanation: A cirrhotic patient with massive hematemesis and altered mental status is at high risk of aspiration; endotracheal intubation for airway protection is the priority before urgent endoscopy in this scenario. Performing EGD first without securing the airway risks catastrophic aspiration during the procedure. Large-volume paracentesis addresses tense ascites but is not the acute lifesaving step during active bleeding and can precipitate hemodynamic instability. Activated charcoal has no role and is contraindicated with active GI bleeding and an unprotected airway. Airway, breathing, circulation: in a bleeding, encephalopathic patient who cannot protect the airway, intubation comes first, then resuscitation and endoscopy.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: While preparing for endoscopy, which combination of pharmacologic therapies is most appropriate to start now?
options: Octreotide infusion plus IV ceftriaxone (or norfloxacin)
Vasopressin infusion plus oral vancomycin as initial therapy
Tranexamic acid infusion plus prophylactic fresh frozen plasma to normalize INR
High-dose IV proton pump inhibitor alone as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For suspected variceal hemorrhage, start a vasoactive agent (octreotide, a splanchnic vasoconstrictor that lowers portal pressure) plus prophylactic antibiotics (ceftriaxone or norfloxacin), which reduce infection, rebleeding, and mortality in cirrhotic GI bleeding. A PPI is reasonable empirically until the source is confirmed but is not the variceal-directed therapy. Vasopressin causes more cardiac/ischemic side effects than octreotide and is no longer preferred, and oral vancomycin treats C. difficile, not bleeding. Tranexamic acid is not recommended in GI bleeding (HALT-IT showed no benefit and increased venous thromboembolism), and prophylactic FFP to normalize INR is discouraged in cirrhosis because the INR poorly reflects in vivo coagulation.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 7.4 g/dL
Platelets 64 ×10³/µL
INR 1.9
Total bilirubin 4.8 mg/dL
Albumin 2.4 g/dL
Creatinine 1.6 mg/dL
Sodium 131 mEq/L
Ammonia 92 µmol/L
Lactate 3.4 mmol/L

stages.label: DEFINITIVE THERAPY
stages.question: Emergent EGD reveals large esophageal varices with active spurting. What is the most appropriate endoscopic intervention?
options: Argon plasma coagulation of the varices
Sclerotherapy with absolute alcohol injection
Endoscopic variceal band ligation
Epinephrine injection alone in this setting
stages.explanation: Endoscopic variceal band ligation (EVL) is the first-line endoscopic treatment for bleeding esophageal varices, achieving hemostasis with fewer complications than sclerotherapy. Sclerotherapy is an alternative when banding is technically difficult but causes more ulceration, strictures, and perforation. Argon plasma coagulation is used for vascular lesions like angiodysplasia or GAVE, not for actively bleeding varices. Epinephrine injection alone is inadequate for varices and is used adjunctively for ulcer bleeding. EVL combined with the vasoactive agent and antibiotics already started provides the best chance of controlling the hemorrhage; band ligation plus pharmacotherapy is the standard of care.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Despite banding and octreotide, the patient continues to bleed massively and becomes increasingly unstable. What is the most appropriate next step to temporize while arranging definitive therapy?
options: Start a norepinephrine infusion and continue current therapy unchanged
Balloon tamponade with a Sengstaken-Blakemore tube as a bridge to TIPS
Repeat band ligation immediately at the bedside without imaging
Emergent splenectomy to reduce portal inflow as the leading concern
stages.explanation: When endoscopic and pharmacologic therapy fail to control variceal hemorrhage, balloon tamponade (Sengstaken-Blakemore or Minnesota tube) provides temporary hemostasis as a bridge to definitive therapy such as transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt (TIPS) or a covered esophageal stent. It requires a secured airway (already in place) and carries risks of esophageal necrosis and perforation if left inflated too long. Repeating banding during torrential bleeding is often not feasible and does not address refractory failure. Norepinephrine supports blood pressure but does not stop variceal bleeding. Emergency splenectomy is not a treatment for acute variceal hemorrhage. Balloon tamponade buys time for TIPS, the definitive salvage procedure.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After successful TIPS and stabilization, which therapy is most appropriate for long-term secondary prophylaxis against rebleeding?
options: Non-selective beta-blocker plus a program of serial endoscopic variceal ligation
Indefinite octreotide infusion at home with appropriate follow-up
Long-term proton pump inhibitor monotherapy with appropriate follow-up
Prophylactic antibiotics continued indefinitely with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Standard secondary prophylaxis after a variceal bleed is the combination of a non-selective beta-blocker (nadolol, propranolol, or carvedilol) to lower portal pressure plus serial endoscopic variceal ligation until varices are eradicated. In this patient, TIPS itself reduces portal pressure, but in general the beta-blocker-plus-EVL combination is the evidence-based regimen. A PPI may help heal band-induced ulcers but does not prevent variceal rebleeding. Continuous octreotide is only for acute bleeding, not chronic prophylaxis. Antibiotics are given for the 7-day peri-bleed window, not indefinitely. He should also be referred for alcohol cessation support and liver transplant evaluation given decompensated cirrhosis.

cases.diagnosis: Upper GI bleed from esophageal varices
pearls: In cirrhotic GI bleeding, secure the airway first if mental status is depressed, then resuscitate; aspiration is a leading cause of death.
Acute variceal bleeding management = vasoactive drug (octreotide) + prophylactic antibiotics (ceftriaxone) + endoscopic band ligation.
Avoid normalizing the INR with FFP in cirrhosis and avoid tranexamic acid (no benefit, increased VTE per HALT-IT).
Balloon tamponade is a temporizing bridge to TIPS when endoscopic therapy fails; secondary prophylaxis is non-selective beta-blocker plus serial EVL.

cases.title: Bloody Vomiting After a Night of Heavy Drinking
cases.chiefComplaint: Streaks of blood in the vomit after repeated forceful vomiting.
cases.history: A 34-year-old man presents after a night of heavy drinking at a celebration. He vomited forcefully many times; the first several episodes contained only food and bile, but the last two showed streaks and small clots of bright red blood. He feels nauseated and has mild epigastric and retrosternal discomfort but denies severe tearing chest pain, shortness of breath, or back pain. He has had no melena, no prior GI bleeding, and no weight loss. He took no NSAIDs. Past medical history is unremarkable. He takes no regular medications and has no allergies. He drinks heavily on weekends, smokes socially, and uses no other drugs. Family history is non-contributory. He has not had similar episodes before and feels he is improving as the retching has stopped.
cases.exam: The patient is alert, mildly uncomfortable, and smells of alcohol. He is well perfused with warm extremities. HEENT exam is unremarkable with no oropharyngeal lesions. The neck is supple with no subcutaneous crepitus. Lungs are clear bilaterally with no crackles. Cardiac exam is normal. The abdomen is soft with mild epigastric tenderness, no rebound, guarding, or masses, and normal bowel sounds. There is no Hamman crunch. Digital rectal exam shows brown stool that is negative for occult blood. There is no jaundice, ascites, or peripheral stigmata of chronic liver disease.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Based on the history of multiple non-bloody vomiting episodes followed by blood-streaked emesis, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Esophageal variceal hemorrhage
Mallory-Weiss tear
Bleeding peptic ulcer
Boerhaave syndrome
stages.explanation: The classic Mallory-Weiss sequence is repeated forceful retching that begins non-bloody and then produces hematemesis, caused by a mucosal tear at the gastroesophageal junction. This young man with binge drinking fits perfectly. A bleeding peptic ulcer usually presents without this retch-then-blood sequence and often with melena or NSAID/H. pylori history. Variceal bleeding occurs in portal hypertension; he has no stigmata of chronic liver disease. Boerhaave syndrome is a full-thickness esophageal rupture presenting with severe chest pain, subcutaneous emphysema, and sepsis-not blood-streaked emesis with a benign exam. The temporal pattern (non-bloody emesis preceding small-volume hematemesis) is the hallmark of a Mallory-Weiss tear.

stages.label: DIFFERENTIAL
stages.question: Which clinical finding, if present, would most strongly redirect concern toward Boerhaave syndrome rather than a Mallory-Weiss tear?
options: Subcutaneous emphysema in the neck with severe chest pain
A history of binge alcohol use as the immediate next step
A small amount of bright red blood mixed in the vomit
Mild epigastric tenderness on palpation in this setting
stages.explanation: Subcutaneous emphysema (crepitus) with severe chest pain signals full-thickness esophageal rupture (Boerhaave), where air escapes into the mediastinum and subcutaneous tissues; this is a surgical emergency. A small amount of blood in the vomit is consistent with the partial-thickness mucosal tear of Mallory-Weiss and does not suggest perforation. Mild epigastric tenderness is nonspecific and expected after repeated vomiting. Binge drinking is a shared risk factor for both conditions and does not distinguish them. The key alarm features for Boerhaave are crepitus, severe persistent chest or back pain, fever, and a toxic appearance-none of which this patient has.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 14.6 g/dL
Platelets 188 ×10³/µL
INR 1.0
BUN 14 mg/dL
Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL
Lipase 38 U/L
Alcohol level 0.18 g/dL

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His bleeding has stopped, he is hemodynamically stable, and labs are reassuring. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Immediate octreotide infusion and antibiotics as monotherapy
Emergent surgical repair of the esophagus as initial therapy
Supportive care with antiemetics, IV fluids,
CT angiography of the chest and broad-spectrum antibiotics
stages.explanation: Most Mallory-Weiss tears are self-limited and stop bleeding spontaneously; management is supportive with antiemetics, IV fluids, acid suppression, and observation, with endoscopy reserved for ongoing or significant bleeding. Surgical repair is unnecessary for a self-limited mucosal tear and is reserved for perforation. Octreotide and antibiotics target variceal bleeding, not a Mallory-Weiss tear in a patient without liver disease. CT angiography and antibiotics would be appropriate if perforation (Boerhaave) were suspected, but this patient has no crepitus, fever, or severe pain. The stable vitals, normal hemoglobin, and cessation of bleeding support a conservative approach.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: If, instead, repeat hematemesis occurred with a drop in hemoglobin, what would be the most appropriate next intervention?
options: Begin a tapering course of oral steroids with appropriate follow-up
Schedule outpatient barium swallow in one week based on current risk
Insertion of a Sengstaken-Blakemore balloon tube based on current risk
Upper endoscopy with endoscopic hemostasis if a bleeding tear is found
stages.explanation: For a Mallory-Weiss tear with ongoing bleeding or hemodynamic significance, upper endoscopy is both diagnostic and therapeutic-options include endoscopic clipping, band ligation, or epinephrine injection to achieve hemostasis. Steroids have no role and could impair healing. A Sengstaken-Blakemore balloon is for refractory variceal bleeding, not a mucosal tear. A barium swallow does not treat bleeding and is contraindicated if perforation is a concern (water-soluble contrast would be used). Endoscopy is the appropriate escalation when conservative management fails, and most patients still do well. The teaching point is that intervention is reserved for the minority who continue to bleed.

pearls: Mallory-Weiss tears classically follow repeated retching that starts non-bloody and then produces small-volume hematemesis; alcohol use and bulimia are risk factors.
It is a partial-thickness mucosal tear at the gastroesophageal junction-distinct from Boerhaave, which is a full-thickness rupture with mediastinal air and crepitus.
Most tears are self-limited; supportive care and acid suppression suffice, with endoscopy reserved for persistent or significant bleeding.
Sudden severe chest pain, subcutaneous emphysema, or a toxic appearance should prompt evaluation for esophageal perforation instead.

cases.title: Black Stools and Fatigue in an Older Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Several days of black tarry stools with lightheadedness and fatigue.
cases.history: A 66-year-old man presents with three days of black, tarry, foul-smelling stools and increasing fatigue and lightheadedness. He reports a few weeks of gnawing epigastric pain that improves with food and worsens at night, and he has been taking over-the-counter ibuprofen 600 mg three times daily for chronic knee pain. He had one episode of coffee-ground emesis this morning. He denies hematochezia, weight loss, dysphagia, or jaundice. Past medical history includes osteoarthritis and hyperlipidemia. Medications: ibuprofen as above, atorvastatin 40 mg daily; no anticoagulants or antiplatelets. Allergies: none. He drinks one or two beers weekly and is a former smoker. Family history is unremarkable. He has never had a documented H. pylori infection or prior endoscopy. He looks pale and tired but is mentating normally.
cases.exam: The patient is pale with dry mucous membranes but alert and oriented. Conjunctivae are pale. Cardiac exam reveals mild tachycardia with no murmurs. Lungs are clear. The abdomen is soft with mild epigastric tenderness to deep palpation, no rebound or guarding, no distension, and normal bowel sounds; there is no hepatosplenomegaly or palpable mass. Digital rectal exam confirms black, tarry, guaiac-positive stool with no fresh blood and no mass. There are no peripheral stigmata of chronic liver disease, no jaundice, and no spider angiomata. Extremities are warm with brisk capillary refill.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: After IV access and a type and screen, which pharmacologic therapy should be initiated before endoscopy?
options: Intravenous proton pump inhibitor
Oral sucralfate slurry
Octreotide infusion
Intravenous metoclopramide for motility only
stages.explanation: In suspected upper GI bleeding from a peptic ulcer, an intravenous proton pump inhibitor should be started before endoscopy; raising intragastric pH stabilizes clots, downstages high-risk stigmata, and can reduce the need for endoscopic intervention. Sucralfate coats ulcers but is not the acute therapy for active bleeding and is hard to administer urgently. Octreotide is for variceal bleeding, which is unlikely given no liver disease and an NSAID/ulcer history. Metoclopramide (a prokinetic) can be used to clear the stomach for better endoscopic visualization but is adjunctive, not the primary therapy. Pre-endoscopy IV PPI is standard care while arranging EGD.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 8.2 g/dL (prior 14.0 one year ago)
MCV 78 fL
Platelets 270 ×10³/µL
INR 1.0
BUN 34 mg/dL
Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL
BUN-to-creatinine ratio 38
H. pylori stool antigen: pending

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic and therapeutic step within 24 hours?
options: CT angiography of the abdomen
Capsule endoscopy in this setting
Colonoscopy with rapid prep
Upper endoscopy (EGD)
stages.explanation: The clinical picture-melena, coffee-ground emesis, NSAID use, elevated BUN-to-creatinine ratio, and microcytic anemia-points to an upper GI source, so upper endoscopy within 24 hours is the appropriate next step; it confirms the diagnosis, risk-stratifies the lesion, and delivers hemostasis. Colonoscopy targets lower GI bleeding, inconsistent with melena and the high BUN/creatinine ratio. CT angiography is reserved for brisk ongoing bleeding when endoscopy is unrevealing or the patient is too unstable. Capsule endoscopy evaluates the small bowel for obscure bleeding after negative EGD and colonoscopy and is never the first test in active hematemesis/melena. EGD is both diagnostic and therapeutic here.

stages.label: ENDOSCOPY
stages.content:
EGD: 1.5 cm ulcer in the duodenal bulb
Base shows a non-bleeding visible vessel (pigmented protuberance)
No active spurting or oozing at the time of exam
Forrest classification: IIa
Biopsies of gastric antrum obtained for H. pylori

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: An ulcer with a non-bleeding visible vessel (Forrest IIa) is identified. What is the most appropriate endoscopic management?
options: Combination therapy: epinephrine injection plus a second modality
Surgical oversew of the ulcer as initial therapy
Epinephrine injection alone as initial therapy
No endoscopic therapy; treat with oral PPI and discharge
stages.explanation: A Forrest IIa ulcer (non-bleeding visible vessel) carries a high rebleeding risk (~50% untreated), so endoscopic hemostasis is indicated, and combination therapy-epinephrine injection plus a second modality such as thermal coagulation or hemoclips-is superior to epinephrine alone. Epinephrine monotherapy provides only temporary vasoconstriction and has higher rebleeding rates, so it should always be paired with a definitive method. No therapy with discharge is inappropriate for a high-risk lesion. Surgery is reserved for failure of endoscopic therapy or perforation. After dual endoscopic therapy, a high-dose IV PPI is continued to further reduce rebleeding for high-risk stigmata.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: H. pylori testing returns positive. Beyond a course of PPI, what is the most important long-term intervention to prevent ulcer recurrence?
options: Eradicate H. pylori with combination antibiotic therapy
Lifelong daily aspirin with appropriate follow-up
Indefinite octreotide injections with appropriate follow-up
Routine surveillance EGD every 3 months indefinitely
stages.explanation: The two dominant causes of peptic ulcer disease are H. pylori and NSAIDs; eradicating H. pylori with combination antibiotic therapy (e.g., a PPI plus clarithromycin and amoxicillin, or bismuth quadruple therapy) and discontinuing NSAIDs are the key steps to prevent recurrence, with confirmation of eradication afterward. Starting lifelong aspirin would increase ulcer and bleeding risk. Octreotide has no role in ulcer prevention. Routine EGD every 3 months is unnecessary, though a repeat EGD is warranted for gastric ulcers to confirm healing and exclude malignancy; this duodenal ulcer does not require that schedule. Treat the cause: eradicate H. pylori and remove the offending NSAID.

pearls: An elevated BUN-to-creatinine ratio (>30) with melena and microcytic anemia points to an upper GI bleed; start IV PPI before EGD.
Forrest classification predicts rebleeding: Ia/Ib (active bleeding) and IIa (visible vessel) are high-risk and require endoscopic hemostasis; IIc (flat spot) and III (clean base) are low-risk.
Use combination endoscopic therapy (epinephrine plus a second modality) for high-risk stigmata; epinephrine alone is insufficient.
Address the cause: eradicate H. pylori, stop NSAIDs, and obtain follow-up EGD for gastric ulcers to confirm healing and rule out malignancy.

cases.title: Severe Chest Pain After Vomiting
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden severe chest pain immediately after a bout of forceful vomiting.
cases.history: A 50-year-old man presents with sudden, severe, tearing chest pain that began immediately after several episodes of violent vomiting following a large meal and heavy alcohol intake. The pain radiates to the back and left shoulder and is worse with swallowing and deep breathing. Over the past hour he has become increasingly short of breath and diaphoretic and notes a strange crackling sensation in his neck. He denies hematemesis of large volume but had one episode of blood-tinged retching. Past medical history includes gastroesophageal reflux and hypertension. Medications: omeprazole 20 mg daily, hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily. Allergies: none. He drinks heavily and smokes one pack daily. Family history is non-contributory. He appears acutely ill and anxious, and the pain has not improved with antacids given by EMS.
cases.exam: The patient is diaphoretic, tachypneic, and in obvious distress. There is palpable subcutaneous crepitus over the neck and supraclavicular regions. A crunching sound synchronous with the heartbeat is auscultated over the precordium (Hamman sign). Breath sounds are diminished at the left base with dullness to percussion. Cardiac exam reveals tachycardia without murmurs. The abdomen is mildly tender in the epigastrium with voluntary guarding but no rigidity or rebound. Peripheral pulses are intact and symmetric. There is no jugular venous distension. He is alert and oriented but appears toxic.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given forceful vomiting followed by chest pain, subcutaneous emphysema, and a Hamman crunch, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Acute myocardial infarction
Spontaneous pneumothorax in this clinical context
Boerhaave syndrome
Aortic dissection as the first step today
stages.explanation: The triad of vomiting, chest pain, and subcutaneous emphysema (with a Hamman crunch from mediastinal air) after forceful retching is classic for Boerhaave syndrome-a full-thickness, transmural esophageal rupture, typically of the distal left posterolateral wall. Myocardial infarction can cause chest pain but does not produce subcutaneous crepitus or a vomiting-then-rupture sequence, and would not explain fever or a left pleural effusion. Aortic dissection causes tearing pain but presents with pulse/blood-pressure differentials and a widened mediastinum, not crepitus or Hamman crunch. Spontaneous pneumothorax causes dyspnea and decreased breath sounds but not the vomiting-pain-crepitus triad with fever. The history and exam are diagnostic of esophageal perforation.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which imaging study is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis and define the leak?
options: CT of the chest with water-soluble oral contrast
Barium esophagram as the primary diagnosis
Transthoracic echocardiography in this clinical context
Coronary CT angiography as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: CT of the chest with water-soluble oral contrast (or contrast esophagography using a water-soluble agent like Gastrografin) is preferred to confirm esophageal perforation and characterize the leak, mediastinal air, and effusions; CT also evaluates for complications and guides drainage. Barium is avoided initially because extravasated barium causes intense mediastinitis and granulomatous reaction-water-soluble contrast is used first, with barium only if the water-soluble study is negative but suspicion remains. Transthoracic echocardiography assesses cardiac structures and pericardial fluid but cannot diagnose esophageal rupture. Coronary CT angiography evaluates coronary arteries and is irrelevant here. The combination of clinical suspicion and CT with water-soluble contrast confirms Boerhaave.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 17.8 ×10³/µL with left shift
Lactate 3.8 mmol/L
Hemoglobin 13.4 g/dL
Chest X-ray: left pleural effusion, pneumomediastinum, subcutaneous emphysema
CT chest with water-soluble contrast: contrast extravasation from the distal esophagus into the left mediastinum and pleural space, pneumomediastinum

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: While arranging definitive treatment, which set of interventions should be initiated immediately?
options: Oral feeding to assess tolerance and oral antibiotics as initial therapy
NPO status, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics, IV fluids,
Beta-blockade and blood pressure control targeting the aorta as monotherapy
Discharge with outpatient PPI and follow-up esophagram as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Esophageal perforation with mediastinal contamination is life-threatening; immediate management is NPO status, aggressive IV fluid resuscitation, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (covering oral flora and often antifungal coverage), IV PPI, and urgent surgical/thoracic consultation. Oral feeding would worsen mediastinal soiling and is contraindicated. Discharge is dangerous given impending mediastinitis and sepsis. Beta-blockade and aortic-targeted blood pressure control treat aortic dissection, which this is not, and would not address the perforation or contamination. Early antibiotics, source control, and surgical involvement drive survival; mortality rises sharply with delays beyond 24 hours.

stages.label: DEFINITIVE THERAPY
stages.question: The perforation is large with gross mediastinal/pleural contamination and the patient presented within 12 hours. What is the most appropriate definitive treatment?
options: Continued antibiotics alone with no procedure in this setting
Permanent esophageal stent placement as sole therapy in the ED
Operative repair with primary closure
Therapeutic anticoagulation for presumed pulmonary embolism
stages.explanation: For a large thoracic perforation diagnosed early (<24 hours) with significant contamination in an operative candidate, surgical repair with primary closure and wide mediastinal/pleural drainage is the definitive treatment of choice and offers the best outcomes. Antibiotics alone (nonoperative management) are reserved for small, contained leaks in stable patients without sepsis-not for gross contamination. Esophageal stenting plus drainage is an option for selected patients or those who are poor surgical candidates, but it is not appropriate as sole ED therapy for a large, contaminating thoracic rupture. Anticoagulation for presumed PE is incorrect and dangerous-the diagnosis is esophageal perforation, not pulmonary embolism. Early surgical repair plus drainage is standard for this presentation.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: Which factor is the most important determinant of survival in Boerhaave syndrome?
options: Whether the patient smoked cigarettes
The patient's body mass index in this setting
The presence of mild epigastric tenderness
Time from perforation to definitive treatment
stages.explanation: The single most important prognostic factor in esophageal perforation is the time from perforation to definitive treatment; mortality climbs substantially when intervention is delayed beyond 24 hours because of progressive mediastinitis and sepsis. Early recognition and source control dramatically improve outcomes. BMI is not a primary determinant of survival here. Mild epigastric tenderness is a nonspecific finding and not a prognostic driver. Smoking contributes to overall morbidity but is not the key survival factor in acute perforation. The teaching point is that time-to-treatment dominates prognosis, underscoring the need for rapid diagnosis and intervention.

pearls: Boerhaave syndrome is a full-thickness esophageal rupture after forceful vomiting; the classic triad is vomiting, chest pain, and subcutaneous emphysema.
Diagnose with water-soluble contrast esophagography or CT chest with water-soluble contrast-avoid barium initially because it incites severe mediastinitis.
Immediate care is NPO, IV broad-spectrum antibiotics, fluids, IV PPI, and urgent surgical consultation; large early thoracic perforations need operative repair and drainage.
Time to definitive treatment is the most important prognostic factor-mortality rises sharply after 24 hours.

cases.title: Progressive Trouble Swallowing Solids and Liquids
cases.chiefComplaint: A year of worsening difficulty swallowing both solids and liquids.
cases.history: A 40-year-old woman presents with about a year of progressive difficulty swallowing. She notes food and even water 'sticking' in her chest, and she has learned to eat slowly, drink extra water, and lift her arms or arch her neck to push food down. She regurgitates undigested food, especially when lying down, and has had nocturnal coughing and one episode of aspiration pneumonia. She has lost 5 kg over the year. There is no pain with swallowing and no acid-tasting reflux. Past medical history is unremarkable. Medications: none. Allergies: none. She does not smoke or drink heavily and has never traveled to Central or South America. Family history is non-contributory. She initially thought the trouble was reflux and tried omeprazole without benefit. She denies fever, hematemesis, or a sensation of a lump unrelated to swallowing.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and in no distress, though slightly thin. The oropharynx is normal with no thrush, masses, or mucosal lesions. The neck is supple without lymphadenopathy or thyromegaly, and there is no palpable neck mass. Lungs are clear without crackles. Cardiac exam is normal. The abdomen is soft, non-tender, and without organomegaly or masses. There is no supraclavicular adenopathy. Skin shows no sclerodactyly, telangiectasias, or calcinosis. Neurologic exam is normal with intact cranial nerves and no focal deficits.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Difficulty swallowing both solids and liquids from the outset most strongly suggests which category of disorder?
options: Oropharyngeal (transfer) dysphagia
Odynophagia from infectious esophagitis
An esophageal motility disorder
A mechanical obstruction such as a stricture or tumor
stages.explanation: Dysphagia to both solids and liquids from the beginning indicates a motility disorder, because a neuromuscular problem affects transport of all consistencies equally. Mechanical obstruction (stricture, ring, tumor) classically causes dysphagia to solids first that later progresses to liquids, as the lumen narrows. Odynophagia (painful swallowing) suggests infectious or pill esophagitis, but this patient has no pain. Oropharyngeal dysphagia involves difficulty initiating a swallow with coughing or nasal regurgitation immediately on swallowing and usually has a neuromuscular or structural cause in the throat; her symptoms localize to the chest with food sticking after the swallow begins. Simultaneous solid-and-liquid dysphagia points to a motility disorder such as achalasia.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Which is the most appropriate initial test to evaluate this patient before motility testing?
options: Esophageal manometry first, before any other study
24-hour ambulatory pH monitoring before discharge planning
Barium swallow as the sole evaluation in this setting
Upper endoscopy to exclude a mechanical or malignant cause
stages.explanation: Even when a motility disorder is suspected, upper endoscopy is the appropriate initial test to exclude mechanical obstruction and, importantly, to rule out 'pseudoachalasia' from a tumor at the gastroesophageal junction-particularly with weight loss in a patient over 40 or with a short symptom duration. A barium swallow is very useful and may show the classic findings, but endoscopy is needed to exclude malignancy and structural disease. Manometry is the confirmatory gold standard for achalasia but is performed after structural disease is excluded. Ambulatory pH monitoring evaluates reflux, not dysphagia, and would not explain her presentation. Endoscopy first protects against missing a malignant cause.

stages.label: ENDOSCOPY & IMAGING
stages.content:
Upper endoscopy: dilated esophagus with retained food and saliva; puckered, tight gastroesophageal junction that pops open with gentle pressure; no mass or stricture; normal-appearing GE junction mucosa
Barium esophagram: dilated esophagus tapering to a smooth, symmetric 'bird-beak' narrowing at the lower esophageal sphincter
Biopsies of the GE junction: no malignancy

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Manometry is ordered to confirm the diagnosis. Which manometric findings are most consistent with achalasia?
options: High-amplitude 'nutcracker' contractions with intact peristalsis
Incomplete relaxation of the lower esophageal sphincter with absent peristalsis
Premature (spastic) contractions with normal LES relaxation in this clinical context
Normal peristalsis with a hypotensive LES as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Achalasia is defined manometrically by impaired/incomplete relaxation of the lower esophageal sphincter (elevated integrated relaxation pressure) together with absent peristalsis (aperistalsis) of the esophageal body, reflecting loss of inhibitory ganglion cells in the myenteric plexus. Premature spastic contractions with normal LES relaxation describe distal esophageal spasm, not achalasia. High-amplitude 'nutcracker'/jackhammer contractions with intact peristalsis describe a hypercontractile disorder. Normal peristalsis with a hypotensive LES is characteristic of reflux-related dysfunction, not achalasia. The combination of a non-relaxing LES and aperistalsis, with the bird-beak on barium and a dilated esophagus, confirms achalasia.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this otherwise healthy 40-year-old, which is the most appropriate definitive treatment?
options: Botulinum toxin injection of the LES as the preferred durable therapy
Pneumatic dilation or laparoscopic Heller myotomy (or POEM)
Calcium channel blockers and nitrates as first-line definitive therapy
Lifelong proton pump inhibitor therapy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Definitive, durable treatment of achalasia in a good surgical candidate is mechanical disruption of the LES-graded pneumatic dilation, laparoscopic Heller myotomy (usually with a partial fundoplication), or per-oral endoscopic myotomy (POEM). PPIs treat acid reflux and do not relieve the non-relaxing LES of achalasia. Botulinum toxin injection relaxes the LES but its effect wanes within months and requires repeat injections; it is reserved for patients who are poor candidates for dilation or surgery. Calcium channel blockers and nitrates provide only modest, temporary relief and are used when more definitive options are not feasible. Pneumatic dilation or myotomy offers the best long-term outcomes in a healthy young patient.

pearls: Dysphagia to solids AND liquids from the start suggests a motility disorder; solids-then-liquids progression suggests mechanical obstruction.
Always perform endoscopy to exclude pseudoachalasia (GE junction tumor), especially with weight loss or age over 50; barium shows the 'bird-beak.'
Manometry confirms achalasia: incomplete LES relaxation plus aperistalsis from loss of inhibitory myenteric neurons.
Durable treatment is pneumatic dilation, Heller myotomy, or POEM; botulinum toxin and nitrates/CCBs are for poor procedural candidates.

cases.title: Intermittent Chest Pain and Swallowing Trouble
cases.chiefComplaint: Episodes of squeezing chest pain and intermittent difficulty swallowing.
cases.history: A 55-year-old woman presents with several months of intermittent, squeezing substernal chest pain and difficulty swallowing that comes and goes. The chest pain can occur at rest, sometimes with cold drinks or stress, and lasts minutes; it is occasionally mistaken for cardiac pain and once prompted an ED visit. The dysphagia is non-progressive and affects both solids and liquids unpredictably. She denies weight loss, regurgitation of old food, exertional pattern to the pain, diaphoresis, or radiation to the arm. A recent cardiac workup, including a normal stress test and coronary angiography, was unremarkable. Past medical history includes anxiety and gastroesophageal reflux. Medications: omeprazole 20 mg daily, sertraline 50 mg daily. Allergies: none. She does not smoke and rarely drinks. Family history is non-contributory. Her symptoms have caused considerable worry about her heart.
cases.exam: The patient is comfortable and in no acute distress. The oropharynx is normal without lesions. The neck is supple without masses or lymphadenopathy. Cardiovascular exam reveals a regular rate and rhythm with no murmurs, rubs, or gallops, and no reproducible chest wall tenderness. Lungs are clear. The abdomen is soft and non-tender without organomegaly. There is no scleroderma skin change, no Raynaud phenomenon, and no peripheral edema. Neurologic exam is intact. During the visit she has a brief episode of substernal tightness that resolves spontaneously without ECG changes on a rhythm strip.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given non-cardiac chest pain with intermittent dysphagia and a negative cardiac workup, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Start empiric long-acting nitrates for presumed angina as the first step today
Begin evaluation of the esophagus, starting with endoscopy to exclude structural disease
Reassure her the pain is purely anxiety and stop further evaluation in this clinical context
Repeat coronary angiography as the first step today
stages.explanation: With cardiac causes excluded by a normal stress test and angiography, the esophagus is the next system to evaluate for chest pain plus dysphagia; upper endoscopy is the appropriate first step to exclude structural disease (stricture, ring, mass) and mucosal disease such as eosinophilic or reflux esophagitis before motility testing. Attributing everything to anxiety without evaluating the dysphagia risks missing a treatable esophageal disorder. Repeating angiography after a recent normal study adds risk without benefit. Empiric nitrates for 'angina' are inappropriate when coronary disease has been excluded, though nitrates may later help a spastic esophageal disorder. Endoscopy first is the safe, guideline-concordant approach.

stages.label: ENDOSCOPY
stages.content:
Upper endoscopy: normal-appearing esophageal mucosa, no stricture, ring, or mass
No evidence of erosive esophagitis or eosinophilic features
Biopsies: unremarkable, no eosinophilic infiltration
GE junction normal

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Endoscopy is normal. What is the most appropriate next diagnostic test?
options: CT coronary angiography
High-resolution esophageal manometry
24-hour Holter monitor
Gastric emptying study
stages.explanation: After a normal endoscopy in a patient with chest pain and dysphagia, high-resolution esophageal manometry is the next step to evaluate for a motility disorder such as distal esophageal spasm. A Holter monitor evaluates arrhythmias, which are not suggested here and have already been effectively excluded. CT coronary angiography would be redundant after a normal invasive angiogram. A gastric emptying study assesses gastroparesis, which does not explain episodic chest pain with dysphagia. Manometry can capture the premature, uncoordinated contractions characteristic of esophageal spasm and distinguish it from achalasia and hypercontractile disorders, directing therapy.

stages.label: MANOMETRY & BARIUM
stages.content:
High-resolution manometry: normal LES relaxation; ≥20% premature (simultaneous) contractions with short distal latency; intervening normal peristalsis
Barium esophagram during a symptomatic episode: 'corkscrew' / 'rosary bead' appearance of the distal esophagus from uncoordinated tertiary contractions

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature best distinguishes this patient's disorder from achalasia?
options: Aperistalsis of the esophageal body as the primary diagnosis
A bird-beak narrowing on barium as the primary diagnosis
A markedly dilated, food-filled esophagus as the primary diagnosis
Normal lower esophageal sphincter relaxation with premature/simultaneous contractions
stages.explanation: Distal (diffuse) esophageal spasm is characterized by normal LES relaxation with premature, simultaneous contractions (short distal latency) and intervening preserved peristalsis-distinct from achalasia, which features impaired LES relaxation and complete aperistalsis. Aperistalsis is the hallmark of achalasia, not spasm. The bird-beak narrowing on barium is the classic achalasia sign, whereas spasm shows the 'corkscrew' or 'rosary bead' pattern. A markedly dilated, food-filled esophagus reflects long-standing achalasia, not spasm. The combination of preserved LES relaxation, premature contractions, and the corkscrew esophagus on barium confirms distal esophageal spasm.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which initial therapy is most appropriate for symptomatic distal esophageal spasm?
options: Pneumatic dilation of the LES as initial therapy
Immediate laparoscopic Heller myotomy as initial therapy
Smooth-muscle relaxants such as calcium channel blockers or nitrates, plus addressing reflux
Lifelong broad-spectrum antibiotics as initial therapy
stages.explanation: First-line therapy for distal esophageal spasm targets the disordered smooth-muscle contraction with relaxants-calcium channel blockers (e.g., diltiazem) or nitrates-while also treating coexisting reflux (which can trigger spasm) and adding a neuromodulator such as a low-dose tricyclic antidepressant for the visceral pain/chest discomfort. Heller myotomy is a treatment for achalasia and is reserved for refractory spasm, not first-line. Antibiotics have no role in a non-infectious motility disorder. Pneumatic dilation targets the non-relaxing LES of achalasia; this patient has normal LES relaxation, so dilation is not indicated. A stepwise approach starting with smooth-muscle relaxants and neuromodulation is appropriate.

stages.label: REFRACTORY DISEASE
stages.question: After several months, her chest pain and dysphagia persist despite adequate trials of a calcium channel blocker, nitrates, optimized PPI therapy, and a tricyclic neuromodulator. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Refer for endoscopic therapy targeting the spastic segment , ideally after repeat manometry confirms persistent spasm
Proceed directly to esophagectomy as the immediate next step
Reassure her that no further options exist and discontinue all therapy as the immediate next step
Initiate long-term opioid analgesics for pain control as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: For distal esophageal spasm that remains symptomatic despite optimized pharmacologic therapy (smooth-muscle relaxants, reflux control, and a neuromodulator), the next step is referral for an endoscopic procedure targeting the spastic muscle-per-oral endoscopic myotomy (POEM) can be tailored to divide the spastic segment, ideally after repeat manometry confirms ongoing spasm and excludes evolution to another motility pattern. Long-term opioids are inappropriate; they worsen esophageal motility (opioid-induced esophageal dysfunction) and risk dependence. Telling her nothing more can be done is incorrect, as procedural options exist for refractory cases. Esophagectomy is far too aggressive for a benign motility disorder and is not indicated. Escalation to a targeted myotomy is the rational refractory-disease step.

pearls: Distal (diffuse) esophageal spasm causes intermittent chest pain and dysphagia that can mimic angina; always exclude cardiac disease first.
Manometry shows premature/simultaneous contractions with short distal latency and NORMAL LES relaxation-unlike achalasia's impaired relaxation and aperistalsis.
Barium may show a 'corkscrew' or 'rosary bead' esophagus during symptomatic episodes.
Treat with smooth-muscle relaxants (calcium channel blockers, nitrates), manage reflux triggers, and add a neuromodulator (e.g., low-dose TCA) for pain.

cases.title: Food Stuck in the Chest in a Young Man with Allergies
cases.chiefComplaint: Solid food intermittently getting stuck, with one recent episode requiring an ER visit.
cases.history: A 28-year-old man presents after an episode in which a piece of steak lodged in his chest, causing drooling and inability to swallow saliva until it passed; the ER had been preparing for endoscopy when it spontaneously cleared. For a couple of years he has had intermittent solid-food dysphagia, especially with dense foods like bread and meat, and he habitually drinks lots of water, chews carefully, and avoids problematic foods. He has not lost weight. Past medical history includes asthma, eczema, and seasonal allergic rhinitis. Medications: an albuterol inhaler as needed, loratadine seasonally; he tried omeprazole previously with little change. Allergies: pollen and several foods cause oral itching. He does not smoke or drink heavily. Family history includes asthma and food allergies. He denies heartburn most days, odynophagia, regurgitation of old food, or nocturnal cough.
cases.exam: The patient is a healthy-appearing young man in no distress. The oropharynx is normal with no thrush or masses. The neck is supple without lymphadenopathy or thyromegaly. Lungs are clear without wheezing today. Cardiac exam is normal. The abdomen is soft and non-tender with no organomegaly. Skin shows mild flexural eczema on the antecubital fossae. There is no scleroderma change and no supraclavicular adenopathy. Neurologic exam is intact. He is able to swallow water comfortably during the visit.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A young atopic man with recurrent solid-food impaction is most likely to have which diagnosis?
options: Eosinophilic esophagitis
Achalasia as the first step today
Esophageal adenocarcinoma
Zenker diverticulum
stages.explanation: Recurrent solid-food dysphagia and food impaction in a young atopic man (asthma, eczema, allergic rhinitis, food sensitivities) is the classic presentation of eosinophilic esophagitis (EoE), an immune/antigen-mediated esophageal disease. Esophageal adenocarcinoma occurs in older patients with chronic GERD/Barrett and causes progressive dysphagia with weight loss-not the picture in a healthy 28-year-old. Achalasia causes dysphagia to both solids and liquids with regurgitation of undigested food, not isolated solid-food impaction in an atopic patient. Zenker diverticulum presents with oropharyngeal dysphagia, regurgitation of undigested food, halitosis, and a neck gurgle in older adults. The atopic history plus food impaction strongly suggests EoE.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate test to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Esophageal manometry in this setting
Upper endoscopy with esophageal biopsies
Barium swallow alone in this setting
Serum IgE level alone in this setting
stages.explanation: Eosinophilic esophagitis is diagnosed by upper endoscopy with esophageal biopsies showing eosinophil-predominant inflammation (≥15 eosinophils per high-power field), correlated with symptoms and after excluding other causes; multiple biopsies from proximal and distal esophagus are taken because involvement can be patchy. A barium swallow may show strictures or a small-caliber esophagus but cannot establish the histologic diagnosis. Manometry evaluates motility disorders, not EoE. A serum IgE level is often elevated in atopic patients but is neither sensitive nor specific for EoE and cannot confirm it. Endoscopy with biopsy is essential-both to count eosinophils and to assess for the characteristic endoscopic features.

stages.label: ENDOSCOPY & BIOPSY
stages.content:
Upper endoscopy: concentric rings ('trachealization'/feline esophagus), linear furrows, and scattered white exudates/plaques; a mid-esophageal narrowing
Biopsies: 38 eosinophils per high-power field in distal and proximal samples
No Candida on staining; no Barrett mucosa

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which is an appropriate first-line treatment for eosinophilic esophagitis?
options: Long-term systemic (oral) corticosteroids as initial therapy
Empiric antifungal therapy as initial therapy
A proton pump inhibitor and/or topical swallowed corticosteroids , with dietary elimination as an option
Immediate esophagectomy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: First-line treatment of EoE includes proton pump inhibitors and/or topical (swallowed) corticosteroids such as fluticasone sprayed and swallowed or viscous budesonide, with dietary elimination (e.g., empiric six-food or targeted elimination) as an effective alternative-the '3 Ds': drugs (PPI/topical steroids), diet, and dilation for strictures. Long-term systemic corticosteroids are avoided because of toxicity and are reserved for severe refractory cases. Antifungal therapy treats Candida esophagitis, which was excluded. Esophagectomy is not a treatment for EoE. The mainstays are swallowed topical steroids and/or PPI plus dietary management, with endoscopic dilation reserved for fibrotic strictures causing persistent dysphagia.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: After 8-12 weeks of therapy, what is the most appropriate way to assess treatment response?
options: Repeat upper endoscopy with biopsies to assess histologic remission
Order a barium swallow only as the immediate next step
Check a follow-up serum eosinophil count only in this setting
No follow-up is needed if symptoms improve in this setting
stages.explanation: Because symptoms and histology do not always correlate in EoE, a follow-up upper endoscopy with biopsies after 8-12 weeks of therapy is used to confirm histologic remission (reduction in eosinophil counts) and guide ongoing management. A peripheral eosinophil count does not reflect esophageal histology and is unreliable for monitoring EoE. A barium swallow assesses anatomy and strictures but not the eosinophilic inflammation that defines disease activity. Skipping follow-up risks ongoing subclinical inflammation that can progress to fibrosis and stricturing despite symptomatic improvement. Histologic reassessment is the standard for confirming response and tailoring long-term therapy.

pearls: Suspect eosinophilic esophagitis in a young atopic patient with solid-food dysphagia and food impaction; PPIs alone often fail to relieve symptoms.
Diagnosis requires endoscopy with biopsies showing ≥15 eosinophils/HPF; endoscopy may reveal rings (trachealization), linear furrows, and white exudates.
First-line treatment: PPI and/or swallowed topical corticosteroids, with dietary elimination as an alternative; dilate fibrotic strictures causing persistent dysphagia.
Reassess with repeat biopsies after 8-12 weeks because symptoms and histology do not always correlate.

cases.title: Long-standing Heartburn and a Surveillance Question
cases.chiefComplaint: Decades of heartburn and a referral to discuss the need for endoscopic surveillance.
cases.history: A 60-year-old man is referred after years of frequent heartburn and acid regurgitation, present for more than 20 years and often nocturnal. His symptoms are reasonably controlled on a daily proton pump inhibitor, but his primary physician is concerned about long-term complications. He denies dysphagia, odynophagia, weight loss, vomiting blood, or black stools. He has never had a screening endoscopy. Past medical history includes obesity and hypertension. Medications: omeprazole 40 mg daily, amlodipine 5 mg daily. Allergies: none. He is a former smoker (30 pack-years, quit 5 years ago) and drinks a few beers weekly. Family history is non-contributory for GI cancers. His main risk factors are chronic GERD, male sex, obesity (central adiposity), age, and prior smoking-the classic constellation prompting evaluation for a GERD complication.
cases.exam: The patient is overweight with central adiposity and is in no distress. The oropharynx is normal. The neck is supple without lymphadenopathy or thyromegaly, and there is no supraclavicular node. Lungs are clear. Cardiac exam is normal. The abdomen is obese, soft, and non-tender, without palpable masses or organomegaly. Digital rectal exam shows brown, guaiac-negative stool. There is no jaundice, no peripheral edema, and no stigmata of chronic liver disease. He appears well-nourished.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given his chronic GERD and multiple risk factors, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Empiric esophagectomy because of risk factors
24-hour pH monitoring as the screening test for Barrett
Upper endoscopy to screen for Barrett esophagus
Continue PPI without further evaluation indefinitely
stages.explanation: A man over 50 (this patient is 60) with chronic GERD (>5 years) and additional risk factors-male sex, obesity, prior smoking-meets criteria for screening upper endoscopy to detect Barrett esophagus, in which the normal squamous epithelium is replaced by metaplastic columnar epithelium with goblet cells. Simply continuing a PPI without screening misses the opportunity to detect Barrett and dysplasia. Esophagectomy is a major surgery never indicated as empiric prophylaxis. Ambulatory pH monitoring quantifies acid reflux but cannot visualize or biopsy the mucosa, so it cannot screen for Barrett metaplasia. Endoscopic screening with biopsy is the correct approach in this high-risk patient.

stages.label: ENDOSCOPY & BIOPSY
stages.content:
Upper endoscopy: salmon-colored mucosa extending 4 cm proximal to the GE junction (tongues of columnar-appearing epithelium)
Biopsies (Seattle protocol, four-quadrant): intestinal metaplasia with goblet cells confirmed
No dysplasia identified on this set of biopsies
No mass, ulcer, or stricture

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which histologic finding defines Barrett esophagus and confirms the diagnosis?
options: Eosinophilic infiltration of the epithelium as the primary diagnosis
Intestinal metaplasia with goblet cells replacing the normal squamous epithelium
Full-thickness invasive adenocarcinoma as the primary diagnosis
Squamous epithelium with reactive changes as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Barrett esophagus is defined by replacement of the normal stratified squamous epithelium of the distal esophagus with metaplastic columnar epithelium containing intestinal-type goblet cells (intestinal metaplasia)-a response to chronic acid injury and a precursor to esophageal adenocarcinoma. Squamous epithelium with reactive changes is not metaplasia and does not define Barrett. Eosinophilic infiltration defines eosinophilic esophagitis, a different disease. Full-thickness invasive adenocarcinoma is malignancy, the feared end-stage complication, not the definition of Barrett itself. The salmon-colored mucosa on endoscopy plus goblet-cell intestinal metaplasia on biopsy confirms Barrett esophagus.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For Barrett esophagus without dysplasia, what is the most appropriate management plan?
options: Immediate esophagectomy as initial therapy
Endoscopic radiofrequency ablation of all Barrett segments now as monotherapy
Stop the PPI and rely on lifestyle changes alone with no surveillance
Continue PPI therapy
stages.explanation: For nondysplastic Barrett esophagus, management is acid suppression with a PPI plus periodic surveillance endoscopy with biopsies (typically every 3-5 years) to detect progression to dysplasia. Radiofrequency ablation is reserved for confirmed dysplasia (low- or high-grade), not for nondysplastic Barrett in most patients. Esophagectomy is far too aggressive for nondysplastic metaplasia and carries high morbidity. Stopping the PPI and abandoning surveillance would remove both symptom and acid control and the safety net for detecting neoplastic progression. The standard is PPI plus scheduled endoscopic surveillance; intervention escalates only when dysplasia appears.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: If surveillance biopsies later show confirmed high-grade dysplasia, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Continue routine 3-year surveillance unchanged as the immediate next step
Double the PPI dose and re-scope in 5 years as the immediate next step
Begin systemic chemotherapy as the immediate next step
Endoscopic eradication therapy
stages.explanation: Confirmed high-grade dysplasia in Barrett esophagus carries a substantial risk of progression to adenocarcinoma, so endoscopic eradication therapy is indicated-endoscopic mucosal resection of any visible/nodular lesions (which also stages depth) combined with radiofrequency ablation of the remaining Barrett segment. Continuing routine 3-year surveillance is inadequate for high-grade dysplasia. Merely increasing the PPI dose does not treat dysplasia and unsafely delays definitive therapy. Systemic chemotherapy is for invasive or metastatic cancer, not for dysplasia confined to the mucosa. Endoscopic eradication has largely replaced esophagectomy for high-grade dysplasia and intramucosal cancer in suitable patients.

pearls: Screen for Barrett esophagus in chronic GERD patients with risk factors (age >50, male, obesity, smoking) using endoscopy with biopsy.
Barrett is defined histologically by intestinal metaplasia with goblet cells replacing squamous epithelium-a precursor to esophageal adenocarcinoma.
Nondysplastic Barrett: PPI therapy plus surveillance endoscopy every 3-5 years.
Low-grade dysplasia and especially high-grade dysplasia warrant endoscopic eradication therapy (EMR of nodules plus radiofrequency ablation).

cases.title: Worsening Solid-Food Dysphagia with Weight Loss
cases.chiefComplaint: Several months of progressively worsening difficulty swallowing solids with weight loss.
cases.history: A 67-year-old man presents with about four months of progressive dysphagia. It began with trouble swallowing meat and bread; now even softer solids stick, and he has shifted toward a soft and liquid diet. He has lost 9 kg unintentionally and feels fatigued. He has a long history of heartburn and was told years ago he had 'Barrett esophagus' but missed his surveillance appointments. He denies hematemesis but has noted occasional dark stools and mild substernal discomfort with swallowing. Past medical history: GERD/Barrett esophagus, hypertension, type 2 diabetes. Medications: omeprazole 40 mg daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily. Allergies: none. He has a 40 pack-year smoking history and drinks moderately. Family history is non-contributory. The progression from solids to softer foods with weight loss is the hallmark pattern that concerns him and his physician.
cases.exam: The patient appears thin and mildly cachectic but is in no acute distress. The oropharynx is normal. The neck is supple, but there is a firm, fixed left supraclavicular lymph node approximately 1.5 cm. Lungs are clear. Cardiac exam is normal. The abdomen is soft and non-tender; the liver edge is palpable 2 cm below the costal margin and feels firm. Digital rectal exam reveals dark stool that is guaiac-positive. There is mild temporal wasting. No jaundice is appreciated. Peripheral pulses are intact and there is no peripheral edema.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Progressive dysphagia to solids then softer foods, with weight loss in an older man with Barrett esophagus, most strongly suggests which diagnosis?
options: Achalasia as the first step today
Esophageal adenocarcinoma
Diffuse esophageal spasm
Eosinophilic esophagitis
stages.explanation: Progressive dysphagia (solids first, then softer foods/liquids) with significant weight loss in an older patient with longstanding GERD and Barrett esophagus, plus smoking, is the classic presentation of esophageal adenocarcinoma-Barrett is its principal precursor. Eosinophilic esophagitis occurs in younger atopic patients with intermittent, non-progressive impaction and without weight loss. Achalasia causes simultaneous solid-and-liquid dysphagia with regurgitation but is not associated with the progressive solid-to-soft pattern plus a supraclavicular node and weight loss. Diffuse esophageal spasm causes intermittent chest pain and non-progressive dysphagia, not weight loss and progressive mechanical obstruction. The progression, alarm features, and Barrett history point to adenocarcinoma.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic test?
options: Upper endoscopy with biopsy
PET scan as the first test
Esophageal manometry
Barium swallow alone
stages.explanation: Alarm-feature dysphagia with weight loss requires upper endoscopy with biopsy as the initial test; it allows direct visualization of any mass and tissue diagnosis to confirm malignancy. Manometry evaluates motility disorders and would not diagnose a tumor. A barium swallow may show an irregular, 'apple-core' narrowing suggestive of cancer but cannot provide tissue and is not the definitive first test when malignancy is suspected. A PET scan is used for staging (detecting distant metastases) after a cancer diagnosis is established, not as the first diagnostic study. Endoscopy with biopsy both visualizes and confirms the lesion histologically.

stages.label: ENDOSCOPY & BIOPSY
stages.content:
Upper endoscopy: ulcerated, friable, partially obstructing mass in the distal esophagus arising in a background of Barrett mucosa
Biopsy: invasive adenocarcinoma
Hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL, MCV 76 fL
Supraclavicular node and palpable firm liver on exam raise concern for metastatic spread

stages.label: STAGING
stages.question: After the cancer diagnosis, which combination of tests is most appropriate to stage the disease?
options: Colonoscopy and capsule endoscopy as the immediate next step
Repeat barium swallow only as the immediate next step
Endoscopic ultrasound plus CT and PET
Esophageal manometry and pH monitoring in this setting
stages.explanation: Staging esophageal cancer combines endoscopic ultrasound-which best assesses tumor depth (T) and regional nodes (N)-with CT of the chest/abdomen and PET (or PET-CT) to detect distant metastatic disease (M). Together these define the TNM stage and guide treatment. A repeat barium swallow adds nothing to staging once cancer is confirmed. Colonoscopy and capsule endoscopy evaluate lower and small-bowel sources of bleeding and are not staging tools for esophageal cancer. Manometry and pH monitoring assess motility and reflux and have no staging role. EUS plus cross-sectional and metabolic imaging (CT and PET) is the standard staging workup; the supraclavicular node and firm liver here heighten concern for metastatic (stage IV) disease.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Staging confirms distant metastatic (stage IV) disease. What is the most appropriate overall treatment approach?
options: Radiofrequency ablation of the tumor as initial therapy
Curative esophagectomy as initial therapy
Antireflux surgery (Nissen fundoplication) as initial therapy
Palliative therapy: systemic chemotherapy
stages.explanation: Metastatic (stage IV) esophageal adenocarcinoma is not curable, so management is palliative: systemic chemotherapy (with biomarker-directed agents where appropriate), dysphagia-relieving measures such as endoscopic stent placement or palliative radiation, and early integration of supportive/palliative care for nutrition and symptom control. Curative esophagectomy is reserved for localized, resectable disease and is inappropriate once distant metastases are present. Radiofrequency ablation treats Barrett dysplasia/intramucosal disease, not invasive metastatic cancer. Antireflux surgery treats GERD and has no role in managing established invasive cancer. The goal in stage IV disease is prolonging life and maximizing quality through palliative, multidisciplinary care.

pearls: Progressive dysphagia (solids then softer foods) with weight loss in an older patient is an alarm presentation-evaluate urgently with endoscopy and biopsy.
Esophageal adenocarcinoma arises from Barrett esophagus and is linked to chronic GERD, obesity, and smoking; it favors the distal esophagus.
Stage with endoscopic ultrasound (T and N) plus CT and PET (distant metastases); a left supraclavicular node and firm liver suggest metastatic spread.
Localized disease may be curable (surgery +/- chemoradiation), but stage IV disease is managed palliatively with chemotherapy, dysphagia relief (stenting/radiation), and supportive care.

cases.title: Progressive Trouble Swallowing Solids
cases.chiefComplaint: Difficulty swallowing solid food for the past 3 months with unintended weight loss.
cases.history: A 62-year-old man presents with 3 months of progressive dysphagia. He first noticed trouble swallowing meat and bread, which now sticks 'in the middle of my chest.' He has since switched to soft foods and liquids, which still pass. He reports unintentional loss of 18 lb over this period and reduced appetite. He denies heartburn, hematemesis, or melena but notes occasional regurgitation of undigested food. PMH is notable for hypertension and GERD treated intermittently with over-the-counter antacids. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily. No drug allergies. Social history: he has a 45-pack-year smoking history and drinks 4-6 alcoholic beverages daily for over 30 years. He works in construction. Family history is unremarkable for GI malignancy. Review of systems is positive for fatigue and a recent change in voice quality his wife noticed. He denies cough with swallowing, fevers, or night sweats. He has had no prior endoscopy.
cases.exam: The patient appears thin with temporal wasting. He is alert and in no acute distress. Oropharynx is clear without thrush or lesions. Neck exam reveals no palpable lymphadenopathy and no thyromegaly; voice is mildly hoarse. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal with clear lungs bilaterally. Abdomen is soft, nontender, without hepatomegaly or palpable masses. No supraclavicular nodes are appreciated. Skin shows no jaundice. Neurologic exam is grossly intact with normal gag reflex and no tongue deviation. There is no peripheral edema. Stool is brown and guaiac-negative on rectal exam.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature of this patient's dysphagia most strongly suggests a mechanical obstructing lesion rather than a motility disorder?
options: Relief of symptoms with carbonated beverages
Progressive dysphagia to solids first, then liquids
Regurgitation of undigested food in this clinical context
Dysphagia to solids and liquids simultaneously from onset
stages.explanation: Progressive dysphagia that begins with solids and later involves liquids is the classic pattern of a mechanical/obstructing lesion such as carcinoma or a stricture, because a narrowing lumen first impedes solids and only later affects liquids. Dysphagia to solids AND liquids from the outset (option C) suggests a motility disorder like achalasia. Regurgitation of undigested food (option A) is nonspecific and occurs in both obstruction and motility disorders. Relief with maneuvers like carbonated beverages or repeated swallows (option D) is more typical of achalasia or functional disorders. In a smoker/drinker with weight loss, the solids-then-liquids progression points strongly toward esophageal cancer until proven otherwise.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic test?
options: CT chest with contrast
Barium esophagram
Upper endoscopy with biopsy
Esophageal manometry
stages.explanation: Upper endoscopy with biopsy is the test of choice for new dysphagia with alarm features (weight loss, age >50, smoking/alcohol), because it permits direct visualization, tissue sampling for histologic diagnosis, and assessment of the lesion. A barium esophagram (option A) can characterize a stricture or mass but cannot obtain tissue and is generally reserved for cases where perforation or a complex anatomic question exists, or when endoscopy is contraindicated. Manometry (option B) evaluates motility disorders and is inappropriate when a structural lesion is suspected. CT chest (option D) is valuable for staging once cancer is confirmed but does not establish a tissue diagnosis. Biopsy is essential to distinguish squamous cell carcinoma from adenocarcinoma.

stages.label: ENDOSCOPY & BIOPSY
stages.content:
EGD: A circumferential, friable, partially obstructing mass at 28 cm from the incisors (mid-esophagus); scope passed with mild resistance
Biopsy: Invasive squamous cell carcinoma, moderately differentiated
No Barrett mucosa or columnar metaplasia identified
Hemoglobin 11.4 g/dL
Albumin 3.2 g/dL
