stages.label: RISK FACTORS
stages.question: Given the mid-esophageal location and squamous histology, which combination of this patient's exposures is the principal driver of his cancer?
options: Tobacco and alcohol use
Barrett esophagus with dysplasia
Chronic GERD and obesity
Helicobacter pylori infection
stages.explanation: Esophageal squamous cell carcinoma is most strongly linked to tobacco and alcohol, which act synergistically and typically produce tumors in the upper-to-mid esophagus, matching this lesion at 28 cm. Chronic GERD and obesity (option A) drive Barrett esophagus and adenocarcinoma, which favors the distal esophagus/GE junction. H. pylori (option C) is associated with gastric cancer and MALT lymphoma, and is actually inversely associated with esophageal adenocarcinoma; it is not a squamous cell driver. Barrett esophagus with dysplasia (option D) is the precursor for adenocarcinoma, not squamous cell carcinoma, and biopsy here showed no Barrett mucosa. Other SCC risk factors include caustic injury, achalasia, hot beverages, and nitrosamines.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After confirming the diagnosis, what is the most appropriate next step before deciding on definitive therapy?
options: Refer for esophagectomy without further workup as initial therapy
Place a self-expanding metal stent for palliation as initial therapy
Begin empiric chemoradiation immediately as initial therapy
Obtain CT of chest/abdomen
stages.explanation: Accurate staging guides therapy, so the next step is locoregional and distant staging with CT chest/abdomen, endoscopic ultrasound (for T and N depth), and typically PET-CT to detect metastases. Treatment is stage-dependent: superficial tumors may be resected endoscopically or surgically, locally advanced disease usually receives neoadjuvant chemoradiation followed by surgery, and metastatic disease is treated palliatively. Empiric chemoradiation (option A) or surgery (option B) without staging risks under- or over-treatment. Palliative stenting (option D) is reserved for incurable obstruction or as a bridge in select cases, not as an initial step before staging in a potentially curable patient. Nutritional optimization given his low albumin should occur in parallel.

pearls: Solids-then-liquids progressive dysphagia plus weight loss in a smoker/drinker is esophageal cancer until proven otherwise.
Squamous cell carcinoma favors the upper/mid esophagus and is driven by tobacco and alcohol; adenocarcinoma favors the distal esophagus and arises from Barrett/GERD.
EGD with biopsy is the diagnostic test of choice for dysphagia with alarm features; barium study cannot provide tissue.
Stage with CT, EUS, and PET before choosing among endoscopic resection, neoadjuvant chemoradiation plus surgery, or palliation.

cases.title: Bad Breath and Coughing Up Old Food
cases.chiefComplaint: Regurgitation of undigested food eaten hours earlier, along with persistent bad breath.
cases.history: A 72-year-old man presents with several months of regurgitating undigested food, sometimes from meals eaten hours earlier or the night before. He describes a sensation of food 'getting stuck' high in his throat and frequent throat-clearing. His wife complains of his persistent halitosis. He reports intermittent gurgling sounds in his neck when he swallows and has had two episodes of nocturnal coughing and choking. He has lost about 8 lb because he eats less to avoid symptoms. He denies heartburn, hematemesis, or pain on swallowing. PMH includes osteoarthritis and benign prostatic hyperplasia. Medications: acetaminophen as needed, tamsulosin 0.4 mg daily. No allergies. Social history: lifelong nonsmoker, rare alcohol, retired postal worker. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for a chronic cough and one episode treated as 'pneumonia' last winter. He denies fevers, night sweats, hoarseness, or neurologic symptoms. He has no difficulty initiating a swallow but feels food hangs up moments later.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and in no distress. Oral exam reveals notable halitosis without thrush or oral lesions. The neck is supple; on careful palpation of the left lower neck there is a soft, ill-defined fullness that produces a faint gurgling sound when compressed. There is no firm mass, lymphadenopathy, or thyromegaly. Voice is normal. Lungs are clear without crackles. Cardiac exam is unremarkable. Abdomen is soft and nontender without organomegaly. Cranial nerves are intact and the gag reflex is preserved; tongue is midline and palate elevates symmetrically. No tremor or focal weakness. Dentition is fair.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Based on the history, where is this patient's swallowing problem most likely localized?
options: Oropharyngeal/proximal esophageal region
Distal esophagus and gastroesophageal junction
Lower esophageal sphincter only
Stomach and pylorus in this clinical context
stages.explanation: The combination of food sticking high in the throat, regurgitation of undigested food, halitosis, a gurgling neck, and aspiration episodes localizes the problem to the oropharyngeal/proximal esophageal region, classic for a Zenker (pharyngoesophageal) diverticulum forming at Killian triangle. Distal esophageal/GEJ disease (option B) typically causes heartburn or solid-food dysphagia lower in the chest, not undigested food regurgitation with halitosis. Gastric/pyloric pathology (option C) causes early satiety, postprandial vomiting of partially digested food, and bloating rather than a gurgling neck. Isolated lower esophageal sphincter dysfunction (option D) describes achalasia, which causes dysphagia to solids and liquids but not the neck fullness and halitosis seen here.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic test?
options: Upper endoscopy as the first study
Barium swallow with video
Esophageal manometry
CT neck without contrast
stages.explanation: A barium swallow with dynamic/video imaging is the preferred first test for suspected Zenker diverticulum because it clearly demonstrates the outpouching, its size, and the cricopharyngeal bar, and it avoids the perforation risk of blind instrumentation. Upper endoscopy first (option A) is relatively hazardous because the scope can inadvertently enter and perforate the diverticular pouch rather than the true lumen; it is done cautiously, usually after imaging. Manometry (option C) may show cricopharyngeal dysfunction but is not the initial diagnostic study and does not delineate the pouch. CT neck (option D) is not the standard initial test and provides less functional detail than a contrast swallow. Imaging first protects the patient and guides therapy.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Barium esophagram (video): A posterior outpouching of contrast arising from the hypopharynx just above the cricopharyngeus on the left, with a prominent cricopharyngeal bar
Contrast pools in the pouch and spills back into the pharynx on subsequent swallows
No distal stricture or mass; lower esophagus and GE junction normal
Chest radiograph: clear lungs, no infiltrate

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: Which mechanism best explains the formation of this patient's diverticulum?
options: Failure of lower esophageal sphincter relaxation with esophageal aperistalsis as the immediate next step
A true full-thickness congenital outpouching of all esophageal layers as the immediate next step
Traction from mediastinal granulomatous inflammation pulling the esophageal wall outward in this setting
Impaired relaxation of the cricopharyngeus causing increased hypopharyngeal pressure
stages.explanation: A Zenker diverticulum is a false (pulsion) diverticulum: incoordination and impaired relaxation of the cricopharyngeus raises hypopharyngeal pressure during swallowing, herniating mucosa and submucosa through Killian triangle, a weak area between the inferior pharyngeal constrictor and the cricopharyngeus. Traction diverticula (option A) form mid-esophagus from external inflammation (e.g., mediastinal TB) pulling all layers outward and are true diverticula. A full-thickness congenital outpouching (option B) describes a true diverticulum, which Zenker is not. Failure of LES relaxation with aperistalsis (option D) defines achalasia, a distinct distal motility disorder. Recognizing the pulsion mechanism explains why definitive treatment addresses the cricopharyngeus.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has a symptomatic 3 cm diverticulum with aspiration. What is the most appropriate definitive management?
options: Cricopharyngeal myotomy with diverticulectomy or endoscopic cricopharyngeal myotomy
Botulinum toxin injection of the lower esophageal sphincter as initial therapy
Lifelong proton pump inhibitor therapy as initial therapy
Pneumatic dilation of the gastroesophageal junction as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Definitive treatment of a symptomatic Zenker diverticulum targets the underlying cricopharyngeal dysfunction with a cricopharyngeal myotomy, performed either via open surgery with diverticulectomy/diverticulopexy or, increasingly, via endoscopic (transoral stapling or flexible endoscopic) septotomy with myotomy. Cutting the cricopharyngeus relieves the high-pressure zone and is essential to prevent recurrence; simply removing the pouch without myotomy fails. PPIs (option A) treat reflux, not a pulsion diverticulum. Botulinum toxin to the LES (option C) and pneumatic dilation of the GEJ (option D) are achalasia treatments aimed at the lower esophageal sphincter and are anatomically irrelevant to this proximal lesion. Given aspiration and weight loss, intervention is clearly indicated.

pearls: Halitosis, regurgitation of undigested food, a gurgling neck mass, and aspiration in an older adult point to Zenker diverticulum.
It is a false (pulsion) diverticulum herniating through Killian triangle due to cricopharyngeal dysfunction.
Diagnose with a barium swallow first; endoscopy carries perforation risk if done blindly into the pouch.
Definitive therapy requires cricopharyngeal myotomy (open or endoscopic), not just diverticulectomy.

cases.title: Fatigue, Brittle Nails, and Food Catching in the Throat
cases.chiefComplaint: Several months of intermittent difficulty swallowing solids along with worsening fatigue.
cases.history: A 46-year-old woman presents with several months of intermittent dysphagia to solids, especially bread and dry meat, with a sensation of food sticking at the level of the upper sternum. Symptoms are not progressive and liquids pass easily. She also reports months of fatigue, exertional dyspnea climbing stairs, and a sore, smooth tongue. She has noticed brittle, spoon-shaped nails and occasional cravings to chew ice. PMH includes heavy menstrual periods for years, attributed to fibroids, and no prior surgery. Medications: a daily multivitamin only. No allergies. Social history: nonsmoker, social alcohol use, works as a teacher. Family history: mother with 'thyroid problems,' no known GI cancer. She is vegetarian. Review of systems is positive for pallor, cold intolerance, and angular cheilosis; she denies weight loss, hematemesis, melena, odynophagia, or heartburn. She reports no recent illness or new medications. Her dysphagia has prompted her to eat slowly and drink water with meals.
cases.exam: The patient is pale, particularly of the conjunctivae and palmar creases. There is angular cheilosis at both corners of the mouth and a smooth, depapillated (atrophic) tongue (glossitis). Nails are brittle with mild koilonychia (spooning). The oropharynx shows no thrush or masses. Neck is supple without lymphadenopathy; thyroid is normal in size. Cardiac exam reveals a soft systolic flow murmur at the left sternal border and mild tachycardia; lungs are clear. Abdomen is soft, nontender, without organomegaly or masses. No splenomegaly. Stool is brown and guaiac-negative. Neurologic exam, including proprioception and vibration sense, is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which set of findings forms the characteristic triad pointing toward the unifying diagnosis?
options: Dysphagia, iron-deficiency anemia, and esophageal web
Dysphagia, vitamin B12 deficiency, and esophageal dysmotility
Heartburn, Barrett esophagus, and peptic stricture
Odynophagia, oral thrush, and immunosuppression
stages.explanation: The classic triad of Plummer-Vinson (Paterson-Brown-Kelly) syndrome is dysphagia, iron-deficiency anemia, and an upper esophageal web. This patient shows solid-food dysphagia, signs of iron deficiency (pallor, koilonychia, glossitis, cheilosis, pica/ice-chewing, heavy menses), and the localization fits a proximal web. B12 deficiency with dysmotility (option B) would more likely cause neurologic signs (which she lacks) and is not the recognized triad. Thrush with immunosuppression (option C) describes Candida esophagitis, which causes odynophagia, not a web. Heartburn with Barrett and peptic stricture (option D) is a reflux complication of the distal esophagus, inconsistent with her proximal, non-acid picture. Identifying the triad directs the workup.

stages.label: LABORATORY RESULTS
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 9.1 g/dL
MCV 71 fL (microcytic)
RDW 18% (elevated)
Ferritin 6 ng/mL (low)
Serum iron 28 µg/dL (low)
Total iron-binding capacity 460 µg/dL (high)
Transferrin saturation 6% (low)
Reticulocyte count low-normal
Vitamin B12 480 pg/mL (normal)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The labs confirm iron-deficiency anemia. What is the best next step to evaluate her dysphagia?
options: Barium esophagram and/or upper endoscopy to identify
24-hour esophageal pH monitoring as the primary diagnosis
Esophageal manometry as the primary diagnosis
Empiric proton pump inhibitor trial as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: With iron-deficiency anemia and proximal solid-food dysphagia, the next step is to look for an esophageal web using a barium esophagram (which shows a thin, shelf-like anterior indentation in the upper esophagus) and/or upper endoscopy, which can both visualize and disrupt the web. Manometry (option A) evaluates motility disorders, not a structural web. An empiric PPI trial (option B) targets reflux, which is not her problem and would delay diagnosis. Esophageal pH monitoring (option D) assesses acid exposure for GERD and is irrelevant here. Importantly, endoscopy also allows surveillance because Plummer-Vinson is a risk factor for proximal esophageal/hypopharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Endoscopy reveals a thin upper esophageal web that is gently dilated. What is the cornerstone of long-term management?
options: Repeated serial pneumatic dilations every month indefinitely
Gluten-free diet as initial therapy
Lifelong proton pump inhibitor therapy as initial therapy
Iron repletion and correction of the underlying source of blood loss
stages.explanation: The cornerstone of management is iron repletion plus identifying and correcting the cause of iron loss (here, menorrhagia/fibroids), because the web and dysphagia frequently improve once iron deficiency is corrected; dilation handles persistent obstruction. PPIs (option A) treat acid reflux, not this iron-deficiency-related web. Indefinite monthly dilations (option C) are excessive; many webs respond to iron therapy and a single dilation, with repeat only as needed. A gluten-free diet (option D) treats celiac disease; while celiac can cause iron deficiency and should be considered if repletion fails, it is not the cornerstone here and there is no enteropathy described. Long-term, she also warrants monitoring given the malignancy association.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which long-term complication is this syndrome specifically associated with, warranting ongoing surveillance?
options: Gastric MALT lymphoma as the leading concern
Squamous cell carcinoma of the hypopharynx
Achalasia as the leading concern
Esophageal adenocarcinoma of the distal esophagus
stages.explanation: Plummer-Vinson syndrome carries an increased risk of squamous cell carcinoma of the hypopharynx and upper/postcricoid esophagus, so patients warrant clinical vigilance and surveillance for new or worsening symptoms. Distal esophageal adenocarcinoma (option A) is tied to Barrett esophagus/GERD, a different pathway not associated with this syndrome. Gastric MALT lymphoma (option C) is linked to chronic Helicobacter pylori infection, unrelated to iron-deficiency webs. Achalasia (option D) is a primary motility disorder and is not a complication of Plummer-Vinson; it has its own (modest) association with esophageal SCC but is a separate entity. Recognizing the malignancy association ensures these patients are not lost to follow-up after iron repletion.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Before attributing all her iron deficiency to menorrhagia, which additional consideration is most appropriate in a 46-year-old?
options: No further evaluation is needed once menses are addressed as the immediate next step
Transfuse packed red blood cells regardless of symptoms as the immediate next step
Start erythropoietin to raise hemoglobin faster as the immediate next step
Evaluate for gastrointestinal blood loss/malabsorption as clinically indicated
stages.explanation: Even with obvious menorrhagia, iron deficiency in a middle-aged adult should prompt consideration of occult GI blood loss or malabsorption; depending on risk factors and response to iron, evaluation may include colonoscopy and celiac serologies, particularly if anemia persists despite repletion or stool testing is positive. Stopping the workup entirely (option A) risks missing a coexisting GI malignancy or celiac disease. Erythropoietin (option C) is not indicated for straightforward iron deficiency with intact marrow and would not address the iron deficit. Transfusion regardless of symptoms (option D) is inappropriate for chronic, hemodynamically stable iron-deficiency anemia, which is treated with iron repletion. Tailoring the search to her age and presentation is good stewardship.

cases.diagnosis: Plummer-Vinson syndrome (esophageal web with iron-deficiency anemia)
pearls: Plummer-Vinson triad: dysphagia, iron-deficiency anemia, and an upper esophageal web; look for koilonychia, glossitis, cheilosis, and pica.
Diagnose the web with barium esophagram and/or endoscopy; endoscopy can also dilate it and screen for malignancy.
Iron repletion plus correcting the bleeding source is the cornerstone; the web often improves once iron is replaced.
The syndrome predisposes to squamous cell carcinoma of the hypopharynx and upper esophagus, mandating surveillance.

cases.title: Painful Swallowing in a Man with HIV
cases.chiefComplaint: One week of pain on swallowing and a sensation of food sticking behind the breastbone.
cases.history: A 39-year-old man presents with one week of progressive odynophagia and retrosternal discomfort with swallowing, worse with solid foods. He notes decreased oral intake and a 'cottony' feeling in his mouth. He was diagnosed with HIV three years ago but stopped antiretroviral therapy about a year ago and has missed clinic visits. He denies fever, chest pain unrelated to swallowing, hematemesis, or vesicular lesions on his lips. PMH is otherwise unremarkable. Medications: none currently (previously on a single-tablet ART regimen). No drug allergies. Social history: he has sex with men, smokes half a pack daily, and drinks alcohol socially. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for a 6-lb weight loss over two months, oral discomfort, and mild fatigue. He denies cough, dyspnea, or skin lesions. He has not taken antibiotics, inhaled corticosteroids, or proton pump inhibitors recently. He reports no prior episodes of similar symptoms.
cases.exam: The patient is thin but in no acute distress. Oral exam reveals multiple white, curd-like plaques on the buccal mucosa and tongue that scrape off with a tongue blade, leaving an erythematous base. There are no oral ulcers, vesicles, or lip lesions. The posterior pharynx is mildly erythematous. Neck is supple without lymphadenopathy; no thrush-related fissuring at the lip corners. Lungs are clear. Cardiac exam is normal. Abdomen is soft and nontender without organomegaly. Skin shows no rash or zoster. There is no hepatosplenomegaly. The remainder of the exam, including neurologic exam, is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given odynophagia, white oral plaques, and untreated HIV, what is the most likely cause of his esophagitis and the most appropriate initial step?
options: HSV esophagitis; start empiric acyclovir
Candida esophagitis; start empiric oral fluconazole
Pill esophagitis; stop the offending medication
CMV esophagitis; obtain CMV PCR before any treatment
stages.explanation: In an immunocompromised patient with oral thrush and odynophagia, Candida esophagitis is by far the most likely cause, and the standard initial approach is empiric oral fluconazole; symptomatic improvement within days both treats and supports the diagnosis. Endoscopy is reserved for patients who fail empiric therapy. CMV esophagitis (option A) causes large, linear/deep ulcers and is diagnosed by biopsy, not first-line empiric PCR, and is less likely with overt thrush. HSV esophagitis (option B) causes small, shallow vesicles/ulcers ('volcano' ulcers) and oral vesicles, which he lacks. Pill esophagitis (option D) follows a culprit medication taken without water, which he denies. The presence of thrush makes Candida the leading diagnosis.

stages.label: LABS & FOLLOW-UP
stages.content:
CD4 count 84 cells/µL
HIV viral load 96,000 copies/mL
KOH prep of oral scraping: budding yeast with pseudohyphae
After 7 days of fluconazole: odynophagia persists and is now severe; patient struggling to swallow saliva
No new vesicular lip or skin lesions

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: He has failed a week of fluconazole with worsening odynophagia. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Barium swallow to look for a stricture in this setting
Double the fluconazole dose and continue another two weeks
Empiric ganciclovir without further testing in this setting
Upper endoscopy with biopsies for histopathology
stages.explanation: Failure of empiric antifungal therapy in a patient with advanced HIV mandates upper endoscopy with biopsy to identify another cause, particularly viral esophagitis (CMV or HSV) or coexisting fluconazole-resistant candidiasis. Biopsy location and histology distinguish the pathogens, guiding targeted therapy. Simply escalating fluconazole (option A) delays diagnosis when a non-Candida process is now likely. Empiric ganciclovir (option C) commits the patient to a toxic drug without confirming CMV and ignores HSV and resistant Candida. A barium swallow (option D) cannot provide tissue and is low-yield here; ulcerative infectious esophagitis is best characterized endoscopically with biopsy. Endoscopy is both diagnostic and therapeutic in directing antimicrobial choice.

stages.label: ENDOSCOPY & BIOPSY
stages.content:
EGD: Scattered Candida plaques plus several large, deep, well-demarcated linear ulcers in the mid-to-distal esophagus
Biopsy from ulcer base: large cells with intranuclear 'owl's eye' inclusions and cytoplasmic inclusions; immunostain positive for cytomegalovirus
No multinucleated giant cells or Cowdry A intranuclear inclusions at ulcer edges (HSV negative)
Fungal elements present on surface plaques consistent with treated Candida

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The biopsy findings best fit which diagnosis, and how does it differ from the main viral mimic?
options: CMV esophagitis, with large/deep linear ulcers
Recurrent Candida only, with biopsy showing owl's eye inclusions as the primary diagnosis
HSV esophagitis, which classically causes large deep ulcers with owl's eye inclusions as the primary diagnosis
Pill esophagitis with secondary infection as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: The biopsy shows CMV esophagitis: CMV produces large, deep, often linear ulcers and infects endothelial/mesenchymal cells, yielding enlarged cells with intranuclear 'owl's eye' inclusions and cytoplasmic inclusions, best obtained by biopsying the ulcer base. Its key mimic, HSV, causes small, shallow, 'volcano-like' ulcers with multinucleated giant cells and Cowdry A intranuclear inclusions found at the ulcer edge. Option 0 incorrectly assigns owl's eye inclusions and large ulcers to HSV. Recurrent Candida (option C) shows pseudohyphae, not owl's eye inclusions. Pill esophagitis (option D) lacks viral inclusions and a culprit medication is absent. Distinguishing CMV from HSV is essential because treatment differs (ganciclovir/valganciclovir vs acyclovir).

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate treatment plan now?
options: Ganciclovir for CMV plus initiation/re-initiation of antiretroviral therapy
Nystatin swish-and-swallow and discharge as initial therapy
Continue fluconazole only and observe as initial therapy
Acyclovir alone as initial therapy
stages.explanation: CMV esophagitis is treated with ganciclovir or oral valganciclovir (foscarnet as an alternative), and because the root problem is advanced immunosuppression from untreated HIV, antiretroviral therapy should be (re)started, ideally in coordination with HIV specialists, to restore CD4 count and provide durable control. Acyclovir alone (option A) targets HSV, not CMV, and would not treat this infection. Continuing only fluconazole (option B) ignores the diagnosed CMV ulcers. Nystatin and discharge (option D) is inadequate—nystatin is weak even for Candida esophagitis and does nothing for CMV. Restoring immune function via ART is central to preventing recurrent opportunistic esophagitis in this patient with a CD4 of 84.

cases.diagnosis: Candida esophagitis with superimposed CMV esophagitis in advanced HIV
pearls: Odynophagia plus oral thrush in HIV warrants empiric fluconazole; endoscopy is reserved for treatment failure.
CMV esophagitis: large/deep linear ulcers with intranuclear 'owl's eye' inclusions—biopsy the ulcer base; treat with ganciclovir/valganciclovir.
HSV esophagitis: small, shallow 'volcano' ulcers with multinucleated giant cells and Cowdry A inclusions at the ulcer edge; treat with acyclovir.
Advanced opportunistic esophagitis signals immune failure—(re)starting antiretroviral therapy is essential.

cases.title: Sudden Painful Swallowing After a New Antibiotic
cases.chiefComplaint: Two days of sharp pain on swallowing and pain behind the breastbone after starting an antibiotic.
cases.history: A 23-year-old woman presents with 2 days of sharp odynophagia and retrosternal chest pain that worsens when she swallows. Symptoms began the morning after she started doxycycline for acne. She admits she took the capsule at bedtime with only a tiny sip of water and immediately lay down to sleep. She has no prior swallowing problems and no heartburn history. She denies fever, cough, vomiting, hematemesis, or shortness of breath. PMH is unremarkable; she is otherwise healthy and not immunocompromised. Medications: doxycycline 100 mg twice daily (started 2 days ago) and a combined oral contraceptive pill. No drug allergies. Social history: nonsmoker, occasional alcohol on weekends, college student. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is otherwise negative; she specifically denies oral lesions, white plaques, prior HIV risk factors, or recent illness. She is able to drink liquids slowly but solids cause significant pain. She has not changed any other medications.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and in no distress, speaking in full sentences. Oral cavity and oropharynx are normal with no thrush, ulcers, or vesicles. Neck is supple without lymphadenopathy. Lungs are clear to auscultation bilaterally. Cardiac exam reveals a regular rate and rhythm with no murmurs; there is no chest wall tenderness on palpation. Abdomen is soft, nontender, with normal bowel sounds and no organomegaly. Skin shows no rash. She demonstrates mild discomfort with a dry swallow but no drooling and protects her airway. The remainder of the exam is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis given the history?
options: Acute coronary syndrome
Eosinophilic esophagitis
Pill-induced esophagitis
Candida esophagitis in this clinical context
stages.explanation: The temporal link between starting doxycycline taken with minimal water at bedtime followed by recumbency and the abrupt onset of odynophagia and retrosternal pain is classic for pill-induced esophagitis. Doxycycline (and other tetracyclines, NSAIDs, bisphosphonates, potassium chloride, iron) is a notorious culprit; pills lodge in the mid-esophagus and cause focal caustic mucosal injury/ulceration. Candida esophagitis (option A) requires immunosuppression or thrush, both absent in this healthy young woman with a normal oral exam. Acute coronary syndrome (option B) is exceedingly unlikely at age 23 with pain clearly provoked by swallowing rather than exertion. Eosinophilic esophagitis (option D) causes dysphagia and food impaction over time, often with atopy, not acute odynophagia after a single pill.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step in management for this otherwise healthy patient?
options: Obtain a CT angiogram of the chest as the primary diagnosis
Immediate upper endoscopy to confirm ulceration
Start empiric fluconazole as the primary diagnosis
Discontinue or properly re-dose the offending medication
stages.explanation: Pill esophagitis in a healthy, hemodynamically stable patient is a clinical diagnosis treated conservatively: stop or appropriately re-administer the offending drug, provide supportive care (analgesia, sometimes a topical agent or short-course acid suppression), and ensure correct pill-taking technique. Most cases heal within days to a couple of weeks. Immediate endoscopy (option A) is unnecessary unless there is bleeding, severe/persistent symptoms, suspicion of stricture, or diagnostic uncertainty. Empiric fluconazole (option C) is inappropriate because there is no evidence of candidal infection in an immunocompetent patient with a normal oral exam. CT angiogram (option D) addresses pulmonary embolism/aortic pathology, not pill-related mucosal injury, and is not indicated by this presentation.

stages.label: CLINICAL COURSE
stages.content:
Doxycycline held; acne plan revised
Patient counseled on pill-taking technique
Acetaminophen provided for pain
Oral intake of liquids tolerated; advised to advance diet as tolerated
Return precautions given for fever, inability to swallow saliva, hematemesis, or worsening chest pain

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: What counseling best prevents recurrence of pill-induced esophagitis?
options: Crush all capsules and take them dry as the immediate next step
Take pills with a full glass of water
Always take medications on an empty stomach at bedtime
Take pills lying down to slow their passage in this setting
stages.explanation: Prevention centers on proper pill-taking technique: swallow medications with a full glass (at least 4-8 oz) of water and stay upright (sitting or standing) for at least 30 minutes afterward so the pill clears the esophagus into the stomach. Lying down (option A) promotes pill retention and mucosal contact, exactly what caused her injury. Crushing capsules and taking them dry (option B) increases caustic mucosal exposure and is not a general solution; many formulations should not be crushed. Taking medications dry at bedtime (option D) recreates the high-risk scenario. Reinforcing these simple behaviors—ample water and remaining upright—reliably reduces recurrence, especially with doxycycline, bisphosphonates, NSAIDs, iron, and potassium chloride.

stages.label: RED FLAGS
stages.question: Which development would prompt escalation to upper endoscopy in this patient?
options: Mild residual chest discomfort improving over 3 days as the immediate next step
Transient bad taste in the mouth as the immediate next step
Persistent or worsening symptoms beyond a couple of weeks, hematemesis, or signs of stricture/obstruction
Resolution of pain after stopping the drug as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Endoscopy is warranted if symptoms persist or worsen beyond the expected healing window (typically up to about 2 weeks), or if alarm features appear—hematemesis, melena, high fever, inability to swallow saliva, or signs of stricture/obstruction—because these suggest deep ulceration, complication, or an alternative diagnosis. Mild discomfort that is improving (option A) and pain resolution after drug cessation (option D) reflect the expected favorable course and need no endoscopy. A transient bad taste (option C) is trivial and unrelated to mucosal complications. Recognizing when conservative management has failed prevents both unnecessary procedures and missed complications such as a stricture requiring dilation.

pearls: Abrupt odynophagia and retrosternal pain after a pill taken with little water at bedtime is classic pill esophagitis.
Common culprits: tetracyclines (doxycycline), NSAIDs, bisphosphonates, potassium chloride, and iron.
Management is conservative—stop/re-dose the drug, supportive care; most heal within 1-2 weeks.
Prevent recurrence by taking pills with a full glass of water and staying upright for 30 minutes; endoscopy only for alarm features or non-healing.

cases.title: Feeling Full After a Few Bites with Weight Loss
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden severe chest and upper abdominal pain with retching and inability to vomit.
cases.history: A 68-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with 4 hours of severe, constant lower chest and epigastric pain that began after a large dinner. She has been retching repeatedly but cannot bring anything up, and she feels she 'cannot burp.' For the past year she has had intermittent postprandial fullness, mild dysphagia, and occasional chest pressure relieved by belching, attributed to reflux. Tonight the pain is far worse and unremitting. She denies hematemesis but feels bloated. PMH includes hypertension, osteoporosis, and GERD. Medications: lisinopril 20 mg daily, alendronate weekly, omeprazole 20 mg daily. No allergies. Social history: former smoker (quit 10 years ago), rare alcohol. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for nausea, retching, chest pressure, and lightheadedness; she denies fever, melena, or prior abdominal surgery. She notes a long-standing 'hiatal hernia' mentioned on a prior imaging study. She has not passed flatus since symptom onset and feels increasingly distended in the upper abdomen.
cases.exam: The patient is uncomfortable, diaphoretic, and restless. She is tachycardic. Oropharynx is clear. Neck is supple. Cardiac exam shows tachycardia without murmur; lungs are clear but breath sounds are slightly diminished at the left base. The upper abdomen/epigastrium is distended and tympanitic with tenderness but no rigidity or rebound; bowel sounds are present but reduced. There is no peritonitis. An attempt to pass a nasogastric tube meets resistance and cannot be advanced into the stomach. Extremities are well-perfused with capillary refill <2 seconds. Rectal exam shows brown, guaiac-negative stool. She has no surgical scars on the abdomen.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given chronic upper-GI symptoms now with acute pain, retching without vomiting, and inability to pass an NG tube, what complication should be suspected?
options: Acute pancreatitis as the first step today
Perforated peptic ulcer as the first step today
Acute coronary syndrome as the first step today
Gastric volvulus complicating a paraesophageal hiatal hernia
stages.explanation: The triad of sudden severe epigastric/chest pain, violent retching with inability to vomit, and inability to pass a nasogastric tube (Borchardt triad) signals gastric volvulus, a feared complication of a paraesophageal hiatal hernia in which the stomach rotates and obstructs. Her known hiatal hernia and chronic postprandial symptoms set the stage. Acute coronary syndrome (option A) must be excluded but does not explain the failed NG passage and retching pattern. Pancreatitis (option C) causes epigastric pain radiating to the back with elevated lipase but not Borchardt triad. Perforated peptic ulcer (option D) typically causes a rigid, peritonitic abdomen with free air, not an inability to advance an NG tube into a rotated stomach. Volvulus is a surgical emergency.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate imaging study?
options: Right upper quadrant ultrasound
Upright chest radiograph
HIDA scan as the primary diagnosis
Esophageal manometry as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: An upright chest radiograph (often showing a retrocardiac air-fluid level or intrathoracic stomach) followed by CT of the chest/abdomen rapidly confirms a paraesophageal hernia and characterizes volvulus, obstruction, and any ischemia or perforation, guiding emergent management. RUQ ultrasound (option A) evaluates the gallbladder and is not the study for suspected gastric volvulus. A HIDA scan (option B) assesses cystic duct patency in cholecystitis and is irrelevant and too slow. Esophageal manometry (option D) is a motility test inappropriate in an acute obstructive emergency. CT is preferred because it not only confirms herniation and rotation but also detects strangulation/ischemia, which dictates the urgency and type of surgical intervention.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 13.8 ×10³/µL
Lactate 2.4 mmol/L (mildly elevated)
Lipase 38 U/L (normal)
Troponin <0.01 ng/mL
Upright chest radiograph: large retrocardiac air-fluid level; gas-filled viscus in the left lower thorax
CT chest/abdomen: organoaxial gastric volvulus with the majority of the stomach herniated through a paraesophageal defect; no free air; wall enhancement preserved

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: CT shows volvulus without frank perforation. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Begin a clear liquid diet and observe overnight without surgical involvement
Schedule elective outpatient endoscopy in 6 weeks as initial therapy
Discharge on a higher-dose proton pump inhibitor as initial therapy
Attempt nasogastric decompression
stages.explanation: Symptomatic gastric volvulus is a surgical emergency: attempt nasogastric decompression (which may not pass if the stomach is fully twisted) to relieve distension and obtain urgent surgical consultation for reduction and repair of the paraesophageal hernia, with fundoplication/gastropexy as indicated. Even without perforation, a twisted, obstructed stomach risks progression to strangulation and necrosis. Discharge on a PPI (option A) ignores a life-threatening mechanical emergency. Elective endoscopy in 6 weeks (option C) is dangerously delayed. Observation with a clear liquid diet and no surgical involvement (option D) risks ischemia and perforation. Time-sensitive surgical management is the standard, with endoscopic detorsion considered only in select non-operative candidates.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After successful repair, what distinguishes the paraesophageal (type II) from the more common sliding (type I) hiatal hernia and explains her risk?
options: Paraesophageal hernias never require surgery with appropriate follow-up
Sliding hernias carry the highest risk of strangulation with appropriate follow-up
In a paraesophageal hernia the GE junction remains in place while the gastric fundus herniates alongside the esophagus, predisposing to volvulus/strangulation
In a paraesophageal hernia the GE junction migrates above the diaphragm while the fundus stays below with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: In a true paraesophageal (type II) hiatal hernia, the gastroesophageal junction stays in its normal position below the diaphragm while the gastric fundus herniates upward alongside the esophagus through the hiatus; this anatomy predisposes to incarceration, volvulus, and strangulation, exactly her complication. A sliding (type I) hernia (option A describes the opposite) involves the GE junction itself migrating above the diaphragm and is usually associated with reflux, not strangulation. Sliding hernias do NOT carry the highest strangulation risk (option B)—paraesophageal types do. Symptomatic or complicated paraesophageal hernias do require surgery (contradicting option D); even some asymptomatic large ones are repaired in appropriate candidates. Recognizing the anatomy explains why her presentation was an emergency.

cases.diagnosis: Gastric volvulus complicating a paraesophageal hiatal hernia
pearls: Borchardt triad—sudden epigastric/chest pain, retching without vomiting, inability to pass an NG tube—signals gastric volvulus.
Paraesophageal (type II) hernia: GE junction stays below the diaphragm while the fundus herniates, predisposing to volvulus and strangulation.
Upright chest x-ray (retrocardiac air-fluid level) plus CT confirms the hernia and detects ischemia.
Symptomatic gastric volvulus is a surgical emergency: decompress and consult surgery urgently.

cases.title: Losing Weight and Full After Small Meals
cases.chiefComplaint: Three months of early fullness with meals and progressive weight loss.
cases.history: A 64-year-old man presents with 3 months of early satiety, vague epigastric discomfort, and a 22-lb unintentional weight loss. He feels full after only a few bites and has reduced his intake. He reports mild nausea and one episode of dark, tarry stool last week. He denies hematemesis or dysphagia. PMH includes chronic dyspepsia and prior Helicobacter pylori infection treated 'years ago.' Medications: omeprazole 20 mg daily, taken intermittently. No allergies. Social history: 30-pack-year smoking history, moderate alcohol, and a diet historically high in salted and smoked foods; he emigrated from a region with high gastric cancer prevalence. Family history: father died of 'stomach cancer.' Review of systems is positive for fatigue and decreased appetite; he denies fevers or night sweats. He has not had prior endoscopy. He notes his belt has loosened and his energy is poor. He has no jaundice or abdominal swelling that he has noticed.
cases.exam: The patient appears thin with mild temporal wasting and pallor. The oral cavity is clear. Notably, there is a firm, fixed left supraclavicular lymph node (Virchow node) and a palpable periumbilical nodule. The thyroid is normal. Lungs are clear; cardiac exam is unremarkable. The epigastrium is mildly tender without a clearly palpable mass; there is no hepatomegaly, ascites, or splenomegaly appreciated, though the exam is limited by body habitus. Rectal exam reveals melenic, guaiac-positive stool; no rectal shelf is definitively felt. Extremities show no edema. Skin shows no jaundice. There is no acanthosis nigricans noted.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which finding on his exam most specifically raises concern for metastatic gastric malignancy?
options: Mild epigastric tenderness
Tachycardia in this clinical context
Pallor as the first step today
A firm left supraclavicular node
stages.explanation: A firm, fixed left supraclavicular lymph node (Virchow node) is a classic sign of metastatic abdominal malignancy, especially gastric cancer, because the thoracic duct drains into the left subclavian venous system; a palpable Sister Mary Joseph (periumbilical) nodule similarly indicates metastatic spread. Mild epigastric tenderness (option A) is nonspecific and common in benign dyspepsia. Pallor (option C) reflects anemia from chronic blood loss but is not specific to malignancy. Tachycardia (option D) is nonspecific. Recognizing Virchow node in a patient with early satiety, weight loss, melena, and strong risk factors (H. pylori, smoked/salted diet, family history, endemic region) strongly suggests advanced gastric adenocarcinoma and should accelerate definitive evaluation.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: Abdominal ultrasound as the primary diagnosis
Continue PPI and recheck in 8 weeks as the primary diagnosis
Stool antigen test for H. pylori and empiric eradication
Upper endoscopy with multiple biopsies of the gastric lesion
stages.explanation: With alarm features (age >60, weight loss, melena/anemia, early satiety, palpable Virchow node), upper endoscopy with multiple biopsies is the essential next step to visualize the gastric lesion and obtain tissue for diagnosis. Linitis plastica can be subtle, so multiple deep biopsies are taken because the diffuse infiltrative type may not form an obvious mass. Testing/treating H. pylori alone (option A) is inappropriate when malignancy is strongly suspected and would dangerously delay diagnosis. Abdominal ultrasound (option C) is low-yield for a gastric primary. Continuing a PPI and rechecking later (option D) ignores alarm features and risks progression. Endoscopy provides both diagnosis and locoregional assessment, after which staging (CT, EUS, PET) and oncology referral follow.

stages.label: ENDOSCOPY & BIOPSY
stages.content:
EGD: Diffusely thickened, rigid, poorly distensible gastric wall with loss of normal rugal folds ('leather bottle' appearance); no discrete ulcer crater
Biopsy: Poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma with signet-ring cells infiltrating the wall (diffuse-type)
Hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL
Albumin 3.0 g/dL
CT abdomen/pelvis: gastric wall thickening with regional and left supraclavicular lymphadenopathy

stages.label: PATHOLOGY
stages.question: The biopsy shows signet-ring cells diffusely infiltrating a rigid, non-distensible stomach. What is this morphologic pattern called?
options: Intestinal-type adenocarcinoma forming a polypoid mass
Linitis plastica
Gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST)
MALT lymphoma as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Diffuse infiltration by signet-ring cells producing a rigid, thickened, non-distensible 'leather bottle' stomach with loss of rugal folds is linitis plastica, the diffuse-type gastric adenocarcinoma; it spreads submucosally, may evade superficial biopsy, and carries a poor prognosis. Intestinal-type adenocarcinoma (option B) tends to form discrete glandular/polypoid masses, often in older patients with H. pylori-related atrophic gastritis. MALT lymphoma (option C) is a B-cell lymphoma linked to H. pylori, with lymphoid (not signet-ring epithelial) infiltrates. GIST (option D) is a mesenchymal tumor (KIT/CD117-positive spindle cells), not an infiltrative signet-ring carcinoma. Identifying linitis plastica explains the diffusely rigid stomach and the need for deep, multiple biopsies.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given the Virchow node and CT findings, what is the most appropriate overall management approach?
options: Endoscopic mucosal resection of the lesion as initial therapy
Complete staging and multidisciplinary oncology evaluation
H. pylori eradication and surveillance endoscopy in 1 year as initial therapy
Immediate curative total gastrectomy alone as initial therapy
stages.explanation: A palpable Virchow node with distant nodal disease indicates likely metastatic (stage IV) disease, so the approach is complete staging (CT, often PET, sometimes diagnostic laparoscopy for peritoneal disease) with multidisciplinary oncology evaluation; treatment is then stage-directed and is typically systemic/palliative when metastatic, with HER2 and biomarker testing to guide therapy. Immediate curative gastrectomy alone (option A) is inappropriate for metastatic disease and would not be curative. H. pylori eradication with surveillance (option B) is irrelevant once invasive carcinoma is diagnosed. Endoscopic mucosal resection (option C) is reserved for early, superficial, node-negative tumors—not a diffuse linitis plastica with metastases. Accurate staging prevents futile surgery and directs appropriate systemic and supportive care.

cases.diagnosis: Diffuse-type gastric adenocarcinoma (linitis plastica) with metastatic spread
pearls: Early satiety, weight loss, and a left supraclavicular (Virchow) node suggest metastatic gastric adenocarcinoma; a periumbilical Sister Mary Joseph nodule is another sign.
Linitis plastica is diffuse signet-ring carcinoma producing a rigid 'leather bottle' stomach—take multiple deep biopsies as superficial ones may miss it.
Risk factors include H. pylori, smoked/salted diet, smoking, family history, and endemic geography.
Stage fully before treatment; metastatic disease (Virchow node) is managed with systemic/palliative therapy, not curative surgery.

cases.title: Persistent Stomach Discomfort and Indigestion
cases.chiefComplaint: Months of dull upper abdominal pain, bloating, and early fullness.
cases.history: A 58-year-old woman presents with about 5 months of dull epigastric discomfort, bloating, mild nausea, and early satiety that have not improved despite intermittent antacid use. She has mild fatigue and a 5-lb weight loss. She denies hematemesis, melena, or dysphagia. PMH includes chronic dyspepsia and a remote diagnosis of Helicobacter pylori 'gastritis' that she does not recall completing treatment for. Medications: calcium carbonate as needed; no regular PPI. No allergies. Social history: nonsmoker, rare alcohol, works as an accountant. Family history is noncontributory for GI cancer. Review of systems is negative for fevers, night sweats, or lymphadenopathy. She has no immunosuppression and takes no NSAIDs. She has never had an endoscopy. She describes the pain as gnawing, sometimes worse between meals, and not clearly relieved by food. She reports no recent travel or sick contacts and no change in bowel habits.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and in no distress. Oral cavity is clear. Neck is supple without lymphadenopathy; no supraclavicular nodes are palpable. Lungs are clear and cardiac exam is normal. The abdomen is soft with mild epigastric tenderness to deep palpation, no rebound or guarding, and no palpable mass or organomegaly; there is no hepatosplenomegaly. Bowel sounds are normal. There is no peripheral lymphadenopathy in the cervical, axillary, or inguinal regions. Rectal exam shows brown, guaiac-negative stool. Skin shows no pallor or jaundice. Extremities are without edema.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given dyspepsia with weight loss in a 58-year-old, what is the most appropriate initial step?
options: Abdominal CT scan as the first step today
Empiric proton pump inhibitor for 8 weeks without further testing
H. pylori stool antigen test only
Upper endoscopy with biopsies given alarm features
stages.explanation: Dyspepsia with alarm features (age >60 or, by many guidelines, >55-60 with weight loss) warrants upper endoscopy with biopsies to exclude malignancy and characterize the mucosa, including testing for H. pylori on biopsy. Empiric PPI alone (option A) is reserved for younger patients without alarm features and would delay diagnosis here. A stand-alone H. pylori stool antigen test (option C) is appropriate in a test-and-treat strategy for low-risk dyspepsia but is insufficient when alarm features mandate direct visualization and tissue. Abdominal CT (option D) is low-yield as a first step for gastric mucosal disease and does not provide tissue. Endoscopy with biopsy is essential to distinguish carcinoma, lymphoma, and benign gastritis.

stages.label: ENDOSCOPY & BIOPSY
stages.content:
EGD: Patchy erythema, thickened gastric folds, and shallow erosions in the gastric body/antrum; no discrete mass
Biopsy: Dense atypical lymphoid infiltrate with lymphoepithelial lesions; immunohistochemistry shows monotypic B cells (CD20+, CD5−, CD10−)
Helicobacter pylori: positive on histology
Flow cytometry/molecular: features consistent with extranodal marginal-zone (MALT) lymphoma
CBC and LDH within normal limits

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The biopsy shows a marginal-zone B-cell lymphoma with lymphoepithelial lesions and positive H. pylori. What is the diagnosis and its key association?
options: Gastric adenocarcinoma as the primary diagnosis
Reactive gastritis with no neoplastic significance as the primary diagnosis
Gastric MALT lymphoma associated with chronic Helicobacter pylori infection
Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma unrelated to infection in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Lymphoepithelial lesions with a monotypic marginal-zone B-cell infiltrate define gastric mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue (MALT) lymphoma, an extranodal marginal-zone lymphoma strongly driven by chronic H. pylori infection; the chronic antigenic stimulation promotes clonal B-cell proliferation. Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (option B) is a high-grade lymphoma with large transformed cells and is not the marginal-zone, H. pylori-driven entity described (though MALT can transform into it). Adenocarcinoma (option C) is an epithelial malignancy with glandular/signet-ring morphology, not a lymphoid infiltrate. Reactive gastritis (option D) lacks the monotypic clonal B-cell population and lymphoepithelial lesions. The H. pylori association is central because eradication is the first-line treatment.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For early-stage, H. pylori-positive gastric MALT lymphoma, what is the appropriate first-line treatment?
options: H. pylori eradication therapy with subsequent endoscopic surveillance for regression
Splenectomy as initial therapy
Combination chemotherapy (R-CHOP) for all patients as initial therapy
Immediate total gastrectomy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: First-line therapy for localized, H. pylori-positive gastric MALT lymphoma is H. pylori eradication (e.g., a quadruple or clarithromycin-based regimen) because the majority of early-stage tumors regress after the bacterial driver is removed; eradication is confirmed and tumor response is monitored with endoscopic surveillance over months. Total gastrectomy (option A) is unnecessarily morbid and not first-line. R-CHOP for all patients (option B) overtreats early disease; chemotherapy/immunotherapy or radiation is reserved for H. pylori-negative cases, those with adverse cytogenetics (e.g., t(11;18)), or disease that fails to regress after eradication. Splenectomy (option C) is irrelevant to gastric MALT. Antibiotic eradication is a striking example of curing a lymphoma by treating an infection.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.content:
Quadruple therapy completed; urea breath test confirms H. pylori eradication
Repeat EGD at 3 months: improving mucosa, residual lymphoid infiltrate
Repeat EGD at 9 months: histologic regression of lymphoma; no residual monotypic B-cell population
Molecular testing: t(11;18) translocation NOT detected
Patient symptomatically improved

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: Her tumor lacked the t(11;18) translocation and regressed after eradication. What is the significance of t(11;18) status in gastric MALT lymphoma?
options: t -positive tumors are typically resistant to eradication
t(11;18) predicts an excellent response to H. pylori eradication in this setting
t(11;18) has no clinical relevance as the immediate next step
t(11;18) indicates the lymphoma is not associated with H. pylori at all
stages.explanation: The t(11;18)(q21;q21) translocation (API2-MALT1) marks gastric MALT lymphomas that are generally resistant to H. pylori eradication and tend to require alternative therapy such as radiation or systemic chemotherapy/immunotherapy. Her tumor being t(11;18)-negative is consistent with the regression she achieved after eradication. Option 0 is the reverse of the truth—eradication-responsive tumors are usually translocation-negative. While t(11;18)-positive cases may be more often H. pylori-independent, the translocation does not prove the lymphoma is unrelated to H. pylori in all instances (option C overstates this). Stating it has no relevance (option D) is incorrect, because cytogenetics meaningfully predict eradication response and guide whether escalated therapy is needed.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: After documented histologic regression, what is the most appropriate long-term plan?
options: Discharge with no further endoscopic follow-up as the immediate next step
Schedule prophylactic gastrectomy as the immediate next step
Begin maintenance chemotherapy indefinitely as the immediate next step
Continue periodic endoscopic surveillance
stages.explanation: Even after regression, patients with gastric MALT lymphoma need ongoing periodic endoscopic surveillance with biopsies and confirmation of sustained H. pylori eradication, because relapse can occur (especially with reinfection) and a minority transform to diffuse large B-cell lymphoma. Discharging with no follow-up (option A) risks missing relapse or transformation. Indefinite maintenance chemotherapy (option B) is unwarranted for a tumor that regressed with antibiotics alone and adds needless toxicity. Prophylactic gastrectomy (option D) is not indicated for a chemosensitive, antibiotic-responsive disease and is excessively morbid. Structured surveillance balances early detection of recurrence against avoiding overtreatment, which is the standard of care after eradication-induced remission.

cases.diagnosis: Gastric MALT lymphoma associated with Helicobacter pylori
pearls: Gastric MALT lymphoma is an extranodal marginal-zone B-cell lymphoma driven by chronic H. pylori; biopsy shows lymphoepithelial lesions.
First-line therapy for early-stage H. pylori-positive disease is antibiotic eradication; most tumors regress, monitored by surveillance endoscopy.
t(11;18)-positive tumors tend to resist eradication and need radiation or chemotherapy/immunotherapy.
Dyspepsia with alarm features (age, weight loss) requires endoscopy with biopsy, not empiric acid suppression alone.

cases.title: Recurrent Ulcers That Won't Heal, with Diarrhea
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent burning abdominal pain and chronic diarrhea despite acid-blocking medication.
cases.history: A 45-year-old man presents with over a year of recurrent epigastric burning pain and chronic watery diarrhea. He has had two duodenal ulcers documented on prior endoscopies that recurred despite high-dose proton pump inhibitor therapy and confirmed absence of Helicobacter pylori. He denies NSAID use entirely. The diarrhea is large-volume and improves somewhat when he takes his PPI, and he has lost 10 lb. PMH includes nephrolithiasis and 'hormone problems' in a relative. Medications: omeprazole 40 mg twice daily. No allergies. Social history: nonsmoker, minimal alcohol, office worker. Family history: a parent and sibling with pituitary tumors and 'high calcium.' Review of systems is positive for heartburn, steatorrhea-like stools, and intermittent flushing-free episodes; he denies hematemesis. He reports the ulcers are unusually located and multiple. He has had recurrent symptoms despite good medication adherence, which prompted referral. He notes a history of kidney stones and was once told his calcium was elevated.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and in no distress. Oral mucosa is normal. Neck is supple without lymphadenopathy or thyromegaly. Lungs are clear; cardiac exam is normal. The abdomen is soft with mild epigastric tenderness, no rebound or guarding, no palpable mass, and no hepatomegaly; bowel sounds are normal. There is no flank tenderness today. Skin shows no flushing, rash, or telangiectasias. Rectal exam reveals soft, brown, guaiac-negative stool. Extremities show no edema. Neurologic exam is normal. There are no visual field deficits on confrontation testing.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature most strongly suggests a gastrin-secreting tumor rather than ordinary peptic ulcer disease?
options: Epigastric pain only after spicy meals as the first step today
Multiple/recurrent ulcers with diarrhea, refractory to therapy
Heartburn relieved completely by antacids as the first step today
A single antral ulcer that healed with PPI as the first step today
stages.explanation: Multiple or recurrent peptic ulcers, especially in atypical locations (distal duodenum/jejunum), that are refractory to therapy in the absence of H. pylori and NSAIDs, accompanied by secretory diarrhea, strongly suggest gastrinoma (Zollinger-Ellison syndrome). His family history of pituitary tumors and hypercalcemia raises MEN1. A single antral ulcer that heals (option A) is typical of ordinary PUD. Complete relief with antacids (option C) and pain only after spicy meals (option E) describe benign dyspepsia/PUD without the red flags of refractoriness, multiplicity, and diarrhea. The combination of refractory multiple ulcers plus diarrhea is the hallmark that prompts measuring gastrin and pursuing a neuroendocrine tumor.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic test?
options: Stool culture as the primary diagnosis
Fasting serum gastrin level
Colonoscopy as the primary diagnosis
Random (non-fasting) gastrin during PPI therapy as definitive proof
stages.explanation: The initial screen for Zollinger-Ellison syndrome is a fasting serum gastrin together with gastric pH; markedly elevated fasting gastrin with a low gastric pH (acidic) supports a gastrinoma, distinguishing it from secondary hypergastrinemia due to achlorhydria. Crucially, PPIs raise gastrin and can confound results, so interpretation must account for acid-suppression status, and confirmatory secretin stimulation testing is used when values are equivocal. A random gastrin while on a PPI taken as 'definitive proof' (option C) is unreliable because acid suppression itself elevates gastrin. Colonoscopy (option B) and stool culture (option D) evaluate other causes of diarrhea and would miss the gastrinoma. Establishing inappropriate hypergastrinemia in the setting of acid hypersecretion is the key first step.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Fasting serum gastrin 1,150 pg/mL (markedly elevated; normal <100)
Gastric pH 1.8 (acidic)
Secretin stimulation test: paradoxical rise in gastrin >200 pg/mL above baseline (positive)
Serum calcium 11.2 mg/dL (elevated)
Intact PTH elevated
Chromogranin A elevated

stages.label: ASSOCIATED SYNDROME
stages.question: His elevated calcium, high PTH, and family history of pituitary tumors point to which associated syndrome?
options: Von Hippel-Lindau syndrome in this setting
Multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1 (MEN1)
Sporadic gastrinoma with no syndromic association
Multiple endocrine neoplasia type 2A (MEN2A)
stages.explanation: The triad of a pancreatic/duodenal neuroendocrine tumor (gastrinoma), primary hyperparathyroidism (elevated calcium and PTH), and pituitary tumors—'the 3 Ps' (pancreas, parathyroid, pituitary)—defines MEN1, which his personal and family findings strongly suggest. MEN2A (option A) features medullary thyroid carcinoma, pheochromocytoma, and parathyroid disease, not gastrinoma. Von Hippel-Lindau (option C) involves hemangioblastomas, renal cell carcinoma, pheochromocytoma, and pancreatic cysts/NETs but is not characterized by the pituitary-parathyroid-gastrinoma pattern seen here. While many gastrinomas are sporadic (option D), his hypercalcemia, elevated PTH, and family history make a syndromic MEN1 association most likely, which has implications for genetic testing and screening of relatives.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After confirming gastrinoma, what is the most appropriate next step for tumor evaluation, and how should acid hypersecretion be managed meanwhile?
options: Localize the tumor while controlling acid hypersecretion with high-dose PPI
Begin chemotherapy immediately without imaging as initial therapy
Stop all acid suppression and observe as initial therapy
Perform total gastrectomy as first-line therapy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Once biochemical diagnosis is made, the gastrinoma must be localized using somatostatin-receptor-based imaging (e.g., Ga-68 DOTATATE PET) and cross-sectional imaging (CT/MRI), often with endoscopic ultrasound for pancreatic/duodenal primaries; meanwhile, acid hypersecretion is controlled with high-dose PPI to heal ulcers and reduce complications. Resection is pursued for localized, resectable disease, with management of associated hyperparathyroidism in MEN1. Stopping acid suppression (option A) would worsen ulcers and risk perforation/bleeding. Immediate chemotherapy without localization (option C) is premature and skips potentially curative surgery. Total gastrectomy as first-line (option D) is outdated since potent PPIs control acid; surgery now targets the tumor itself. Localization plus acid control is the rational sequence.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Genetic testing confirms a MEN1 mutation and he has symptomatic primary hyperparathyroidism. How should the hyperparathyroidism be addressed in relation to his gastrinoma?
options: Add a thiazide diuretic to lower serum calcium as the immediate next step
Ignore the hypercalcemia and treat only the gastrinoma as the immediate next step
Start lifelong calcitonin and avoid surgery as the immediate next step
Treat the primary hyperparathyroidism , as correcting hypercalcemia often reduces gastrin levels
stages.explanation: In MEN1-associated Zollinger-Ellison syndrome, primary hyperparathyroidism is typically addressed first because hypercalcemia stimulates gastrin secretion; subtotal/total parathyroidectomy that corrects hypercalcemia frequently lowers gastrin levels and improves control of acid hypersecretion. Ignoring the hypercalcemia (option A) leaves a driver of gastrin release untreated and risks nephrolithiasis and bone disease. Calcitonin (option B) is a temporizing hypocalcemic agent, not definitive therapy for parathyroid hyperplasia, and tachyphylaxis limits it. A thiazide diuretic (option C) actually reduces urinary calcium excretion and can worsen hypercalcemia, so it is contraindicated here. Coordinating parathyroid surgery with gastrinoma management is a hallmark of MEN1 care.

cases.diagnosis: Zollinger-Ellison syndrome (gastrinoma), likely associated with MEN1
pearls: Suspect gastrinoma with multiple/recurrent ulcers in atypical locations, secretory diarrhea, and refractoriness despite H. pylori-negative, NSAID-free status.
Screen with fasting gastrin plus gastric pH; PPIs elevate gastrin, so interpret carefully and confirm with secretin stimulation when needed.
MEN1 = the 3 Ps (pancreas/gastrinoma, parathyroid/hypercalcemia, pituitary); check calcium/PTH and family history.
Localize the tumor with somatostatin-receptor imaging/EUS while controlling acid with high-dose PPI; resect localized disease.

cases.title: Nausea and Vomiting Undigested Food Hours After Eating
cases.chiefComplaint: Months of nausea, bloating, early fullness, and vomiting of food eaten hours earlier.
cases.history: A 44-year-old woman presents with several months of nausea, early satiety, postprandial bloating, and intermittent vomiting of recognizable, undigested food eaten hours before. Symptoms worsen after solid, fatty meals. She has lost 7 lb and her blood glucose control has become erratic, with unexpected post-meal lows followed by highs. PMH includes type 1 diabetes mellitus for 22 years with known background retinopathy and peripheral neuropathy. Medications: insulin glargine and insulin aspart; no opioids or GLP-1 receptor agonists. No allergies. Social history: nonsmoker, rare alcohol, works as a nurse. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for bloating and a sensation of fullness; she denies hematemesis, melena, fevers, or dysphagia to liquids. She reports no abdominal surgeries. She notes her symptoms are not relieved by antacids. She has had no recent change in medications and denies cannabis use. She describes the vomitus as containing food from a meal eaten that morning.
cases.exam: The patient is comfortable and not dehydrated, with moist mucous membranes. Oral cavity is clear. The abdomen is soft and mildly distended with a succussion splash audible on shaking the patient; there is mild epigastric fullness but no focal tenderness, guarding, rebound, or palpable mass, and no organomegaly. Bowel sounds are present. Neurologic exam shows diminished vibration and monofilament sensation in both feet consistent with peripheral neuropathy. There is no orthostatic change in this visit's vitals. Skin is intact without rash. Rectal exam reveals brown, guaiac-negative stool. There is no abdominal scar.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In this long-standing diabetic with vomiting of undigested food and a succussion splash, what condition is most likely, and what must be excluded first?
options: Cyclic vomiting syndrome; first start an antiemetic
Cholelithiasis; first obtain RUQ ultrasound in this clinical context
Gastroparesis; first exclude mechanical gastric outlet obstruction
Acute gastroenteritis; first obtain stool studies
stages.explanation: Vomiting of undigested food hours after eating, early satiety, bloating, and a succussion splash in a patient with long-standing diabetes and autonomic/peripheral neuropathy strongly suggest gastroparesis. However, the essential first step is to exclude mechanical gastric outlet obstruction (e.g., with upper endoscopy) because obstruction can mimic gastroparesis and requires entirely different management. Acute gastroenteritis (option B) is self-limited and would not cause months of retained-food vomiting. Cyclic vomiting syndrome (option C) features discrete stereotyped episodes with wellness between, not chronic postprandial retention, and antiemetics before excluding obstruction is premature. Cholelithiasis (option D) causes biliary colic, not vomiting of food eaten hours earlier with a succussion splash. Ruling out obstruction before diagnosing gastroparesis is the key teaching point.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: After endoscopy excludes obstruction, what is the gold-standard test to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Esophageal manometry as the primary diagnosis
Hydrogen breath test as the primary diagnosis
4-hour solid-phase gastric emptying scintigraphy
Abdominal CT as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: The gold standard for confirming gastroparesis is 4-hour solid-phase gastric emptying scintigraphy, which quantifies delayed emptying (e.g., >10% retention at 4 hours, or >60% at 2 hours) after a standardized radiolabeled meal, with medications affecting motility held and glucose controlled beforehand. Abdominal CT (option A) evaluates structural disease and obstruction but does not measure emptying. Esophageal manometry (option C) assesses esophageal motility, not gastric emptying. A hydrogen breath test (option D) evaluates carbohydrate malabsorption or small-bowel bacterial overgrowth, not gastric emptying. Scintigraphy demonstrating delayed emptying with retained food confirms the functional diagnosis once a mechanical cause is ruled out, completing the diagnostic pathway.

stages.label: DIAGNOSTICS
stages.content:
EGD: retained food in the stomach despite overnight fast; no obstructing lesion, no stricture, pylorus patent
Hemoglobin A1c 9.4%
Basic metabolic panel within normal limits; no hypokalemia
4-hour gastric emptying scintigraphy: 38% of radiolabeled solid meal retained at 4 hours (markedly delayed)
TSH normal

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line management strategy?
options: Dietary modification , optimize glycemic control,
High-fiber, high-fat diet with no glycemic changes as initial therapy
Long-term opioid analgesics for symptom control as initial therapy
Immediate surgical gastrectomy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: First-line management of diabetic gastroparesis combines dietary modification (small, frequent meals that are low in fat and insoluble fiber, with more liquid-based nutrition as needed), optimization of glycemic control (hyperglycemia itself slows emptying), and a prokinetic agent, most commonly metoclopramide (used at the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration given tardive dyskinesia risk), with antiemetics for symptom relief. A high-fiber, high-fat diet (option A) worsens emptying and bezoar risk. Long-term opioids (option C) further slow gastric motility and can cause narcotic bowel syndrome, worsening symptoms. Surgical gastrectomy (option D) is a last resort, not first-line. Tightening glucose control also improves her erratic readings caused by mismatched insulin timing and delayed emptying.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which complication is she at particular risk for from retained gastric contents?
options: Hemorrhoids as the leading concern
Diverticulitis
Gastric bezoar formation
Acute appendicitis
stages.explanation: Chronic gastric stasis predisposes to bezoar formation, where undigested food and fiber aggregate into a concretion that can worsen obstruction-like symptoms and even cause outlet blockage; this is why low-insoluble-fiber diets are advised in gastroparesis. Acute appendicitis (option B) arises from appendiceal luminal obstruction and is unrelated to gastric emptying. Diverticulitis (option C) involves colonic diverticula and is not a consequence of delayed gastric emptying. Hemorrhoids (option D) relate to venous engorgement in the anorectum and have no link to gastric stasis. Recognizing bezoar risk reinforces both the dietary recommendations and the value of prokinetic therapy and good glycemic control in preventing complications of gastroparesis.

pearls: Vomiting of undigested food hours after eating, early satiety, and a succussion splash in a long-standing diabetic suggest gastroparesis.
Always exclude mechanical gastric outlet obstruction (endoscopy) before diagnosing gastroparesis.
Confirm with 4-hour solid-phase gastric emptying scintigraphy; hyperglycemia itself delays emptying.
Treat with small low-fat/low-fiber meals, glycemic optimization, and a prokinetic (metoclopramide); avoid opioids and watch for bezoars.

cases.title: Sweating and Lightheadedness After Eating
cases.chiefComplaint: Episodes of sweating, palpitations, and lightheadedness within 20 minutes of eating.
cases.history: A 55-year-old man presents for evaluation of recurrent symptoms that began about 6 weeks ago. Roughly 15-30 minutes after meals — especially those rich in sweets, juice, or refined carbohydrates — he develops diaphoresis, palpitations, flushing, abdominal cramping, and an urge to lie down, sometimes followed by loose stools. He also describes a separate set of spells 1-3 hours after eating with tremor, hunger, and difficulty concentrating that improve with eating. He underwent subtotal gastrectomy with Billroth II reconstruction 4 months ago for a bleeding gastric ulcer refractory to medical therapy. He has lost 5 kg since surgery and now eats smaller meals out of fear. PMH: peptic ulcer disease, hypertension. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily, pantoprazole 40 mg daily. No diabetes; he takes no insulin or sulfonylureas. Allergies: none. Social: drinks alcohol socially, former smoker (15 pack-years, quit 2 years ago). Family history: father with type 2 diabetes. He denies chest pain, syncope, melena, or fevers.
cases.exam: The patient appears well and is in no distress at rest. Cardiac exam reveals a regular rate and rhythm without murmurs. Lungs are clear. The abdomen is soft and non-tender with a well-healed midline surgical scar; bowel sounds are normal and there is no organomegaly, mass, or rebound. There is no peripheral edema. Neurologic exam is non-focal with normal mentation. No tremor, goiter, or exophthalmos. Orthostatic vitals show a 12 bpm rise in heart rate without a significant drop in blood pressure on standing. Skin is warm and dry at present.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature of the history most strongly points toward the underlying mechanism of his early post-meal symptoms?
options: The presence of a well-healed surgical scar as the first step today
A family history of type 2 diabetes as the first step today
Onset of symptoms within 15-30 minutes of meals high in simple carbohydrates
Symptoms occurring 1-3 hours after meals and relieved by eating
stages.explanation: Early dumping syndrome classically begins 15-30 minutes after a meal, particularly one rich in simple sugars, when hyperosmolar chyme rapidly enters the small bowel and drives fluid shifts plus vasoactive peptide release — producing diaphoresis, palpitations, cramping, and diarrhea. The 1-3 hour post-meal spells (option A) describe late dumping (reactive hypoglycemia), a related but distinct phenomenon and not the early-phase mechanism. A healed scar (option C) confirms prior surgery but is not itself the symptom mechanism. Family history of diabetes (option D) raises baseline metabolic risk but does not explain meal-triggered vasomotor symptoms in a non-diabetic patient. The tight temporal link to high-carbohydrate meals is the key discriminator.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step to confirm the suspected diagnosis?
options: Obtain a CT angiogram of the mesenteric vessels as the primary diagnosis
Apply a validated clinical scoring tool
Order a 72-hour supervised fast as the primary diagnosis
Measure a fasting serum insulin and proinsulin level as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Dumping syndrome is primarily a clinical diagnosis in a patient with prior gastric surgery and characteristic post-prandial symptoms; a validated scoring tool such as the Sigstad score supports it, and a modified oral glucose tolerance test can provoke symptoms and document early tachycardia/hematocrit rise or late hypoglycemia. A 72-hour fast (option A) and fasting insulin/proinsulin (option D) target insulinoma, which causes fasting — not post-prandial, meal-provoked — hypoglycemia and is not suggested here. CT angiography (option B) evaluates mesenteric ischemia, which causes pain rather than vasomotor flushing and tachycardia tightly tied to sugary meals. The post-gastrectomy context and symptom timing make dumping the clear pre-test favorite.

stages.label: DIAGNOSTIC DATA
stages.content:
Sigstad dumping score: 11 (>7 supports dumping)
Modified oral glucose challenge: HR rise from 88 to 116 bpm at 30 min with reproduction of flushing and cramping
Capillary glucose at 150 min: 52 mg/dL with tremor and diaphoresis, resolving after juice
Hemoglobin 13.8 g/dL, hematocrit 41%
TSH 2.1 µIU/mL
Hemoglobin A1c 5.4%
Fasting insulin appropriately low during documented hypoglycemia

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line management to recommend?
options: Prescribe a continuous glucose monitor and a high-sugar rescue protocol as initial therapy
Refer for surgical revision of the gastrojejunostomy as initial therapy
Start a long-acting somatostatin analog (octreotide LAR) as initial therapy
Begin dietary modification: small, frequent meals, separating solids from liquids,
stages.explanation: First-line therapy for both early and late dumping is dietary modification: small, frequent meals; avoiding rapidly absorbed simple sugars; separating liquids from solids by ~30 minutes; and adding soluble fiber or complex carbohydrates to slow gastric emptying. Most patients improve substantially with diet alone. Octreotide (option A) is reserved for refractory symptoms after diet fails because of cost and side effects.
