Surgical revision (option B) is a last resort for severe, refractory cases. A high-sugar rescue protocol (option D) would paradoxically worsen dumping by delivering the very osmotic load that provokes symptoms and rebound hypoglycemia; rescue carbohydrate should be measured and limited. Diet is the foundational, evidence-based starting point.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After 8 weeks of strict dietary measures, he still has disabling late hypoglycemic spells. What is the most appropriate next pharmacologic step?
options: Add acarbose with meals
Prescribe pantoprazole at a higher dose
Begin a sulfonylurea such as glipizide
Start metformin in this setting
stages.explanation: Acarbose, an alpha-glucosidase inhibitor, slows intestinal carbohydrate breakdown and absorption, blunting the rapid glucose surge and subsequent reactive hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia of late dumping; it is a recommended next step when diet alone fails. Metformin (option B) lowers glucose and would not help — and could aggravate — hypoglycemia. A sulfonylurea (option C) stimulates insulin secretion and would dangerously worsen reactive hypoglycemia. Increasing the PPI dose (option D) addresses acid, not the osmotic and glycemic physiology driving dumping. If acarbose is insufficient, a somatostatin analog is the next escalation. Acarbose is the appropriate, mechanism-directed add-on for refractory late dumping.

cases.diagnosis: Dumping syndrome (post-gastrectomy, with early and late features)
pearls: Early dumping (15-30 min: vasomotor flushing, palpitations, cramping, diarrhea) reflects osmotic fluid shifts and vasoactive peptide release; late dumping (1-3 h) is reactive hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia.
Dumping is a clinical diagnosis after gastric surgery; the Sigstad score and a provocative oral glucose challenge support it.
Dietary modification — small frequent meals, separating solids from liquids, limiting simple sugars — is first-line and resolves most cases.
Acarbose helps refractory late dumping; octreotide and, last, surgical revision are reserved for diet-refractory disease.

cases.title: Fatigue, Tingling Feet, and a Sore Tongue
cases.chiefComplaint: Several months of progressive fatigue, numbness in the feet, and a burning tongue.
cases.history: A 61-year-old woman reports 5-6 months of worsening fatigue, exertional dyspnea, and difficulty concentrating. Over the past 2 months she has noticed symmetric tingling and numbness in both feet, an unsteady gait in the dark, and a sore, smooth, burning tongue. She has lost mild appetite but no significant weight. PMH: Hashimoto thyroiditis on levothyroxine, vitiligo. She is a lifelong vegetarian but does consume eggs and dairy. Medications: levothyroxine 88 µg daily; no metformin, no PPI, no chronic acid suppression. Allergies: none. She drinks alcohol rarely and has never smoked. Family history: mother with autoimmune thyroid disease; a sister with type 1 diabetes. Review of systems is notable for occasional early satiety and mild dyspepsia but no overt GI bleeding, diarrhea, or steatorrhea. She has no prior gastric surgery and no known malabsorptive condition. She denies fever, night sweats, or lymphadenopathy.
cases.exam: She appears pale with mild scleral pallor and patchy depigmented skin patches on the hands consistent with vitiligo. The tongue is smooth, erythematous, and atrophic (glossitis). Cardiac exam shows a soft systolic flow murmur and tachycardia at rest. Lungs are clear. The abdomen is soft and non-tender without organomegaly. Neurologic exam reveals diminished vibratory and proprioceptive sense at the great toes bilaterally, a positive Romberg sign, and brisk knee reflexes with reduced ankle reflexes. Strength is intact. There is no jaundice, and stool is brown and guaiac-negative.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which initial laboratory pattern would best fit the suspected unifying diagnosis?
options: Normocytic anemia with elevated reticulocyte count
Microcytic anemia with low ferritin and high RDW
Macrocytic anemia with elevated reticulocytes and indirect bilirubin only
Macrocytic anemia with hypersegmented neutrophils
stages.explanation: The clinical picture — fatigue, glossitis, symmetric sensory neuropathy with proprioceptive loss and Romberg sign, plus autoimmune comorbidities — points to vitamin B12 deficiency, which produces a macrocytic (megaloblastic) anemia with hypersegmented neutrophils and a low serum B12. Microcytic anemia with low ferritin (option A) indicates iron deficiency, which does not cause subacute combined degeneration. A normocytic anemia with high reticulocytes (option C) suggests hemolysis or acute blood loss, not megaloblastic ineffective erythropoiesis. Option D describes a high-reticulocyte hemolytic pattern; in B12 deficiency the reticulocyte count is inappropriately low because of ineffective erythropoiesis, even though LDH and indirect bilirubin may rise from intramedullary hemolysis. The macrocytic, hyposproliferative pattern fits best.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 9.6 g/dL
MCV 112 fL
Reticulocyte count 0.8% (inappropriately low)
Peripheral smear: macro-ovalocytes and hypersegmented neutrophils
Vitamin B12 96 pg/mL (low)
Methylmalonic acid markedly elevated; homocysteine elevated
Folate normal
LDH elevated; indirect bilirubin mildly elevated
TSH 2.3 µIU/mL on levothyroxine

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which test would best confirm the autoimmune etiology underlying her B12 deficiency?
options: Anti-intrinsic factor antibody , with elevated serum gastrin
Anti-tissue transglutaminase (anti-tTG) IgA in this clinical context
Stool elastase as the primary diagnosis
Hydrogen breath test as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Pernicious anemia from autoimmune atrophic gastritis is confirmed by anti-intrinsic factor antibodies (highly specific) and supported by anti-parietal cell antibodies; loss of acid-secreting parietal cells produces achlorhydria and a compensatory elevated serum gastrin, along with low pepsinogen I. Anti-tTG IgA (option A) screens for celiac disease, which can cause malabsorption but not the parietal-cell-directed autoimmunity and hypergastrinemia described. Stool elastase (option C) evaluates pancreatic exocrine insufficiency. A hydrogen breath test (option D) assesses small intestinal bacterial overgrowth or carbohydrate malabsorption. Her constellation of autoimmune disease, glossitis, and neurologic findings makes intrinsic-factor antibody testing the targeted confirmatory step.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Anti-intrinsic factor antibody is positive and gastrin is elevated. What is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Parenteral vitamin B12 replacement, with transition to high-dose oral B12 as appropriate
Oral folic acid supplementation as initial therapy
A trial of a gluten-free diet as initial therapy
High-dose oral iron as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Because pernicious anemia abolishes intrinsic-factor-dependent B12 absorption, replacement is given parenterally (IM cyanocobalamin or hydroxocobalamin), typically with a loading schedule followed by maintenance; high-dose oral B12 can maintain levels in some patients via passive absorption once stores are repleted, but IM is the reliable initial route, especially with neurologic involvement. Folic acid alone (option A) can correct the anemia but will NOT halt — and may mask while worsening — the neurologic deterioration of B12 deficiency, a classic pitfall. Iron (option B) does not address B12 deficiency. A gluten-free diet (option D) treats celiac disease, which is not the cause here. Lifelong B12 replacement is required because the underlying autoimmune gastritis is permanent.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Given her autoimmune atrophic gastritis, which long-term surveillance concern is most relevant?
options: Increased risk of hepatocellular carcinoma requiring biannual ultrasound as the leading concern
Increased risk of colorectal cancer requiring annual colonoscopy as the leading concern
Increased risk of gastric adenocarcinoma
Increased risk of pancreatic cancer requiring annual MRCP as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Chronic autoimmune atrophic gastritis causes achlorhydria, which drives compensatory hypergastrinemia; sustained gastrin stimulation of enterochromaffin-like (ECL) cells predisposes to type 1 gastric carcinoid (neuroendocrine) tumors. The same atrophic, metaplastic mucosa also carries an increased risk of gastric adenocarcinoma. Upper endoscopy is therefore indicated to assess mucosal changes and detect carcinoids, with surveillance based on findings. Atrophic gastritis is not specifically linked to colorectal (option B), hepatocellular (option C), or pancreatic (option D) malignancy. The parietal-cell-directed, gastrin-mediated pathophysiology makes gastric neoplasia — adenocarcinoma and ECL-cell carcinoid — the pertinent surveillance issue.

cases.diagnosis: Autoimmune atrophic gastritis with pernicious anemia (vitamin B12 deficiency)
pearls: B12 deficiency causes macrocytic anemia with hypersegmented neutrophils and an inappropriately low reticulocyte count; elevated methylmalonic acid distinguishes it from folate deficiency.

Subacute combined degeneration (dorsal columns + lateral corticospinal tracts) produces proprioceptive loss, positive Romberg, and mixed reflexes; giving folate alone masks anemia while neurologic injury progresses.
Pernicious anemia is confirmed by anti-intrinsic factor antibodies (specific) plus anti-parietal cell antibodies, achlorhydria, and elevated gastrin.
Autoimmune atrophic gastritis raises the risk of gastric adenocarcinoma and gastrin-driven type 1 ECL-cell carcinoid tumors, warranting endoscopic evaluation.

cases.title: Chronic Diarrhea and Right Lower Quadrant Pain
cases.chiefComplaint: Four months of non-bloody diarrhea, crampy right-sided abdominal pain, and weight loss.
cases.history: A 24-year-old woman presents with about 4 months of intermittent crampy right lower quadrant abdominal pain and 4-6 loose, non-bloody stools daily, sometimes waking her at night. She has lost 6 kg, reports low-grade fevers and fatigue, and notes mouth ulcers that come and go. Over the last 3 weeks she developed perianal pain and now reports drainage of pus and stool-like material from a small opening near the anus. PMH: unremarkable. Medications: occasional ibuprofen; combined oral contraceptive pill. Allergies: none. Social: non-smoker — she specifically asks whether smoking affects her gut. Family history: a maternal uncle with 'inflammatory bowel disease.' Review of systems also notes intermittent joint aches in the knees and red, tender shin nodules a few weeks ago. She denies recent travel, antibiotic use, or sick contacts. There is no relationship between symptoms and gluten ingestion and no prior abdominal surgery.
cases.exam: The patient is thin and mildly uncomfortable. Oral exam shows two small aphthous ulcers. The abdomen is soft with focal tenderness and fullness in the right lower quadrant; bowel sounds are normal and there is no rebound or guarding. A fullness is palpable but no discrete mass. Perianal inspection reveals an external opening with purulent drainage and a palpable cord toward the anal verge, consistent with a fistula, plus a skin tag. There is mild conjunctival pallor. The skin over the anterior shins shows fading tender erythematous nodules. The remainder of the exam, including joints at rest, is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which combination of features most strongly suggests the underlying diagnosis over its main mimic?
options: Continuous bloody diarrhea limited to the rectosigmoid
Perianal fistula, oral aphthae, RLQ involvement,
Recent antibiotic exposure with watery diarrhea as the first step today
Symptom resolution with a gluten-free diet as the first step today
stages.explanation: Perianal fistulizing disease, oral aphthous ulcers, right-lower-quadrant (ileocecal) involvement, fevers, weight loss, and extraintestinal manifestations (erythema nodosum, arthralgia) are hallmarks of Crohn disease, which is transmural and can affect any segment from mouth to anus with skip lesions. Continuous bloody diarrhea confined to the rectosigmoid (option A) describes ulcerative colitis, which characteristically spares the perianal region of fistulae and does not skip. Gluten-responsive symptoms (option C) suggest celiac disease. Recent antibiotics with watery diarrhea (option D) suggest C. difficile. The fistula plus oral and skin findings make transmural Crohn disease the leading diagnosis.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL, MCV 78 fL
CRP 48 mg/L, ESR 52 mm/hr
Albumin 3.1 g/dL
Fecal calprotectin 620 µg/g (elevated)
Stool cultures, ova/parasites, and C. difficile toxin: negative
MRI pelvis: trans-sphincteric perianal fistula with a small ischiorectal collection
CT enterography: segmental wall thickening and mural enhancement of the terminal ileum with skip areas in the right colon; no free air

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step to confirm and characterize the disease?
options: Exploratory laparotomy
Empiric gluten-free diet trial
Ileocolonoscopy with biopsies
Capsule endoscopy as the immediate next test
stages.explanation: Ileocolonoscopy with intubation of the terminal ileum and biopsies is the standard for confirming Crohn disease, defining extent and severity, and obtaining histology (transmural inflammation, focal crypt distortion, and non-caseating granulomas may be seen). The endoscopic pattern of discontinuous 'skip' lesions, aphthous and serpiginous ulcers, and cobblestoning is characteristic. Capsule endoscopy (option B) risks retention with a known stricturing terminal ileum and is not first-line here. A gluten-free trial (option C) does not address the established inflammatory and fistulizing findings. Laparotomy (option D) is not a diagnostic step and is reserved for surgical complications. Endoscopy with biopsy is the definitive characterization tool.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Colonoscopy and biopsies confirm ileocolonic Crohn disease with active perianal fistulizing disease and an abscess. What is the most appropriate management strategy?
options: Drain/control the abscess
Recommend smoking initiation, as nicotine is protective as initial therapy
Start long-term oral corticosteroids as definitive therapy as initial therapy
Begin maintenance mesalamine (5-ASA) monotherapy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Perianal fistulizing Crohn disease with an abscess requires source control first — surgical drainage (often with a seton) plus antibiotics such as ciprofloxacin/metronidazole — followed by an anti-TNF biologic (infliximab is best-evidenced for fistula healing), frequently combined with an immunomodulator for durable response. Mesalamine (option A) has minimal efficacy in Crohn disease, especially fistulizing/small-bowel disease. Chronic systemic corticosteroids (option C) induce remission only short-term, do not heal fistulae, and cause cumulative toxicity, so they are not maintenance therapy. Smoking (option D) WORSENS Crohn disease and accelerates recurrence — the opposite of its effect in ulcerative colitis. Abscess control plus anti-TNF therapy is guideline-concordant.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Two years later she develops postprandial cramping, audible borborygmi, and intermittent obstructive episodes with the same terminal ileal segment. Which complication is most likely?
options: Colonic adenocarcinoma of the rectum as the leading concern
Fibrostenotic stricture of the terminal ileum causing partial small-bowel obstruction
Toxic megacolon as the leading concern
Primary sclerosing cholangitis as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Transmural inflammation in Crohn disease heals with fibrosis, producing fibrostenotic strictures — most commonly at the terminal ileum — that cause postprandial cramping, borborygmi, and recurrent partial small-bowel obstruction, exactly as described. Toxic megacolon (option A) is a complication of severe colitis (more typical of ulcerative colitis or C. difficile) involving the colon, not an isolated ileal stricture. Primary sclerosing cholangitis (option B) is far more associated with ulcerative colitis and presents with cholestasis, not obstruction. Rectal adenocarcinoma (option D) is a long-term cancer risk but would not present acutely with mechanical small-bowel obstruction localized to the ileum. Stricturing disease is the expected fibrostenotic complication here.

cases.diagnosis: Crohn disease (ileocolonic) with perianal fistulizing involvement
pearls: Crohn disease is transmural and can affect any segment mouth-to-anus with skip lesions; perianal fistulae, oral aphthae, and cobblestoning are classic.
Fecal calprotectin and CRP gauge mucosal inflammation; ileocolonoscopy with biopsy (non-caseating granulomas) plus CT/MR enterography define extent and complications.
Perianal fistula with abscess needs source control (drainage/seton + antibiotics) before/with an anti-TNF biologic; mesalamine is largely ineffective in Crohn.
Smoking worsens Crohn disease (opposite of ulcerative colitis); transmural healing leads to fibrostenotic strictures and obstruction.

cases.title: Bloody Stools With a Constant Urge to Go
cases.chiefComplaint: Six weeks of bloody diarrhea with mucus and a persistent urge to defecate.
cases.history: A 27-year-old man reports 6 weeks of frequent loose stools mixed with blood and mucus, now up to 8 times daily including nocturnal episodes. He describes painful straining and a constant sensation of incomplete evacuation (tenesmus), with occasional lower abdominal cramping relieved by passing stool. He has mild fatigue and a 3 kg weight loss but no high fevers. PMH: unremarkable. Medications: none regularly; he recently quit smoking 4 months ago and noticed symptoms began afterward. Allergies: none. Social: works as an accountant, moderate alcohol use, no recent travel or antibiotics. Family history: a cousin with 'colitis.' Review of systems is notable for occasional red, painful eye episodes and mild low-back stiffness in the mornings, but no oral ulcers, perianal drainage, or skin nodules. He denies recent sick contacts, undercooked food, or new medications. Bowel symptoms are continuous rather than episodic, and bleeding is present with nearly every bowel movement.
cases.exam: The patient appears mildly fatigued but not toxic. Sclerae are anicteric; one eye shows mild conjunctival injection. The abdomen is soft with mild tenderness in the left lower quadrant and lower abdomen; no rebound, guarding, distension, or palpable mass; bowel sounds are normal. Digital rectal examination reveals bright red blood and mucus with no fissure, mass, or fluctuance, and no perianal fistula or skin tag. There is mild conjunctival pallor. Spine shows mildly reduced lumbar flexion. The remainder of the examination is unremarkable, with no oral lesions and warm, well-perfused extremities.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature best distinguishes this presentation from its principal inflammatory mimic?
options: Improvement of symptoms after smoking cessation
Skip lesions with perianal fistulae as the first step today
Right lower quadrant mass with non-bloody diarrhea
Continuous, bloody diarrhea with tenesmus
stages.explanation: Ulcerative colitis classically causes continuous (non-skipping) mucosal inflammation that begins at the rectum and extends proximally, producing bloody diarrhea, mucus, and tenesmus from rectal involvement. Skip lesions with perianal fistulae (option A) and a right-lower-quadrant mass with non-bloody diarrhea (option C) describe Crohn disease. Smoking cessation (option D) is an important historical clue — UC often presents or flares after quitting smoking — but it is an epidemiologic association, not the defining clinical feature; moreover, symptoms here began (not improved) after he quit. The continuous, rectally-based bloody diarrhea with tenesmus is the discriminating clinical pattern of UC.

stages.label: LABS & ENDOSCOPY
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 11.6 g/dL
CRP 28 mg/L, ESR 34 mm/hr
Albumin 3.6 g/dL
Fecal calprotectin 480 µg/g
Stool studies (culture, O&P, C. difficile toxin): negative
p-ANCA positive; ASCA negative
Flexible sigmoidoscopy: continuous granular, friable, erythematous mucosa with loss of vascular pattern and superficial ulceration beginning at the rectum and extending to the descending colon, with a sharp demarcation proximally

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Histology shows crypt distortion, crypt abscesses, and mucosal/submucosal inflammation without granulomas. Which extraintestinal condition should be screened for given its strong association?
options: Glomerulonephritis as the primary diagnosis
Primary sclerosing cholangitis
Pancreatic exocrine insufficiency
Pheochromocytoma as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC) is strongly associated with ulcerative colitis; an elevated alkaline phosphatase should prompt MRCP evaluation, and PSC also markedly increases colorectal cancer risk, intensifying surveillance. The histology described — crypt distortion, crypt abscesses, and inflammation confined to mucosa/submucosa without granulomas — is typical of UC. Pancreatic exocrine insufficiency (option B), glomerulonephritis (option C), and pheochromocytoma (option D) are not characteristic associations of UC. Other UC-linked extraintestinal manifestations include uveitis/episcleritis, sacroiliitis/ankylosing spondylitis, erythema nodosum, and pyoderma gangrenosum. PSC is the high-yield association to screen for here.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Disease is classified as mild-to-moderate left-sided ulcerative colitis. What is the most appropriate first-line therapy to induce and maintain remission?
options: Oral plus topical mesalamine
Anti-TNF biologic monotherapy as first-line
Broad-spectrum antibiotics as initial therapy
Long-term oral corticosteroids as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For mild-to-moderate left-sided UC, combined oral and topical (suppository/enema) mesalamine is first-line for both induction and maintenance; topical therapy directly treats the rectosigmoid where tenesmus and bleeding originate, and the combination outperforms either alone. Long-term oral corticosteroids (option B) can induce but not maintain remission and carry cumulative toxicity. Anti-TNF biologics (option C) are reserved for moderate-to-severe or 5-ASA-refractory disease, not first-line for mild-to-moderate disease. Antibiotics (option D) have no role in standard UC induction (active infection was excluded). 5-ASA, oral plus rectal, is the guideline-recommended starting therapy.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which long-term surveillance recommendation is most appropriate for this patient with ulcerative colitis?
options: Lifetime avoidance of all colonoscopy due to perforation risk with appropriate follow-up
Annual upper endoscopy for gastric cancer with appropriate follow-up
No surveillance is needed unless symptoms recur with appropriate follow-up
Surveillance colonoscopy beginning about 8 years after diagnosis , given increased colorectal cancer risk
stages.explanation: Long-standing colitis increases colorectal cancer risk in proportion to disease duration and extent; surveillance colonoscopy with biopsies/chromoendoscopy typically begins about 8 years after diagnosis (or sooner — and more frequently — if PSC coexists, where surveillance starts at PSC diagnosis). Deferring all surveillance until symptoms recur (option A) misses dysplasia, which is often asymptomatic. Annual upper endoscopy (option C) is unwarranted; UC raises colorectal, not gastric, cancer risk. Avoiding colonoscopy entirely (option D) is incorrect and unsafe given the surveillance benefit. Risk-stratified colonoscopic surveillance is the standard of care.

pearls: UC is continuous mucosal inflammation starting at the rectum; bloody diarrhea, mucus, and tenesmus are hallmarks, with crypt abscesses and no granulomas on biopsy.
Smoking cessation can precipitate or worsen UC — the inverse of its effect in Crohn disease.
Screen for primary sclerosing cholangitis (alkaline phosphatase, MRCP); it is strongly linked to UC and sharply raises colorectal cancer risk.
First-line for mild-to-moderate left-sided UC is combined oral and topical 5-ASA; surveillance colonoscopy starts ~8 years after diagnosis (sooner with PSC).

cases.title: Worsening Bloody Diarrhea With Fever and a Distended Belly
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe bloody diarrhea, high fever, and progressive abdominal distension in a known colitis patient.
cases.history: A 33-year-old man with a 5-year history of ulcerative colitis is admitted to the ICU after a week of escalating bloody diarrhea (up to 12 stools daily) that has now paradoxically decreased to 3 stools while his abdomen has become markedly distended and painful. He reports high fevers, rigors, and confusion. His outpatient regimen is mesalamine; he ran out of his maintenance medication a month ago and recently took loperamide and ibuprofen for cramping. PMH: ulcerative colitis (left-sided at last colonoscopy). Medications: mesalamine (non-adherent), recent loperamide, ibuprofen. Allergies: none. Social: occasional alcohol, non-smoker, no recent travel. Family history: non-contributory. On arrival he is tachycardic, febrile, and minimally responsive to questions, with reduced urine output over 12 hours. He denies recent antibiotics before this illness, but a C. difficile assay is pending given his hospitalization. The combination of antimotility agents, NSAIDs, and a severe flare is noted as potentially contributory to his deterioration.
cases.exam: The patient is lethargic and ill-appearing. He is tachycardic with thready pulses and cool extremities. The abdomen is markedly distended and tympanitic with diffuse tenderness; bowel sounds are absent. There is voluntary guarding but no clear rebound or rigidity at this moment. Mucous membranes are dry and capillary refill is delayed. Cardiopulmonary exam shows tachypnea with clear lungs. He is oliguric. Mentation is impaired (responds slowly, oriented only to person). Skin is warm centrally but mottled peripherally, consistent with early distributive/septic shock. No jaundice or peripheral edema is noted.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which immediate diagnostic study is most important to confirm the suspected complication?
options: Colonoscopy to assess mucosal severity in this clinical context
Barium enema as the first step today
Abdominal radiograph to measure colonic diameter
Outpatient stool calprotectin as the first step today
stages.explanation: The picture — severe colitis flare, fever, tachycardia, altered mentation, and a distended, tympanitic abdomen with paradoxically decreased stool frequency — is classic for toxic megacolon. A plain abdominal radiograph (often supplemented by CT) is the key initial study, demonstrating colonic dilation (transverse colon typically >6 cm) and screening for perforation/free air. Colonoscopy (option A) is contraindicated in suspected toxic megacolon because insufflation risks perforation. Barium enema (option C) is likewise contraindicated for perforation risk. Outpatient calprotectin (option D) is irrelevant in an unstable, potentially perforating patient. Imaging to measure colonic diameter and exclude free air is the correct, safe diagnostic step.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 19.8 ×10³/µL with bandemia
Hemoglobin 9.4 g/dL
Na 131 mEq/L, K 2.9 mEq/L
Lactate 4.1 mmol/L
Albumin 2.4 g/dL, CRP 180 mg/L
C. difficile NAAT: pending
Abdominal radiograph: transverse colon dilated to 8 cm with loss of haustration and mucosal islands; no free air under the diaphragm
CT abdomen/pelvis: marked colonic dilation, diffuse wall thickening, no pneumoperitoneum

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Beyond colonic dilation on imaging, which set of criteria is required to diagnose toxic megacolon?
options: Radiographic colonic dilation PLUS systemic toxicity with additional signs such as dehydration, electrolyte disturbance, hypotension, or altered mental status
Colonic dilation alone is sufficient as the primary diagnosis
Free air on imaging is required as the primary diagnosis
A positive C. difficile test is mandatory for the diagnosis as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Toxic megacolon is a clinical-radiographic diagnosis: radiographic evidence of colonic dilation (commonly transverse colon >6 cm) PLUS systemic toxicity. The Jalan criteria require at least 3 of fever >38°C, HR >120, neutrophilic leukocytosis, or anemia, plus at least one of dehydration, electrolyte disturbance, hypotension, or altered consciousness — all present here. Dilation alone (option A) is megacolon, not toxic megacolon, which requires systemic toxicity. A positive C. difficile test (option C) is a common precipitant but not required — IBD flare alone can cause it. Free air (option D) indicates perforation, a complication mandating surgery, but is not required for the diagnosis. Dilation plus systemic toxicity defines it.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial medical management bundle while monitoring closely?
options: Continue loperamide and add an opioid for pain control as initial therapy
Start oral mesalamine and discharge with close follow-up as initial therapy
Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation, electrolyte correction, bowel rest with nasogastric decompression, IV corticosteroids, broad-spectrum antibiotics,
Immediate barium enema followed by oral antibiotics as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Initial management combines aggressive resuscitation (IV fluids, correction of hypokalemia and other electrolytes), bowel rest with nasogastric decompression, IV corticosteroids to treat the underlying severe IBD flare, broad-spectrum antibiotics for the systemic toxicity/risk of perforation, and — critically — discontinuation of all antimotility agents (loperamide), anticholinergics, opioids, and NSAIDs, which precipitate and worsen toxic megacolon. Continuing loperamide and adding opioids (option A) is dangerous and contraindicated. Oral mesalamine with discharge (option C) grossly under-treats a life-threatening emergency. Barium enema (option D) is contraindicated due to perforation risk. The comprehensive medical bundle with cessation of precipitating drugs is correct, alongside early surgical consultation.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Despite 48-72 hours of maximal medical therapy he remains febrile and toxic with persistent/worsening dilation, and a repeat film shows new free air. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Add a second antibiotic and recheck imaging in 5 days
Continue medical therapy for another week
Attempt colonoscopic decompression based on current risk
Urgent surgical consultation for total/subtotal colectomy
stages.explanation: Failure to improve after 48-72 hours of maximal medical therapy, clinical deterioration, or evidence of perforation (free air) are indications for urgent surgery — typically subtotal/total colectomy with end ileostomy — which is life-saving in toxic megacolon. Continuing medical therapy for a week (option A) or simply adding antibiotics and delaying imaging (option B) risks fatal perforation and worsening sepsis. Colonoscopic decompression (option D) is hazardous in toxic megacolon and not appropriate, particularly with free air already present. When medical therapy fails or perforation occurs, prompt colectomy is the definitive intervention and should not be delayed.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which precipitating factor in his history most plausibly contributed to the development of toxic megacolon?
options: Regular aerobic exercise as the leading concern
Use of antimotility agents
A high-fiber diet as the leading concern
Adherence to mesalamine maintenance therapy as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Antimotility agents (loperamide, opioids), anticholinergics, and NSAIDs can precipitate toxic megacolon by impairing colonic motility and worsening inflammation during a severe flare, and hypokalemia further depresses colonic smooth-muscle tone — all present in this patient (loperamide, ibuprofen, K 2.9). Adherence to mesalamine (option B) would protect against flares, not precipitate the crisis (he was, in fact, non-adherent). A high-fiber diet (option C) and aerobic exercise (option D) are not recognized precipitants. Recognizing and removing these precipitating drugs and correcting electrolytes is central to both prevention and treatment of toxic megacolon.

pearls: Suspect toxic megacolon when a colitis flare shows fever, tachycardia, leukocytosis, and a distended abdomen with paradoxically decreasing stool output; confirm with abdominal radiograph (transverse colon >6 cm).
Colonoscopy and barium enema are contraindicated in suspected toxic megacolon due to perforation risk.
Antimotility agents, anticholinergics, opioids, NSAIDs, and hypokalemia precipitate it — stop them and correct electrolytes.
Failure of 48-72 h of maximal medical therapy, clinical deterioration, or perforation (free air) mandates urgent subtotal/total colectomy.

cases.title: Watery Diarrhea After a Course of Antibiotics
cases.chiefComplaint: Frequent watery, foul-smelling diarrhea and crampy abdominal pain after recent antibiotic treatment.
cases.history: A 71-year-old woman, hospitalized 5 days ago for community-acquired pneumonia, develops 8-10 watery, foul-smelling stools per day with lower abdominal cramping and low-grade fever on hospital day 5. She finished a course of ceftriaxone and azithromycin 2 days ago. PMH: type 2 diabetes, hypertension, GERD on a chronic proton-pump inhibitor, prior diverticulosis. Medications: ceftriaxone/azithromycin (recently completed), omeprazole 20 mg daily, metformin, lisinopril. Allergies: penicillin (rash). Social: lives in an assisted-living facility; non-smoker; no recent travel. Family history: non-contributory. She reports no blood in the stool but notes mucus. She is mildly orthostatic and has reduced oral intake. Review of systems is otherwise notable for fatigue. No recent immunosuppression beyond age and diabetes. Nursing reports several other residents at her facility had diarrhea recently. She has not received antidiarrheal medication, and her abdomen has become slightly more distended over the past day.
cases.exam: The patient is fatigued but conversant. Mucous membranes are dry. The abdomen is mildly distended with diffuse lower-abdominal tenderness and hyperactive bowel sounds; there is no rebound, guarding, or rigidity, and no palpable mass. Cardiopulmonary exam shows tachycardia with clear lungs and resolving crackles at the right base. Skin turgor is reduced. There is no peripheral edema. Rectal exam reveals loose, watery, guaiac-negative stool. She is alert and oriented. Extremities are warm. No jaundice. The remainder of the examination is unremarkable, with no costovertebral angle tenderness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic test given the clinical picture?
options: Colonoscopy with biopsy as the first step as the first step today
Stool ova and parasite examination as the first step today
Stool testing for Clostridioides difficile
CT abdomen with contrast as the first test as the first step today
stages.explanation: Recent antibiotic exposure, healthcare/facility setting, advanced age, PPI use, and new watery diarrhea make Clostridioides difficile infection the leading diagnosis; stool testing using a multistep algorithm (glutamate dehydrogenase plus toxin EIA, with NAAT to resolve discordant results) is the appropriate first test, performed only on unformed stool. CT (option A) is useful if complications like ileus, megacolon, or perforation are suspected, but is not the initial diagnostic test for uncomplicated suspected C. difficile. Ova and parasite testing (option C) targets parasitic causes unlikely in this nosocomial, antibiotic-associated context. Colonoscopy (option D) can reveal pseudomembranes but is invasive and not first-line; it is reserved for diagnostic uncertainty. Stool C. difficile testing is the targeted, guideline-based first step.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
WBC 16.4 ×10³/µL
Creatinine 1.2 mg/dL (baseline 0.9)
Lactate 1.6 mmol/L
Albumin 3.0 g/dL
Stool C. difficile: GDH positive, toxin EIA positive
Stool culture and O&P: negative
Abdominal radiograph: mild colonic distension, no free air

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She is hemodynamically stable without ileus or megacolon. What is the most appropriate first-line antibiotic therapy for this initial episode?
options: Intravenous vancomycin as monotherapy
Oral fidaxomicin
Oral metronidazole alone
Loperamide to control stool frequency
stages.explanation: Current IDSA/SHEA guidelines recommend oral fidaxomicin or oral vancomycin as first-line for an initial episode of C. difficile infection; oral metronidazole (option A) is now reserved for when neither preferred agent is available, given inferior outcomes. Intravenous vancomycin (option C) is ineffective because it is not delivered to the colonic lumen — oral (or rectal in ileus) vancomycin is required for luminal activity. Loperamide (option D) is contraindicated as monotherapy because slowing motility can precipitate toxin retention, ileus, and toxic megacolon. The offending antibiotics should also be stopped if possible. Oral fidaxomicin or vancomycin is the correct evidence-based therapy.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Two days into therapy she becomes hypotensive with WBC 28 ×10³/µL, lactate 4.2 mmol/L, and a markedly distended abdomen with ileus. How should therapy be escalated for fulminant disease?
options: Switch to oral metronidazole monotherapy as the leading concern
High-dose oral vancomycin PLUS intravenous metronidazole, with urgent surgical consultation
Stop all antibiotics and observe as the leading concern
Begin loperamide and IV fluids only as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Fulminant C. difficile infection (hypotension/shock, ileus, or megacolon) is treated with high-dose oral vancomycin (125-500 mg) — given per rectum as a retention enema if ileus prevents oral delivery — PLUS intravenous metronidazole, with prompt surgical consultation for possible colectomy. Oral metronidazole monotherapy (option A) is inadequate for fulminant disease. Stopping all antibiotics (option C) abandons treatment of a life-threatening infection. Loperamide with fluids alone (option D) is dangerous and contraindicated, risking toxic megacolon. The combination of high-dose luminal vancomycin and IV metronidazole, plus surgical evaluation, is the standard escalation for fulminant C. difficile colitis.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: She recovers, but 3 weeks after finishing treatment her symptoms and a positive toxin test recur for the first time. What is the most appropriate management of this first recurrence?
options: No treatment, since recurrence is self-limited with appropriate follow-up
A tapered/pulsed oral vancomycin regimen or fidaxomicin
A 6-month course of daily metronidazole with appropriate follow-up
Repeat the exact same regimen she just completed at the same dosing with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: For a first recurrence, options include a prolonged tapered-and-pulsed oral vancomycin regimen, fidaxomicin (standard or extended-pulse), or vancomycin if fidaxomicin was used initially; fecal microbiota transplantation or approved microbiota-based therapies are reserved for multiple recurrences. Simply repeating the identical regimen at the same dosing (option A) is less effective for recurrence than a taper/pulse or switching agents. Chronic daily metronidazole for months (option C) risks cumulative neurotoxicity and is not recommended. Withholding treatment (option D) is inappropriate, as recurrent C. difficile can progress and is rarely self-limited in a frail elderly patient. A tapered/pulsed vancomycin or fidaxomicin strategy is the evidence-based approach to first recurrence.

cases.diagnosis: Clostridioides difficile colitis (with fulminant course and subsequent recurrence)
pearls: Suspect C. difficile with recent antibiotics, healthcare/facility exposure, advanced age, and PPI use; test only unformed stool using a GDH/toxin plus NAAT algorithm.
First-line therapy for an initial episode is oral fidaxomicin or oral vancomycin; metronidazole is now second-line, and IV vancomycin does not reach the colonic lumen.
Fulminant disease (hypotension, ileus, megacolon) needs high-dose oral/rectal vancomycin plus IV metronidazole and surgical consultation; avoid antimotility agents.
First recurrence is treated with tapered/pulsed vancomycin or fidaxomicin; fecal microbiota-based therapy is for multiple recurrences.

cases.title: Months of Watery Diarrhea With a Normal-Looking Colon
cases.chiefComplaint: Chronic non-bloody, watery diarrhea persisting despite a normal-appearing colonoscopy.
cases.history: A 66-year-old woman reports 7 months of persistent, watery, non-bloody diarrhea, 6-9 times daily including nocturnal stools, with mild crampy abdominal discomfort and occasional fecal urgency. She has lost 2 kg and feels fatigued. Symptoms do not improve with fasting initially but she notes they persist day and night. PMH: hypothyroidism, osteoarthritis, GERD, and a remote history of celiac disease screening that was negative. Medications: she takes daily naproxen for joint pain, a proton-pump inhibitor (omeprazole) for reflux, a recently started SSRI (sertraline), and levothyroxine. Allergies: none. Social: non-smoker, minimal alcohol, no recent travel or antibiotics, no sick contacts. Family history: autoimmune thyroid disease and rheumatoid arthritis. A recent colonoscopy for the diarrhea was reported as endoscopically normal, but biopsies were not initially taken. She denies blood in the stool, fevers, and steatorrhea. Her diarrhea is described as secretory in quality and does not clearly relate to specific foods.
cases.exam: The patient appears well and well-hydrated, in no distress. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal. The abdomen is soft, non-distended, and non-tender, with normal bowel sounds and no organomegaly or mass. There is no peripheral edema or rash, and no clubbing. Rectal examination reveals soft brown stool that is guaiac-negative, with normal sphincter tone and no masses. The thyroid is non-enlarged. There are mild Heberden nodes at the distal interphalangeal joints consistent with osteoarthritis. The remainder of the examination, including a neurologic survey, is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given chronic watery diarrhea with an endoscopically normal colon, what is the single most important next step?
options: Repeat colonoscopy with random mucosal biopsies of normal-appearing colon
Begin a long-term opioid antidiarrheal without further workup
Reassure and treat empirically as irritable bowel syndrome in this clinical context
Order a CT enterography as the first step today
stages.explanation: Microscopic colitis presents with chronic watery, non-bloody diarrhea and a grossly NORMAL-appearing colon; the diagnosis is made only on histology, so random biopsies of normal-looking mucosa are essential — endoscopy without biopsy will miss it. Labeling this as IBS (option A) is premature in an older woman with new nocturnal, secretory diarrhea and red-flag features; IBS is a diagnosis of exclusion. CT enterography (option B) evaluates small-bowel structural disease and would be normal here. Starting chronic antidiarrheals empirically (option D) treats a symptom without securing the diagnosis. Biopsy of normal-appearing colon is the key step that establishes microscopic colitis.

stages.label: BIOPSY RESULTS
stages.content:
Colonoscopy: grossly normal mucosa throughout
Random biopsies: surface epithelial damage with a markedly thickened subepithelial collagen band on trichrome stain and increased lamina propria lymphocytes/plasma cells (collagenous subtype)
Increased intraepithelial lymphocytes also noted
Stool studies (culture, O&P, C. difficile): negative
TSH 2.0 µIU/mL; celiac serologies (anti-tTG IgA) negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which medications in her history are most relevant as recognized associations with this condition and should be reviewed?
options: NSAIDs , proton-pump inhibitors, and SSRIs
Levothyroxine and an SSRI only
Inhaled corticosteroids and antihistamines
Acetaminophen and calcium supplements
stages.explanation: Microscopic colitis is associated with several drug classes — NSAIDs (her naproxen), proton-pump inhibitors (omeprazole), SSRIs (sertraline), and others such as ranitidine, statins, and acarbose. Reviewing and, when feasible, discontinuing implicated agents (especially NSAIDs and PPIs) is part of management and can improve symptoms. Levothyroxine alone (option A) is not a recognized trigger, though autoimmune thyroid disease is an associated comorbidity. Acetaminophen and calcium (option C) and inhaled corticosteroids/antihistamines (option D) are not established associations. Identifying and stopping NSAIDs, PPIs, and SSRIs is a relevant, modifiable component of care in microscopic colitis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After stopping the NSAID and PPI she still has bothersome diarrhea. What is the most effective first-line pharmacologic therapy?
options: Long-term systemic prednisone
Mesalamine enemas as monotherapy
Oral budesonide
Broad-spectrum antibiotics
stages.explanation: Budesonide is the best-evidenced, most effective first-line pharmacologic therapy for microscopic colitis, inducing remission with limited systemic glucocorticoid exposure because of high first-pass hepatic metabolism. Long-term systemic prednisone (option B) is less effective for maintenance, has higher relapse rates, and causes more toxicity. Mesalamine (option C) is inferior to budesonide and not first-line. Antibiotics (option D) have no role, as this is a non-infectious inflammatory condition (infection was excluded). Antidiarrheals like loperamide may help mild symptoms, but budesonide is the standard first-line agent for symptomatic microscopic colitis after removing offending drugs.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which statement best characterizes the prognosis and the two histologic subtypes of this disease?
options: It comprises collagenous
It is a premalignant condition equivalent to ulcerative colitis as the immediate next step
It frequently progresses to colorectal cancer, mandating yearly colonoscopy before discharge planning
It invariably requires colectomy as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Microscopic colitis has two histologic subtypes — collagenous (thickened subepithelial collagen band) and lymphocytic (increased intraepithelial lymphocytes) — that present similarly and are managed the same way. It carries a benign prognosis and does NOT increase colorectal cancer risk, so it does not warrant intensified cancer surveillance (option A is wrong). It does not require colectomy (option C). It is not premalignant and is distinct from the dysplasia risk of ulcerative colitis (option D). Many patients respond well to budesonide, though relapse after stopping therapy is common and may need re-treatment or low-dose maintenance. Recognizing the benign nature and the two subtypes is high-yield.

pearls: Microscopic colitis causes chronic watery, non-bloody diarrhea with a grossly normal colon — diagnosis requires random biopsies of normal-appearing mucosa.
Two subtypes: collagenous (thick subepithelial collagen band) and lymphocytic (increased intraepithelial lymphocytes); both are managed identically.
Associated with NSAIDs, PPIs, and SSRIs and with autoimmune disease; review and stop offending drugs as part of management.
Budesonide is first-line therapy; the disease is benign with no increased colorectal cancer risk, though relapses are common.

cases.title: Crampy Left-Sided Pain With Blood in the Stool
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden crampy left lower abdominal pain followed by an urge to defecate and bloody stool.
cases.history: A 75-year-old woman presents with sudden onset of crampy left lower quadrant abdominal pain about 4 hours ago, followed within an hour by an urgent bowel movement that contained bright red and maroon blood mixed with stool. The pain is moderate and now somewhat improved. She had a recent episode of gastroenteritis with volume depletion and was recently started on a diuretic. PMH: hypertension, atrial fibrillation (rate-controlled), prior abdominal aortic aneurysm repair, constipation, hyperlipidemia. Medications: metoprolol, hydrochlorothiazide (recently increased), atorvastatin, and an OTC NSAID for arthritis. She is NOT on anticoagulation due to prior falls. Allergies: none. Social: former smoker (30 pack-years), occasional alcohol. Family history: coronary artery disease. Review of systems is notable for mild nausea but no vomiting, no fevers initially, and no prior similar episodes. The bleeding is modest, not massive, and the pain preceded the bleeding. She denies syncope and reports she remains able to walk, though she feels lightheaded when standing.
cases.exam: The patient is uncomfortable but not toxic-appearing. The abdomen is soft with tenderness localized to the left lower quadrant and left flank; bowel sounds are present, and there is no rebound, guarding, rigidity, or palpable pulsatile mass. The degree of abdominal tenderness is proportionate to the clinical picture rather than out of proportion. Rectal examination reveals maroon and bright red blood mixed with stool. Cardiac exam shows an irregularly irregular rhythm without murmurs. Extremities are warm with palpable distal pulses. She is mildly orthostatic. There is no peritonitis. Skin is well-perfused, and mucous membranes are slightly dry.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which clinical pattern best supports the suspected diagnosis over acute mesenteric ischemia?
options: Painless massive lower GI bleeding as the first step today
Epigastric pain radiating to the back with hypotension as the first step today
Crampy left-sided pain followed by hematochezia, with tenderness proportionate to exam findings
Severe pain markedly out of proportion to a benign abdominal exam, with minimal bleeding
stages.explanation: Ischemic colitis classically presents in an older patient with the relatively rapid onset of crampy abdominal pain (often left-sided) followed within hours by an urge to defecate and mild-to-moderate hematochezia, with abdominal tenderness that is proportionate to the exam. Pain markedly out of proportion to a benign exam with little bleeding (option A) is the hallmark of acute mesenteric ischemia of the small bowel — a different, more catastrophic entity. Painless massive lower GI bleeding (option C) suggests diverticular bleeding or angiodysplasia. Epigastric pain radiating to the back with hypotension (option D) suggests a ruptured aneurysm or pancreatitis. The pain-then-bleeding sequence with proportionate tenderness fits ischemic colitis.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 13.1 ×10³/µL
Hemoglobin 12.4 g/dL
Lactate 2.0 mmol/L
Creatinine 1.3 mg/dL
CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast: segmental wall thickening and mucosal edema of the descending colon near the splenic flexure with pericolic fat stranding; patent SMA/IMA on visualized images; no free air or pneumatosis
Stool studies pending

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The CT shows changes near the splenic flexure. Why is this location characteristically involved?
options: It is the most common site of diverticular bleeding as the primary diagnosis
It is where bacterial overgrowth concentrates as the primary diagnosis
It is a 'watershed' area between the SMA
It is the segment with the richest collateral blood supply in this clinical context
stages.explanation: The splenic flexure (Griffiths point) and the rectosigmoid junction (Sudeck point) are colonic 'watershed' zones at the boundaries between the superior and inferior mesenteric artery territories; their limited collateral perfusion makes them especially vulnerable to low-flow/non-occlusive ischemia, which is why ischemic colitis localizes there, particularly after hypotension or hypovolemia. These are not areas of richest collateral supply (option A — the opposite is true). Diverticular bleeding (option C) is most common in the right colon and is typically painless. Bacterial overgrowth (option D) is unrelated to this vascular anatomy. The watershed concept explains the splenic-flexure predilection of ischemic colitis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She is hemodynamically stable without peritonitis or gangrene. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Emergent mesenteric angiography with intra-arterial vasodilators as initial therapy
Therapeutic anticoagulation with heparin as initial therapy
Supportive care: IV fluids, bowel rest, correcting hypoperfusion, stopping offending vasoconstrictive/precipitating agents,
Immediate surgical colectomy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Most non-gangrenous ischemic colitis is managed supportively: IV fluid resuscitation to restore perfusion, bowel rest, correction of the precipitating hypovolemia/hypotension, discontinuation of vasoconstrictors and other precipitants (here, optimizing the diuretic and NSAID), and antibiotics for moderate-to-severe disease, with serial monitoring for deterioration. The majority resolve without surgery. Immediate colectomy (option A) is reserved for peritonitis, gangrene, perforation, or clinical deterioration, none present here. Intra-arterial vasodilators via angiography (option C) are used in acute mesenteric ischemia, not typical colonic ischemic colitis. Anticoagulation (option D) is not standard therapy and could worsen GI bleeding. Supportive care with monitoring is correct for stable disease.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which finding during her hospital course would most strongly indicate the need for urgent surgical intervention?
options: Development of peritoneal signs, hemodynamic deterioration, or CT evidence of pneumatosis/portal venous gas suggesting transmural infarction
Resolution of pain and bleeding within 24 hours with appropriate follow-up
A single additional bloody stool with appropriate follow-up
Mild persistent left lower quadrant tenderness on day 2 with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Indications for urgent surgery in ischemic colitis include peritonitis, hemodynamic instability/deterioration despite resuscitation, evidence of transmural necrosis (pneumatosis intestinalis, portal venous gas, perforation/free air), or persistent symptoms with clinical worsening — signs that ischemia has progressed to gangrene. Resolution of pain and bleeding within 24 hours (option A) signals favorable recovery, not surgery. A single additional bloody stool (option C) or mild persistent tenderness on day 2 (option D) without systemic deterioration is expected in the recovering course and does not by itself warrant operation. Peritoneal signs, hemodynamic decline, and imaging signs of infarction are the red flags mandating surgical intervention.

pearls: Ischemic colitis: older patient with rapid-onset crampy (often left-sided) pain THEN mild-to-moderate hematochezia, with tenderness proportionate to exam.
Watershed zones — splenic flexure (Griffiths) and rectosigmoid (Sudeck) — are most vulnerable, especially after hypotension/hypovolemia.
Most non-gangrenous cases resolve with supportive care: fluids, bowel rest, removing precipitants, and antibiotics for moderate-to-severe disease.
Surgery is for peritonitis, hemodynamic deterioration, or transmural infarction (pneumatosis, portal venous gas, perforation).

cases.title: Sudden Severe Belly Pain in a Patient With an Irregular Pulse
cases.chiefComplaint: Abrupt, severe, diffuse abdominal pain that seems far worse than the abdominal exam suggests.
cases.history: A 78-year-old man presents with abrupt onset of severe, constant, diffuse abdominal pain that began 3 hours ago, accompanied by nausea, one episode of vomiting, and an urgent bowel movement. The pain is excruciating and unrelenting. PMH: atrial fibrillation (he admits he frequently misses his anticoagulant), prior myocardial infarction, hypertension, heart failure, and peripheral arterial disease. Medications: apixaban (poorly adherent), metoprolol, furosemide, atorvastatin. Allergies: none. Social: former heavy smoker (40 pack-years), occasional alcohol. Family history: vascular disease. He reports no prior similar episodes and no chronic postprandial pain or food fear. On further history, he has had palpitations over the past day. The acuteness and severity of the pain, in a patient with atrial fibrillation off anticoagulation, raise concern for an embolic event. He denies hematemesis. Early in the course the abdomen was described as soft despite the dramatic pain, and bowel movements may contain blood later in the course.
cases.exam: The patient is in obvious distress, writhing in pain. Strikingly, early abdominal examination reveals a soft, only mildly tender abdomen with no rebound or guarding — pain markedly out of proportion to physical findings. Bowel sounds are present early. Cardiac exam shows an irregularly irregular rhythm without murmurs. Peripheral pulses are diminished in the lower extremities. There is no pulsatile abdominal mass. As time passes, the abdomen begins to develop more diffuse tenderness. Rectal exam is initially guaiac-negative. The patient is diaphoretic. Mucous membranes are moist. No jaundice or flank ecchymosis is present.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which single feature most strongly suggests acute mesenteric ischemia in this patient?
options: A pulsatile abdominal mass as the first step today
Gradual cramping over several weeks as the first step today
Pain out of proportion to a benign early abdominal exam in a patient with atrial fibrillation off anticoagulation
Pain that consistently improves with eating as the first step today
stages.explanation: The hallmark of acute mesenteric ischemia (AMI) is sudden, severe pain that is out of proportion to a relatively benign early abdominal exam; in this patient the atrial fibrillation with poor anticoagulant adherence makes an arterial embolus to the superior mesenteric artery (the most common AMI mechanism) the leading concern. Gradual cramping over weeks (option B) describes chronic mesenteric ischemia. A pulsatile mass (option C) suggests an aortic aneurysm. Pain improving with eating (option D) is not a feature of intestinal ischemia — postprandial pain (food fear) characterizes chronic mesenteric ischemia. The pain-out-of-proportion finding in an embolism-prone patient is the key clue to AMI.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate diagnostic test to confirm the diagnosis?
options: CT angiography of the abdomen/pelvis
Abdominal ultrasound as the primary diagnosis
Colonoscopy as the primary diagnosis
Plain abdominal radiograph alone
stages.explanation: CT angiography is the test of choice for suspected acute mesenteric ischemia: it is rapid and accurate for identifying arterial embolus/thrombus or venous occlusion, bowel-wall changes, pneumatosis, and portal venous gas, and it guides intervention. Abdominal ultrasound (option A) is limited by bowel gas and operator dependence and is not reliable for the SMA in this setting. A plain radiograph alone (option B) is insensitive early and shows late findings (pneumatosis, ileus) only after infarction. Colonoscopy (option D) evaluates the colon, not the SMA-supplied small bowel, and is inappropriate and risky here. CTA should not be delayed; lactate and other labs are supportive but CTA is definitive for diagnosis.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 18.6 ×10³/µL
Lactate 4.8 mmol/L (elevated)
Metabolic panel: anion-gap metabolic acidosis
Amylase mildly elevated
ECG: atrial fibrillation, rate 116
CT angiography: abrupt filling defect in the superior mesenteric artery just distal to the origin of the middle colic branch with non-enhancement of several small-bowel loops; no pneumatosis or portal venous gas yet

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: CTA confirms an embolic SMA occlusion without yet-evident bowel necrosis. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Bowel rest and observation alone for 48 hours as initial therapy
Discharge with oral anticoagulation and outpatient follow-up as initial therapy
Immediate resuscitation, IV broad-spectrum antibiotics, systemic anticoagulation ,
Start a beta-blocker and recheck lactate in the morning as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Embolic SMA occlusion is a surgical emergency. Management is immediate fluid resuscitation, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics, prompt systemic anticoagulation with heparin, and urgent revascularization — surgical embolectomy or endovascular thrombectomy/thrombolysis — with laparotomy to assess and resect any non-viable bowel if peritonitis or necrosis is present. Discharge with oral anticoagulation (option A) would be fatal. Observation alone for 48 hours (option C) allows transmural infarction and death; time is bowel. Simply adding a beta-blocker and waiting (option D) ignores the vascular emergency. Rapid revascularization plus resuscitation and anticoagulation, with surgical exploration as needed, is the standard of care and must not be delayed.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which laboratory/clinical evolution would most strongly indicate that bowel infarction has occurred and the prognosis is worsening?
options: Conversion of atrial fibrillation to sinus rhythm as the leading concern
Rising lactate with worsening metabolic acidosis, peritoneal signs,
A mild rise in amylase only as the leading concern
Normalizing lactate and resolving pain as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Progressive bowel infarction is heralded by a rising lactate, worsening anion-gap metabolic acidosis, development of peritoneal signs (rebound, rigidity) as transmural necrosis irritates the peritoneum, and imaging evidence of pneumatosis intestinalis and portal venous gas — all markers of advanced, often non-viable bowel and high mortality. Normalizing lactate with resolving pain (option A) indicates improvement, not deterioration. An isolated mild amylase rise (option C) is nonspecific and not a reliable marker of infarction. Conversion to sinus rhythm (option D) might reduce future embolic risk but does not reflect bowel viability. Recognizing the rising-lactate/acidosis/peritonitis/pneumatosis pattern is critical, as it mandates emergent surgical resection.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After successful embolectomy and bowel resection, he stabilizes. What is the most appropriate long-term secondary-prevention step to reduce recurrent embolic events?
options: Lifelong prophylactic antibiotics with appropriate follow-up
Long-term aspirin monotherapy only with appropriate follow-up
Long-term therapeutic anticoagulation for atrial fibrillation with adherence support
No further therapy once the embolus is removed with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: The embolic source here is atrial fibrillation, so the cornerstone of secondary prevention is durable therapeutic anticoagulation — a DOAC such as apixaban (or warfarin where indicated) — paired with adherence support, since his non-adherence precipitated the event. Aspirin monotherapy (option A) is inadequate stroke/systemic-embolism prophylaxis for atrial fibrillation and does not substitute for anticoagulation. Stopping all therapy after embolectomy (option C) leaves him at high risk of recurrent arterial embolism to the gut, brain, or limbs. Prophylactic antibiotics (option D) play no role in preventing cardioembolism. Addressing the underlying arrhythmia with reliable anticoagulation is the definitive way to prevent recurrent embolic mesenteric ischemia.

cases.diagnosis: Acute mesenteric ischemia (embolic superior mesenteric artery occlusion)
pearls: Pain out of proportion to a benign early abdominal exam in an atrial fibrillation patient off anticoagulation is acute mesenteric ischemia until proven otherwise — most often an SMA embolus.
CT angiography is the diagnostic test of choice; do not delay for serial labs, though elevated lactate and anion-gap acidosis support advanced ischemia.
Management: resuscitation, broad-spectrum antibiotics, systemic heparin, and urgent revascularization (embolectomy or endovascular), with laparotomy for necrosis/peritonitis.
Rising lactate, peritoneal signs, pneumatosis intestinalis, and portal venous gas signal transmural infarction and carry high mortality.

cases.title: Fear of Eating and Steady Weight Loss
cases.chiefComplaint: Months of dull abdominal pain that reliably comes on after meals, leading her to avoid eating.
cases.history: A 70-year-old woman reports about 6 months of dull, crampy mid-abdominal pain that reliably begins 15-30 minutes after eating and lasts 1-2 hours. Because eating predictably triggers pain, she has become afraid to eat ('food fear') and now takes only small meals; she has lost 9 kg unintentionally. She also reports early satiety and intermittent nausea. PMH: long-standing tobacco use, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, coronary artery disease with prior PCI, peripheral arterial disease with claudication, and a carotid endarterectomy. Medications: aspirin, atorvastatin, metoprolol, lisinopril. Allergies: none. Social: current smoker (50 pack-years), minimal alcohol. Family history: extensive atherosclerotic disease. Review of systems is negative for hematochezia, fevers, or jaundice. An extensive prior workup — upper endoscopy, abdominal ultrasound, and CT abdomen without dedicated vascular protocol — was unrevealing, and she was previously told her symptoms might be 'functional.' The strong link between eating and pain, plus profound weight loss and diffuse atherosclerosis, raises concern for an ischemic intestinal process.
cases.exam: The patient is thin and appears chronically ill with temporal wasting. The abdomen is scaphoid and soft; there may be a faint epigastric bruit on auscultation, and there is mild diffuse tenderness without rebound, guarding, or a pulsatile mass. Bowel sounds are normal. Peripheral pulses are diminished in both lower extremities with a femoral bruit. Cardiac exam is regular without murmur. There is no jaundice, ascites, or peripheral edema. Skin shows changes of chronic arterial insufficiency in the lower legs. The remainder of the examination, including a stool guaiac test, is unremarkable (guaiac-negative).

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which triad of features best characterizes the suspected diagnosis?
options: Painless GI bleeding, anemia, and weight gain as the first step today
Crampy left-sided pain with bloody diarrhea after hypotension as the first step today
Postprandial abdominal pain, food fear/avoidance,
Sudden severe pain out of proportion to exam with atrial fibrillation
stages.explanation: Chronic mesenteric ischemia ('intestinal angina') classically presents with the triad of postprandial abdominal pain, food fear with consequent reduced intake, and weight loss, typically in an older patient with widespread atherosclerosis — exactly this patient. Painless GI bleeding with weight gain (option A) does not fit. Sudden severe pain out of proportion to exam in an atrial-fibrillation patient (option C) describes ACUTE mesenteric ischemia. Crampy left-sided pain with bloody diarrhea after hypotension (option D) describes ischemic colitis. The chronic, eating-triggered pain with food fear and weight loss against a backdrop of atherosclerosis is the defining presentation of chronic mesenteric ischemia.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic test, and what is typically required to attribute symptoms to mesenteric stenosis?
options: CT angiography demonstrating significant stenosis, typically of at least two of the three mesenteric arteries because of rich collateral circulation
Repeat upper endoscopy alone as the primary diagnosis
Empiric antidepressant for functional pain as the primary diagnosis
Plain abdominal radiograph as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: CT angiography (with mesenteric duplex ultrasound or MR angiography as alternatives) is the preferred test, demonstrating atherosclerotic stenosis of the mesenteric arteries (celiac, SMA, IMA). Because of extensive collateral circulation, symptomatic chronic mesenteric ischemia usually requires significant stenosis of at least two of the three main vessels (most importantly the SMA) before symptoms develop — single-vessel disease is often asymptomatic. Repeat upper endoscopy (option A) and plain radiographs (option C) will not reveal the vascular lesion and have already been unhelpful. Treating empirically for functional pain (option D) is the prior misstep that delayed diagnosis. Vascular imaging documenting multivessel stenosis is the diagnostic key.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
Albumin 3.0 g/dL
Hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL
Mesenteric duplex: elevated peak systolic velocities in the celiac axis and SMA consistent with high-grade stenosis
CT angiography: severe ostial atherosclerotic stenosis of the celiac artery and the superior mesenteric artery (>70%), with a patent but diseased IMA and prominent collateral vessels
No bowel-wall pneumatosis; no acute occlusion

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Imaging confirms severe celiac and SMA stenosis. What is the most appropriate definitive management for her symptomatic disease?
options: Revascularization — endovascular angioplasty with stenting or surgical bypass — together with aggressive atherosclerosis risk-factor modification including smoking cessation
Therapeutic anticoagulation alone as initial therapy
Reassurance that symptoms are functional and observation as initial therapy
Long-term opioids for pain and a high-calorie oral diet only as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Symptomatic chronic mesenteric ischemia warrants revascularization to relieve symptoms, restore nutrition, and prevent progression to acute mesenteric ischemia/infarction; endovascular angioplasty with stenting is first-line in most patients, with open surgical bypass reserved for unsuitable anatomy or endovascular failure. This must be paired with aggressive risk-factor control — smoking cessation, statin, antiplatelet therapy, and blood-pressure control. Opioids with diet alone (option A) neither treat the stenosis nor prevent infarction. Anticoagulation alone (option C) does not address the fixed atherosclerotic stenosis. Reassurance/observation (option D) risks fatal acute-on-chronic ischemia. Revascularization plus medical optimization is the definitive, guideline-supported approach.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If her symptomatic high-grade mesenteric stenosis is left untreated, which outcome is the most feared complication?
options: Spontaneous resolution with no risk as the leading concern
Development of celiac disease as the leading concern
Progression to acute mesenteric ischemia with bowel infarction
Conversion to ulcerative colitis as the leading concern
stages.explanation: The most feared consequence of untreated symptomatic chronic mesenteric ischemia is acute-on-chronic mesenteric ischemia — thrombosis of an already critically stenosed vessel causing bowel infarction, a catastrophic, high-mortality event. This risk, along with progressive malnutrition, is why revascularization is pursued in symptomatic patients. Celiac disease (option B) is an immune reaction to gluten unrelated to mesenteric atherosclerosis. Ulcerative colitis (option C) is an inflammatory bowel disease with no causal link to vascular stenosis. Spontaneous resolution (option D) does not occur with fixed high-grade atherosclerotic stenosis. Preventing progression to acute infarction is a central rationale for treating symptomatic chronic mesenteric ischemia.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: While arranging revascularization, which single medical intervention will most improve her long-term vascular prognosis?
options: Switching her ACE inhibitor to an angiotensin-receptor blocker
Complete smoking cessation
Adding a daily multivitamin before discharge planning
Restricting all dietary fat to near zero
stages.explanation: Smoking is the strongest modifiable driver of progressive atherosclerosis, and complete cessation yields the greatest long-term reduction in cardiovascular and mesenteric-vascular events — making it the single most impactful medical intervention in this current heavy smoker, alongside her statin and antiplatelet therapy. Switching an ACE inhibitor to an ARB (option B) offers no added atherosclerotic benefit when the ACE inhibitor is tolerated. A multivitamin (option C) does not alter atherosclerotic risk. Near-zero dietary fat (option D) is neither evidence-based nor sustainable and risks worsening her malnutrition; a balanced heart-healthy diet is preferred. Smoking cessation is the highest-yield risk-factor modification for her diffuse atherosclerotic disease.

pearls: Chronic mesenteric ischemia triad: postprandial pain, food fear/avoidance, and weight loss in an older patient with diffuse atherosclerosis.
Because of rich collateral circulation, symptomatic disease usually requires significant stenosis of at least two of the three mesenteric arteries (especially the SMA).
CT angiography (or duplex/MRA) is the diagnostic test of choice; it is frequently misdiagnosed as 'functional' pain, delaying care.
Treat symptomatic disease with revascularization (endovascular stenting first-line) plus aggressive risk-factor control; untreated disease can progress to acute infarction.

cases.title: Fatigue, Bloating, and a Recurrent Itchy Rash
cases.chiefComplaint: Several months of fatigue, abdominal bloating, and an intensely itchy rash on her elbows.
cases.history: A 31-year-old woman presents with 6 months of progressive fatigue, intermittent bloating, and loose, occasionally foul-smelling stools 2-3 times daily. She also describes an intensely pruritic, blistering rash over both elbows and knees that comes and goes. She has unintentionally lost about 4 kg. She reports no fevers, hematochezia, or nocturnal symptoms. PMH is notable for autoimmune hypothyroidism on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily and a history of unexplained iron-deficiency anemia treated with oral ferrous sulfate 325 mg daily without sustained improvement. She has no surgical history. Allergies: none. Medications: levothyroxine, ferrous sulfate, occasional ibuprofen. Social: works as a teacher, drinks alcohol socially, never smoked. She follows a typical Western diet including wheat-based products. Family history: mother has type 1 diabetes; a maternal aunt has 'a thyroid problem' and chronic diarrhea. Review of systems is positive for occasional mouth ulcers and easy bruising but negative for joint pain, jaundice, or melena.
cases.exam: The patient is thin but in no distress. Conjunctivae are mildly pale. The oral mucosa shows two small aphthous ulcers. The abdomen is soft, mildly distended, with normoactive bowel sounds and no tenderness, hepatosplenomegaly, or masses. Over the extensor surfaces of both elbows and knees are grouped, excoriated papulovesicles, several crusted from scratching. There is no jaundice, peripheral edema, or lymphadenopathy. Neurologic exam shows intact sensation without peripheral neuropathy. Stool is brown and guaiac negative.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given her chronic diarrhea, refractory iron-deficiency anemia, weight loss, and pruritic extensor-surface rash, which initial serologic test is most appropriate?
options: Fecal calprotectin alone as the first step today
Hydrogen breath test as the first step today
IgA tissue transglutaminase antibody with total IgA level
Antinuclear antibody (ANA) panel as the first step today
Anti-mitochondrial antibody as the first step today
stages.explanation: The constellation of malabsorptive diarrhea, refractory iron deficiency, weight loss, autoimmune thyroid disease, and a classic dermatitis herpetiformis-type rash strongly suggests celiac disease. IgA anti-tTG is the recommended first-line serologic screen, and a concurrent total IgA detects selective IgA deficiency (which causes false-negative IgA-based testing). Anti-mitochondrial antibody screens for primary biliary cholangitis, which does not fit. ANA is nonspecific and not diagnostic here. Fecal calprotectin suggests mucosal inflammation but does not distinguish celiac disease and is more useful for IBD. A hydrogen breath test evaluates lactose intolerance or SIBO, neither of which explains the rash or autoimmune associations.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 10.4 g/dL
MCV 76 fL
Ferritin 8 ng/mL
IgA tissue transglutaminase (tTG-IgA) 142 U/mL (normal <20)
Total IgA 210 mg/dL (normal)
TSH 2.1 µIU/mL
Folate 4 ng/mL
Vitamin B12 410 pg/mL
AST 22 U/L, ALT 26 U/L

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Her tTG-IgA is markedly elevated with normal total IgA. What is the most appropriate next step to confirm the diagnosis in this adult?
options: Begin a strict gluten-free diet immediately and recheck serology in 6 months
Capsule endoscopy of the small bowel as the primary diagnosis
Skin biopsy of the rash for direct immunofluorescence as the sole confirmatory test
Esophagogastroduodenoscopy with duodenal biopsies while she remains on a gluten-containing diet
HLA-DQ2/DQ8 genetic testing as the confirmatory test as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: In adults, the standard of care is to confirm strongly positive celiac serology with duodenal biopsies obtained during EGD while the patient is still consuming gluten; histology shows villous atrophy, crypt hyperplasia, and intraepithelial lymphocytosis. Starting a gluten-free diet before biopsy can normalize histology and serology, obscuring the diagnosis. HLA-DQ2/DQ8 has high negative predictive value but poor specificity (common in the general population), so it rules out rather than confirms. A skin biopsy with direct immunofluorescence showing granular IgA at dermal papillae confirms dermatitis herpetiformis and can be diagnostic of celiac disease, but standard adult confirmation still relies on duodenal histology here. Capsule endoscopy is not the confirmatory test.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: The patient's pruritic vesicular rash on the elbows and knees is biopsied. Which finding on direct immunofluorescence is characteristic of this associated skin condition?
options: Negative immunofluorescence with eosinophilic spongiosis
Intercellular IgG in a 'net-like' pattern
C3 deposition along dermal vessels only
Linear IgG along the basement membrane
Granular IgA deposits in the dermal papillae
stages.explanation: The rash is dermatitis herpetiformis, the cutaneous manifestation of celiac disease. Direct immunofluorescence of perilesional skin shows granular IgA deposits at the tips of the dermal papillae, driven by anti-epidermal transglutaminase (TG3) antibodies.
