Linear IgG/C3 along the basement membrane characterizes bullous pemphigoid. Intercellular 'net-like' IgG describes pemphigus vulgaris. Isolated perivascular C3 is nonspecific. Dermatitis herpetiformis responds to a gluten-free diet and to dapsone for rapid symptom control.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Biopsies confirm villous atrophy. Beyond initiating a lifelong strict gluten-free diet, which of the following is the most appropriate additional management step?
options: Initiate rifaximin for associated bacterial overgrowth as initial therapy
Prescribe a low-FODMAP diet instead of a gluten-free diet as initial therapy
Screen for and replete micronutrient deficiencies and assess bone density
Start mesalamine to maintain remission as initial therapy
Begin systemic corticosteroids to induce mucosal remission as initial therapy
stages.explanation: After diagnosis, a lifelong strict gluten-free diet is the cornerstone of therapy, but malabsorption commonly causes deficiencies of iron, folate, vitamin D, calcium, and other nutrients, so screening and repletion are essential, along with DEXA assessment given osteoporosis risk. Referral to a dietitian improves adherence. Systemic corticosteroids are reserved for refractory celiac disease, not initial treatment. Mesalamine treats IBD, not celiac disease. A low-FODMAP diet helps functional symptoms but does not treat the autoimmune enteropathy; gluten elimination is required. Rifaximin treats SIBO, which is not established here. Repeat serology in 6-12 months helps confirm dietary adherence and mucosal healing.

pearls: IgA anti-tTG with a simultaneous total IgA is the first-line serologic screen for celiac disease; selective IgA deficiency causes false-negative IgA-based assays.
Confirm adult celiac disease with duodenal biopsies obtained while the patient is still eating gluten — villous atrophy, crypt hyperplasia, and intraepithelial lymphocytosis.
Dermatitis herpetiformis shows granular IgA in the dermal papillae on direct immunofluorescence and responds to a gluten-free diet plus dapsone.
Celiac disease clusters with other autoimmune conditions (type 1 diabetes, autoimmune thyroid disease) and causes refractory iron-deficiency anemia and metabolic bone disease.

cases.title: Bloating and Diarrhea After Dairy
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent bloating, cramping, and loose stools that occur after eating dairy.
cases.history: A 22-year-old man presents with 8 months of intermittent abdominal bloating, audible borborygmi, crampy lower-abdominal pain, flatulence, and loose watery stools. He has begun to notice that symptoms reliably appear 1-2 hours after consuming milk, ice cream, or large amounts of cheese, and resolve within several hours. Symptoms do not occur on days he avoids dairy. He has no weight loss, blood in the stool, nocturnal diarrhea, fevers, or joint pains. PMH is unremarkable. He takes no regular medications and has no allergies. Social history: he is a college student of East Asian descent, drinks alcohol on weekends, does not smoke, and has not traveled recently or gone camping. There are no sick contacts. Family history: his mother and older brother 'can't handle milk' and avoid dairy. He recently started drinking protein shakes made with milk to gain weight for the gym, which he feels worsened his symptoms.
cases.exam: The patient appears well and is in no distress. He is not pale or jaundiced. The abdomen is soft and mildly distended with active, hyperresonant bowel sounds. There is mild diffuse tympany to percussion but no focal tenderness, rebound, guarding, organomegaly, or palpable masses. There is no perianal disease or skin rash. The remainder of the exam, including cardiopulmonary and neurologic, is normal. Rectal exam reveals soft brown stool that is guaiac negative.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Based on the temporal relationship of his symptoms to dairy intake and his benign exam, which mechanism best explains his presentation?
options: Autoimmune destruction of small-bowel villi triggered by gluten
Deficiency of intestinal lactase causing osmotic, fermentative diarrhea
IgE-mediated allergy to cow's milk protein as the first step today
Pancreatic exocrine insufficiency causing fat malabsorption
Bile acid malabsorption as the first step today
stages.explanation: His pattern — bloating, flatulence, cramps, and osmotic diarrhea appearing 1-2 hours after dairy and resolving when dairy is avoided, in a man of East Asian descent with affected relatives — is classic for lactase deficiency (lactose intolerance). Undigested lactose draws water into the lumen (osmotic load) and is fermented by colonic bacteria into hydrogen, CO2, and short-chain fatty acids, causing gas and diarrhea. IgE-mediated milk-protein allergy causes urticaria, wheezing, or anaphylaxis, not isolated osmotic diarrhea. Celiac disease is triggered by gluten, not dairy, and typically causes weight loss/malabsorption. Pancreatic insufficiency and bile-acid malabsorption are not suggested by his focused dietary trigger or benign history.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: If objective confirmation is desired, which test is the noninvasive diagnostic study of choice?
options: 72-hour fecal fat collection
Lactose hydrogen breath test
Colonoscopy with random biopsies
Serum IgE to cow's milk
Anti-tissue transglutaminase IgA
stages.explanation: The lactose hydrogen breath test is the noninvasive test of choice: after an oral lactose load, malabsorbed lactose fermented by colonic bacteria produces a rise in breath hydrogen of ≥20 ppm over baseline, usually with reproduction of symptoms. Anti-tTG screens for celiac disease, which is not suggested here. Colonoscopy is unnecessary in a young patient with a clear dietary trigger and no alarm features. A 72-hour fecal fat study evaluates steatorrhea/fat malabsorption, not carbohydrate intolerance. Serum IgE evaluates milk-protein allergy. Often, an empiric trial of dairy elimination with symptom resolution is sufficient and avoids testing altogether.

stages.label: BREATH TEST
stages.content:
Lactose hydrogen breath test:
Baseline breath hydrogen 6 ppm
Peak breath hydrogen after 25 g lactose 48 ppm (rise of 42 ppm)
Symptoms reproduced: bloating, cramping, loose stool during test
Stool studies (sent for completeness): negative for ova/parasites, negative Giardia antigen

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His breath test confirms lactose malabsorption. What is the most appropriate first-line management?
options: Empiric metronidazole for presumed Giardia as initial therapy
Start a gluten-free diet as initial therapy
Begin loperamide scheduled daily indefinitely as initial therapy
Lifelong complete elimination of all dairy products as initial therapy
Dietary modification with attention to calcium
stages.explanation: Lactose intolerance is managed by reducing lactose load rather than total elimination — most patients tolerate small amounts, especially with meals. Options include lactose-reduced/lactose-free milk, hard aged cheeses and yogurt (lower lactose), and oral lactase enzyme supplements taken with dairy. Because dairy avoidance risks low calcium and vitamin D intake, counseling on adequate intake (or supplementation) is important, particularly in a young man building bone mass. Complete lifelong elimination is unnecessary and nutritionally suboptimal. Metronidazole is not indicated — Giardia testing was negative. A gluten-free diet treats celiac disease, not lactose intolerance. Scheduled loperamide treats symptoms but ignores the easily modifiable cause.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Six months later he returns reporting new daily watery diarrhea, a 5-kg weight loss, and nocturnal symptoms despite strict lactose avoidance. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Recommend complete elimination of all carbohydrates as the immediate next step
Prescribe chronic high-dose loperamide as the immediate next step
Reassure him that this is expected with lactose intolerance and continue current management
Double the dose of lactase supplements as the immediate next step
Reassess for an alternative or additional diagnosis with targeted testing
stages.explanation: Weight loss, nocturnal diarrhea, and symptoms that persist despite lactose avoidance are alarm features that are NOT explained by lactose intolerance, which should improve with dietary change and never causes weight loss or nighttime stools. These red flags mandate reassessment for another process — celiac disease, inflammatory bowel disease, or other malabsorptive/structural disease — with targeted serologies, inflammatory markers, and endoscopic evaluation as indicated. Reassurance, escalating lactase, eliminating all carbohydrates, or chronic antidiarrheals would inappropriately delay the diagnosis of a potentially serious condition. Recognizing when a 'benign' diagnosis no longer fits is the key teaching point.

pearls: Primary lactase deficiency is common in adults of East Asian, African, and Native American descent and presents with osmotic, fermentative diarrhea and gas after dairy.
The lactose hydrogen breath test (rise ≥20 ppm with symptom reproduction) confirms the diagnosis, but an empiric dairy-elimination trial is often sufficient.
Treatment is lactose restriction (not total elimination) plus lactase supplements; ensure adequate calcium and vitamin D intake.
Weight loss, blood in stool, or nocturnal diarrhea are alarm features inconsistent with lactose intolerance and require evaluation for celiac disease, IBD, or other pathology.

cases.title: Persistent Bloating and Fatigue After Bowel Surgery
cases.chiefComplaint: Months of worsening bloating, loose stools, and fatigue, now with tingling in her feet.
cases.history: A 54-year-old woman reports 9 months of progressive bloating, abdominal distension, flatulence, and 3-4 loose, sometimes greasy stools per day. She has lost 6 kg and feels increasingly fatigued, with new numbness and tingling in both feet over the past 2 months. PMH includes Crohn disease status post ileocecal resection 4 years ago with creation of an ileocolonic anastomosis, type 2 diabetes (with known gastroparesis), and prior cholecystectomy. Medications: metformin 1000 mg twice daily, azathioprine 100 mg daily, a daily multivitamin, and occasional loperamide. She had been in clinical remission. Allergies: sulfa (rash). Social: retired, drinks rare alcohol, never smoked. Family history: noncontributory. ROS is positive for early satiety and intermittent right-lower-quadrant fullness but negative for fevers, hematochezia, and recent travel or antibiotic use. Her diarrhea does not improve with fasting and is not clearly tied to specific foods.
cases.exam: The patient appears mildly fatigued and thin. Conjunctivae are slightly pale. The abdomen is soft and distended with active, high-pitched bowel sounds and mild tympany; a well-healed right-lower-quadrant surgical scar is present. There is no rebound, guarding, organomegaly, or palpable mass, and no perianal disease. Neurologic exam reveals diminished vibratory sensation and proprioception at both great toes with mildly reduced ankle reflexes; strength is preserved. There is no glossitis or jaundice. Stool is guaiac negative.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Considering her altered post-surgical anatomy, gastroparesis, bloating, malabsorptive stools, and signs suggesting B12 deficiency, which diagnosis best unifies her presentation?
options: Small intestinal bacterial overgrowth
Celiac disease as the first step today
Recurrence of active Crohn disease at the anastomosis
Microscopic colitis as the first step today
Pancreatic exocrine insufficiency
stages.explanation: She has multiple risk factors for SIBO: an ileocecal resection with loss of the ileocecal valve (allowing colonic bacteria to reflux into the small bowel), surgically altered anatomy creating potential stasis, and diabetic gastroparesis with dysmotility. SIBO produces bloating, flatulence, malabsorptive diarrhea, weight loss, and characteristic deficiency of vitamin B12 (consumed by bacteria) with neurologic signs. Crohn recurrence is possible and must be excluded but typically causes inflammatory rather than purely malabsorptive symptoms and would not explain the B12 picture as neatly. Celiac disease is less likely given anatomy and prior remission. Pancreatic insufficiency is possible but less unifying. Microscopic colitis causes watery, nonmalabsorptive diarrhea without B12 deficiency.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 11.0 g/dL
MCV 104 fL
Vitamin B12 142 pg/mL (low)
Methylmalonic acid elevated
Folate 18 ng/mL (high-normal)
Ferritin 60 ng/mL
tTG-IgA 3 U/mL (negative), total IgA normal
CRP 4 mg/L
Fecal calprotectin 60 µg/g (mildly elevated)
Albumin 3.6 g/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Her labs show macrocytosis, low B12 with elevated methylmalonic acid, and high-normal folate. Which test is the most appropriate next step to confirm the suspected diagnosis noninvasively?
options: Glucose or lactulose hydrogen/methane breath test
Empiric lifelong vitamin B12 injections without further workup
CT enterography as the primary diagnosis
Repeat colonoscopy with biopsies of the anastomosis
Schilling test as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: The pattern of low B12 with HIGH folate is a classic clue to SIBO: luminal bacteria consume B12 but synthesize folate, raising serum folate while depleting B12. A glucose or lactulose hydrogen/methane breath test is the recommended noninvasive confirmatory study; an early rise in hydrogen or methane after the substrate supports bacterial overgrowth. Repeat colonoscopy evaluates for Crohn recurrence (worth considering given mildly elevated calprotectin) but does not diagnose SIBO. The Schilling test is largely obsolete and unavailable. Empiric B12 injections treat the deficiency but ignore the underlying cause. CT enterography assesses structural disease/strictures but is not the first confirmatory test for SIBO. Jejunal aspirate culture is the gold standard but invasive and rarely performed.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: Which feature of her surgical history most directly predisposes her to small intestinal bacterial overgrowth?
options: Prior small-bowel inflammation that has resolved
Loperamide use as the immediate next step
Loss of the ileocecal valve from ileocecal resection
Removal of the gallbladder before discharge planning
Use of azathioprine as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: The ileocecal valve normally acts as a barrier preventing the heavily colonized colonic contents from refluxing into the relatively sterile small bowel. Resection removing this valve (as in her ileocecal resection) is a major anatomic risk factor for SIBO, compounded by her diabetic gastroparesis-related dysmotility (stasis allows bacterial proliferation). Cholecystectomy does not predispose to SIBO. Azathioprine is an immunosuppressant relevant to Crohn control but is not a direct cause of overgrowth. Resolved prior inflammation is not itself a driver. Loperamide slows motility and could theoretically contribute but is occasional here and far less important than the loss of the valve and underlying dysmotility.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Breath testing supports SIBO and colonoscopy shows no active Crohn disease. What is the most appropriate management plan?
options: Strict gluten-free diet as initial therapy
Total parenteral nutrition as first-line therapy as initial therapy
Course of oral antibiotics , repletion of vitamin B12,
High-dose systemic corticosteroids for presumed Crohn flare as initial therapy
Long-term daily loperamide alone as initial therapy
stages.explanation: SIBO management combines a course of oral antibiotics to reduce bacterial load (rifaximin is commonly used; other broad-spectrum agents are alternatives), correction of nutritional deficiencies — here parenteral or high-dose vitamin B12 — and, importantly, addressing the underlying predisposing factors such as optimizing diabetic gastroparesis/dysmotility and recognizing the anatomic loss of the ileocecal valve. Relapse is common and may require repeated or rotating antibiotic courses. Systemic corticosteroids are inappropriate since active Crohn was excluded. A gluten-free diet treats celiac disease, which is excluded by negative serology. TPN is far too aggressive for a patient who can be managed with antibiotics and oral/parenteral repletion. Loperamide alone treats a symptom while ignoring the cause and worsens stasis.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After her antibiotic course she improves but symptoms recur 3 months later. Which long-term strategy is most appropriate?
options: Stop all monitoring since SIBO resolved once with appropriate follow-up
Indefinite continuous broad-spectrum antibiotics at full dose with appropriate follow-up
Address ongoing risk factors
Permanent total parenteral nutrition with appropriate follow-up
Refer for total small-bowel resection with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: SIBO frequently recurs, particularly when the predisposing anatomy (absent ileocecal valve) and dysmotility (diabetic gastroparesis) cannot be reversed. Rational long-term management targets those risk factors — for example a prokinetic to improve motility and ongoing surveillance/repletion of B12 and other nutrients — and treats relapses with intermittent or cyclic antibiotic courses rather than indefinite continuous full-dose antibiotics, which promote resistance and adverse effects. Total small-bowel resection is not a treatment for SIBO and would cause short-bowel syndrome. Permanent TPN is unwarranted. Discontinuing monitoring ignores the high recurrence rate and ongoing deficiency risk.

pearls: Loss of the ileocecal valve, surgically altered anatomy, and dysmotility (e.g., diabetic gastroparesis) are major risk factors for SIBO.
Low vitamin B12 with HIGH or high-normal folate is a classic biochemical clue — luminal bacteria consume B12 but synthesize folate.
Glucose or lactulose hydrogen/methane breath testing is the practical noninvasive diagnostic; jejunal aspirate culture is the gold standard but rarely done.
Treat with antibiotics (e.g., rifaximin) plus nutrient repletion, and address the underlying cause; recurrence is common and may need cyclic therapy and prokinetics.

cases.title: Recurrent Abdominal Pain and Changing Bowel Habits
cases.chiefComplaint: More than six months of crampy lower abdominal pain that improves after a bowel movement.
cases.history: A 29-year-old woman presents with about 8 months of recurrent crampy lower-abdominal pain occurring at least weekly. The pain is relieved by defecation and is associated with a change in stool frequency and form — she alternates between days of looser, more frequent stools and days of harder, less frequent stools. She notes bloating and a sensation of incomplete evacuation. Symptoms worsen during stressful periods at work and around her menstrual cycle. She denies weight loss, blood in the stool, nocturnal symptoms waking her from sleep, fevers, or a family history of GI cancer or IBD. PMH includes anxiety and migraine. Medications: an oral contraceptive and as-needed sumatriptan; no recent antibiotics. Allergies: none. Social: works as an attorney, exercises regularly, drinks coffee daily and alcohol occasionally, never smoked, no recent travel or camping. Diet is varied. Family history: mother with hypothyroidism, no colorectal cancer or celiac disease. She is frustrated and worried something serious is being missed.
cases.exam: The patient appears well and is in no acute distress. She is not pale or jaundiced. The abdomen is soft and nondistended with normoactive bowel sounds; there is mild, nonlocalized lower-abdominal tenderness to deep palpation without rebound or guarding. No organomegaly, masses, or hernias are appreciated. There is no perianal disease, fissure, or external hemorrhoid. Rectal exam reveals normal tone and brown, guaiac-negative stool. The rest of the physical examination is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which set of clinical criteria is most appropriate to apply when evaluating this patient's recurrent abdominal pain and altered bowel habits?
options: Amsterdam II criteria as the first step today
Truelove and Witts criteria
Rome IV criteria for irritable bowel syndrome
Rovsing criteria as the first step today
Light's criteria as the first step today
stages.explanation: The Rome IV criteria define irritable bowel syndrome as recurrent abdominal pain at least 1 day per week on average over the prior 3 months (with symptom onset ≥6 months earlier) associated with two or more of: relation to defecation, change in stool frequency, or change in stool form. This patient fits well. Light's criteria differentiate exudative from transudative pleural effusions. Amsterdam criteria identify families at risk for Lynch syndrome. 'Rovsing sign' relates to appendicitis, not a diagnostic criteria set. Truelove and Witts criteria grade severity of ulcerative colitis flares. Recognizing IBS as a positive symptom-based diagnosis (not purely one of exclusion) is key.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: She meets symptom criteria and has no alarm features. Which limited workup is most appropriate before making a positive diagnosis of IBS?
options: A limited evaluation: CBC,
No testing of any kind is ever warranted as the immediate next step
CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast as the immediate next step
Immediate colonoscopy for all patients with these symptoms as the immediate next step
Empiric 2-week course of broad-spectrum antibiotics first as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: In a young patient meeting Rome IV criteria without alarm features, guidelines support a positive diagnosis with only limited testing to exclude mimics: a CBC to detect anemia and celiac serology (anti-tTG IgA), and in diarrhea-predominant or mixed patients, fecal calprotectin and/or CRP to screen for inflammatory bowel disease. Routine colonoscopy in everyone is unnecessary and low yield absent alarm features or age-based screening indications. CT imaging is not indicated for this presentation. Doing no testing at all misses easily checked mimics like celiac disease and anemia. Empiric antibiotics are not appropriate initial management for suspected IBS.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 13.6 g/dL
MCV 88 fL
CRP <1 mg/L
tTG-IgA negative, total IgA normal
TSH 2.4 µIU/mL
Fecal calprotectin 22 µg/g (normal)
Stool occult blood: negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Her limited workup is normal. Which feature most argues AGAINST an organic disease and supports a functional diagnosis of IBS?
options: Family history of colorectal cancer at age 45 in this clinical context
Pain relieved by defecation with no weight loss, no rectal bleeding,
Awakening from sleep with diarrhea as the primary diagnosis
Microcytic anemia as the primary diagnosis
Unintentional 10-kg weight loss as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Reassuring features that support IBS and argue against organic disease include pain relieved by defecation, a normal exam and labs, and the ABSENCE of alarm features — no weight loss, no rectal bleeding, no nocturnal/sleep-disrupting symptoms, and no concerning family history. Microcytic anemia, nocturnal diarrhea, significant unintentional weight loss, and a family history of early colorectal cancer are all RED FLAGS that should prompt further structural evaluation (e.g., colonoscopy) rather than reassurance. This patient has none of those, so a confident positive diagnosis of IBS can be made.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: You diagnose IBS with mixed bowel habits. What is the most appropriate initial management approach?
options: Start long-term opioids for pain control as initial therapy
Recommend total colectomy to definitively cure symptoms as initial therapy
Begin chronic systemic corticosteroids as initial therapy
Establish a therapeutic relationship with education/reassurance, dietary modification ,
Schedule annual surveillance colonoscopy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: First-line IBS management emphasizes a strong, reassuring therapeutic relationship and patient education about the disorder's benign nature, alongside lifestyle and dietary measures — soluble fiber and a trial of a low-FODMAP diet are reasonable — and symptom-targeted pharmacotherapy (antispasmodics for pain, agents directed at the predominant bowel pattern, and consideration of gut-directed neuromodulators or psychological therapies given her stress and anxiety triggers). Long-term opioids are contraindicated and can cause narcotic bowel syndrome. Systemic corticosteroids have no role in functional disease. Surveillance colonoscopy is not indicated absent alarm features. Colectomy is never appropriate for IBS and would not relieve a functional disorder.

pearls: IBS is a positive, symptom-based diagnosis using the Rome IV criteria: recurrent abdominal pain ≥1 day/week related to defecation or a change in stool frequency or form.
In young patients without alarm features, limited testing (CBC, celiac serology, and fecal calprotectin/CRP if diarrhea-predominant) suffices — routine colonoscopy is not required.
Alarm features (weight loss, rectal bleeding, nocturnal symptoms, anemia, older age, or family history of CRC/IBD) mandate structural evaluation.
Initial treatment centers on education/reassurance, dietary measures (soluble fiber, low-FODMAP), and symptom-targeted therapy; avoid opioids.

cases.title: Weight Loss, Migratory Joint Pains, and Diarrhea
cases.chiefComplaint: A year of progressive weight loss, diarrhea, and migrating joint pains.
cases.history: A 50-year-old man presents with roughly 12 months of fatigue, intermittent low-grade fevers, chronic non-bloody diarrhea, and a 12-kg unintentional weight loss. For 2-3 years preceding the GI symptoms he had migratory arthralgias affecting the knees, wrists, and ankles, attributed to 'seronegative arthritis.' More recently he notes abdominal bloating and steatorrhea. He also reports mild cognitive fogginess and intermittent abdominal pain. PMH: the longstanding joint complaints; otherwise healthy. Medications: occasional NSAIDs; no immunosuppressants. Allergies: none. Social: he is a Caucasian farmer in the midwestern United States with extensive soil and livestock exposure, drinks alcohol occasionally, and has never smoked; no foreign travel. Family history: noncontributory. ROS is positive for low-grade fevers, joint pains, and new generalized hyperpigmentation; negative for rash, oral ulcers, hematochezia, and recent antibiotic use. He has lost muscle mass and feels weak.
cases.exam: The patient is cachectic with diffuse skin hyperpigmentation. There is mild generalized lymphadenopathy. The abdomen is soft with mild diffuse tenderness, active bowel sounds, no rebound, and no hepatosplenomegaly. Cardiac exam reveals a soft systolic murmur. Joints are without active synovitis at present, though he has limited, mildly tender wrists. Neurologic exam shows mild gait unsteadiness and slowed cognition; cranial nerves are grossly intact, but the examiner notes subtle abnormal eye movements. There is no jaundice or peripheral edema beyond mild ankle puffiness. Stool is guaiac negative and appears greasy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A middle-aged man with years of migratory arthralgias preceding chronic malabsorptive diarrhea, weight loss, fever, lymphadenopathy, and neurologic/cardiac involvement most likely has which diagnosis?
options: Crohn disease in this clinical context
Celiac disease
Whipple disease
Tropical sprue
Intestinal tuberculosis
stages.explanation: This multisystem illness — chronic arthralgias that PRECEDE GI symptoms by years, malabsorptive diarrhea, weight loss, fever, lymphadenopathy, plus cardiac and neurologic involvement (cognitive change, gait/eye-movement abnormalities) — in a middle-aged Caucasian man with soil/animal exposure is classic for Whipple disease, caused by Tropheryma whipplei. Celiac disease lacks the prominent arthralgia-first course, fever, lymphadenopathy, and CNS/cardiac features. Crohn disease can cause arthralgias and diarrhea but not this systemic, slowly progressive picture with CNS involvement. Intestinal TB is plausible but he has no exposure/travel history and the chronic migratory arthralgia-first pattern points elsewhere. Tropical sprue requires residence/travel in the tropics, which he lacks.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which test is most likely to establish the diagnosis?
options: Stool ova and parasite examination as the primary diagnosis
Colonoscopy with terminal ileal biopsy showing noncaseating granulomas in this clinical context
Upper endoscopy with small-bowel biopsy showing PAS-positive, diastase-resistant macrophages
Anti-tissue transglutaminase IgA as the primary diagnosis
Serum anti-Saccharomyces cerevisiae antibody (ASCA) as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Whipple disease is confirmed by small-bowel (duodenal/jejunal) biopsy showing the lamina propria infiltrated by foamy macrophages containing periodic acid-Schiff (PAS)-positive, diastase-resistant material representing Tropheryma whipplei; PCR for T. whipplei can support the diagnosis. Anti-tTG screens for celiac disease. Noncaseating granulomas on ileal biopsy suggest Crohn disease. Stool ova and parasites would identify giardiasis or other parasites, not Whipple. ASCA is associated with Crohn disease, not Whipple. The PAS-positive macrophage finding is the high-yield, classic histologic hallmark.

stages.label: LABS & BIOPSY
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL
MCV 84 fL
Albumin 2.6 g/dL
CRP 48 mg/L
ESR 60 mm/hr
tTG-IgA negative
Duodenal biopsy: lamina propria filled with foamy macrophages containing PAS-positive, diastase-resistant granules; villous blunting
Tissue PCR positive for Tropheryma whipplei
CSF PCR (sent given CNS signs): positive for T. whipplei

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Biopsy confirms Whipple disease with positive CSF PCR. What is the most appropriate antibiotic management?
options: No antibiotics; treat with corticosteroids alone as initial therapy
Oral vancomycin for 14 days as initial therapy
A single dose of azithromycin as initial therapy
Induction with an intravenous agent that crosses the blood-brain barrier , followed by prolonged oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for about a year
A short 7-day course of oral metronidazole as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Whipple disease requires prolonged antibiotic therapy. Because of frequent and serious CNS involvement (confirmed here by positive CSF PCR), induction uses a parenteral agent that penetrates the blood-brain barrier — classically intravenous ceftriaxone (or penicillin G) for about 2 weeks — followed by prolonged oral maintenance, typically trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for approximately 1 year, to prevent relapse. Short courses of metronidazole, a single azithromycin dose, or 14 days of oral vancomycin are all inadequate and do not reliably treat CNS disease. Corticosteroids alone do not treat the infection; relapse, especially neurologic, is common with inadequate therapy. Clinicians should also watch for immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome after starting treatment.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Untreated or inadequately treated Whipple disease most characteristically relapses in which site, carrying the worst prognosis?
options: The skin as the leading concern
The central nervous system
The thyroid gland
The bone marrow
The kidneys as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Central nervous system relapse is the most feared and prognostically grave complication of Whipple disease; it may present with cognitive decline, ophthalmoplegia, myoclonus, and the highly specific (though rare) oculomasticatory myorhythmia. CNS relapse can occur even after apparent GI response, which is why regimens must include a CNS-penetrating induction agent and prolonged maintenance, and why CSF PCR is checked. The skin, kidneys, thyroid, and bone marrow are not the characteristic sites of devastating relapse. The cardiac valves (endocarditis) are another important site of involvement, but neurologic relapse carries the worst prognosis.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Two weeks after starting therapy, he develops new high fevers, worsening arthralgias, and an inflammatory flare despite a fall in bacterial burden. What is the most likely explanation?
options: Antibiotic treatment failure requiring an entirely different drug class
A new superimposed bacterial gastroenteritis as the immediate next step
Progression to lymphoma as the immediate next step
Drug-induced lupus from trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole in this setting
Immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome as the immune system recovers
stages.explanation: After effective antibiotics reduce the Tropheryma whipplei burden, a subset of patients develop immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS) — paradoxical recurrence of fever, arthralgias, and inflammation as the recovering immune system reacts, typically within days to weeks of starting treatment. It is managed supportively, often with corticosteroids, while CONTINUING the antibiotics rather than abandoning them. True treatment failure is less likely this early and would warrant reassessment, not an automatic class switch. A new bacterial gastroenteritis would not explain the systemic inflammatory flare with arthralgias. Drug-induced lupus has a different, more delayed serologic picture. Whipple disease itself is not a lymphoma, and progression to lymphoma would not occur in this timeframe.

pearls: Suspect Whipple disease in a middle-aged Caucasian man with migratory arthralgias preceding chronic diarrhea, weight loss, fever, lymphadenopathy, and CNS/cardiac involvement.
Diagnosis rests on small-bowel biopsy showing PAS-positive, diastase-resistant foamy macrophages; confirm with T. whipplei PCR.
Treat with a CNS-penetrating induction antibiotic (e.g., IV ceftriaxone) followed by ~1 year of oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole.
CNS relapse is the most feared complication; oculomasticatory myorhythmia is pathognomonic but rare.

cases.title: Chronic Diarrhea and Anemia After a Year Abroad
cases.chiefComplaint: Months of persistent diarrhea, weight loss, and fatigue that began while living overseas.
cases.history: A 43-year-old man presents with about 5 months of chronic, watery-to-greasy diarrhea, abdominal bloating, glossitis, and a 9-kg weight loss. Symptoms began roughly 2 months into a prolonged stay in rural South Asia, where he worked on an agricultural development project for nearly a year, and have persisted since his return 6 weeks ago. He describes pale, bulky, foul-smelling stools and increasing fatigue and breathlessness on exertion. He denies bloody stools, high fevers, or nocturnal pain. PMH is unremarkable. Medications: a daily multivitamin; he took no malaria prophylaxis consistently. Allergies: none. Social: married, works in international development, drinks alcohol rarely, never smoked. He ate extensively from local food and water sources abroad. Family history: noncontributory. ROS notable for a sore, smooth tongue, mild paresthesias, and exertional dyspnea; negative for rash, arthralgias, and recent antibiotic use. A single stool study abroad for ova and parasites was reportedly negative.
cases.exam: The patient appears thin and pale. The tongue is smooth, erythematous, and atrophic (glossitis), and there is angular cheilitis. Conjunctivae are pale. The abdomen is soft, mildly distended, with active bowel sounds and diffuse mild tenderness; no organomegaly or masses. There is mild pedal edema. Cardiac exam reveals a soft flow murmur and tachycardia. Neurologic exam shows mildly diminished vibratory sensation in the toes but preserved strength and reflexes. Stool is guaiac negative and appears pale and greasy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A traveler returning from rural South Asia with months of malabsorptive diarrhea, weight loss, glossitis, and signs of macrocytic anemia most likely has which diagnosis?
options: Celiac disease
Giardiasis as the first step today
Whipple disease
Acute viral gastroenteritis
Tropical sprue
stages.explanation: Tropical sprue is a malabsorptive disorder affecting residents of or travelers to tropical regions (notably South and Southeast Asia, the Caribbean), presenting with chronic diarrhea, steatorrhea, weight loss, and deficiencies of folate and vitamin B12 producing a megaloblastic (macrocytic) anemia with glossitis. The temporal link to prolonged tropical residence and persistence after return fits well. Giardiasis causes malabsorptive diarrhea but typically does not produce megaloblastic anemia and is usually identified on stool antigen. Celiac disease is gluten-driven and not tied to tropical exposure. Whipple disease features arthralgias, fever, and CNS findings. Acute viral gastroenteritis is self-limited over days, not months.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 9.6 g/dL
MCV 112 fL (macrocytic)
Peripheral smear: macro-ovalocytes and hypersegmented neutrophils
Folate 1.8 ng/mL (low)
Vitamin B12 160 pg/mL (low)
Albumin 3.0 g/dL
tTG-IgA negative, total IgA normal
Stool: positive for fat (Sudan stain); ova and parasites negative x3; Giardia antigen negative
Fecal calprotectin normal

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Stool studies are repeatedly negative for pathogens and celiac serology is negative. Which finding on small-bowel biopsy would best support tropical sprue and help distinguish it from celiac disease?
options: Partial villous blunting with chronic inflammatory cells
Trophozoites adherent to the mucosal surface as the primary diagnosis
Total (subtotal) villous atrophy strictly limited to the proximal duodenum with intraepithelial lymphocytosis that resolves on a gluten-free diet
PAS-positive macrophages in the lamina propria as the primary diagnosis
Noncaseating granulomas as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Tropical sprue causes partial villous blunting with crypt hyperplasia and chronic inflammatory infiltrate (including eosinophils) that characteristically affects the DISTAL small bowel (ileum) as well as proximal segments — reflecting diffuse involvement — and is associated with B12 and folate deficiency. Celiac disease classically produces more proximal (duodenal) villous atrophy with prominent intraepithelial lymphocytosis that responds to gluten withdrawal; negative serology here argues against celiac disease. PAS-positive macrophages indicate Whipple disease. Noncaseating granulomas suggest Crohn disease. Adherent trophozoites indicate giardiasis. The diffuse small-bowel involvement and megaloblastic deficiencies favor tropical sprue.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: You diagnose tropical sprue. What is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Corticosteroids alone as initial therapy
Prolonged antibiotics PLUS folic acid
A gluten-free diet alone as initial therapy
Metronidazole for 7 days only as initial therapy
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for 1 year as in Whipple disease as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Tropical sprue is treated with a prolonged course of antibiotics — typically tetracycline or doxycycline for up to 3-6 months — combined with repletion of folate and vitamin B12, which both treats the presumed bacterial/postinfectious component and corrects the megaloblastic anemia and malabsorption. Folate repletion alone often produces a rapid clinical and hematologic response, but antibiotics are needed to address the underlying disorder and prevent relapse. A gluten-free diet treats celiac disease, not tropical sprue. A 7-day metronidazole course treats giardiasis. The year-long TMP-SMX regimen is for Whipple disease. Corticosteroids are not the treatment for tropical sprue.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: Which combination of vitamin deficiencies is most characteristic of tropical sprue and explains this patient's megaloblastic anemia and glossitis?
options: Iron and vitamin B6
Vitamin C and vitamin K
Folate and vitamin B12
Vitamin D and calcium only
Thiamine and niacin
stages.explanation: Tropical sprue characteristically causes malabsorption of folate and, with more chronic/distal small-bowel involvement, vitamin B12. Deficiency of these two vitamins produces megaloblastic (macrocytic) anemia with macro-ovalocytes, hypersegmented neutrophils, and glossitis — exactly this patient's picture. Folate is absorbed proximally and is often deficient early; B12 (absorbed in the terminal ileum) becomes deficient as the disease involves the distal bowel, distinguishing it from purely proximal disorders. Vitamin C/K, thiamine/niacin, iron/B6, and vitamin D/calcium deficiencies do not produce the classic megaloblastic anemia with glossitis seen here, although fat-soluble vitamin and other deficiencies can coexist with generalized malabsorption.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Before committing to a months-long course of treatment, which feature most reliably distinguishes tropical sprue from celiac disease in this patient?
options: Improvement of symptoms when gluten is removed from the diet as initial therapy
A positive response to oral iron as initial therapy
The presence of any villous blunting on biopsy as initial therapy
The presence of diarrhea and weight loss as initial therapy
Negative celiac serology with relevant tropical exposure
stages.explanation: Both disorders cause malabsorption with villous changes, weight loss, and diarrhea, so those overlapping features (villous blunting, diarrhea/weight loss) do NOT distinguish them. What favors tropical sprue here is the combination of negative celiac serology with a normal total IgA, a clear epidemiologic exposure (prolonged tropical residence), megaloblastic anemia from combined folate and B12 deficiency, and biopsy involvement extending into the DISTAL small bowel (ileum) — whereas celiac disease is more proximal and gluten-responsive. Symptom improvement with gluten withdrawal points to celiac disease, not tropical sprue. A response to oral iron is nonspecific. Confirming the distinction matters because treatment differs: prolonged antibiotics plus folate/B12 for sprue versus a gluten-free diet for celiac disease.

pearls: Suspect tropical sprue in residents of or travelers to the tropics with chronic malabsorptive diarrhea, weight loss, and megaloblastic anemia after infectious causes are excluded.
Folate and vitamin B12 deficiency (megaloblastic anemia, glossitis) is characteristic; biopsy shows villous blunting affecting distal as well as proximal small bowel.
Treat with prolonged tetracycline/doxycycline (months) plus folate and B12 repletion.
Unlike celiac disease, it is not gluten-driven, has negative celiac serology, and tends to involve the ileum, reflecting diffuse small-bowel disease.

cases.title: Sudden Vomiting and Diarrhea on a Cruise
cases.chiefComplaint: Abrupt onset of vomiting and watery diarrhea that started two days into a cruise.
cases.history: A 26-year-old woman is seen the day after disembarking from a 5-day cruise. About 36 hours into the trip she developed abrupt nausea, repeated forceful (she describes it as 'explosive') vomiting, watery non-bloody diarrhea, low-grade fever, diffuse crampy abdominal pain, and myalgias. Many other passengers and several crew members fell ill around the same time. Her symptoms have already begun to improve over the past 12 hours, though she still feels weak and has had reduced oral intake. She denies blood or mucus in the stool, high fevers, recent antibiotic use, or travel outside the cruise. PMH: none. Medications: oral contraceptive only. Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker, occasional alcohol, no camping or untreated water exposure. Family history: noncontributory. ROS: positive for thirst and lightheadedness on standing; negative for severe persistent diarrhea, neurologic symptoms, or urinary complaints. She is otherwise healthy and not pregnant.
cases.exam: The patient appears mildly fatigued with slightly dry mucous membranes. There is mild orthostatic change in heart rate and blood pressure. The abdomen is soft with mildly hyperactive bowel sounds and diffuse mild tenderness without rebound, guarding, organomegaly, or masses. There is no skin rash, no meningismus, and no focal neurologic deficit. Capillary refill is just under 2 seconds and skin turgor is mildly decreased. Rectal exam, when performed, shows soft brown guaiac-negative stool. The rest of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the explosive vomiting and watery diarrhea, the short incubation, and a clear outbreak affecting many people on a cruise ship, which pathogen is the most likely cause?
options: Giardia lamblia
Entamoeba histolytica
Vibrio cholerae
Shigella dysenteriae
Norovirus
stages.explanation: Norovirus is the classic cause of explosive, predominantly vomiting-and-watery-diarrhea outbreaks in closed/semi-closed settings such as cruise ships, dormitories, and nursing homes. It is highly contagious, has a short incubation (~12-48 hours), and causes a brief, self-limited illness with prominent vomiting, myalgias, and low-grade fever. Shigella and Entamoeba cause dysentery (bloody, mucoid stools with tenesmus), which she lacks. Giardia causes more subacute/chronic malabsorptive diarrhea after freshwater exposure, not an explosive ship-wide outbreak. Cholera causes profuse 'rice-water' watery diarrhea, mainly in endemic regions with contaminated water, not a typical cruise vomiting outbreak in this setting.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate diagnostic approach for this otherwise healthy young woman?
options: Clinical diagnosis based on the syndrome
CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast as the primary diagnosis
Stool ova and parasite exam plus Giardia antigen as the first step
Immediate colonoscopy with biopsies as the primary diagnosis
Blood cultures and empiric IV antibiotics as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Acute, self-limited viral gastroenteritis in a healthy adult is a clinical diagnosis grounded in the characteristic syndrome and epidemiology (an outbreak on a cruise). Specific pathogen testing (e.g., norovirus PCR) is used mainly for public health/outbreak investigation rather than routine individual care, and is not needed to manage this patient. Colonoscopy and CT imaging are unnecessary and not indicated for uncomplicated watery diarrhea without alarm features. Blood cultures and empiric antibiotics are inappropriate for a viral illness. Stool O&P and Giardia testing would be pursued for persistent/chronic diarrhea or relevant exposures, not for a brief explosive outbreak illness already improving.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She is mildly dehydrated but tolerating sips. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Empiric metronidazole as initial therapy
Loperamide plus broad-spectrum antibiotics for all viral gastroenteritis
Immediate hospital admission for total parenteral nutrition as monotherapy
Oral rehydration with an oral rehydration solution
Empiric ciprofloxacin as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Treatment of viral gastroenteritis is supportive: oral rehydration with an oral rehydration solution (glucose-electrolyte) is first-line for mild-to-moderate dehydration, advancing diet as tolerated, with antiemetics (e.g., ondansetron) to facilitate oral intake. Antibiotics — ciprofloxacin or metronidazole — are not indicated for a viral illness and would not help. Routine loperamide-plus-antibiotics is inappropriate; antimotility agents are generally avoided when an invasive/inflammatory process (bloody diarrhea, high fever) is suspected, though this watery, afebrile-to-low-grade illness is low risk. IV fluids and admission are reserved for severe dehydration or inability to tolerate oral intake, which she does not have.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Before discharge, what is the most important public health and counseling measure to reduce transmission of this organism?
options: No precautions are needed since she is improving with appropriate follow-up
Routine alcohol-based hand sanitizer alone is fully sufficient with appropriate follow-up
Prophylactic antibiotics for all close contacts with appropriate follow-up
Strict hand hygiene with soap
Administer the norovirus vaccine to her contacts with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Norovirus is extremely contagious and spreads via the fecal-oral route, contaminated surfaces, and food handled by infected persons. Key measures are meticulous hand hygiene with soap and water, thorough environmental disinfection (the virus is relatively resistant and a chlorine-based agent is preferred), and avoiding preparing food for others while symptomatic and for at least 48 hours after symptoms resolve. Alcohol-based sanitizers are less effective against norovirus than soap-and-water washing, so they are not fully sufficient. Prophylactic antibiotics do nothing for a virus and are inappropriate. Saying no precautions are needed ignores ongoing shedding. There is no licensed norovirus vaccine currently available for routine use.

pearls: Norovirus causes explosive vomiting and watery diarrhea in outbreaks on cruise ships and other closed settings, with a short incubation and brief self-limited course.
Diagnosis is clinical; pathogen testing is mainly for outbreak/public-health purposes, not routine individual care.
Management is supportive — oral rehydration solution, antiemetics as needed; antibiotics are not indicated.
Prevent spread with soap-and-water hand washing (more effective than alcohol gel against norovirus), surface disinfection, and no food handling until 48 hours after symptoms resolve.

cases.title: Greasy, Foul-Smelling Diarrhea After a Camping Trip
cases.chiefComplaint: Two weeks of foul-smelling, greasy diarrhea, bloating, and gas after a backcountry camping trip.
cases.history: A 35-year-old man presents with about 2 weeks of malodorous, greasy, loose stools that float, along with marked bloating, sulfurous belching, abdominal cramps, and excessive flatulence. Symptoms began roughly a week after a 5-day backcountry backpacking trip during which he drank from a mountain stream using only a basic, possibly inadequate filter. He has had mild nausea and a few kilograms of weight loss but no blood in the stool, no high fevers, and no nocturnal pain. PMH is unremarkable. Medications: none regularly; he tried over-the-counter loperamide with only partial relief. Allergies: none. Social: avid hiker, nonsmoker, occasional alcohol, monogamous relationship. Two friends from the same trip have similar but milder symptoms. Family history: noncontributory. ROS positive for bloating, belching, and intermittent watery-to-greasy diarrhea; negative for dysentery, rash, arthralgias, or recent foreign travel beyond the camping trip.
cases.exam: The patient appears well and is not dehydrated. The abdomen is soft and mildly distended with active bowel sounds and diffuse mild tympany; there is mild generalized tenderness but no rebound, guarding, organomegaly, or masses. There is no rash, jaundice, or lymphadenopathy. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable. Rectal exam reveals soft, greasy-appearing brown stool that is guaiac negative.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given foul-smelling, greasy, malabsorptive diarrhea with bloating and belching after drinking from a mountain stream, which organism is most likely responsible?
options: Giardia lamblia
Norovirus as the first step today
Shigella sonnei
Campylobacter jejuni
Enterotoxigenic E. coli
stages.explanation: Giardia lamblia is the classic cause of greasy, foul-smelling (steatorrheic), non-bloody diarrhea with prominent bloating, sulfurous 'rotten-egg' belching, and flatulence, often after drinking untreated fresh/stream water while hiking or camping ('beaver fever'). The subacute course over 1-2 weeks and similarly affected trip companions fit well. Norovirus causes acute, brief, explosive vomiting/diarrhea outbreaks, not 2 weeks of malabsorptive stools. Shigella and Campylobacter cause inflammatory, often bloody dysentery with fever. Enterotoxigenic E. coli causes traveler's diarrhea that is typically watery and self-limited over a few days, without the prominent malabsorptive/steatorrheic and chronic bloating picture.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the preferred first-line test to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Colonoscopy with biopsy as the primary diagnosis
Serum anti-Giardia IgG titers as the primary diagnosis
Hydrogen breath test as the primary diagnosis
Stool Giardia antigen or stool nucleic acid amplification test
Single stool ova-and-parasite microscopy is the most sensitive single test
stages.explanation: Stool antigen testing (enzyme immunoassay) and stool nucleic acid amplification tests (PCR/multiplex GI panels) are the preferred, most sensitive first-line tests for Giardia and have largely supplanted microscopy. A single stool O&P exam is relatively insensitive because of intermittent cyst shedding (sensitivity improves with three specimens), so it is not the best single test. Serum IgG titers cannot distinguish current from past infection and are not used for diagnosis. Colonoscopy is unnecessary for this clinical picture. A hydrogen breath test evaluates carbohydrate malabsorption (lactose intolerance) or SIBO, not Giardia.

stages.label: STOOL STUDIES
stages.content:
Stool Giardia antigen EIA: POSITIVE
Stool multiplex PCR: Giardia lamblia detected; no bacterial pathogens, no C. difficile
Stool occult blood: negative
Fecal leukocytes: none
No other ova or parasites identified

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The Giardia antigen is positive. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Oral tinidazole or a course of metronidazole
Azithromycin for 5 days as initial therapy
Supportive care only with no antiparasitic therapy
Albendazole plus ivermectin as initial therapy
Oral vancomycin as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Symptomatic giardiasis is treated with a nitroimidazole antiparasitic: tinidazole as a single dose (highly effective and convenient) or a several-day course of metronidazole; nitazoxanide is another effective option. Oral vancomycin treats Clostridioides difficile colitis, not Giardia. Azithromycin is used for Campylobacter or as traveler's-diarrhea therapy in some settings, not first-line for Giardia. Supportive care alone is reasonable only for asymptomatic carriers in some contexts, but this patient is clearly symptomatic with malabsorption and warrants treatment. Albendazole has some antigiardial activity but the albendazole-plus-ivermectin combination is not standard first-line therapy for Giardia.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What counseling is most appropriate to prevent reinfection on future trips?
options: Take prophylactic metronidazole before every camping trip based on current risk
Drink only carbonated stream water with appropriate follow-up
No precautions are necessary once treated with appropriate follow-up
Boil, filter , or chemically treat backcountry water
Use alcohol hand sanitizer as the sole protective measure based on current risk
stages.explanation: Giardia cysts are transmitted via contaminated fresh water and the fecal-oral route. Prevention centers on treating backcountry water — boiling is most reliable, with filtration (using a filter rated to remove Giardia cysts) and chemical disinfection (iodine/chlorine dioxide, with adequate contact time) as alternatives — plus good hand hygiene, especially around food. Prophylactic antibiotics before trips are not recommended and promote resistance/side effects. 'Carbonating' stream water does not disinfect it. Assuming no precautions are needed risks reinfection. Alcohol-based sanitizer alone is insufficient against Giardia cysts and is not a substitute for water treatment and hand washing.

pearls: Giardiasis causes greasy, foul-smelling, non-bloody diarrhea with bloating and sulfurous belching, classically after drinking untreated stream water while hiking/camping.
Stool antigen EIA or nucleic acid amplification is the preferred first-line test; single O&P microscopy is insensitive due to intermittent shedding.
First-line treatment is a nitroimidazole — single-dose tinidazole or a course of metronidazole (nitazoxanide is an alternative).
Prevent with boiling/filtering/chemically treating backcountry water and good hand hygiene.

cases.title: Fatigue, Anemia, and a Change in Stools
cases.chiefComplaint: Months of fatigue and a recent change in the caliber and pattern of his bowel movements.
cases.history: A 62-year-old man presents with several months of progressive fatigue and exertional dyspnea. He has noticed his stools have become narrower ('pencil-thin') with increasing constipation alternating with some looser stools, occasional dark/tarry-appearing bowel movements, and crampy left-lower-quadrant discomfort. He reports a 5-kg unintentional weight loss. He has never had a screening colonoscopy. PMH: hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily, atorvastatin 20 mg daily, daily low-dose aspirin. Allergies: none. Social: former smoker (20 pack-years, quit 5 years ago), drinks 2-3 beers most nights, diet high in red and processed meat, sedentary. Family history: father had 'colon polyps' in his 70s; no early colorectal cancer known. ROS positive for fatigue, dyspnea on exertion, and the stool changes; negative for fevers, overt large-volume hematochezia, or vomiting. He attributes his fatigue to aging and a busy schedule.
cases.exam: The patient appears pale and mildly fatigued. Conjunctivae are pale. The abdomen is soft with mild left-lower-quadrant tenderness; bowel sounds are normal, and there is no distension, palpable mass, or hepatosplenomegaly. There is no lymphadenopathy. Cardiac exam reveals a soft systolic flow murmur and mild tachycardia. Rectal exam shows no palpable mass; stool is dark and guaiac positive. There is no jaundice or peripheral edema. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A 62-year-old man with iron-deficiency-type anemia, weight loss, a change in stool caliber, and guaiac-positive stool most urgently warrants which evaluation?
options: Empiric oral iron and proton pump inhibitor without further workup
Stool Giardia antigen testing as the first step today
Reassurance and a high-fiber diet with repeat visit in 6 months
Abdominal ultrasound only as the first step today
Colonoscopy to evaluate for a colorectal malignancy
stages.explanation: In an adult over 50 (here 62) with iron-deficiency anemia, weight loss, change in stool caliber, and guaiac-positive stool, colorectal cancer must be excluded, and colonoscopy is the test of choice — it allows direct visualization, biopsy, and removal of lesions throughout the colon. Reassurance/diet or empiric iron and PPI without evaluation would dangerously delay diagnosis of a likely malignancy. Iron-deficiency anemia in a man (or postmenopausal woman) is GI blood loss until proven otherwise and mandates luminal evaluation. Giardia testing does not fit this presentation. Abdominal ultrasound is inadequate for evaluating the colonic mucosa. These features are classic alarm symptoms.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 9.0 g/dL
MCV 74 fL (microcytic)
Ferritin 6 ng/mL (low)
Transferrin saturation 8% (low)
Fecal immunochemical test (FIT): positive
CEA 12 ng/mL (elevated)
LFTs within normal limits

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: His presentation features and labs fit a LEFT-sided colorectal cancer. Which set of features is most characteristic of left-sided (distal) tumors compared with right-sided tumors?
options: Profuse secretory diarrhea with flushing as the primary diagnosis
Painless jaundice and a palpable gallbladder as the primary diagnosis
Iron-deficiency anemia and vague fatigue with an occult, often advanced right-sided mass
Early satiety and gastric outlet obstruction as the primary diagnosis
Change in stool caliber, obstructive symptoms, constipation,
stages.explanation: Left-sided (descending colon, sigmoid, rectum) cancers grow in a narrower lumen with more formed stool, so they classically cause obstructive symptoms: changes in stool caliber ('pencil-thin' stools), constipation or altered bowel habits, crampy pain, and visible hematochezia — matching this patient's distal presentation and LLQ findings. Right-sided (cecum, ascending) cancers occur in a capacious lumen with liquid stool and tend to bleed occultly, presenting later with iron-deficiency anemia, fatigue, and sometimes a palpable mass. Painless jaundice with a palpable gallbladder suggests pancreatic/biliary cancer. Secretory diarrhea with flushing suggests a neuroendocrine tumor. Early satiety/gastric outlet obstruction points to gastric pathology.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Colonoscopy reveals a near-obstructing sigmoid mass; biopsy confirms invasive adenocarcinoma. What is the most appropriate next step in staging?
options: Repeat colonoscopy in 1 year as the immediate next step
No staging is needed before surgery as the immediate next step
PET scan as the sole staging modality as the immediate next step
CT of the chest, abdomen,
Bone scan only as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: After histologic confirmation of colon adenocarcinoma, staging includes contrast-enhanced CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis to evaluate for nodal and distant (especially hepatic and pulmonary) metastases, along with a baseline CEA (already elevated here, useful for prognosis and post-treatment surveillance). For rectal cancers, pelvic MRI and endorectal ultrasound add local T/N staging. A PET scan is not the routine sole initial staging test and is reserved for selected/equivocal cases. A bone scan alone is inappropriate. Proceeding to surgery without staging risks missing metastatic disease that would change management. Repeat colonoscopy in a year ignores the diagnosed cancer.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Staging shows a localized sigmoid adenocarcinoma without distant metastases. What is the cornerstone of curative treatment for this localized colon cancer?
options: Endoscopic ablation of the visible mucosal surface as initial therapy
Definitive chemoradiation without surgery as initial therapy
Palliative care only as initial therapy
Immunotherapy alone as first-line for all stages as initial therapy
Surgical resection , with adjuvant chemotherapy if high-risk/node-positive
stages.explanation: For localized (non-metastatic) colon adenocarcinoma, surgical resection — a segmental colectomy removing the tumor-bearing segment with its draining regional lymph nodes (for adequate staging and cure) — is the cornerstone of curative therapy. Adjuvant chemotherapy (e.g., FOLFOX) is added for high-risk stage II or node-positive (stage III) disease. Definitive chemoradiation without surgery is the paradigm for anal cancer and has a defined neoadjuvant role in rectal (not colon) cancer, not as curative therapy for colon cancer. Immunotherapy is used for metastatic disease, particularly mismatch-repair-deficient tumors, not as universal first-line for all stages. Palliative care alone is inappropriate for curable localized disease. Endoscopic ablation cannot treat an invasive near-obstructing cancer.

pearls: Iron-deficiency anemia in a man or postmenopausal woman is GI blood loss until proven otherwise and mandates colonoscopy.

Left-sided colon cancers cause obstructive symptoms — change in stool caliber, constipation/altered habits, and hematochezia; right-sided cancers cause occult bleeding with iron-deficiency anemia and present later.
Stage colon adenocarcinoma with CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and a baseline CEA; surgical resection with regional lymphadenectomy is the curative cornerstone for localized disease.
Add adjuvant chemotherapy for node-positive (stage III) or high-risk stage II disease.

cases.title: Early Colon Cancer with a Striking Family History
cases.chiefComplaint: Newly diagnosed colon cancer at age 40 with several relatives affected by cancer at young ages.
cases.history: A 40-year-old woman was recently found to have a right-sided colon adenocarcinoma after presenting with iron-deficiency anemia and vague abdominal discomfort. She is otherwise healthy. She is referred to discuss her striking family history: her father was diagnosed with colon cancer at 45 and a sister with endometrial cancer at 43; a paternal uncle had colon cancer at 48, and her paternal grandfather had 'a stomach or bowel cancer' in his early 50s. There is no history of hundreds of colonic polyps in any relative. PMH: unremarkable; no prior colonoscopy before this diagnosis. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker, rare alcohol, married with two children. ROS: aside from the anemia-related fatigue, negative for weight loss, overt bleeding, and obstructive symptoms. She is anxious about her own prognosis and her children's risk, and she wants to understand whether this is hereditary.
cases.exam: The patient appears well though mildly pale. The abdomen is soft and nontender with normal bowel sounds and no palpable mass, organomegaly, or lymphadenopathy. There is no mucocutaneous pigmentation, no osteomas, and no skin tumors. Rectal exam reveals no mass; stool is guaiac positive. The remainder of the physical examination, including skin and thyroid, is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given early-onset colon cancer, a family history of colon and endometrial cancer across generations, and the ABSENCE of hundreds of polyps, which hereditary syndrome is most likely?
options: Peutz-Jeghers syndrome as the first step today
MUTYH-associated polyposis as the first step today
Familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP)
Sporadic colon cancer with no hereditary basis
Lynch syndrome
stages.explanation: Lynch syndrome (HNPCC) is the most common hereditary colorectal cancer syndrome and classically presents with early-onset colorectal cancer (often right-sided), a striking autosomal-dominant family history spanning generations, and associated extracolonic cancers — most notably endometrial cancer (seen in her sister) — WITHOUT the hundreds of polyps of polyposis syndromes. FAP causes hundreds-to-thousands of adenomatous polyps, which her family lacks. Peutz-Jeghers features mucocutaneous pigmentation and hamartomatous polyps (absent here). MUTYH-associated polyposis is a polyposis syndrome. The multigenerational early cancers with endometrial involvement make a hereditary cause (Lynch) far more likely than sporadic disease.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which clinical criteria are used to identify families whose history suggests Lynch syndrome based on the pattern of colorectal cancers?
options: Duke criteria as the primary diagnosis
Amsterdam II criteria
Light's criteria in this clinical context
Rome IV criteria in this clinical context
Ranson criteria in this clinical context
stages.explanation: The Amsterdam II criteria identify families likely to have Lynch syndrome, summarized by the '3-2-1' rule: ≥3 relatives with a Lynch-associated cancer (colorectal, endometrial, small bowel, ureter/renal pelvis), spanning ≥2 successive generations, with ≥1 diagnosed before age 50, one being a first-degree relative of the other two, and FAP excluded. The revised Bethesda guidelines complement these by indicating which tumors to test for microsatellite instability. Light's criteria classify pleural effusions. Rome IV defines IBS. Ranson criteria estimate pancreatitis severity. Duke criteria diagnose infective endocarditis. This patient's family pattern meets the Amsterdam-type history.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial step to evaluate her tumor for Lynch syndrome?
options: Germline multigene panel sequencing as the very first test before any tumor analysis before discharge planning
Karyotype analysis of peripheral blood as the immediate next step
Serum CEA trend over time as the immediate next step
Repeat colonoscopy in 5 years as the immediate next step
Tumor testing with immunohistochemistry for mismatch-repair proteins and/or microsatellite instability testing
stages.explanation: The standard initial step is TUMOR-based screening: immunohistochemistry for the four mismatch-repair proteins (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2) and/or microsatellite instability (MSI) testing. Loss of an MMR protein or high MSI flags a likely Lynch tumor and directs subsequent confirmatory germline testing of the corresponding gene (with MLH1 loss prompting BRAF/MLH1-promoter-methylation testing to exclude sporadic causes). Universal tumor MMR/MSI testing of colorectal (and endometrial) cancers is recommended. Germline panel sequencing follows abnormal tumor screening or strong pretest suspicion, but tumor IHC/MSI is the practical initial step. CEA trends, deferring colonoscopy, and karyotyping do not establish Lynch syndrome.

stages.label: TUMOR & GERMLINE TESTING
stages.content:
Tumor immunohistochemistry: ABSENT staining for MSH2 and MSH6; intact MLH1 and PMS2
Microsatellite instability: MSI-high
BRAF V600E: not applicable (MLH1 retained)
Germline testing: pathogenic MSH2 mutation identified
Diagnosis: Lynch syndrome confirmed

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her germline testing confirms a pathogenic MSH2 mutation. Which surveillance and risk-reduction plan is most appropriate for this patient with Lynch syndrome?
options: Routine average-risk colonoscopy every 10 years and no other screening as initial therapy
Prophylactic total proctocolectomy for all Lynch patients regardless of disease as initial therapy
Annual chest CT as the primary surveillance as initial therapy
No further surveillance once the index cancer is treated as initial therapy
Frequent colonoscopy , gynecologic surveillance with discussion of risk-reducing hysterectomy/oophorectomy after childbearing,
stages.explanation: Lynch syndrome demands intensified, lifelong surveillance: colonoscopy every 1-2 years beginning at a young age because of accelerated adenoma-to-carcinoma progression. Given the high endometrial and ovarian cancer risk (especially with MSH2), gynecologic surveillance and discussion of risk-reducing hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy after completion of childbearing are recommended. Surveillance for other Lynch-associated cancers (e.g., upper endoscopy for gastric/duodenal cancer, urinalysis for urothelial cancer) is considered based on family history and gene. Average-risk 10-year colonoscopy is inadequate. Prophylactic proctocolectomy is not mandated for everyone (extended colectomy is considered when colon cancer/neoplasia is found). Annual chest CT is not the primary surveillance. Stopping surveillance is unsafe. Cascade genetic testing of at-risk relatives, including her children, is essential.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: She asks about her two children's risk. What is the most appropriate counseling regarding the inheritance and management of her confirmed pathogenic MSH2 mutation?
options: The children should simply begin average-risk colorectal screening at age 45 with appropriate follow-up
It is autosomal dominant
Lynch syndrome is not heritable, so no family testing is needed with appropriate follow-up
The mutation is autosomal recessive, so the children are at risk only if their father is also a carrier with appropriate follow-up
All children should undergo prophylactic colectomy now regardless of testing with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Lynch syndrome is inherited in an autosomal DOMINANT fashion, so each of her children has a 50% chance of inheriting the pathogenic MSH2 variant — regardless of the father's status. The appropriate step is cascade predictive germline testing of at-risk relatives, generally offered in early adulthood (around the late teens to early 20s for colorectal-predisposing genes), so that confirmed carriers begin intensified colonoscopic surveillance early while non-carriers can follow average-risk guidelines. It is not recessive, so the father's carrier status is irrelevant to their risk. The condition is clearly heritable, so saying no testing is needed is wrong. Prophylactic colectomy in asymptomatic children without testing or neoplasia is inappropriate. Average-risk screening starting at 45 is inadequate for a confirmed carrier.

pearls: Suspect Lynch syndrome with early-onset, often right-sided colorectal cancer and a multigenerational family history of colorectal and endometrial cancers WITHOUT extensive polyposis.
The Amsterdam II criteria (3-2-1 rule) and revised Bethesda guidelines identify families/tumors that warrant testing.
Initial evaluation uses tumor MMR immunohistochemistry and/or MSI testing; abnormal results prompt confirmatory germline testing (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2).
Management includes colonoscopy every 1-2 years, gynecologic surveillance with risk-reducing hysterectomy/BSO after childbearing, and cascade testing of relatives.

cases.title: Young Man with Rectal Bleeding and a Family Secret
cases.chiefComplaint: Intermittent bright red blood in the stool and loose stools for two months.
cases.history: A 20-year-old college sophomore presents with two months of intermittent bright red blood mixed in his stool, occasional loose bowel movements, and mild crampy lower abdominal discomfort. He denies weight loss, fevers, or night sweats. He takes no medications, has no allergies, and uses no tobacco, alcohol, or drugs. He has never had a colonoscopy. He came in because his older sister was recently diagnosed with colon cancer at age 31 and underwent a total colectomy; his father died at 42 of 'bowel cancer,' and a paternal aunt had her colon removed in her twenties. He recalls his father having dental cysts and a 'bump' removed from his jaw, and an eye doctor once noting pigmented spots on his retinas during a routine exam. He is anxious about his risk. Review of systems is otherwise negative. He eats a typical diet, exercises regularly, and reports no joint pain, skin changes, or oral ulcers.
cases.exam: Well-appearing young man in no distress. Conjunctivae pink without pallor. Oral exam without ulcers; a small bony mandibular protuberance is palpable. Abdomen soft, nontender, no hepatosplenomegaly or masses. No skin pigmentation around the lips or mucocutaneous freckling. Digital rectal exam reveals brown stool with streaks of bright red blood and a few small palpable polypoid lesions in the rectal vault; no mass, normal sphincter tone. No lymphadenopathy. Fundoscopic exam not performed in clinic.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given his striking family history and symptoms, what is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: CT colonography to count polyps noninvasively in this clinical context
Capsule endoscopy to evaluate the small bowel first
Full colonoscopy with biopsies to assess polyp burden
Reassure him and repeat fecal occult blood testing in one year
Flexible sigmoidoscopy only, as polyps cluster distally
stages.explanation: Full colonoscopy is the gold standard here: it allows direct visualization, accurate counting of polyps throughout the colon, and biopsy for histology, which is essential when a polyposis syndrome is suspected. Reassurance with FOBT is inappropriate given symptomatic bleeding, palpable rectal polyps, and a family history strongly suggesting an inherited syndrome. Flexible sigmoidoscopy alone would miss proximal polyps and underestimate burden. CT colonography cannot biopsy and poorly detects small/flat polyps. Capsule endoscopy evaluates the small bowel and is not the initial test when the colon is the symptomatic site; it may have a later role for duodenal/small-bowel surveillance.

stages.label: COLONOSCOPY
stages.content:
Colonoscopy: Hundreds of sessile and pedunculated polyps carpeting the entire colon and rectum, too numerous to count (estimated >100)
Multiple polyps biopsied: tubular and tubulovillous adenomas with low-grade dysplasia; no invasive carcinoma identified
Terminal ileum: normal
Estimated polyp density greatest in the left colon and rectum

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which germline mutation most likely underlies this presentation?
options: MLH1 mismatch-repair gene mutation
STK11 (LKB1) mutation
MUTYH biallelic mutation
SMAD4 mutation in this clinical context
APC tumor-suppressor gene mutation
stages.explanation: Hundreds to thousands of adenomatous polyps with autosomal-dominant inheritance, plus extracolonic clues (mandibular osteomas, congenital hypertrophy of the retinal pigment epithelium, dental abnormalities suggesting Gardner variant), are classic for familial adenomatous polyposis caused by a germline APC mutation. MLH1 causes Lynch syndrome, which produces few polyps but early cancers, not carpeting polyposis. MUTYH-associated polyposis is autosomal recessive and usually causes 10-100 polyps, an attenuated phenotype, and would not fit a clear dominant pedigree. STK11 causes Peutz-Jeghers (hamartomas, mucocutaneous pigmentation). SMAD4 causes juvenile polyposis/HHT, with juvenile hamartomatous polyps, not hundreds of adenomas.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has classic FAP with hundreds of adenomas and confirmed low-grade dysplasia. What is the most appropriate management recommendation regarding his colon?
options: Prophylactic total colectomy , planned electively
Repeat colonoscopy in 3 years given only low-grade dysplasia as initial therapy
Begin sulindac and celecoxib to regress polyps and avoid surgery as monotherapy
Annual colonoscopic polypectomy of the largest polyps indefinitely
Start oral mesalamine and high-fiber diet as initial therapy
stages.explanation: In classic FAP the lifetime risk of colorectal cancer approaches 100% if the colon is left in place, so prophylactic colectomy is the cornerstone of management; with hundreds of polyps, endoscopic clearance is impossible. Surgery is typically performed in the late teens to early twenties once polyposis is established, and this patient already has confirmed adenomas with dysplasia. Endoscopic polypectomy cannot keep pace with hundreds of polyps. NSAIDs (sulindac/celecoxib) can reduce polyp number but do not eliminate cancer risk and are adjuncts, not substitutes for colectomy. A 3-year interval is unsafe with this polyp burden. Mesalamine/fiber have no role in FAP cancer prevention.

stages.label: SURVEILLANCE
stages.question: After colectomy, which lifelong surveillance is most important to reduce his greatest remaining cancer-related mortality risk?
options: Annual chest CT for lung metastases as the immediate next step
Upper endoscopy with duodenoscopy to monitor for duodenal/ampullary adenomas
Bone scan for osteoma surveillance as the immediate next step
Brain MRI every 2 years for medulloblastoma as the immediate next step
Yearly PSA and testicular ultrasound as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: After colectomy, duodenal and periampullary adenocarcinoma becomes a leading cause of cancer death in FAP, so regular upper endoscopy with a side-viewing duodenoscope (staged by Spigelman classification) is essential, along with surveillance of any retained rectum.
