Desmoid tumors are also a major morbidity source. Chest CT for lung mets is not indicated routinely. PSA/testicular ultrasound are unrelated. Brain MRI is not standard (Turcot variant medulloblastoma risk is small and not screened with routine MRI). Osteomas are benign and need no surveillance. Thyroid exam/ultrasound for papillary thyroid cancer is also reasonable, but upper GI surveillance addresses the highest mortality risk.

cases.diagnosis: Familial adenomatous polyposis (classic FAP, Gardner variant) due to germline APC mutation
pearls: Classic FAP (>100 adenomas) carries near-100% lifetime colorectal cancer risk; prophylactic colectomy in the late teens to early twenties is the standard of care.
Gardner variant clues include osteomas (mandible/skull), epidermoid cysts, supernumerary teeth, desmoid tumors, and congenital hypertrophy of the retinal pigment epithelium (CHRPE).
After colectomy, duodenal/periampullary adenocarcinoma and desmoid tumors are leading causes of death; perform lifelong EGD with a side-viewing scope and stage by Spigelman classification.
MUTYH-associated polyposis is autosomal recessive with an attenuated (10-100 polyp) phenotype, distinguishing it from autosomal-dominant APC-driven FAP.

cases.title: Routine Screening Test in a Healthy Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Follow-up visit to discuss results of his first screening colonoscopy.
cases.history: A 58-year-old man returns to discuss the results of his first screening colonoscopy, which he underwent after his primary care physician recommended average-risk colorectal cancer screening. He has no GI symptoms: no rectal bleeding, change in bowel habits, weight loss, or abdominal pain. His past medical history includes well-controlled hypertension on lisinopril 20 mg daily and hyperlipidemia on atorvastatin 20 mg daily. He has no drug allergies. He is a never-smoker, drinks two beers on weekends, and walks daily. Diet is mixed with moderate red meat intake. Family history is notable only for hypertension; no relatives have had colorectal cancer, advanced polyps, or inflammatory bowel disease. He had no complications from the procedure and the prep was described as excellent. He feels well and is asking how often he will need future colonoscopies and whether anything was concerning. He has had normal recent labs including a normal complete blood count.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, comfortable man. No conjunctival pallor. Cardiopulmonary exam normal. Abdomen soft, nontender, no organomegaly or masses, normal bowel sounds. No palpable inguinal lymphadenopathy. Digital rectal exam reveals normal sphincter tone, no masses, and brown stool that is heme-negative. Skin without jaundice. No peripheral edema. The remainder of the focused exam is unremarkable, consistent with an asymptomatic patient presenting for routine results review.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: At what age does the U.S. Preventive Services Task Force currently recommend initiating average-risk colorectal cancer screening?
options: Age 55 as the first step today
Age 40 as the first step today
Age 60 as the first step today
Age 50 as the first step today
Age 45
stages.explanation: Current USPSTF (and major society) guidance recommends starting average-risk colorectal cancer screening at age 45, lowered from 50 because of rising incidence of early-onset colorectal cancer. Age 40 is used for those with a first-degree relative with CRC (start at 40 or 10 years before the relative's diagnosis), not the average-risk threshold. Age 50 was the prior recommendation but is now outdated for the general population. Ages 55 and 60 are too late and would miss the benefit window. Screening is generally individualized up to age 75 and not routinely recommended after 85.

stages.label: COLONOSCOPY & PATHOLOGY
stages.content:
Colonoscopy: cecum reached, excellent bowel prep
Two polyps identified and removed completely
- Sigmoid colon: 6 mm polyp
- Descending colon: 5 mm polyp
Pathology: both tubular adenomas with low-grade dysplasia; no villous features, no high-grade dysplasia
No additional polyps or masses; no sessile serrated lesions

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on these findings, how should this patient's polyp burden be characterized?
options: Advanced adenoma requiring short-interval surveillance
Sessile serrated polyposis syndrome
Low-risk adenomas (1-2 small tubular adenomas)
Hyperplastic polyps of no clinical significance
Malignant polyp requiring surgical resection
stages.explanation: Two tubular adenomas, each under 10 mm, with only low-grade dysplasia and no villous histology, constitute low-risk adenomas. Advanced adenoma requires at least one polyp ≥10 mm, villous/tubulovillous histology, or high-grade dysplasia, none of which are present. These are confirmed adenomas, not hyperplastic polyps, so they are clinically meaningful but low-risk. Sessile serrated polyposis syndrome requires multiple serrated lesions meeting specific criteria, not seen here. There is no carcinoma, so surgical resection is unnecessary; complete endoscopic removal is curative for these polyps.

stages.label: SURVEILLANCE
stages.question: Given complete removal of 1-2 small tubular adenomas with low-grade dysplasia and a high-quality exam, what is the recommended surveillance colonoscopy interval?
options: Repeat in 7-10 years
No further colonoscopy ever needed
Repeat in 6 months in this setting
Repeat in 1 year in this setting
Repeat in 3 years in this setting
stages.explanation: Per U.S. Multi-Society Task Force guidelines, 1-2 tubular adenomas smaller than 10 mm with low-grade dysplasia carry a low risk of metachronous advanced neoplasia, so the recommended surveillance interval is 7-10 years (many endoscopists choose 7). A 3-year interval applies to higher-risk findings such as 3-4 adenomas, an adenoma ≥10 mm, villous histology, or high-grade dysplasia. Six-month and 1-year intervals are reserved for piecemeal removal of large lesions or malignant polyps. Recommending no future colonoscopy is incorrect; he remains at average risk and needs continued surveillance until screening is stopped for age/comorbidity.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: If instead his pathology had shown one 14 mm tubulovillous adenoma with high-grade dysplasia (completely resected), what would the appropriate surveillance interval be?
options: 3 years
6 months before discharge planning
5 years as the immediate next step
10 years before discharge planning
No surveillance needed
stages.explanation: An adenoma ≥10 mm, villous/tubulovillous histology, or high-grade dysplasia defines an advanced adenoma, for which the guideline-recommended surveillance interval is 3 years after complete resection. Ten years is appropriate only for low-risk findings or a normal exam. Five years is not a standard interval for this risk category in current USMSTF guidance. Six months applies to piecemeal endoscopic mucosal resection of large (≥20 mm) lesions to confirm complete removal. 'No surveillance' is never appropriate after an advanced adenoma given the elevated risk of metachronous advanced neoplasia.

cases.diagnosis: Low-risk colonic adenomas on screening colonoscopy (average-risk patient)
pearls: Average-risk colorectal cancer screening now begins at age 45 per USPSTF; a first-degree relative with CRC moves the start to age 40 or 10 years before the relative's diagnosis.
Low-risk adenomas (1-2 tubular adenomas <10 mm, low-grade dysplasia) warrant a 7-10 year surveillance interval.
Advanced adenoma features (≥10 mm, villous histology, or high-grade dysplasia) or 3-10 adenomas shorten the interval to 3 years.
Surveillance intervals assume complete polyp removal and an adequate bowel prep; a poor prep mandates an early repeat exam.

cases.title: Blood on the Toilet Paper
cases.chiefComplaint: Painless bright red blood on the toilet paper after bowel movements for several weeks.
cases.history: A 45-year-old office worker reports several weeks of intermittent bright red blood seen on the toilet paper and occasionally dripping into the bowl after he passes a bowel movement. The blood is not mixed into the stool. He denies pain with defecation, though he notes occasional anal itching and a sensation of fullness. He strains frequently and has had hard stools because of a low-fiber diet and long sedentary work hours. He has no weight loss, no change in stool caliber, and no family history of colon cancer or inflammatory bowel disease. Past medical history is unremarkable; he takes only an occasional ibuprofen for headaches and has no allergies. He does not smoke and drinks socially. He has never had a colonoscopy. He is worried because of the recurrent bleeding but feels otherwise well, with normal energy and no lightheadedness. He has not noticed any external lumps but admits he has not examined the area closely.
cases.exam: Comfortable, well-nourished man. No conjunctival pallor; capillary refill brisk. Abdomen soft, nontender, no masses or organomegaly. Perianal inspection shows no external swelling, thrombosis, fissure, or fistulous opening, and no skin tags. Anoscopy reveals engorged bluish vascular cushions above the dentate line at the right anterior and left lateral positions, one with a small amount of fresh blood; no ulceration or mass. Digital rectal exam: normal tone, no palpable mass; the soft internal cushions are not readily palpable. Stool brown, heme-positive on the glove.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The bleeding is painless, bright red, and seen on the toilet paper above the dentate line on anoscopy. Which lesion best explains this pattern?
options: Thrombosed external hemorrhoid
Rectal adenocarcinoma
Internal hemorrhoids
Anal fissure in this clinical context
External hemorrhoids
stages.explanation: Internal hemorrhoids arise above the dentate line from the internal hemorrhoidal plexus, which is covered by insensate columnar mucosa, so they characteristically cause painless bright red bleeding with defecation, exactly as described and confirmed on anoscopy. External hemorrhoids arise below the dentate line, are covered by somatically innervated anoderm, and cause pain and palpable lumps rather than painless bleeding. An anal fissure causes severe pain with defecation, not painless bleeding. Rectal adenocarcinoma can bleed but typically presents with altered bowel habits, weight loss, or a palpable mass, and would not be seen as engorged vascular cushions. A thrombosed external hemorrhoid is acutely painful and palpable.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Internal hemorrhoids are graded by degree of prolapse. He has bleeding but no prolapse on straining or exam. How are his hemorrhoids best classified?
options: Grade II (prolapse with spontaneous reduction)
Cannot be graded without MRI
Grade III (prolapse requiring manual reduction)
Grade IV (irreducible prolapse)
Grade I (no prolapse)
stages.explanation: Grade I internal hemorrhoids bleed but do not prolapse beyond the anal canal, matching this patient who has bleeding with no prolapse on straining. Grade II hemorrhoids prolapse with defecation/straining but reduce spontaneously. Grade III prolapse and require manual reduction. Grade IV are chronically prolapsed and irreducible (and risk strangulation). MRI is not used for hemorrhoid grading; classification is clinical, based on inspection during straining and anoscopy. Accurate grading guides therapy, with conservative measures favored for grades I-II and procedural options reserved for higher grades or refractory bleeding.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line management for his symptomatic grade I internal hemorrhoids?
options: Sclerotherapy with phenol injection as initial therapy
High-fiber diet, increased water intake,
Surgical hemorrhoidectomy as initial therapy
Rubber band ligation as initial therapy
Topical nitroglycerin ointment as initial therapy
stages.explanation: First-line therapy for symptomatic grade I-II internal hemorrhoids is conservative: increased dietary fiber and fluids, fiber supplements, avoiding straining and prolonged toilet sitting, and short-term topical agents for symptom relief. This alone resolves most low-grade hemorrhoidal bleeding. Rubber band ligation is an office procedure reserved for grades I-III that fail conservative therapy or have persistent bleeding. Surgical hemorrhoidectomy is reserved for grade III-IV or refractory disease. Topical nitroglycerin treats anal fissures by relaxing the internal sphincter, not hemorrhoids. Sclerotherapy is a procedural option for low-grade disease but is second-line after conservative measures fail.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: He is 45 years old, has visible blood, and has never been screened for colorectal cancer. Beyond treating hemorrhoids, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Check serum CEA level to screen for colon cancer before discharge planning
Order abdominal CT with contrast as the immediate next step
Order a colonoscopy to exclude proximal pathology
Attribute all bleeding to hemorrhoids and defer any further workup
Begin empiric oral iron and recheck in one year as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Even when hemorrhoids are an obvious bleeding source, rectal bleeding must not be reflexively attributed to them without excluding more proximal disease, especially in a 45-year-old who is now due for average-risk screening. Colonoscopy both evaluates the bleeding and fulfills screening. Attributing all bleeding to hemorrhoids risks missing a synchronous neoplasm. Abdominal CT is poor for mucosal lesions and not the test for hematochezia. CEA is a tumor marker for monitoring known colorectal cancer, not a screening test, and lacks sensitivity/specificity. Empiric iron without evaluation is inappropriate and delays diagnosis of any serious cause.

pearls: Internal hemorrhoids (above the dentate line) cause painless bright red bleeding; external hemorrhoids (below the dentate line, somatically innervated) cause pain and palpable lumps.
Internal hemorrhoid grading is clinical: I = bleeding without prolapse, II = prolapse with spontaneous reduction, III = manual reduction needed, IV = irreducible.
First-line therapy for grade I-II hemorrhoids is conservative (fiber, fluids, less straining); rubber band ligation and surgery are reserved for refractory or higher-grade disease.
Never attribute rectal bleeding solely to hemorrhoids without excluding proximal pathology, especially in patients age 45+ or with alarm features.

cases.title: Searing Pain Every Time She Goes
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe sharp pain during bowel movements with bright red blood for ten days.
cases.history: A 32-year-old woman presents with ten days of severe, sharp, tearing anal pain that begins with defecation and lasts up to an hour afterward, described as 'passing glass.' She notices small amounts of bright red blood on the toilet paper and streaking the surface of the stool. The pain is so intense that she now avoids and delays bowel movements, which has made her stools harder. The problem began after a prolonged episode of constipation following a recent course of oral opioids for a dental procedure. She has no diarrhea, weight loss, fevers, or perianal discharge. Past medical history is unremarkable; she takes a daily multivitamin, the dental opioids have been stopped, and she has no allergies. She is not pregnant and has had no prior anal surgery. She does not smoke and drinks rarely. There is no family history of inflammatory bowel disease or colorectal cancer. She is otherwise healthy and active but is distressed by the cyclic pain and fear of defecation.
cases.exam: Anxious but well-appearing woman. Abdomen soft, nontender, no masses. Gentle perianal inspection, with careful lateral traction of the buttocks, reveals a linear tear in the anoderm at the posterior midline with a small sentinel skin tag distally; the internal sphincter fibers are visible at the base. Marked sphincter spasm and guarding are present. No fluctuance, induration, external opening, or purulent discharge to suggest abscess or fistula. Digital rectal exam and anoscopy are deferred because of severe pain and spasm; they are planned after symptom control. No inguinal lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A linear posterior midline anoderm tear with severe pain on defecation and bright red blood is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Anal fissure
Anal carcinoma
Anal fistula in this clinical context
Thrombosed external hemorrhoid
Perianal abscess
stages.explanation: An anal fissure is a longitudinal tear in the anoderm distal to the dentate line, classically at the posterior midline, causing severe sharp pain during and after defecation with bright red bleeding; a sentinel tag and exposed internal sphincter fibers support chronicity. A thrombosed external hemorrhoid presents as a tender purplish perianal lump, not a linear tear. A perianal abscess produces a fluctuant, erythematous, warm swelling, absent here. An anal fistula has an external opening with discharge. Anal carcinoma typically presents as a mass or non-healing ulcer, often with atypical features; a classic posterior midline fissure in a young patient after constipation points clearly to fissure.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: Fissures most commonly occur at the posterior midline. What is the principal reason this location is predisposed?
options: It is the site of highest fecal bacterial concentration as the immediate next step
Relatively poor blood supply to the posterior midline anoderm plus high resting internal sphincter pressure
It is the only region innervated by somatic nerves as the immediate next step
The dentate line is absent posteriorly as the immediate next step
Venous engorgement is greatest posteriorly as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: The posterior midline anoderm is relatively ischemic because perfusion from the inferior rectal arteries is poorest there, and elevated resting internal anal sphincter tone further reduces blood flow, impairing healing and predisposing to chronic fissures. This ischemic-spasm cycle is the rationale for therapies that relax the sphincter. Bacterial concentration is not the driver. Somatic innervation extends throughout the anoderm below the dentate line, not just posteriorly, and explains pain but not location. Venous engorgement relates to hemorrhoids, not fissures. The dentate line is present circumferentially. Fissures off the midline (lateral) should raise suspicion for secondary causes like Crohn disease, infection, or malignancy.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment for this acute anal fissure?
options: Immediate broad-spectrum antibiotics as initial therapy
Lateral internal sphincterotomy as initial therapy
Rubber band ligation as initial therapy
Conservative therapy: fiber, increased fluids, sitz baths,
Botulinum toxin injection into the internal sphincter
stages.explanation: First-line management of an acute anal fissure is conservative: dietary fiber, increased fluid intake, stool softeners to prevent hard stools, and warm sitz baths to relax the sphincter and relieve pain; topical agents that relax the sphincter (nitroglycerin or calcium-channel blocker ointment) are commonly added. Most acute fissures heal with these measures. Lateral internal sphincterotomy is the most effective definitive treatment but is reserved for chronic or refractory fissures because of the small risk of incontinence. Botulinum toxin is a second-line option for fissures failing medical therapy. Rubber band ligation treats hemorrhoids. Antibiotics are not indicated; there is no infection.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: If her fissure persisted beyond 8 weeks and medical therapy with topical nitroglycerin failed, what is the most appropriate definitive treatment?
options: Topical antibiotics indefinitely
Hemorrhoidectomy in this setting
Lateral internal sphincterotomy
Diverting colostomy in this setting
Long-term daily opioids for pain control
stages.explanation: A fissure persisting beyond 6-8 weeks despite optimal medical therapy is chronic, and lateral internal sphincterotomy is the gold-standard definitive treatment, with high healing rates by reducing internal sphincter hypertonia; botulinum toxin is an alternative for those wishing to avoid surgery or with incontinence risk. Long-term opioids worsen constipation and perpetuate the fissure. Hemorrhoidectomy addresses hemorrhoids, not fissures. Diverting colostomy is far too aggressive for a benign fissure. Topical antibiotics have no role. For atypical or lateral, multiple, or non-healing fissures, evaluate for secondary causes (Crohn disease, infection, malignancy) before sphincterotomy.

pearls: Anal fissure causes severe sharp pain during and after defecation with bright red blood; the tear is classically at the posterior midline.
Pathogenesis is an ischemia-spasm cycle: poor posterior midline perfusion plus high internal sphincter tone impairs healing.
First-line therapy is conservative (fiber, fluids, sitz baths, stool softeners) plus topical sphincter relaxants (nitroglycerin or calcium-channel blockers).
Chronic or refractory fissures are treated definitively with lateral internal sphincterotomy; lateral or atypical fissures warrant evaluation for Crohn disease, infection, or malignancy.

cases.title: A Painful Swelling Near the Anus
cases.chiefComplaint: Increasingly painful swelling next to the anus with fever for three days.
cases.history: A 38-year-old man presents to the emergency department with three days of progressively worsening, throbbing pain and a tender swelling just to the side of the anus, now accompanied by subjective fevers and difficulty sitting. He reports intermittent crampy abdominal pain and several months of loose, occasionally bloody stools, along with a 4 kg unintentional weight loss and intermittent low-grade fevers, which he had attributed to stress. He has no prior anorectal surgery. Past medical history is otherwise unremarkable; he takes no regular medications, occasionally uses acetaminophen, and has no known allergies. He smokes half a pack of cigarettes daily and drinks socially. Family history includes a cousin with 'bowel disease.' He has had occasional mouth ulcers and joint aches over the past year. He denies urinary symptoms and is sexually active with one partner. He is concerned the swelling is getting larger and warmer and that the pain now keeps him awake at night.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable man shifting to avoid sitting. Abdomen soft with mild right lower quadrant tenderness, no peritoneal signs, normal bowel sounds. Perianal inspection reveals a 3 cm erythematous, warm, fluctuant swelling lateral to the anal verge with surrounding induration; pressure expresses a small amount of pus. There is a perianal skin tag and a tiny secondary opening with serous drainage suggesting a possible fistula tract. Digital rectal exam is markedly tender with fullness in the ischiorectal region; no rectal mass. Oral exam shows a shallow aphthous ulcer. No inguinal lymphadenopathy beyond mild tender nodes.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: He has a fluctuant, warm, tender perianal swelling expressing pus. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Urgent CT abdomen/pelvis before any intervention
Sitz baths and stool softeners only
Topical metronidazole and discharge
Incision and drainage of the abscess
Oral antibiotics alone and outpatient follow-up
stages.explanation: A perianal abscess is a surgical problem: prompt incision and drainage is the definitive treatment, because antibiotics cannot penetrate or sterilize an undrained pus collection and delay risks extension to ischiorectal/supralevator spaces or necrotizing infection. I&D provides immediate relief and source control. Oral antibiotics alone are inadequate and are reserved as adjuncts for systemic illness, cellulitis, immunosuppression, or valvular disease. Sitz baths/softeners are supportive only. CT can help define deep or complex (e.g., supralevator) abscesses but should not delay drainage of an obvious fluctuant collection. Topical metronidazole does not treat an abscess and discharge without drainage is unsafe.

stages.label: POST-DRAINAGE & LABS
stages.content:
Incision and drainage performed: ~15 mL purulent fluid expressed; wound packed
Wound culture: mixed enteric flora (E. coli, anaerobes)
WBC 14.8 ×10³/µL (neutrophil predominant)
Hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL (MCV 78 fL)
CRP 64 mg/L
ESR 48 mm/hr
A persistent fistulous opening with feculent-appearing drainage is noted after the abscess decompresses

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: His recurrent bloody diarrhea, weight loss, oral ulcers, microcytic anemia, and a perianal fistula raise concern for which underlying condition?
options: Diverticular disease
Celiac disease
Ulcerative colitis
Irritable bowel syndrome
Crohn disease
stages.explanation: Perianal fistulizing disease together with chronic bloody diarrhea, weight loss, aphthous oral ulcers, arthralgias, and inflammatory anemia strongly suggests Crohn disease, which characteristically causes transmural inflammation and perianal fistulae/abscesses. Ulcerative colitis is mucosal, limited to the colon, and does not typically cause fistulae or perianal disease. Irritable bowel syndrome lacks bleeding, weight loss, fever, anemia, and inflammatory markers. Celiac disease causes malabsorptive diarrhea and anemia but not perianal fistulae or transmural complications. Diverticular disease is uncommon at 38 and does not explain oral ulcers, perianal fistula, or chronic bloody diarrhea. The elevated CRP/ESR and microcytic anemia further support an inflammatory bowel process.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Following drainage, what is the most appropriate next diagnostic step to confirm the suspected underlying diagnosis and characterize the fistula?
options: Repeat incision and drainage in one week before discharge planning
Empiric lifelong antibiotics without further testing
Anal manometry as the immediate next step
Colonoscopy with ileal intubation
Stool culture for ova and parasites only before discharge planning
stages.explanation: To confirm Crohn disease and assess disease extent, colonoscopy with intubation and biopsy of the terminal ileum is the key test, revealing skip lesions, aphthous/linear ulceration, cobblestoning, and granulomas; pelvic MRI is the best modality to map fistula anatomy and detect occult abscess before definitive therapy. Repeat I&D is not diagnostic and is needed only if pus reaccumulates. Stool studies help exclude infection but cannot diagnose Crohn disease. Anal manometry assesses sphincter function (relevant before some surgeries) but does not establish the diagnosis. Empiric antibiotics without diagnosis would miss the underlying disease that drives recurrent fistulizing complications.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Colonoscopy and MRI confirm Crohn disease with a complex perianal fistula. What is the most appropriate management of the fistula in the context of active Crohn disease?
options: Observation with no intervention as initial therapy
Antibiotics alone indefinitely as initial therapy
Seton placement for drainage plus medical therapy with an anti-TNF agent
Diverting colostomy as first-line therapy as initial therapy
Immediate wide fistulotomy of the entire tract as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Complex perianal Crohn fistulae are best managed with a combined approach: surgical seton placement to maintain drainage and prevent recurrent abscess, paired with medical therapy—anti-TNF biologics such as infliximab are first-line and significantly improve fistula healing, often with an antibiotic (metronidazole/ciprofloxacin) as a bridge. Aggressive fistulotomy of a complex tract in active Crohn disease risks sphincter damage and incontinence and poor healing, so it is avoided. Antibiotics alone do not heal Crohn fistulae durably. Diverting colostomy is reserved for severe refractory perianal disease, not first-line. Observation alone allows recurrent abscess and progression.

cases.diagnosis: Perianal abscess with fistula-in-ano as the presenting manifestation of Crohn disease
pearls: A fluctuant perianal abscess requires prompt incision and drainage; antibiotics alone are inadequate and reserved for systemic illness, cellulitis, immunosuppression, or valvular disease.
Perianal fistulizing disease plus bloody diarrhea, weight loss, oral ulcers, and inflammatory anemia should prompt evaluation for Crohn disease.
Confirm Crohn disease with ileocolonoscopy and biopsy; use pelvic MRI to map fistula anatomy and detect occult abscess.
Complex Crohn perianal fistulae are managed with seton drainage plus anti-TNF therapy; avoid aggressive fistulotomy to preserve continence.

cases.title: Distended and Unable to Pass Gas
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive abdominal distension, cramping, and inability to pass gas or stool for two days.
cases.history: An 80-year-old nursing-home resident is brought in with two days of progressive abdominal distension, intermittent crampy lower abdominal pain, and an inability to pass flatus or stool. He has a long history of chronic constipation requiring daily laxatives and a remote history of recurrent similar but milder episodes that resolved spontaneously. He has Parkinson disease and takes carbidopa-levodopa, plus polyethylene glycol and senna for constipation. He is bedbound much of the day. He has dementia and is a poor historian; staff report he has not had a bowel movement in four days. He has no prior abdominal surgery. He has had no fevers but has been increasingly uncomfortable and nauseated, with one episode of vomiting. No hematemesis or melena. He has no known drug allergies. Family history is noncontributory. He does not smoke or drink. His baseline mobility is limited, and he has a history of psychiatric medication use that contributes to slow gut transit. He appears uncomfortable and bloated.
cases.exam: Elderly man in mild distress with a markedly distended, tympanitic abdomen. Bowel sounds are high-pitched early then diminished. There is generalized mild tenderness without rebound or guarding; no peritoneal signs. No surgical scars. The abdomen is asymmetrically distended, more prominent in the left upper quadrant. Digital rectal exam reveals an empty, collapsed rectal vault with no palpable mass and no stool. No hernias palpable. He is mildly dry with tacky mucous membranes. No jaundice. Cardiopulmonary exam is unremarkable aside from mild tachycardia attributable to discomfort and dehydration.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: An elderly, chronically constipated, bedbound man has marked distension, obstipation, and an empty rectum. What is the most appropriate initial imaging?
options: Nuclear motility study in this clinical context
Barium enema as the first test
Right upper quadrant ultrasound
Plain abdominal radiographs
MRI of the abdomen and pelvis
stages.explanation: Plain abdominal radiographs are the appropriate first imaging in suspected large-bowel obstruction or volvulus: they are fast, inexpensive, and can reveal a massively dilated sigmoid loop with the classic 'coffee-bean' or 'bent inner-tube' sign pointing to the right upper quadrant. RUQ ultrasound evaluates the gallbladder/biliary tree, not bowel obstruction. MRI is slow and not first-line in this acute setting. Nuclear motility studies assess chronic transit, irrelevant in an acute obstruction. A water-soluble (not barium) contrast enema can be diagnostic and even therapeutic for some volvuli, but it follows plain films and is contraindicated if perforation or ischemia is suspected; barium specifically risks barium peritonitis if perforation occurs.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
Abdominal X-ray: massively dilated, inverted-U sigmoid loop arising from the pelvis pointing toward the right upper quadrant ('coffee-bean' sign); proximal colonic distension
No free air under the diaphragm on upright film
WBC 9.8 ×10³/µL
Lactate 1.4 mmol/L
Na 138 mEq/L, K 3.6 mEq/L, creatinine 1.1 mg/dL
CT confirms a sigmoid volvulus with a 'whirl' sign at the twisted mesentery; no pneumatosis, no free air

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The radiographic findings confirm the diagnosis. What is it?
options: Sigmoid volvulus
Toxic megacolon
Malignant large bowel obstruction
Adhesive small bowel obstruction
Cecal volvulus
stages.explanation: A massively dilated sigmoid loop with the coffee-bean sign pointing to the right upper quadrant, plus a CT whirl sign at the twisted mesentery, is diagnostic of sigmoid volvulus, the most common volvulus in elderly, chronically constipated, institutionalized patients. Cecal volvulus typically displaces a dilated cecum toward the left upper quadrant and occurs in younger patients with a mobile cecum. Malignant LBO shows a colonic mass/transition point, not a twisted loop. Toxic megacolon is associated with colitis and systemic toxicity, absent here. Adhesive small bowel obstruction shows dilated small bowel with valvulae conniventes and usually a surgical history, neither present in this case.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has no peritonitis, no free air, normal lactate, and no signs of bowel ischemia. What is the most appropriate initial treatment for the sigmoid volvulus?
options: Emergent sigmoid colectomy as initial therapy
Neostigmine infusion as initial therapy
Manual disimpaction and discharge as initial therapy
Endoscopic detorsion with rectal tube placement
High-dose IV antibiotics and observation only
stages.explanation: In a stable patient without ischemia or perforation, endoscopic detorsion via flexible sigmoidoscopy is the initial treatment of choice; it relieves the torsion, allows decompression, and a rectal tube is left to maintain decompression and prevent early recurrence. Emergent colectomy is reserved for failed endoscopic detorsion or signs of ischemia/perforation (peritonitis, free air, elevated lactate, pneumatosis). Antibiotics and observation alone do not relieve the mechanical twist. Neostigmine treats colonic pseudo-obstruction (Ogilvie syndrome), a functional disorder, not a mechanical volvulus. Manual disimpaction and discharge ignores the obstruction and risks ischemic perforation.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Endoscopic detorsion succeeds with return of flatus and stool. Given the high recurrence of sigmoid volvulus, what is the most appropriate definitive recommendation during the same admission, if he is an acceptable operative candidate?
options: Discharge with no further intervention; recurrence is rare
Elective sigmoid colectomy during the same hospitalization
Permanent rectal tube at home with appropriate follow-up
Lifelong daily enemas as definitive therapy
Repeat sigmoidoscopic detorsion scheduled every month prophylactically
stages.explanation: Sigmoid volvulus recurs in roughly 50-60% of cases after endoscopic detorsion alone, so elective sigmoid colectomy during the same admission (after decompression and resuscitation) is recommended for acceptable surgical candidates to prevent recurrence and the risk of future ischemic strangulation. Discharge without intervention ignores the high recurrence rate. Lifelong enemas do not correct the redundant, mobile sigmoid and will not prevent recurrence. A permanent home rectal tube is impractical and unsafe. Scheduled monthly prophylactic detorsion is not a recognized strategy and exposes the patient to repeated risk. In poor surgical candidates, individualized nonoperative management may be chosen.

pearls: Sigmoid volvulus classically affects elderly, chronically constipated, institutionalized patients and shows a 'coffee-bean' loop pointing to the right upper quadrant on plain film.
First-line treatment without ischemia/perforation is endoscopic detorsion via sigmoidoscopy with rectal tube decompression.
Signs of ischemia or perforation (peritonitis, free air, elevated lactate, pneumatosis) mandate emergent surgery rather than endoscopic detorsion.
Because recurrence after detorsion is 50-60%, elective sigmoid colectomy during the same admission is recommended for good surgical candidates.

cases.title: Belly Swelling and No Bowel Movements
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive abdominal distension, cramping, and no bowel movement or gas for three days.
cases.history: A 69-year-old woman presents with three days of worsening abdominal distension, crampy lower abdominal pain, and complete inability to pass stool or flatus (obstipation). Over the preceding three months she describes a change in bowel habit with progressively narrower stool caliber, intermittent blood streaking the stool, and an unintentional 6 kg weight loss with fatigue. She has had no nausea until today, when she vomited once. Past medical history includes type 2 diabetes on metformin 1000 mg twice daily and hypertension on amlodipine 5 mg daily. She has never had a colonoscopy and is overdue for screening. She has no prior abdominal surgery. She has no drug allergies. Her mother had colon cancer at age 72. She is a former smoker (20 pack-years, quit 10 years ago) and drinks rarely. Review of systems is positive for decreased appetite. She is increasingly uncomfortable from the distension and is anxious about the progressive symptoms and weight loss over recent months.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable woman with a distended, tympanitic abdomen. Bowel sounds are high-pitched and hyperactive in runs. There is mild diffuse tenderness without rebound or guarding; no peritoneal signs. No surgical scars and no palpable hernias. A vague fullness is appreciated in the left lower quadrant. Digital rectal exam reveals an empty rectal vault with no palpable mass low in the rectum and trace heme-positive stool on the glove. Conjunctivae mildly pale. Cardiopulmonary exam normal aside from mild tachycardia. No supraclavicular or inguinal lymphadenopathy. She appears mildly volume-depleted with tacky mucous membranes.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: She has distension, obstipation, and alarm features over months. After plain films suggest large bowel obstruction, what is the most appropriate next imaging study?
options: Barium enema as the first step today
Right upper quadrant ultrasound
MRI enterography as the first step today
CT of the abdomen and pelvis with contrast
Repeat plain films in 24 hours
stages.explanation: CT of the abdomen and pelvis with contrast is the test of choice to confirm large bowel obstruction, localize the transition point, characterize an obstructing mass, assess for perforation, and stage for metastatic disease, all critical in suspected malignant LBO. MRI enterography is used for small-bowel Crohn evaluation, not acute LBO. RUQ ultrasound evaluates the biliary system, not colonic obstruction. Barium enema risks barium peritonitis if perforation is present and is largely supplanted by CT; a water-soluble contrast study may sometimes be used but does not stage disease. Simply repeating plain films delays definitive diagnosis in a patient with obstruction and worrisome features.

stages.label: CT FINDINGS & LABS
stages.content:
CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast: annular ('apple-core') soft-tissue mass in the sigmoid colon with an abrupt transition point and marked proximal colonic dilation (cecum 9 cm)
No free intraperitoneal air; no pneumatosis
Three hypodense hepatic lesions concerning for metastases
WBC 11.0 ×10³/µL, Hemoglobin 10.4 g/dL (MCV 76 fL)
Lactate 1.6 mmol/L, creatinine 1.3 mg/dL
CEA 38 ng/mL (elevated)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which finding most strongly indicates that the obstruction is malignant rather than functional or benign?
options: An annular 'apple-core' sigmoid mass with an abrupt transition point
Mild leukocytosis as the primary diagnosis
Cecal dilation to 9 cm as the primary diagnosis
The presence of high-pitched bowel sounds as the primary diagnosis
Hypertension and tachycardia as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: An annular, circumferential 'apple-core' lesion with an abrupt transition point at the mass, plus an elevated CEA and likely hepatic metastases, is the hallmark of malignant (colorectal cancer) obstruction. High-pitched bowel sounds simply indicate mechanical obstruction of any cause and do not distinguish malignant from benign. Cecal dilation reflects the degree of obstruction and perforation risk but not its etiology. Mild leukocytosis is nonspecific. Hypertension and tachycardia reflect discomfort and volume status, not malignancy. The constellation of a constricting mass, transition point, weight loss, anemia, and tumor marker elevation establishes a malignant mechanical large bowel obstruction.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She is hemodynamically stable without perforation, but has an obstructing sigmoid cancer with likely liver metastases. Regarding relief of the obstruction, which option best describes appropriate decision-making?
options: Long-term nasogastric decompression alone is curative as initial therapy
Neostigmine to relieve the obstruction as initial therapy
Endoscopic detorsion with a rectal tube as initial therapy
Immediate total colectomy is mandatory in all malignant obstructions as initial therapy
Self-expanding metallic stent can serve as definitive palliation or a bridge to surgery
stages.explanation: For an obstructing left-sided colorectal cancer in a stable patient, a self-expanding metallic colonic stent is a valid option, either as definitive palliation (especially with metastatic disease) or as a bridge to elective single-stage surgery, avoiding emergency resection and stoma when feasible; emergency surgery is indicated for perforation, peritonitis, or stent failure. Mandatory immediate total colectomy is not required and is more morbid emergently. NG decompression alone treats neither the obstruction nor the tumor and is not curative. Neostigmine treats colonic pseudo-obstruction, not mechanical malignant obstruction. Endoscopic detorsion with a rectal tube treats sigmoid volvulus, not a tumor.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: While arranging therapy, the cecum measures 9 cm and is rising. Why is the cecal diameter a critical parameter to monitor in this large bowel obstruction?
options: It indicates the depth of tumor invasion as the leading concern
It correlates with CEA level as the leading concern
By the law of Laplace, the cecum is at highest risk of ischemia
It predicts response to chemotherapy as the leading concern
Cecal size determines stent length as the leading concern
stages.explanation: By the law of Laplace, wall tension is proportional to radius, so the cecum—the colon segment with the largest diameter—bears the greatest tension and is the most likely site of ischemia and perforation in a closed-loop or distal obstruction; risk rises sharply as cecal diameter exceeds about 12 cm. This makes serial monitoring of cecal diameter essential to time urgent decompression. Cecal size does not predict chemotherapy response or tumor invasion depth, nor does it correlate with CEA. While stent sizing matters, it is the obstructing lesion's length and location—not cecal diameter—that guides stent selection. Rising cecal diameter is a warning sign demanding prompt action.

cases.diagnosis: Malignant large bowel obstruction from obstructing sigmoid colorectal carcinoma (with probable hepatic metastases)
pearls: CT with contrast is the test of choice for large bowel obstruction: it localizes the transition point, characterizes the mass, detects perforation, and stages disease.
An annular 'apple-core' lesion with abrupt transition, weight loss, anemia, and elevated CEA points to malignant obstruction.
Self-expanding metallic stents can palliate or bridge obstructing left-sided colon cancer to elective surgery; emergency resection is for perforation, peritonitis, or stent failure.
By the law of Laplace the cecum is most prone to ischemic perforation; cecal diameter >12 cm signals impending perforation and demands urgent decompression.

cases.title: Cramping Belly After an Old Operation
cases.chiefComplaint: Colicky abdominal pain, vomiting, and abdominal distension for one day.
cases.history: A 56-year-old woman presents with one day of intermittent, colicky, cramping mid-abdominal pain that comes in waves every few minutes, associated with multiple episodes of bilious vomiting and progressive abdominal distension. She last passed a small amount of stool yesterday and has had no flatus for several hours. She underwent an open total abdominal hysterectomy 12 years ago and an appendectomy in her twenties. She has had two prior similar episodes that resolved with hospital bowel rest. Past medical history includes hypothyroidism on levothyroxine 100 mcg daily and GERD on omeprazole 20 mg daily. She has no drug allergies. She does not smoke and drinks occasionally. Family history is noncontributory for GI malignancy or inflammatory bowel disease. She denies fever, hematochezia, or melena. The vomiting has provided transient relief of the cramping. She is uncomfortable and nauseated but able to converse, and she recalls that her prior episodes were attributed to scar tissue from her surgeries.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable woman with a distended abdomen and a well-healed lower-midline surgical scar. Bowel sounds are high-pitched with audible rushes early. There is mild diffuse tenderness, most in the periumbilical region, without rebound, guarding, or rigidity; no peritoneal signs. No palpable hernias at the umbilicus, groin, or incision. Digital rectal exam reveals a small amount of stool, no mass, heme-negative. Mucous membranes are slightly dry. There is no fever, no localized severe tenderness, and no skin changes over the abdomen. Cardiopulmonary exam unremarkable apart from mild tachycardia attributable to pain and mild dehydration.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given colicky pain, bilious vomiting, distension, and prior abdominal surgery, what initial imaging best confirms and characterizes the suspected obstruction?
options: Upright and supine abdominal radiographs
Colonoscopy as the first step today
MRI of the pelvis as the first step today
HIDA scan as the first step today
Right upper quadrant ultrasound in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Upright and supine abdominal radiographs are the appropriate first step for suspected small bowel obstruction, showing dilated small-bowel loops (>3 cm) with multiple air-fluid levels and a paucity of colonic/rectal gas; CT abdomen/pelvis is then used to confirm the diagnosis, identify the transition point, assess severity, and detect ischemia or a closed loop. RUQ ultrasound evaluates the biliary tree. MRI pelvis is not the acute test for SBO. Colonoscopy evaluates the colon, not the small bowel, and would not relieve a proximal mechanical obstruction. HIDA scan assesses biliary/gallbladder function and is irrelevant to mechanical SBO.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
Upright abdominal X-ray: multiple dilated small-bowel loops with several air-fluid levels; minimal gas in the colon
CT abdomen/pelvis: dilated proximal small bowel with a transition point in the mid-ileum; collapsed distal bowel; adhesive band suspected; no closed loop, no pneumatosis, no portal venous gas, normal bowel-wall enhancement
WBC 8.6 ×10³/µL
Lactate 1.2 mmol/L
Na 137 mEq/L, K 3.5 mEq/L, creatinine 1.0 mg/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely underlying cause of her small bowel obstruction?
options: Crohn disease stricture
Incarcerated inguinal hernia
Gallstone ileus
Small bowel malignancy
Postoperative adhesions
stages.explanation: Postoperative adhesions are the most common cause of small bowel obstruction in patients with prior abdominal surgery; her open hysterectomy and appendectomy, recurrent self-limited episodes, and a CT showing a transition point with a suspected adhesive band without a mass make adhesions the clear leading diagnosis. An incarcerated hernia is excluded by exam (no palpable hernia) and CT. Crohn stricture would usually show bowel-wall thickening/inflammation and a relevant history. Small bowel malignancy is uncommon and typically shows a mass at the transition point. Gallstone ileus requires pneumobilia and an ectopic gallstone, none of which are present here.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She has a partial adhesive SBO with no signs of ischemia, strangulation, or perforation (normal lactate, normal bowel-wall enhancement, no peritonitis). What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Neostigmine infusion as initial therapy
Oral laxatives and discharge as initial therapy
Immediate exploratory laparotomy with adhesiolysis as initial therapy
Nonoperative trial: NPO, nasogastric tube decompression, IV fluids, electrolyte correction,
Colonic stent placement as initial therapy
stages.explanation: An uncomplicated adhesive SBO without ischemia or perforation is initially managed nonoperatively: bowel rest (NPO), nasogastric decompression to relieve distension and vomiting, IV fluid resuscitation, electrolyte correction, and serial abdominal exams with monitoring; many partial adhesive obstructions resolve within 24-72 hours. A water-soluble contrast challenge can be both prognostic and therapeutic. Immediate laparotomy is reserved for strangulation, ischemia, perforation, closed-loop obstruction, or failure of nonoperative management. Oral laxatives and discharge are unsafe with an active obstruction. Neostigmine treats colonic pseudo-obstruction. Colonic stents address obstructing colonic lesions, not small-bowel adhesions.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: On hospital day 2, despite NG decompression, she develops fever, worsening focal tenderness with guarding, tachycardia to 120, and a rising lactate to 3.8 mmol/L. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Urgent surgical exploration for suspected strangulation/ischemia
Repeat plain films and reassess in the morning
Start oral antibiotics and discharge as the leading concern
Administer neostigmine as the leading concern
Continue conservative management for another 48 hours
stages.explanation: New fever, focal peritoneal signs (guarding), tachycardia, and a rising lactate signal bowel strangulation/ischemia—a failure of nonoperative management—mandating urgent surgical exploration with adhesiolysis and resection of any nonviable bowel. Continuing conservative therapy in the face of evolving ischemia risks perforation, sepsis, and death. Oral antibiotics and discharge are dangerously inappropriate. Merely repeating films and waiting until morning delays life-saving surgery. Neostigmine treats colonic pseudo-obstruction and would not address mechanical strangulation; it could worsen the situation. The clinical and biochemical deterioration here is the classic trigger to convert from medical to operative management.

cases.diagnosis: Adhesive small bowel obstruction (with later strangulation requiring surgery)
pearls: Adhesions from prior abdominal surgery are the leading cause of small bowel obstruction; classic features are colicky pain, bilious vomiting, distension, and obstipation.
Plain films show dilated small-bowel loops with air-fluid levels; CT confirms the diagnosis, locates the transition point, and detects ischemia or closed-loop obstruction.
Uncomplicated partial adhesive SBO is managed nonoperatively (NPO, NG decompression, IV fluids, serial exams); a water-soluble contrast challenge can be diagnostic and therapeutic.
Fever, focal peritonitis, tachycardia, leukocytosis, or rising lactate signal strangulation/ischemia and mandate urgent surgery.

cases.title: A Ballooning Belly After Hip Surgery
cases.chiefComplaint: Massive abdominal distension developing over two days after recent surgery.
cases.history: A 77-year-old man in the ICU develops progressive, massive abdominal distension over two days following emergent hip fracture repair. He has had no bowel movement since surgery, with minimal flatus, and reports vague continuous abdominal discomfort but not the severe colicky pain typical of mechanical obstruction. He has not vomited. His course is complicated by pneumonia treated with antibiotics, electrolyte derangements, and management with opioids for pain. Past medical history includes Parkinson disease, chronic kidney disease, type 2 diabetes, and hypothyroidism. Medications include scheduled opioids, levothyroxine, carbidopa-levodopa, and recent anticholinergic agents. He has no prior abdominal surgery. He has no known drug allergies. He is bedbound postoperatively. There is no history of colorectal cancer. He is intermittently confused at baseline post-op. Nursing notes increasing abdominal girth and decreased oral intake. The team is concerned about the dramatic distension despite the absence of a clear mechanical cause, in the setting of immobility, opioids, electrolyte abnormalities, and serious illness.
cases.exam: Elderly post-op man with a strikingly distended, tympanitic abdomen. Bowel sounds are present but hypoactive. The abdomen is diffusely mildly tender without rebound, guarding, or rigidity; no localized severe tenderness. No surgical scars on the abdomen and no palpable hernias. Digital rectal exam reveals an empty rectal vault with no obstructing mass and no stool. No peritoneal signs are present at this time. He is mildly confused but arousable. Cardiopulmonary exam notable for decreased breath sounds at the right base. He appears uncomfortable from the distension rather than acutely peritonitic.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Massive colonic distension develops post-operatively without a mechanical cause. What is the essential first diagnostic step before treating presumed pseudo-obstruction?
options: Give a stimulant laxative and reassess in 48 hours as the first step today
Place a nasogastric tube and discharge from ICU as the first step today
Start neostigmine empirically as the first step today
Proceed directly to surgery as the first step today
Obtain imaging to exclude a mechanical obstruction
stages.explanation: Acute colonic pseudo-obstruction (Ogilvie syndrome) is a diagnosis that requires excluding a true mechanical obstruction first, so CT with contrast (or a water-soluble contrast enema) is essential to confirm colonic dilation without an obstructing lesion before treatment. Starting neostigmine empirically without excluding mechanical obstruction or distal toxic megacolon/ischemia risks harm. Going straight to surgery is premature in a functional disorder that often responds to medical therapy. A stimulant laxative with a 48-hour wait risks perforation in a markedly dilated colon. Placing an NG tube alone does not decompress the colon, and discharging from the ICU ignores a potentially life-threatening problem.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast: massive dilation of the cecum and ascending/transverse colon (cecal diameter 11 cm) with no transition point and no obstructing mass; gas extends to the rectum
No pneumatosis, no free air, normal bowel-wall enhancement
K 2.9 mEq/L, Mg 1.4 mg/dL, Ca 8.2 mg/dL
WBC 9.0 ×10³/µL, Lactate 1.3 mmol/L, creatinine 1.8 mg/dL (baseline elevated)

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His cecum is 11 cm with no mechanical obstruction and no ischemia/perforation. What is the most appropriate initial management of his acute colonic pseudo-obstruction?
options: Immediate subtotal colectomy as initial therapy
Supportive care: correct electrolytes , stop opioids/anticholinergics, NPO, NG
High-volume osmotic laxatives by mouth as initial therapy
Endoscopic detorsion with a rectal tube as initial therapy
Neostigmine bolus right now without any other measures as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Initial management of Ogilvie syndrome is conservative and addresses precipitants: aggressively correct hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, and hypocalcemia; discontinue opioids and anticholinergics; keep NPO; place NG and rectal tubes for decompression; treat underlying infection; and mobilize the patient. Many cases resolve with these measures when the cecum is under ~12 cm and there are no danger signs. Immediate subtotal colectomy is reserved for perforation or ischemia. Neostigmine is the pharmacologic treatment but is used after conservative measures fail (and after metabolic correction), not as the very first step ignoring electrolytes. Oral osmotic laxatives can worsen dilation and risk perforation. Endoscopic detorsion treats volvulus, not pseudo-obstruction.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After 48 hours of conservative care the cecum dilates to 12 cm and he still has no ischemia or perforation. What is the most appropriate pharmacologic therapy?
options: Intravenous neostigmine with continuous cardiac monitoring
Intravenous glycopyrrolate as the immediate next step
Intravenous metoclopramide alone before discharge planning
Subcutaneous octreotide as the immediate next step
Oral loperamide as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: When conservative measures fail and the cecum reaches roughly 10-12 cm without ischemia or perforation, IV neostigmine—an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor that boosts parasympathetic colonic motility—is the pharmacologic treatment of choice, producing rapid decompression in most patients; it must be given with continuous cardiac monitoring and atropine available because of the risk of bradycardia. Glycopyrrolate is an anticholinergic and would worsen colonic atony. Loperamide slows motility and is contraindicated. Octreotide is not standard therapy for Ogilvie syndrome. Metoclopramide is a prokinetic for the upper gut and is not effective monotherapy for colonic pseudo-obstruction; neostigmine is far more effective for the colon.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Neostigmine and decompression are tried, but the cecum now exceeds 12 cm and he develops focal right-sided tenderness with rising lactate. What complication must be urgently addressed?
options: Acute pancreatitis as the leading concern
Cecal ischemia and impending perforation requiring urgent colonoscopic decompression or surgery
Simple constipation as the leading concern
Gastroparesis as the leading concern
Spontaneous resolution as the leading concern
stages.explanation: A cecal diameter exceeding ~12 cm with new focal tenderness and rising lactate signals cecal ischemia and impending perforation—the most feared complication of Ogilvie syndrome—mandating urgent intervention: colonoscopic decompression if no overt perforation/ischemia, or surgery (cecostomy or resection) if perforation or frank ischemia is present. This is not spontaneous resolution, which would show decreasing distension and clinical improvement. It is not simple constipation, given the systemic and biochemical signs of ischemia. Gastroparesis affects the stomach and would not cause cecal perforation. Acute pancreatitis presents with epigastric pain, elevated lipase, and a different imaging picture, not isolated cecal dilation with focal right-sided ischemic signs.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which single best summarizes the threshold concept that should drive escalation of care throughout this patient's course?
options: Opioids should be continued to control discomfort with appropriate follow-up
Cecal diameter and signs of ischemia/perforation drive escalation from conservative care to neostigmine to colonoscopic decompression or surgery
Once neostigmine is given, no further monitoring is needed with appropriate follow-up
Treatment is guided solely by the patient's pain score with appropriate follow-up
Antibiotics resolve the colonic dilation with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: The escalation framework in acute colonic pseudo-obstruction is anchored to cecal diameter and signs of ischemia/perforation: conservative care and correction of precipitants first; neostigmine when the cecum reaches roughly 10-12 cm or conservative therapy fails; colonoscopic decompression when pharmacologic therapy fails; and surgery for perforation or ischemia. Pain score alone is unreliable, especially in confused, opioid-treated patients. Neostigmine requires ongoing monitoring (recurrence is common). Antibiotics treat infection but do not resolve the dilation itself. Continuing opioids perpetuates colonic atony and should be minimized. Recognizing the cecal-diameter and ischemia thresholds prevents the catastrophic outcome of perforation.

pearls: Ogilvie syndrome is massive colonic dilation without mechanical obstruction, typically in elderly, ill, immobile, post-op patients on opioids/anticholinergics with electrolyte derangements.
Always exclude mechanical obstruction (CT or water-soluble contrast enema) before treating; first-line therapy is supportive—correct electrolytes, stop offending drugs, decompress, mobilize.
IV neostigmine (with cardiac monitoring and atropine available) is the pharmacologic treatment when conservative measures fail; colonoscopic decompression is next.
Cecal diameter >12 cm or signs of ischemia/perforation are danger thresholds demanding urgent colonoscopic decompression or surgery.

cases.title: An Elderly Woman with Waxing and Waning Obstruction
cases.chiefComplaint: Several days of crampy abdominal pain, vomiting, and distension that seem to come and go.
cases.history: An 82-year-old woman presents with four days of intermittent crampy abdominal pain, nausea, and several episodes of vomiting, along with abdominal distension that seems to wax and wane. She has had decreasing oral intake and no bowel movement for two days. Notably, over the preceding weeks she had episodes of right upper quadrant pain, fever, and chills that she did not seek care for, which had partially resolved. She has a long history of cholelithiasis with recurrent biliary colic, declined cholecystectomy years ago, and has type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and chronic kidney disease. Medications include metformin, lisinopril, and aspirin 81 mg daily. She has no prior abdominal surgery and no known drug allergies. She does not smoke or drink. Family history is noncontributory. She lives alone and is normally independent. The episodic, partially relieving nature of her symptoms is striking, as if something is intermittently obstructing and then moving along the bowel. She appears mildly dehydrated and uncomfortable but is alert and able to give her history.
cases.exam: Elderly woman appearing mildly dehydrated and uncomfortable. Abdomen is distended and tympanitic with high-pitched bowel sounds in rushes. There is mild diffuse tenderness and mild right upper quadrant tenderness without rebound or guarding; no Murphy sign elicited today; no peritoneal signs. No surgical scars and no palpable abdominal-wall or groin hernias. Digital rectal exam reveals an empty rectal vault, no mass, heme-negative stool. Mucous membranes are dry. There is no jaundice. Cardiopulmonary exam is unremarkable apart from mild tachycardia attributable to pain and volume depletion. No flank tenderness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: She has features of small bowel obstruction with intermittent ('tumbling') symptoms and a history of gallstones. What is the most appropriate imaging to define the cause?
options: Upper GI endoscopy
Plain abdominal film only
Right upper quadrant ultrasound only
MRCP as the initial emergency study
CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast
stages.explanation: CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast is the most informative study: it confirms small bowel obstruction, localizes the transition point, and can detect the pathognomonic combination of pneumobilia, an ectopic gallstone, and a cholecystoenteric fistula. A plain film may show suggestive findings (Rigler triad: SBO + pneumobilia + ectopic gallstone) but is far less sensitive than CT. RUQ ultrasound alone evaluates the gallbladder but cannot fully characterize the obstruction or the migrated stone. Upper GI endoscopy does not reach the typical obstruction site (often the terminal ileum) and is not the diagnostic test for SBO. MRCP focuses on the biliary tree and is not the first-line emergency study for acute SBO.

stages.label: CT FINDINGS & LABS
stages.content:
CT abdomen/pelvis: dilated small-bowel loops with a transition point in the distal ileum caused by an obstructing 3.5 cm calcified gallstone (ectopic gallstone)
Pneumobilia (air in the biliary tree)
Cholecystoduodenal fistula identified; contracted, inflamed gallbladder
No free air, no pneumatosis, normal bowel-wall enhancement at this time
WBC 13.1 ×10³/µL, Lactate 1.5 mmol/L
Na 134 mEq/L, K 3.4 mEq/L, creatinine 1.6 mg/dL
Total bilirubin 1.0 mg/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The CT shows small bowel obstruction, pneumobilia, and an ectopic gallstone. What is this classic radiographic triad called, and what is the diagnosis?
options: Virchow triad — venous thrombosis
Rigler triad — gallstone ileus
Cushing triad — raised intracranial pressure
Beck triad — cardiac tamponade
Charcot triad — ascending cholangitis
stages.explanation: The combination of small bowel obstruction, pneumobilia, and an ectopic (intraluminal) gallstone is Rigler triad, diagnostic of gallstone ileus, in which a large gallstone erodes through an inflamed gallbladder into the gut (usually via a cholecystoduodenal fistula) and impacts in the bowel, classically at the narrow terminal ileum. Charcot triad (fever, jaundice, RUQ pain) indicates ascending cholangitis. Beck triad (hypotension, muffled heart sounds, JVD) indicates cardiac tamponade. Virchow triad describes thrombosis risk factors. Cushing triad (hypertension, bradycardia, irregular respirations) indicates raised intracranial pressure. The pneumobilia here reflects the biliary-enteric fistula, not infection.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After resuscitation, the obstructing stone remains impacted in the distal ileum. What is the definitive treatment of the obstruction?
options: Colonic stent placement as initial therapy
Ursodeoxycholic acid to dissolve the stone as initial therapy
Surgical enterolithotomy , the primary operation to relieve the obstruction
Endoscopic retrograde cholecystectomy as initial therapy
Long-term nasogastric decompression alone as initial therapy
stages.explanation: The definitive treatment of gallstone ileus is surgical relief of the mechanical obstruction by enterolithotomy—a longitudinal enterotomy proximal to the impaction to extract the stone. In elderly, comorbid patients, the priority is relieving the obstruction; concurrent cholecystectomy and fistula repair (one-stage) is more morbid and is reserved for selected fit patients, with many surgeons deferring biliary surgery. There is no such procedure as 'endoscopic retrograde cholecystectomy.' NG decompression alone does not clear a large impacted stone. Oral bile acids cannot dissolve a 3.5 cm calcified stone causing acute obstruction. Colonic stents address colonic lesions, not a small-bowel stone impaction.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: At operation, the surgeon performs enterolithotomy and finds the patient frail with significant comorbidities. What is the most appropriate decision regarding the cholecystoduodenal fistula and gallbladder?
options: It is acceptable to leave the fistula/gallbladder
Create a permanent biliary diversion as the immediate next step
Always perform immediate cholecystectomy and fistula takedown in every patient as the immediate next step
Ligate the common bile duct as the immediate next step
Remove the entire small bowel segment containing the fistula as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: In a frail, high-risk elderly patient, the accepted approach is a simple enterolithotomy alone, leaving the cholecystoduodenal fistula and gallbladder in place; many fistulae close spontaneously, and the added operative time/morbidity of one-stage cholecystectomy and fistula repair is not justified in poor-risk patients. Mandatory cholecystectomy in every patient is incorrect—it is individualized. The fistula is at the gallbladder-duodenum, so resecting a distant small-bowel segment is wrong. Permanent biliary diversion is unnecessary and excessive. Ligating the common bile duct would cause biliary obstruction and is never appropriate. Recurrent gallstone ileus risk is low but warrants follow-up; elective definitive biliary surgery can be considered later if the patient improves.

cases.diagnosis: Gallstone ileus (mechanical small bowel obstruction from an ectopic gallstone via a cholecystoenteric fistula)
pearls: Rigler triad—small bowel obstruction, pneumobilia, and an ectopic gallstone—is diagnostic of gallstone ileus; CT is far more sensitive than plain films.
Gallstone ileus results from a large stone eroding through an inflamed gallbladder into the gut (usually a cholecystoduodenal fistula) and impacting at the narrow terminal ileum.
Symptoms are often intermittent ('tumbling obstruction') as the stone advances; it classically affects elderly women with longstanding gallstones.
Definitive treatment is enterolithotomy; in frail patients leaving the fistula/gallbladder is acceptable, with one-stage cholecystectomy reserved for selected fit patients.

cases.title: A Confused Man With a Flapping Tremor
cases.chiefComplaint: Brought in by his wife for two days of worsening confusion and lethargy.
cases.history: A 59-year-old man with known alcohol-related cirrhosis (Child-Pugh B) is admitted after his wife noted progressive confusion, daytime sleepiness, and a reversed sleep-wake pattern over two days. He has become forgetful and slurs his words. He has a history of esophageal varices status post banding and was recently started on furosemide and spironolactone for ascites. He admits to missing several doses of his medications and reports constipation for four days. He stopped drinking alcohol six months ago. He has had no fever, cough, dysuria, hematemesis, or melena. Medications: spironolactone 100 mg daily, furosemide 40 mg daily, propranolol 20 mg twice daily. No recent sedatives or opioids. Allergies: none. Social: former heavy alcohol use (~6 beers/day for 25 years), quit 6 months ago; remote tobacco; no IV drug use. Family history: father with 'liver trouble.' His wife denies any falls or head trauma. He has not had a recent paracentesis or GI bleed that she is aware of.
cases.exam: The patient is somnolent but arousable, oriented to person only, with slow responses. Scleral icterus is present. There is spider angiomata over the chest and palmar erythema. When asked to hold his arms outstretched with wrists extended, there is a coarse flapping tremor (asterixis). The abdomen is distended with shifting dullness and a fluid wave; it is non-tender with no rebound or guarding. No caput medusae ulceration. Fetor hepaticus is noted. Neurologic exam shows no focal deficits, symmetric reflexes, and downgoing toes. No nuchal rigidity. No signs of active GI bleeding on rectal exam; stool is brown, guaiac negative.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Based on the presentation, what is the most likely cause of this patient's altered mental status?
options: Alcohol withdrawal delirium in this clinical context
Wernicke encephalopathy from thiamine deficiency
Hepatic encephalopathy
Subdural hematoma from an unwitnessed fall
stages.explanation: The combination of known cirrhosis, fluctuating confusion with reversed sleep-wake cycle, asterixis, and fetor hepaticus is classic for hepatic encephalopathy (HE), especially with identifiable precipitants (constipation, medication nonadherence). Asterixis and the absence of focal deficits point away from a structural lesion. Wernicke encephalopathy (confusion, ophthalmoplegia, ataxia) is possible in alcoholics but does not cause asterixis and is less consistent here. Subdural hematoma would typically show focal findings or a trauma history and lacks asterixis. Alcohol withdrawal delirium causes agitation, tremor, autonomic hyperactivity, and hallucinations, but this man quit six months ago and is somnolent rather than hyperadrenergic, making withdrawal very unlikely.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which of the following is the most important next step to guide management of his encephalopathy?
options: Search for and treat precipitating factors
Restrict dietary protein to less than 20 g/day
Obtain a serum ammonia level to confirm the diagnosis before treating
Order an urgent EEG to assess for triphasic waves
stages.explanation: HE is a clinical diagnosis; the priority is to identify and reverse precipitants—GI bleeding, infection (including SBP), constipation, electrolyte disturbances (hypokalemia, hyponatremia), dehydration, and medication nonadherence. Serum ammonia correlates poorly with HE grade and should not delay treatment or be used to confirm the diagnosis. EEG triphasic waves are nonspecific and not required. Protein restriction is outdated and harmful—it worsens the malnutrition and muscle wasting that impair ammonia handling; patients should receive adequate protein (1.2–1.5 g/kg/day). Therefore, the highest-yield step is an active search for treatable precipitants while initiating therapy.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 6.1 ×10³/µL
Hgb 11.8 g/dL
Platelets 92 ×10³/µL
Na 134 mEq/L
K 3.3 mEq/L
BUN 18 mg/dL
Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL
Total bilirubin 3.2 mg/dL
Albumin 2.7 g/dL
INR 1.6
AST 64 U/L  ALT 41 U/L
Venous ammonia 96 µmol/L (elevated)
Urinalysis: no pyuria, nitrite negative
Diagnostic paracentesis: PMN 60 cells/µL (no SBP)
CT head (non-contrast): no acute intracranial process

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacologic therapy to initiate now?
options: Lactulose titrated to 2–3 soft stools per day
Intravenous mannitol as initial therapy
Oral metronidazole as initial therapy
Oral neomycin as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Lactulose, a nonabsorbable disaccharide, is first-line for HE. It acidifies the colonic lumen (converting NH3 to non-absorbable NH4+) and acts as a cathartic, and is titrated to produce 2–3 soft stools daily—directly addressing this patient's constipation precipitant. Neomycin and metronidazole are older antibiotics with toxicity (oto/nephrotoxicity, neuropathy) and are not preferred. Mannitol is used for cerebral edema in acute liver failure, not for the diffuse, non-edematous encephalopathy of cirrhosis. Correcting his hypokalemia is also important, since hypokalemia increases renal ammoniagenesis. Lactulose is the cornerstone of acute treatment.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After 48 hours of lactulose his mentation improves. What therapy is best added to reduce his risk of recurrent episodes?
options: Lifelong oral neomycin as the immediate next step
Discontinue lactulose now that he has improved
Rifaximin 550 mg twice daily
Branched-chain amino acid infusions as primary therapy
stages.explanation: Rifaximin, a minimally absorbed antibiotic that reduces ammonia-producing gut flora, is added to lactulose for secondary prophylaxis and significantly reduces HE recurrence and HE-related hospitalizations. Neomycin is not used long-term given oto- and nephrotoxicity. Branched-chain amino acids may help select malnourished patients but are not first-line maintenance therapy and do not replace lactulose/rifaximin. Discontinuing lactulose would predispose to recurrence—maintenance lactulose titrated to 2–3 stools/day is continued indefinitely. The standard maintenance regimen after a first overt HE episode (or in recurrent disease) is lactulose plus rifaximin.

cases.diagnosis: Hepatic encephalopathy precipitated by constipation and diuretic-related hypokalemia in a patient with alcohol-related cirrhosis
pearls: Hepatic encephalopathy is a clinical diagnosis—do not wait for or rely on ammonia levels, which correlate poorly with severity.
Always hunt for precipitants: GI bleed, infection/SBP, constipation, hypokalemia, hyponatremia, dehydration, and nonadherence.
Lactulose titrated to 2–3 soft stools/day is first-line; add rifaximin for secondary prevention of recurrence.
Avoid protein restriction—maintain 1.2–1.5 g/kg/day protein to prevent muscle wasting that worsens ammonia handling.

cases.title: Fever and a Tense Belly in a Cirrhotic
cases.chiefComplaint: Admitted with two days of fever, diffuse abdominal pain, and worsening abdominal distension.
cases.history: A 61-year-old man with hepatitis C and alcohol-related cirrhosis (Child-Pugh C, MELD-Na 22) presents with two days of low-grade fevers, diffuse dull abdominal pain, and increasing abdominal girth despite his diuretics. Over the past day his family reports he has become more confused and sleepy. He denies vomiting, hematemesis, melena, dysuria, or diarrhea. He has chronic ascites requiring large-volume paracentesis roughly every 3 weeks; his last tap was 10 days ago. He has had one prior episode of variceal bleeding. Medications: spironolactone 100 mg daily, furosemide 40 mg daily, nadolol 40 mg daily, lactulose 30 mL three times daily. No recent antibiotics or proton pump inhibitor. Allergies: none. Social: former alcohol and tobacco use; remote injection drug use. Family history: noncontributory. He has not traveled recently and has had no sick contacts. He reports adherence to his medications and a low-sodium diet.
cases.exam: The patient is mildly confused and oriented to person and place only, with subtle asterixis. He is icteric. The abdomen is markedly distended and tense with a positive fluid wave and shifting dullness; there is diffuse mild tenderness without rebound or rigidity. Bowel sounds are present but hypoactive. No surgical scars suggesting prior perforation; no localized peritoneal signs to suggest a surgical abdomen. There are spider angiomata and gynecomastia. No flank ecchymosis. Lower extremities show 2+ pitting edema. No focal neurologic deficit and no meningismus.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the single most important diagnostic test to perform now?
options: CT abdomen with contrast to exclude bowel perforation
Abdominal ultrasound to quantify ascites volume
Blood cultures and broad-spectrum antibiotics, then observe
Diagnostic abdominal paracentesis with cell count
stages.explanation: Any cirrhotic patient with ascites who develops fever, abdominal pain, encephalopathy, or clinical deterioration must undergo prompt diagnostic paracentesis—this is the only way to diagnose spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP) and should not be delayed. Cell count with differential is the key initial study (ascitic PMN ≥250 cells/µL diagnoses SBP). Blood cultures are useful adjuncts but cannot diagnose SBP, and antibiotics should follow the diagnostic tap rather than precede it when the tap can be done immediately. Ultrasound only quantifies fluid. Contrast CT risks nephrotoxicity in a tenuous patient and is reserved for suspected secondary (surgical) peritonitis; the exam here lacks localized peritoneal signs.

stages.label: ASCITIC FLUID ANALYSIS
stages.content:
Ascitic WBC 820 cells/µL
Ascitic PMN 540 cells/µL (>250)
Ascitic total protein 0.8 g/dL
Ascitic albumin 0.5 g/dL  (serum albumin 2.4 g/dL → SAAG 1.9)
Ascitic glucose 88 mg/dL
Ascitic LDH 90 U/L (serum LDH 180 U/L)
Gram stain: pending
Blood cultures: drawn ×2
Serum creatinine 1.3 mg/dL
INR 1.9, total bilirubin 5.4 mg/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
