stages.explanation: The combination of PSC and ulcerative colitis markedly amplifies colorectal cancer risk above that of colitis alone, so guidelines recommend starting colonoscopic surveillance at the time of PSC diagnosis and repeating it annually with biopsies, regardless of colitis activity. Deferring to age 50 (option B) ignores this elevated risk; quiescent disease does not lower the surveillance need. Annual upper endoscopy for gastric cancer (option C) is not indicated. Low-dose CT chest (option D) is lung cancer screening for heavy smokers and is unrelated. The PSC-IBD phenotype, often with right-sided colonic predominance, is the rationale for intensified annual colonoscopy from the time PSC is recognized.
stages.hint: PSC plus colitis dramatically raises one luminal GI cancer risk, changing screening timing.

stages.label: TREATMENT
stages.question: Regarding disease-directed therapy for his liver disease, which statement is most accurate?
options: High-dose ursodeoxycholic acid (28-30 mg/kg/day) improves survival and is recommended in this setting
Prophylactic biliary stenting prevents progression to cirrhosis as the immediate next step
Long-term corticosteroids halt progression of the strictures as the immediate next step
No medical therapy reliably alters disease course
stages.explanation: No medical therapy has been proven to alter the natural history of primary sclerosing cholangitis; management is supportive, with endoscopic dilation of dominant strictures, treatment of cholangitis, and ultimately liver transplantation for end-stage disease, which offers the best long-term survival. High-dose ursodiol (option A) was studied and not only failed to improve outcomes but was associated with harm at very high doses, so it is not recommended. Corticosteroids (option B) help IgG4 cholangitis, not classic PSC. Prophylactic stenting (option D) does not prevent progression and risks complications. Recognizing that PSC lacks effective medical therapy and that transplant is definitive for advanced disease is high-yield.
stages.hint: There is no proven disease-modifying drug; one definitive intervention exists for end-stage disease.

cases.diagnosis: Primary sclerosing cholangitis with ulcerative colitis
pearls: Suspect PSC in any IBD patient (especially UC) with a cholestatic enzyme pattern; MRCP showing multifocal beaded strictures is diagnostic and AMA is negative.
Cholangiocarcinoma is the major malignancy of PSC (10-20% lifetime); surveil with MRCP/ultrasound and CA 19-9 and evaluate new dominant strictures promptly.
PSC plus colitis sharply increases colorectal cancer risk, so begin annual colonoscopic surveillance at PSC diagnosis.
No medical therapy alters PSC progression; care is supportive and liver transplantation is the definitive treatment for end-stage disease.

cases.title: Fatigue, Aching Joints, and New Diabetes
cases.chiefComplaint: A year of fatigue and aching hands, now with newly diagnosed diabetes.
cases.history: A 55-year-old man presents with about a year of progressive fatigue and joint aches, particularly in the second and third metacarpophalangeal joints of both hands, with morning stiffness. He was recently found to have type 2 diabetes on routine screening and started on metformin. He also reports decreased libido and mild erectile dysfunction over the past several months. He denies abdominal pain, jaundice, or significant alcohol intake (two beers monthly). No IV drug use, transfusions, or tattoos. Allergies: none. Medications: metformin 500 mg twice daily. Family history is striking: his older brother was 'diagnosed with iron in the liver' in his fifties, and his father died of liver disease attributed to alcohol, though he reportedly drank little. He works as an electrician, is of Northern European ancestry, and has noticed his skin looks more 'tanned' than usual despite limited sun exposure. Review of systems is positive for the bronzing of skin and mild reduction in exercise tolerance. He has no chest pain but reports occasional palpitations.
cases.exam: He has a diffuse bronze-gray skin pigmentation, most noticeable on sun-exposed and non-exposed areas. There is tenderness and mild bony enlargement of the second and third MCP joints bilaterally without active synovitis. The liver edge is palpable 2 cm below the costal margin and mildly firm; there is no splenomegaly, ascites, or stigmata of chronic liver disease such as spider angiomata or palmar erythema. Cardiac exam reveals a regular rhythm without murmurs or gallops. Testicular exam shows mildly small, soft testes. Neurologic exam is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which combination of screening tests best evaluates the suspected underlying disorder?
options: Alpha-1 antitrypsin level and phenotype
Fasting transferrin saturation
Anti-mitochondrial antibody and IgM
Serum ceruloplasmin and 24-hour urinary copper
stages.explanation: The constellation of fatigue, arthropathy of the second/third MCP joints, new diabetes, hypogonadism, bronze skin, hepatomegaly, and a family history of 'liver iron' is classic for hereditary hemochromatosis. The best initial screen is fasting transferrin saturation plus serum ferritin; a transferrin saturation above 45% with elevated ferritin warrants HFE genetic testing. Ceruloplasmin and urinary copper (option A) screen for Wilson disease, which presents younger with neuropsychiatric features. Alpha-1 antitrypsin testing (option C) evaluates for that deficiency, typically with emphysema. AMA and IgM (option D) target primary biliary cholangitis. Iron studies are the correct, high-yield first step for this iron-overload phenotype.
stages.hint: The phenotype screams iron overload; pick the iron-study screen.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Transferrin saturation 78% (ref 20-50%)
Serum ferritin 1450 ng/mL (ref 30-400)
Fasting glucose 162 mg/dL
HbA1c 7.4%
AST 62 U/L
ALT 70 U/L
Alkaline phosphatase 96 U/L
Total bilirubin 0.8 mg/dL
INR 1.0
HFE gene testing: homozygous C282Y mutation
Transthoracic echocardiogram: mild concentric left ventricular hypertrophy, normal ejection fraction

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Transferrin saturation is 78%, ferritin 1450, and HFE shows C282Y homozygosity. What is the diagnosis?
options: Secondary iron overload from transfusions
Hereditary hemochromatosis (HFE-related)
Porphyria cutanea tarda as the primary diagnosis
Nonalcoholic steatohepatitis with hyperferritinemia
stages.explanation: Elevated transferrin saturation and ferritin together with C282Y homozygosity on HFE testing confirm hereditary hemochromatosis, the most common HFE genotype causing clinical iron overload, especially in those of Northern European descent. Secondary (transfusional) iron overload (option A) is excluded by his lack of transfusions or chronic anemia. NASH (option C) can raise ferritin as an acute-phase reactant but does not cause a transferrin saturation of 78% with C282Y homozygosity. Porphyria cutanea tarda (option D) can coexist with mild iron overload and HFE mutations but presents with photosensitive blistering skin lesions, not this systemic iron-overload picture. The genotype plus iron indices clinch HHC.
stages.hint: The genetic test result names the disease directly.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the first-line treatment to reduce his iron burden?
options: Oral deferasirox as initial therapy
Therapeutic phlebotomy
A strict low-iron vegetarian diet alone
Subcutaneous deferoxamine infusions
stages.explanation: Therapeutic phlebotomy is first-line for hereditary hemochromatosis: regular venesection (often weekly initially) depletes iron stores, targeting a ferritin in the lower-normal range, then maintenance phlebotomy a few times yearly. It is inexpensive, effective, and improves fatigue, skin pigmentation, and survival, and can reverse early hepatic fibrosis. Iron chelators such as deferasirox (option A) or deferoxamine (option C) are reserved for patients who cannot tolerate phlebotomy (e.g., severe anemia or poor venous access), as in transfusional overload. Dietary change alone (option D) is insufficient to remove established iron stores, though avoiding iron and vitamin C supplements and limiting alcohol are reasonable adjuncts. Phlebotomy is the clear answer.
stages.hint: The cheapest, oldest, and most effective therapy simply removes blood.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which surveillance is most important given his ferritin and likely hepatic involvement?
options: Annual brain MRI for iron deposition as the leading concern
Routine bone marrow biopsy to monitor iron as the leading concern
Assessment for cirrhosis
Annual chest CT for lung cancer as the leading concern
stages.explanation: A ferritin above 1000 ng/mL with elevated transaminases predicts a higher likelihood of advanced fibrosis or cirrhosis, so assessing the degree of liver fibrosis (elastography or biopsy) is important. If cirrhosis is established, the patient needs hepatocellular carcinoma surveillance with ultrasound every 6 months, because cirrhotic hemochromatosis carries a substantially increased HCC risk that persists even after iron removal. Chest CT (option A) and brain MRI (option C) are not indicated by this presentation. Bone marrow biopsy (option D) is not used to monitor iron in hemochromatosis. Counseling family members for HFE testing and screening for cardiac and endocrine involvement are also part of comprehensive care, but HCC surveillance in cirrhosis is the key oncologic concern.
stages.hint: Ferritin >1000 with high transaminases flags advanced liver disease and its cancer risk.

pearls: Classic HHC triad-plus: fatigue, arthropathy (2nd/3rd MCP joints), diabetes, hypogonadism, bronze skin, and cardiomyopathy in Northern European patients.
Screen with fasting transferrin saturation and ferritin; saturation >45% with high ferritin prompts HFE testing, and C282Y homozygosity confirms.
Therapeutic phlebotomy is first-line; chelation is reserved for those unable to tolerate phlebotomy.
Ferritin >1000 ng/mL with elevated transaminases predicts cirrhosis; cirrhotic patients need HCC surveillance and first-degree relatives should be screened.

cases.title: A Young Man with Tremor and Abnormal Liver Tests
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening hand tremor and abnormal liver enzymes with recent personality changes.
cases.history: A 21-year-old college student is referred for abnormal liver enzymes discovered during evaluation of a new resting and action tremor of both hands. Over the past 6 months his roommates and family have noted slurred speech, deteriorating handwriting, and uncharacteristic irritability and declining academic performance. He has had two episodes of jaundice over the past year that resolved. He denies alcohol or drug use, acetaminophen overuse, or hepatotoxic supplements. Allergies: none. He takes no regular medications. Family history is notable for a maternal aunt who died young of an unexplained 'liver and brain disease' and parents who are second cousins. He is of Mediterranean descent. Review of systems reveals mild dysphagia for liquids, increased clumsiness, and depressed mood with one episode of suicidal ideation that prompted his college counselor to refer him. He has no fevers, recent travel, or risky sexual behavior. He reports occasional difficulty with fine-motor tasks like buttoning shirts.
cases.exam: He has mild scleral icterus. Neurologic exam shows a coarse postural and kinetic tremor of both hands, mild dysarthria, and bradykinesia with cogwheel-like rigidity; gait is mildly wide-based. Affect is flat. The liver span is mildly increased and the spleen tip is palpable. There is no asterixis at rest. Slit-lamp examination by ophthalmology demonstrates golden-brown rings at the corneal limbus. There is no jaundice of the palms, no spider angiomata, and no significant peripheral edema. Mental status shows slowed processing but he is oriented.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given hepatic and neuropsychiatric findings in a young adult, which initial screening test is most appropriate?
options: Serum acetaminophen level
Serum ceruloplasmin
Serum ferritin and transferrin saturation
Anti-smooth muscle antibody
stages.explanation: A young patient with combined liver disease and movement/psychiatric symptoms and possible Kayser-Fleischer rings should be evaluated for Wilson disease (hepatolenticular degeneration). Low serum ceruloplasmin is the standard initial screen, though it can be normal in up to a third of patients because it is an acute-phase reactant, so it is interpreted alongside 24-hour urinary copper and slit-lamp exam. Iron studies (option B) screen for hemochromatosis, which presents later in life without neuropsychiatric features. ASMA (option C) targets autoimmune hepatitis. Acetaminophen level (option D) is reasonable in acute liver failure but does not explain his chronic neuropsychiatric decline or corneal rings. Ceruloplasmin is the correct first screen.
stages.hint: Think of the copper-carrying protein when liver and brain are both involved in a young person.

stages.label: LABS & STUDIES
stages.content:
Ceruloplasmin 8 mg/dL (ref 20-35)
24-hour urinary copper 180 mcg (ref <40)
AST 88 U/L
ALT 76 U/L
Alkaline phosphatase 52 U/L (relatively low)
Total bilirubin 3.2 mg/dL
Haptoglobin low; reticulocytes elevated (Coombs-negative hemolysis)
Slit-lamp: Kayser-Fleischer rings confirmed bilaterally
Brain MRI: T2 hyperintensity in the basal ganglia ('face of the giant panda' sign in midbrain)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Ceruloplasmin is low, urinary copper elevated, and KF rings present. Which finding combination is most specific to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Positive anti-mitochondrial antibody as the primary diagnosis
Low ceruloplasmin alone as the primary diagnosis
Kayser-Fleischer rings plus low ceruloplasmin, supported by elevated 24-hour urinary copper
Elevated transferrin saturation as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Wilson disease is diagnosed by combining features: Kayser-Fleischer rings together with a low ceruloplasmin are highly suggestive, and elevated 24-hour urinary copper excretion (often >100 mcg) supports the diagnosis; elevated hepatic copper on biopsy or genetic ATP7B testing can confirm difficult cases. A relatively low alkaline phosphatase and Coombs-negative hemolytic anemia are classic clues. Low ceruloplasmin alone (option A) is insufficient because it can be low in other states and normal in some Wilson patients. Elevated transferrin saturation (option C) indicates iron overload, not copper. AMA (option D) indicates PBC. The neuropsychiatric picture with KF rings makes the copper-overload diagnosis, and the supportive copper studies confirm it.
stages.hint: No single test is enough; combine the eye finding, the protein, and urine copper.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has predominant neurologic symptoms. Which initial chelation strategy is most appropriate?
options: High-dose penicillamine started immediately at full dose as initial therapy
Trientine with careful titration, plus zinc, while monitoring for neurologic worsening
Zinc monotherapy as sole initial therapy for symptomatic neurologic disease as monotherapy
Immediate liver transplantation before any medical therapy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Symptomatic Wilson disease requires copper removal with a chelator. In patients with prominent neurologic disease, trientine is often preferred over penicillamine because penicillamine can paradoxically worsen neurologic symptoms early in up to 10-20%; chelation is titrated carefully with monitoring, and zinc (which blocks intestinal copper absorption) may be combined or used for maintenance. Full-dose penicillamine immediately (option A) risks neurologic deterioration. Zinc monotherapy (option C) is generally reserved for maintenance or presymptomatic patients, not initial therapy of symptomatic neurologic disease. Transplantation (option D) is reserved for acute liver failure or decompensated cirrhosis refractory to medical therapy, not first-line here. Lifelong therapy and a low-copper diet (avoiding shellfish, organ meats, nuts) are essential.
stages.hint: Penicillamine can worsen the brain early; favor a gentler chelator with monitoring.

stages.label: SCREENING
stages.question: Which recommendation regarding his family is most appropriate?
options: No family screening is needed because the disease is sporadic in this setting
Screen relatives only if they develop symptoms as the immediate next step
Only screen his children, not his siblings as the immediate next step
Screen first-degree relatives for Wilson disease, as it is autosomal recessive
stages.explanation: Wilson disease is an autosomal recessive disorder caused by ATP7B mutations, and siblings of an affected patient have a 25% chance of being affected. First-degree relatives, especially siblings, should be screened with ceruloplasmin, liver enzymes, slit-lamp exam, and ATP7B genetic testing when available, because early presymptomatic treatment prevents irreversible disease. Saying it is sporadic (option A) is incorrect. Screening only children (option C) misses the higher-risk siblings; children of an affected patient are usually carriers unless the other parent also carries a mutation. Waiting for symptoms (option D) defeats the purpose, since treating before organ damage is the goal. Proactive family screening is standard of care.
stages.hint: Autosomal recessive means a sibling has a 1-in-4 chance; screen them before symptoms.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If this patient instead presented with acute liver failure, which feature would most suggest Wilson disease as the cause?
options: Coombs-negative hemolytic anemia with a low alkaline phosphatase-to-bilirubin ratio
Very high transaminases (>3000 U/L) with negative viral serologies
Markedly elevated alkaline phosphatase with high alkaline phosphatase-to-bilirubin ratio
Eosinophilia with a drug-reaction rash as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Acute liver failure from Wilson disease characteristically combines Coombs-negative (nonimmune) hemolytic anemia from sudden copper release with a disproportionately low alkaline phosphatase and a low alkaline phosphatase-to-total-bilirubin ratio (<4), along with a relatively modest AST/ALT elevation and high bilirubin. A high alkaline phosphatase (option A) is the opposite of the classic pattern. Transaminases in Wilsonian acute liver failure are often not as strikingly elevated as in viral or ischemic hepatitis (option C). Eosinophilia with rash (option D) suggests a drug hypersensitivity reaction. Recognizing the low ALP, hemolysis, and low ALP/bilirubin ratio identifies fulminant Wilson disease, which is uniformly fatal without urgent liver transplantation.
stages.hint: Look for hemolysis plus a paradoxically low alkaline phosphatase relative to bilirubin.

pearls: Suspect Wilson disease in a patient under 40 with combined liver disease and neuropsychiatric or movement symptoms; KF rings and low ceruloplasmin are key clues.
Diagnosis combines KF rings, low ceruloplasmin, and high 24-hour urinary copper; ATP7B genetics or hepatic copper confirm difficult cases.
For neurologic-predominant disease, favor trientine over penicillamine (which can worsen neuro symptoms); zinc is used for maintenance/presymptomatic patients.
Fulminant Wilson disease shows Coombs-negative hemolysis with low alkaline phosphatase and a low ALP-to-bilirubin ratio and requires urgent transplantation; screen siblings.

cases.title: Cirrhosis and Breathlessness in a Lifelong Nonsmoker
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive shortness of breath and abnormal liver tests in a man who never smoked.
cases.history: A 43-year-old man is referred for cirrhosis of uncertain cause and worsening exertional dyspnea over 2 years. He has never smoked and has minimal alcohol intake (one or two drinks monthly). He has no history of viral hepatitis risk factors, obesity, or diabetes; his metabolic workup for fatty liver was unremarkable. He reports a chronic productive cough and dyspnea climbing one flight of stairs, attributed previously to 'asthma.' He denies abdominal pain but has noticed increasing abdominal girth and mild ankle swelling. Allergies: none. Medications: an albuterol inhaler used occasionally. Family history is notable for a brother with early-onset emphysema (diagnosed in his forties, also a nonsmoker) and a father who died of liver disease. He works in an office with no significant occupational exposures. Review of systems is positive for fatigue and easy bruising. On further questioning, his prior pulmonary function tests reportedly showed airflow obstruction with hyperinflation at the lung bases, which puzzled his prior physicians given his age and never-smoker status.
cases.exam: He is mildly dyspneic at rest with a prolonged expiratory phase and diminished breath sounds, most pronounced at the lung bases. There is mild scleral icterus and a few spider angiomata on the chest. The abdomen is distended with shifting dullness consistent with ascites; the liver edge is firm and nodular and the spleen tip is palpable. There is mild bilateral pedal edema and palmar erythema. No asterixis is present. Cardiac exam reveals no murmur. Digital clubbing is absent. He has scattered ecchymoses on the extremities.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Cirrhosis plus basilar-predominant emphysema in a young never-smoker most strongly suggests which underlying disorder, and what is the best initial test?
options: Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency
Sarcoidosis; serum ACE level as the first step today
Cystic fibrosis; sweat chloride testing as the first step today
Hereditary hemochromatosis; transferrin saturation
stages.explanation: The combination of unexplained cirrhosis and early-onset, basilar-predominant emphysema (panacinar) in a never-smoker, with a family history of early emphysema and liver disease, is the textbook presentation of alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency. The best initial test is a serum alpha-1 antitrypsin level, followed by phenotyping (Pi typing) or genotyping to identify the deficient alleles (most commonly the Z allele, ZZ genotype). Cystic fibrosis (option A) presents earlier with bronchiectasis, sinus disease, and pancreatic insufficiency. Sarcoidosis (option C) causes upper-lobe disease and granulomas, not basilar emphysema with cirrhosis. Hemochromatosis (option D) does not cause emphysema. Recognizing the lung-liver combination points directly to alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency.
stages.hint: Basilar (not apical) emphysema in a nonsmoker plus liver disease points to one missing protease inhibitor.

stages.label: LABS & STUDIES
stages.content:
Serum alpha-1 antitrypsin 32 mg/dL (ref 100-200)
Pi typing: ZZ (homozygous)
AST 70 U/L
ALT 64 U/L
Total bilirubin 2.4 mg/dL
Albumin 2.9 g/dL
INR 1.5
Platelets 92 ×10³/µL
PFTs: FEV1/FVC reduced, increased TLC, decreased DLCO
CT chest: panacinar emphysema, basilar predominant
Liver biopsy (later): PAS-positive, diastase-resistant globules in periportal hepatocytes

stages.label: PATHOLOGY
stages.question: Which liver histology finding is characteristic of this disorder?
options: Mallory-Denk bodies with neutrophilic infiltration
Florid bile duct lesions with granulomas in this setting
Hepatic iron deposition with Prussian blue staining
PAS-positive, diastase-resistant globules in hepatocytes
stages.explanation: In alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency, the abnormal Z protein misfolds and accumulates within hepatocytes as intracellular globules that are periodic acid-Schiff (PAS) positive and resistant to diastase digestion, a pathognomonic finding. These accumulated polymers cause hepatocellular injury and can lead to cirrhosis. Mallory-Denk bodies (option A) are seen in alcoholic and nonalcoholic steatohepatitis. Hepatic iron with Prussian blue staining (option C) indicates hemochromatosis. Florid bile duct lesions with granulomas (option D) characterize primary biliary cholangitis. The PAS-positive, diastase-resistant globules reflect retained misfolded antitrypsin and confirm the hepatic manifestation of the disease.
stages.hint: Retained misfolded protein forms granules that survive diastase and stain with PAS.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which intervention is appropriate for his lung disease, and which is NOT effective for his liver disease?
options: Intravenous alpha-1 antitrypsin augmentation therapy may help the lungs but does NOT treat the liver disease
Augmentation therapy reverses established hepatic cirrhosis as initial therapy
Inhaled corticosteroids cure both lung and liver involvement as initial therapy
Phlebotomy reduces hepatic protein accumulation as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Intravenous alpha-1 antitrypsin augmentation (pooled human protein) can slow progression of emphysema in selected patients with significant lung disease, but it does not treat the liver disease, because hepatic injury results from intracellular accumulation of misfolded protein rather than from circulating deficiency. Augmentation does not reverse cirrhosis (option B). Inhaled corticosteroids (option C) may help airway symptoms but cure neither organ. Phlebotomy (option D) treats iron overload, not antitrypsin accumulation. The cornerstone for lung disease is smoking avoidance, bronchodilators, vaccination, and augmentation in appropriate candidates, while liver disease is managed supportively, with liver transplantation being curative for end-stage hepatic disease (and it also corrects the protein deficiency).
stages.hint: Augmentation addresses the circulating deficiency in the lungs, but liver injury is from intracellular buildup.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Given his decompensated cirrhosis (ascites, INR 1.5, low albumin, thrombocytopenia), what is the definitive treatment for end-stage liver disease in this condition?
options: Lung transplantation alone with appropriate follow-up
Long-term corticosteroids with appropriate follow-up
Liver transplantation, which also corrects the protein deficiency
Repeated large-volume paracentesis indefinitely
stages.explanation: Liver transplantation is the definitive treatment for end-stage liver disease in alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency and has the added benefit of correcting the protein deficiency, because the transplanted liver produces normal alpha-1 antitrypsin and halts further pulmonary protease damage from deficiency. Repeated paracentesis (option A) is only palliative for ascites and does not address the underlying failing liver. Corticosteroids (option C) have no role in treating this cirrhosis. Lung transplantation alone (option D) would not address his decompensated liver disease and would leave the hepatic source of the abnormal protein in place. His decompensation markers (ascites, coagulopathy, hypoalbuminemia, thrombocytopenia) indicate the need for transplant evaluation and MELD-based listing.
stages.hint: Replacing the failing organ also fixes the source of the missing protein.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: What is the single most important lifestyle counseling point for this patient?
options: Avoidance of all physical activity in this setting
Daily aspirin to protect the liver in this setting
Strict avoidance of tobacco smoke
High-iron diet to support red cell production
stages.explanation: Complete avoidance of tobacco smoke, including secondhand smoke, is the single most important intervention, because smoking dramatically accelerates emphysema progression and markedly shortens survival in alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency by inactivating the small amount of residual antitrypsin and amplifying neutrophil elastase-mediated lung destruction. A high-iron diet (option B) is irrelevant and potentially harmful in liver disease. Daily aspirin (option C) is not protective and may worsen bleeding in cirrhosis with thrombocytopenia and coagulopathy. Avoiding all activity (option D) is unnecessary and harmful; appropriate activity and pulmonary rehabilitation are beneficial. Vaccination against respiratory pathogens and alcohol avoidance are also important, but never smoking is paramount for these patients.
stages.hint: One exposure accelerates lung destruction faster than anything else in this disease.

cases.diagnosis: Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency (PiZZ) with cirrhosis and emphysema
pearls: Suspect alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency with unexplained cirrhosis plus early basilar (panacinar) emphysema in a never-smoker; confirm with serum level and Pi typing (ZZ).
Liver histology shows PAS-positive, diastase-resistant globules from retained misfolded Z protein.
IV augmentation therapy can slow lung disease but does NOT treat liver disease, which arises from intracellular protein accumulation.
Liver transplantation is definitive for end-stage liver disease and corrects the deficiency; absolute tobacco avoidance is the most important counseling point.

cases.title: A New Liver Nodule on Surveillance Imaging
cases.chiefComplaint: A liver nodule discovered on routine surveillance ultrasound.
cases.history: A 63-year-old man with known hepatitis C-related cirrhosis (sustained virologic response achieved 3 years ago after direct-acting antiviral therapy) presents to discuss a new nodule found on his semiannual surveillance ultrasound. He feels generally well, with stable energy and appetite, and denies abdominal pain, weight loss, early satiety, or jaundice. He has had no variceal bleeding and takes nadolol for portal hypertension. He drinks no alcohol now (history of moderate use, quit 5 years ago). Allergies: none. Medications: nadolol 40 mg daily and a daily multivitamin. His cirrhosis is well compensated (Child-Pugh A). Family history is noncontributory for liver cancer. He is a retired teacher and former smoker. Review of systems is otherwise negative. He is anxious because the ultrasound report mentioned a 2.5 cm lesion in the right hepatic lobe that was not present on his prior study 6 months ago, and he wants to know what it means and what should be done next.
cases.exam: He is well-appearing and not jaundiced. The abdomen is soft and nontender; the liver is firm with a nodular edge consistent with cirrhosis, and there is no palpable mass or hepatomegaly beyond the cirrhotic contour. The spleen tip is just palpable. There is no ascites by shifting dullness, no caput medusae, and only a single small spider angioma on the chest. There is no asterixis and he is fully alert and oriented. No peripheral edema. Cardiopulmonary exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A 2.5 cm nodule is found in a cirrhotic liver on surveillance ultrasound. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Immediate percutaneous biopsy of the nodule
Begin systemic chemotherapy empirically
Multiphase contrast-enhanced CT or MRI of the liver
Repeat ultrasound in 6 months in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Any nodule 1 cm or larger detected on surveillance ultrasound in a cirrhotic patient warrants prompt characterization with multiphase contrast-enhanced CT or MRI, which can diagnose hepatocellular carcinoma noninvasively using the LI-RADS system based on imaging features. Repeating ultrasound in 6 months (option A) is appropriate only for nodules under 1 cm. Immediate biopsy (option C) is not first-line because diagnostic imaging features often establish HCC without biopsy and biopsy carries small risks of bleeding and tract seeding; it is reserved for indeterminate lesions. Empiric chemotherapy (option D) before a diagnosis is inappropriate. Multiphase cross-sectional imaging is the standard next step to characterize this nodule.
stages.hint: A nodule ≥1 cm in cirrhosis needs dedicated multiphase imaging, not just a repeat ultrasound.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) 320 ng/mL (ref <10)
AST 44 U/L
ALT 40 U/L
Total bilirubin 1.0 mg/dL
Albumin 3.8 g/dL
INR 1.1
Platelets 110 ×10³/µL
Multiphase MRI: 2.5 cm right-lobe lesion with arterial-phase hyperenhancement and washout on portal venous/delayed phases with an enhancing capsule (LI-RADS 5)
No macrovascular invasion; no extrahepatic disease on staging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The MRI shows arterial hyperenhancement with washout and a capsule (LI-RADS 5) in a 2.5 cm lesion, with AFP 320. What does this establish?
options: Hemangioma; reassurance only as the primary diagnosis
Metastatic colon cancer; colonoscopy required before any treatment
Definite hepatocellular carcinoma
Focal nodular hyperplasia; no further action
stages.explanation: In a patient with cirrhosis, a lesion showing nonrim arterial-phase hyperenhancement with washout and an enhancing capsule meets LI-RADS 5 criteria, which is essentially diagnostic of hepatocellular carcinoma; biopsy is not required and the diagnosis can be made on imaging alone, here further supported by a markedly elevated AFP. A hemangioma (option B) shows peripheral nodular discontinuous enhancement with progressive centripetal fill-in, not washout. Focal nodular hyperplasia (option C) typically shows a central scar and no washout, and is uncommon in cirrhosis. Metastatic colon cancer (option D) would be unusual to diagnose by these features and is far less likely than HCC in this surveillance setting. The classic imaging pattern confirms HCC.
stages.hint: Arterial enhancement plus washout plus capsule in cirrhosis is a noninvasive HCC diagnosis.

stages.label: STAGING
stages.question: He has a single 2.5 cm tumor, no vascular invasion, no extrahepatic spread, Child-Pugh A, and good performance status. What is his BCLC stage?
options: BCLC stage D (terminal)
BCLC stage 0/A
BCLC stage C (advanced)
BCLC stage B (intermediate)
stages.explanation: Using the Barcelona Clinic Liver Cancer (BCLC) system, a solitary tumor (or up to three nodules ≤3 cm) without vascular invasion or extrahepatic spread, with preserved liver function (Child-Pugh A) and good performance status, is early-stage disease (BCLC 0 or A), eligible for potentially curative therapy. Stage B (option B) denotes multinodular disease beyond curative criteria but still compensated. Stage C (option C) requires vascular invasion, extrahepatic spread, or performance-status impairment. Stage D (option D) reflects end-stage liver function or very poor performance status. His single small tumor with compensated cirrhosis places him in the early curable category, which determines the appropriate treatment pathway.
stages.hint: Single small tumor, no spread, preserved liver function and performance status = the earliest band.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which treatment offers a potentially curative option for this early-stage HCC?
options: Systemic atezolizumab plus bevacizumab as monotherapy
Transarterial chemoembolization (TACE) as the only option
Surgical resection, ablation, or liver transplantation
Best supportive care/hospice as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Early-stage HCC (BCLC 0/A) is treated with potentially curative modalities: surgical resection (favored when liver function is preserved and there is no clinically significant portal hypertension), local ablation such as radiofrequency or microwave ablation (effective for tumors ≤3 cm), or liver transplantation (ideal when within Milan criteria, as it treats both the tumor and the underlying cirrhosis). Systemic atezolizumab/bevacizumab (option A) is first-line for advanced (BCLC C) disease. Best supportive care/hospice (option C) is for terminal stage D. TACE (option D) is the standard for intermediate-stage (BCLC B) disease, not curative-intent for a single small early tumor. Given his single small lesion and compensated cirrhosis, a curative-intent approach is appropriate.
stages.hint: Early-stage disease gets curative-intent options: cut it out, burn it, or transplant.

cases.diagnosis: Hepatocellular carcinoma (BCLC early stage) in HCV cirrhosis
pearls: In cirrhosis, a surveillance nodule ≥1 cm needs multiphase CT/MRI; <1 cm gets short-interval ultrasound follow-up.
LI-RADS 5 (arterial hyperenhancement + washout + capsule) is diagnostic of HCC without biopsy; AFP supports but does not establish the diagnosis.
BCLC staging integrates tumor burden, vascular invasion/spread, liver function (Child-Pugh), and performance status to guide therapy.
Early-stage HCC (BCLC 0/A) is treated with curative-intent resection, ablation, or transplantation; cured-HCV cirrhotics still need lifelong HCC surveillance.

cases.title: Fever and Right-Sided Pain After a Biliary Procedure
cases.chiefComplaint: Fever, chills, and right-upper-quadrant pain a week after a bile-duct procedure.
cases.history: A 60-year-old man is admitted with 4 days of fevers to 39 °C, rigors, and right-upper-quadrant pain. He underwent endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) with sphincterotomy and stone extraction 8 days ago for choledocholithiasis. He initially improved but then developed worsening malaise, anorexia, and drenching night sweats. He has type 2 diabetes (on metformin) and hypertension. He denies diarrhea, recent travel, or sick contacts. Allergies: penicillin causes a rash. Medications: metformin 1000 mg twice daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily. He drinks alcohol rarely and does not use IV drugs. Family history is noncontributory. He is a retired postal worker. Review of systems is positive for fevers, right-sided abdominal pain that worsens with deep inspiration, and mild shortness of breath. He has lost 4 pounds. He reports the pain sometimes radiates to the right shoulder. He has had no jaundice since the ERCP cleared his duct stones, and his stools and urine are normal in color.
cases.exam: He appears ill and diaphoretic. There is tender hepatomegaly with the liver edge palpable 4 cm below the costal margin and marked tenderness to percussion over the right lower ribs and right upper quadrant. There is dullness to percussion at the right lung base with decreased breath sounds, suggesting a sympathetic effusion. No Murphy sign or rebound tenderness. There is no scleral icterus. Bowel sounds are normal. No splenomegaly or ascites. Skin shows no rash or stigmata of chronic liver disease. He is alert and oriented.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given fever, RUQ pain, and tender hepatomegaly after recent ERCP, which is the most appropriate initial imaging study?
options: Plain abdominal radiograph as the first step today
Right-upper-quadrant ultrasound or contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen
Barium swallow as the first step today
Nuclear hepatobiliary (HIDA) scan as the first step today
stages.explanation: Fever, rigors, RUQ pain, and tender hepatomegaly after a recent biliary procedure raise concern for a pyogenic liver abscess from ascending biliary infection. The best initial imaging is right-upper-quadrant ultrasound or, preferably, contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen, which can identify and characterize an abscess and guide drainage. A plain radiograph (option A) is insensitive for hepatic abscess. A barium swallow (option C) evaluates the esophagus and is irrelevant here. A HIDA scan (option D) assesses cystic-duct patency for acute cholecystitis, not abscess. Cross-sectional imaging with CT (or ultrasound) is the appropriate first step to localize the suspected hepatic collection and plan intervention.
stages.hint: You suspect a collection in the liver; image it with ultrasound or CT to find and drain it.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 18.4 ×10³/µL (88% neutrophils)
Hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL
Alkaline phosphatase 268 U/L
AST 70 U/L
ALT 66 U/L
Total bilirubin 1.6 mg/dL
Lactate 1.8 mmol/L
Blood cultures: pending
Contrast CT abdomen: 6 cm rim-enhancing, fluid-density lesion in the right hepatic lobe with surrounding edema; small right pleural effusion
Chest X-ray: elevated right hemidiaphragm with small effusion

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: CT shows a 6 cm rim-enhancing fluid collection in the right lobe with neutrophilic leukocytosis after ERCP. What is the most likely diagnosis and likely route of infection?
options: Amebic liver abscess from fecal-oral exposure in this clinical context
Simple hepatic cyst as the primary diagnosis
Pyogenic liver abscess, most likely from ascending biliary infection
Hepatocellular carcinoma with central necrosis in this clinical context
stages.explanation: A rim-enhancing fluid collection with neutrophilic leukocytosis and systemic sepsis following ERCP for choledocholithiasis is a pyogenic liver abscess, and the most likely route is ascending biliary infection (the biliary tree is the most common source in the modern era, especially after instrumentation or obstruction). Amebic abscess (option A) is associated with travel to endemic regions and fecal-oral exposure, not recent ERCP, and is usually solitary in the right lobe without the same biliary context. A simple hepatic cyst (option C) does not produce rim enhancement, fever, or leukocytosis. HCC with necrosis (option D) is unlikely without cirrhosis and would not explain the acute septic picture. The post-ERCP biliary source makes pyogenic abscess the answer.
stages.hint: Recent duct instrumentation plus a pus-filled rim-enhancing collection points to a bacterial, biliary source.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of management for this 6 cm pyogenic liver abscess?
options: Antibiotics alone without any drainage as initial therapy
Metronidazole monotherapy without drainage as initial therapy
Image-guided percutaneous drainage plus broad-spectrum antibiotics
Immediate hepatic lobectomy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: The cornerstone of pyogenic liver abscess management combines source control through image-guided percutaneous drainage (needle aspiration or catheter placement, generally favored for abscesses larger than about 3-5 cm) with broad-spectrum IV antibiotics covering enteric gram-negatives and anaerobes (e.g., a beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor or a carbapenem; given his penicillin allergy, a fluoroquinolone plus metronidazole or a carbapenem after allergy assessment). Antibiotics alone (option A) may suffice only for small abscesses but a 6 cm collection generally needs drainage. Metronidazole monotherapy (option C) treats amebic, not pyogenic, abscess and lacks gram-negative coverage. Immediate lobectomy (option D) is reserved for failure of percutaneous and antibiotic therapy or specific complications, not first-line. Drainage plus antibiotics is standard.
stages.hint: A large pus collection needs both drainage and antibiotics, not antibiotics alone.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Aspirate and blood cultures should be sent. Which organisms are most commonly implicated in pyogenic liver abscess?
options: Enteric gram-negative bacilli
Mycobacterium tuberculosis as the immediate next step
Candida species exclusively as the immediate next step
Entamoeba histolytica and Giardia as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Pyogenic liver abscesses are most often polymicrobial or caused by enteric gram-negative bacilli, especially Escherichia coli and Klebsiella pneumoniae (the latter increasingly a cause of monomicrobial abscess, sometimes with metastatic spread), along with enteric anaerobes and streptococci such as the Streptococcus anginosus group. Entamoeba and Giardia (option A) cause amebic abscess and intestinal infection, respectively, not pyogenic abscess. Mycobacterium tuberculosis (option C) causes hepatic involvement rarely and not this acute pyogenic picture. Candida (option D) liver abscesses occur mainly in neutropenic or immunocompromised hosts, not typically post-ERCP. Sending aspirate and blood cultures guides targeted therapy; empiric coverage should target enteric gram-negatives and anaerobes pending results.
stages.hint: Think of the gut flora that ascend the biliary tree.

pearls: Pyogenic liver abscess often follows biliary obstruction or instrumentation; presents with fever, rigors, RUQ pain, and tender hepatomegaly with neutrophilic leukocytosis.
Contrast CT or ultrasound shows a rim-enhancing fluid collection; biliary tract is the most common source today.
Management is image-guided percutaneous drainage plus broad-spectrum antibiotics covering enteric gram-negatives and anaerobes.
E. coli and Klebsiella pneumoniae are leading pathogens; send aspirate and blood cultures to tailor therapy.

cases.title: Right-Sided Pain and Fever After Overseas Travel
cases.chiefComplaint: Fever and right-upper-quadrant pain weeks after returning from abroad.
cases.history: A 34-year-old man is admitted with 2 weeks of intermittent fevers, right-upper-quadrant pain, and malaise. He returned 6 weeks ago from a 3-month backpacking trip through rural Mexico and Central America, where he frequently ate street food and drank untreated water. He recalls a self-limited episode of bloody diarrhea about a month into the trip that resolved without treatment. He now has dull, constant RUQ pain that radiates to the right shoulder, drenching sweats, anorexia, and a 6-pound weight loss. He denies current diarrhea, jaundice, or cough. Allergies: none. Medications: none. He drinks alcohol socially and does not use IV drugs. He is HIV-negative on a recent test. Family history is noncontributory. He works as a freelance photographer. Review of systems is positive for fevers, RUQ pain worse with movement, and fatigue. He has had no prior abdominal surgery and no known liver disease. He mentions the diarrhea during the trip contained blood and mucus but he attributed it to 'traveler's stomach' and never sought care.
cases.exam: He appears mildly ill. There is tender hepatomegaly with point tenderness over the lower right intercostal spaces and the right upper quadrant; the liver edge is smooth and tender. There is dullness and reduced breath sounds at the right lung base. No Murphy sign and no rebound or guarding. There is no scleral icterus and no splenomegaly. Bowel sounds are normal. No peripheral lymphadenopathy. Skin shows no rash. He is alert and oriented. No stigmata of chronic liver disease are present.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A young traveler with prior bloody diarrhea now has fever and tender hepatomegaly. What is the best initial imaging plus the most useful confirmatory test?
options: Plain abdominal film plus blood smear for malaria
Upper endoscopy plus H. pylori testing as the first step today
HIDA scan plus stool ova and parasites only in this clinical context
RUQ ultrasound/CT of the liver plus Entamoeba histolytica serology
stages.explanation: A young traveler to an endemic region with a history of bloody (dysenteric) diarrhea now presenting with fever and tender hepatomegaly should be evaluated for amebic liver abscess. The best initial imaging is RUQ ultrasound or CT to detect the abscess, and the most useful confirmatory test is Entamoeba histolytica serology (antibody testing), which is positive in the great majority of cases of invasive amebiasis. A HIDA scan (option B) evaluates cystic-duct patency, not abscess; stool O&P alone is insensitive because the colonic infection may have cleared. A plain film and malaria smear (option C) do not address a hepatic abscess. Upper endoscopy/H. pylori (option D) is unrelated. Imaging plus serology is the correct pairing.
stages.hint: Image the liver and check antibodies to the dysentery-causing parasite.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 13.5 ×10³/µL (neutrophil predominant, NO eosinophilia)
Alkaline phosphatase 210 U/L
AST 58 U/L, ALT 60 U/L
Total bilirubin 1.2 mg/dL
Entamoeba histolytica serology (IgG): positive
Stool antigen for E. histolytica: positive
RUQ ultrasound: single 7 cm hypoechoic lesion in the right hepatic lobe near the dome
CT abdomen: solitary right-lobe abscess; no biliary dilation; small right pleural effusion

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Serology is positive and a solitary right-lobe abscess is seen. If aspirated, what is the classic appearance of the contents?
options: Clear serous fluid as the primary diagnosis
Thick reddish-brown 'anchovy paste' fluid typically without bacteria on Gram stain
Frank green pus with abundant neutrophils and bacteria as the primary diagnosis
Bile-stained fluid with cholesterol crystals as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Amebic liver abscess characteristically contains thick, reddish-brown, odorless fluid classically described as 'anchovy paste' or 'chocolate sauce,' representing necrotic hepatocytes and blood; trophozoites are found mainly at the abscess wall, and the aspirate is typically sterile on routine bacterial Gram stain and culture, distinguishing it from pyogenic abscess. Frank green pus with bacteria (option A) describes a pyogenic abscess. Clear serous fluid (option C) suggests a simple cyst. Bile-stained fluid with cholesterol crystals (option D) is not typical of either abscess type. The anchovy-paste appearance, positive serology, solitary right-lobe location, and dysentery history together support the amebic diagnosis. Most amebic abscesses do not require aspiration and respond to medical therapy.
stages.hint: Necrotic liver and blood give a brown, sterile fluid with a fishy nickname.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the appropriate medical treatment sequence for amebic liver abscess?
options: Antifungal therapy with fluconazole as initial therapy
Albendazole and surgical resection of the cyst as initial therapy
A tissue amebicide , followed by a luminal agent to eradicate intestinal colonization
Broad-spectrum antibiotics targeting enteric gram-negatives and anaerobes as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Amebic liver abscess is treated with a tissue (systemic) amebicide, metronidazole or tinidazole, to kill invasive trophozoites in the liver, followed by a luminal agent such as paromomycin (or iodoquinol) to eradicate residual intestinal colonization and prevent relapse and transmission. Failure to give the luminal agent leaves the gut reservoir and risks recurrence. Broad-spectrum antibacterials (option B) are appropriate for pyogenic abscess, not amebic. Antifungal therapy (option C) is irrelevant. Albendazole and surgical cyst resection (option D) describe management of echinococcal (hydatid) disease, not amebiasis. Most amebic abscesses resolve with medical therapy alone; drainage is reserved for selected cases. The two-step amebicide regimen is the standard answer.
stages.hint: Treat the liver, then mop up the gut reservoir with a second drug.

stages.label: DRAINAGE
stages.question: When is therapeutic aspiration/drainage of an amebic liver abscess indicated?
options: Routinely in all amebic abscesses regardless of size as the immediate next step
Never, because aspiration always causes fatal hemorrhage as the immediate next step
Only after a 6-week course of antibiotics as the immediate next step
For large abscesses at risk of rupture, those failing to respond to medical therapy, diagnostic uncertainty , or left-lobe abscesses threatening the pericardium
stages.explanation: Most amebic liver abscesses respond to medical therapy alone, so drainage is selective. Indications for aspiration/drainage include large abscesses at risk of imminent rupture, lack of clinical response to amebicides after several days, diagnostic uncertainty (to obtain fluid to exclude a pyogenic abscess), and left-lobe abscesses that threaten rupture into the pericardium. Routine drainage of all abscesses (option A) is unnecessary and exposes patients to procedural risk. Claiming aspiration is never done (option C) is false; it is safely performed when indicated. Waiting 6 weeks of antibiotics (option D) is not a defined criterion and would delay needed intervention in complicated cases. Recognizing the selective indications for drainage is the high-yield point.
stages.hint: Drain only when it is big and about to rupture, not improving, or the diagnosis is unclear.

stages.label: PUBLIC HEALTH
stages.question: Which additional step is important to prevent recurrence and limit transmission?
options: Annual liver biopsy as the immediate next step
Mandatory cholecystectomy as the immediate next step
Confirming eradication of intestinal colonization with a luminal agent
Lifelong daily metronidazole prophylaxis as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: After treating the abscess, it is important to eradicate intestinal colonization with a luminal amebicide (paromomycin or iodoquinol), since the asymptomatic carrier state in the colon is the reservoir for both relapse and ongoing fecal-oral transmission; counseling on safe food and water practices during future travel reduces reinfection risk. Lifelong daily metronidazole (option A) is unnecessary, poorly tolerated, and does not address luminal colonization well. Cholecystectomy (option C) plays no role in amebiasis. Annual liver biopsy (option D) is not indicated. The combination of luminal eradication and hygiene counseling addresses both the individual's relapse risk and public health transmission, which is the comprehensive management goal.
stages.hint: Clear the gut reservoir and prevent re-exposure through safe food and water.

pearls: Suspect amebic liver abscess in a traveler to endemic areas with a history of dysentery, now with fever and a solitary right-lobe abscess; serology and stool antigen confirm.
Aspirate (when done) is sterile reddish-brown 'anchovy paste'; eosinophilia is typically absent.
Treat with a tissue amebicide (metronidazole/tinidazole) THEN a luminal agent (paromomycin) to eradicate intestinal carriage.
Most respond to drugs alone; drain only for impending rupture, nonresponse, diagnostic uncertainty, or left-lobe abscess threatening the pericardium.

cases.title: Sudden Abdominal Swelling and Liver Pain
cases.chiefComplaint: Rapidly enlarging abdomen with right-upper-quadrant pain over several days.
cases.history: A 38-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with 1 week of rapidly progressive abdominal distension and dull right-upper-quadrant pain. She reports early satiety, nausea, and new ankle swelling. She has no history of liver disease, alcohol use disorder (rare social drinking), or viral hepatitis risk factors. She had an unprovoked deep vein thrombosis 2 years ago and was found to have a mild thrombocytosis at that time; she did not continue anticoagulation. She takes a combined oral contraceptive pill. Allergies: none. Other medications: none. Family history includes a brother with a clotting disorder. She is a teacher, does not smoke, and has had no recent travel. Review of systems is positive for fatigue, the abdominal swelling, and right-sided pain that worsens when lying flat. She denies fever, hematemesis, melena, or confusion. She notes her legs feel heavy and her abdomen has grown visibly over just a few days, which alarmed her enough to come in.
cases.exam: She is uncomfortable but alert. The abdomen is distended and tense with a fluid wave and shifting dullness consistent with ascites; the liver is enlarged and tender to palpation. There is no splenomegaly appreciated through the ascites. There is mild bilateral pedal edema. No spider angiomata or palmar erythema; no caput medusae. She has no scleral icterus. There is no asterixis and her mental status is normal. Cardiac and pulmonary exams are unremarkable with no jugular venous distension and no pericardial rub.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: She has new tender hepatomegaly and rapid-onset ascites with a hypercoagulable history. Which initial imaging study is most appropriate to evaluate the suspected cause?
options: HIDA scan as the first step today
Upper endoscopy as the first step today
Doppler ultrasound of the hepatic veins
Plain abdominal radiograph
stages.explanation: New tender hepatomegaly with rapidly accumulating ascites in a young woman with a thrombophilic history (prior unprovoked DVT, possible myeloproliferative neoplasm, estrogen-containing contraceptive) strongly suggests Budd-Chiari syndrome (hepatic venous outflow obstruction). Doppler ultrasound of the hepatic veins and IVC is the appropriate first-line, noninvasive study to assess for hepatic vein thrombosis and absent or reversed flow. A plain radiograph (option B) cannot evaluate venous flow. Upper endoscopy (option C) assesses for varices but does not diagnose the outflow obstruction. A HIDA scan (option D) evaluates the biliary tree. Doppler ultrasound directly interrogates the hepatic venous outflow and is the best initial test for suspected Budd-Chiari syndrome.
stages.hint: You need to look at flow in the hepatic veins; pick the test that shows venous flow.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
AST 96 U/L
ALT 110 U/L
Total bilirubin 2.1 mg/dL
Albumin 3.2 g/dL
INR 1.4
Platelets 520 ×10³/µL (elevated)
Ascitic fluid: serum-ascites albumin gradient (SAAG) 1.6 g/dL (high); total protein 3.2 g/dL
Doppler ultrasound: absent flow in the hepatic veins with hepatic vein thrombosis; enlarged caudate lobe
JAK2 V617F mutation: positive

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Doppler shows absent hepatic vein flow with caudate lobe enlargement, and JAK2 V617F is positive. What is the diagnosis and the most likely underlying cause?
options: Cirrhosis from occult alcohol use as the primary diagnosis
Constrictive pericarditis as the primary diagnosis
Nephrotic syndrome causing ascites in this clinical context
Budd-Chiari syndrome due to a myeloproliferative neoplasm
stages.explanation: Hepatic vein thrombosis with absent venous flow and a characteristically hypertrophied caudate lobe (which has separate venous drainage to the IVC) defines Budd-Chiari syndrome, and the positive JAK2 V617F mutation with thrombocytosis points to an underlying myeloproliferative neoplasm (such as polycythemia vera or essential thrombocythemia) as the prothrombotic driver, compounded by estrogen-containing contraception. Occult alcoholic cirrhosis (option A) would not cause acute hepatic vein thrombosis or this acute course. Constrictive pericarditis (option C) can cause hepatic congestion but the Doppler shows actual hepatic vein thrombosis, and JVD/pericardial findings are absent. Nephrotic syndrome (option D) causes a low-SAAG ascites, whereas hers is high-SAAG from sinusoidal hypertension. The findings establish JAK2-driven Budd-Chiari syndrome.
stages.hint: Hepatic vein thrombosis plus a JAK2 mutation names both the syndrome and its driver.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of initial medical management once Budd-Chiari syndrome is confirmed?
options: Immediate liver transplantation for all patients as initial therapy
Avoidance of anticoagulation due to variceal bleeding risk as initial therapy
Anticoagulation plus treatment of the underlying thrombophilia
High-dose corticosteroids as initial therapy
stages.explanation: The cornerstone of initial therapy for Budd-Chiari syndrome is anticoagulation, typically starting with heparin and transitioning to long-term anticoagulation, together with treating the underlying prothrombotic disorder (here, a JAK2-positive myeloproliferative neoplasm, often cytoreduction and hematology involvement) and managing ascites with sodium restriction and diuretics. A stepwise approach escalates to angioplasty/stenting of stenotic segments, TIPS for those failing medical therapy, and liver transplantation for fulminant or refractory cases. Transplant for all (option A) is not first-line. Corticosteroids (option C) have no role. Withholding anticoagulation (option D) is incorrect; anticoagulation is essential even though variceal bleeding risk must be managed, because untreated thrombosis progresses. Anticoagulation plus treating the cause is the answer.
stages.hint: It is a clot; the foundation of therapy is to anticoagulate and treat the clotting disorder.

stages.label: ESCALATION
stages.question: If she develops worsening liver function and refractory ascites despite anticoagulation, which intervention is the next appropriate step?
options: Transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt or hepatic vein angioplasty/stenting
Splenectomy as the immediate next step
Discontinue anticoagulation and observe as the immediate next step
Begin lactulose and discharge as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Budd-Chiari syndrome is managed with a stepwise escalation: when medical therapy (anticoagulation plus diuretics) fails to control ascites or liver function declines, restoring hepatic venous outflow is the goal, achieved by angioplasty/stenting of short stenoses or, more commonly, a transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt (TIPS) to decompress the congested liver; liver transplantation is reserved for fulminant disease or failure of these measures. Splenectomy (option B) plays no role. Discontinuing anticoagulation (option C) would worsen thrombosis. Lactulose and discharge (option D) ignores the deteriorating outflow obstruction and is unsafe. TIPS or angioplasty/stenting is the correct escalation when first-line measures fail, bridging some patients to transplant if needed.
stages.hint: When medical therapy fails, restore or bypass the blocked venous outflow.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Given the JAK2 mutation, which additional evaluation is most appropriate?
options: Refer to gastroenterology for colonoscopy as the immediate next step
Cardiology referral for echocardiographic strain imaging as the immediate next step
Endocrinology referral for cortisol testing as the immediate next step
Hematology referral for evaluation/treatment of a myeloproliferative neoplasm
stages.explanation: A positive JAK2 V617F mutation with thrombocytosis in the setting of an unprovoked splanchnic vein thrombosis indicates an underlying myeloproliferative neoplasm, so hematology referral is essential for bone marrow evaluation, possible cytoreductive therapy (e.g., hydroxyurea), and decisions about long-term anticoagulation; a broader thrombophilia workup (e.g., for antiphospholipid syndrome, paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria, and other inherited thrombophilias) is also indicated because multiple prothrombotic factors often coexist. Colonoscopy (option A), cardiac strain imaging (option C), and cortisol testing (option D) do not address the demonstrated hematologic thrombophilia. Identifying and treating the underlying myeloproliferative neoplasm is central to preventing recurrent thrombosis and guiding her ongoing care, including stopping estrogen-containing contraception.
stages.hint: A JAK2 mutation with high platelets and clotting belongs to the blood specialists.

cases.diagnosis: Budd-Chiari syndrome (hepatic vein thrombosis) from a JAK2-positive myeloproliferative neoplasm
pearls: Budd-Chiari syndrome = hepatic venous outflow obstruction; suspect with the triad of abdominal pain, ascites, and hepatomegaly, often in a hypercoagulable patient.
Doppler ultrasound shows absent/reversed hepatic vein flow and a hypertrophied caudate lobe (separate IVC drainage); ascites is high-SAAG.
Myeloproliferative neoplasms (often JAK2 V617F-positive) are a leading cause; estrogen-containing contraceptives add risk.
Treat with anticoagulation plus management of the underlying thrombophilia; escalate stepwise to angioplasty/stenting, TIPS, then transplantation.

cases.title: An Incidental Liver Cyst in a Sheep Farmer
cases.chiefComplaint: A liver cyst found incidentally during imaging for vague right-sided fullness.
cases.history: A 49-year-old man is referred after an abdominal ultrasound, ordered for several months of vague right-upper-quadrant fullness and mild discomfort, revealed a large liver cyst. He has occasional dull RUQ pressure but no fevers, jaundice, or weight loss. He reports no abdominal pain crises. Allergies: none. Medications: none. He emigrated from a rural sheep-farming region of the Mediterranean a decade ago and worked closely with sheep and herding dogs for many years; he recalls dogs being fed raw offal on the farm. He drinks alcohol rarely and does not use IV drugs. Family history is noncontributory. He now works in construction. Review of systems is otherwise negative; he specifically denies cough, hemoptysis, or chest pain. He has had no prior abdominal surgery and no known liver disease. He is mostly worried because the cyst is large and he read online that such cysts can be dangerous, prompting him to seek specialist evaluation rather than ignore it.
cases.exam: He is well-appearing and not jaundiced. The abdomen is soft with mild fullness and a palpable, smooth, nontender mass in the right upper quadrant just below the costal margin that moves with respiration. There is no rebound or guarding. No splenomegaly or ascites. There is no rash or urticaria currently. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal. There are no stigmata of chronic liver disease. No lymphadenopathy is appreciated.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A large liver cyst in a former sheep farmer with dog exposure most suggests which diagnosis, and which imaging feature would support it?
options: Hepatocellular carcinoma; arterial enhancement with washout in this clinical context
Simple hepatic cyst; thin-walled anechoic cyst with no internal structure
Pyogenic abscess; rim-enhancing fluid with surrounding edema in this clinical context
Echinococcal cyst; a cyst with internal daughter cysts
stages.explanation: A large hepatic cyst in someone with prolonged sheep and dog exposure from an endemic Mediterranean region strongly suggests cystic echinococcosis (Echinococcus granulosus, hydatid disease), in which dogs are definitive hosts and humans are accidental intermediate hosts. The supportive imaging feature is a cyst containing internal daughter cysts (a multivesicular or 'honeycomb' appearance), sometimes with membrane detachment ('water-lily' sign) and a calcified wall on CT. A simple cyst (option A) is thin-walled and anechoic without internal structure. A pyogenic abscess (option C) presents acutely with fever and rim enhancement. HCC (option D) shows solid arterial enhancement with washout, not a daughter-cyst-containing fluid lesion. Daughter cysts within the lesion point to echinococcosis.
stages.hint: Cysts within the cyst plus sheep and dog exposure point to a tapeworm larva.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 7.8 ×10³/µL with mild eosinophilia (8%)
Liver enzymes: normal
Echinococcus serology (IgG ELISA): positive
CT abdomen: 9 cm right-lobe cyst with multiple peripheral daughter cysts and partial rim calcification (consistent with WHO CE2/active stage)
Chest X-ray: clear lung fields, no pulmonary cysts

stages.label: SAFETY
stages.question: When planning intervention for this hydatid cyst, which complication must be specifically guarded against?
options: Cyst rupture causing spillage of antigenic fluid
Acute pancreatitis as the immediate next step
Massive variceal hemorrhage as the immediate next step
Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: The feared complication of hydatid cyst rupture or careless aspiration is spillage of protoscolex-laden, highly antigenic cyst fluid, which can trigger an anaphylactic reaction and disseminate viable protoscolices throughout the peritoneum, causing secondary echinococcosis. This risk drives the use of protective measures during any intervention: pre- and peri-procedural antihelminthic therapy (albendazole) and instillation of a scolicidal agent, with great care to avoid spillage. Variceal hemorrhage (option A) relates to portal hypertension, not present here. Acute pancreatitis (option C) and spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (option D) are unrelated to cyst manipulation. Recognizing the anaphylaxis-and-seeding danger of rupture is the key safety consideration that shapes the entire management approach for hydatid disease.
stages.hint: Spilling the antigenic fluid can cause shock and scatter the parasite.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For an active, complex hydatid cyst of this size, which management approach is most appropriate?
options: A single dose of metronidazole as initial therapy
Simple aspiration of the cyst without any drug coverage as initial therapy
Observation alone without any therapy as initial therapy
Antihelminthic therapy combined with surgery or PAIR , chosen by cyst stage
stages.explanation: Active hepatic hydatid cysts are managed by combining antihelminthic therapy (albendazole, sometimes with praziquantel) with definitive intervention: surgery (pericystectomy or, in selected cases, the PAIR technique of puncture, aspiration, injection of a scolicidal agent, and reaspiration) chosen according to cyst stage, size, location, and complexity, with albendazole given before and after to reduce recurrence and the consequences of spillage. Observation alone (option A) may suit small inactive/calcified (CE4/CE5) cysts but not this large active CE2 cyst. Simple aspiration without drug coverage (option C) is dangerous because of rupture/anaphylaxis and seeding risk. Metronidazole (option D) treats amebiasis, not echinococcosis. The combined drug-plus-procedure strategy, staged by WHO classification, is the standard.
stages.hint: Pair the worm-killing drug with a definitive procedure, never aspirate blind.

stages.label: TECHNIQUE
stages.question: If PAIR (puncture-aspiration-injection-reaspiration) is performed, what is injected after aspiration to kill the protoscolices?
options: Normal saline only as the immediate next step
Broad-spectrum antibiotics as the immediate next step
A scolicidal agent such as hypertonic saline or ethanol
Contrast dye for follow-up imaging in this setting
stages.explanation: In the PAIR procedure, after the cyst is punctured and partially aspirated, a scolicidal agent such as hypertonic (e.g., 15-20%) saline or absolute ethanol is injected to kill the protoscolices and germinal layer, then reaspirated after a dwell time, all under albendazole cover to minimize the consequences of any spillage. Normal saline (option A) is not scolicidal and would not sterilize the cyst. Antibiotics (option B/C) do not kill the parasite. Contrast dye (option D) is not therapeutic. PAIR is suited to certain uni- or paucilocular cysts and is contraindicated when there is communication with the biliary tree (risk of sclerosant-induced cholangitis) or for superficially located cysts at high rupture risk. The scolicidal injection is the defining step.
stages.hint: You must inject something that actually kills the larval scolices, not just rinse.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Which counseling point helps prevent reinfection and transmission of this parasite?
options: Avoid freshwater swimming as the immediate next step
Practice good hand hygiene, avoid feeding raw offal/viscera to dogs,
Avoid contact with cats and clean litter boxes as the immediate next step
Cook all pork thoroughly to avoid larvae as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Echinococcus granulosus cycles between definitive canine hosts (dogs) and intermediate hosts (sheep and other livestock); humans acquire infection by ingesting eggs shed in dog feces. Prevention centers on hand hygiene, not feeding raw offal/viscera to dogs (which breaks the cycle by preventing dogs from acquiring the tapeworm), and regular deworming of dogs in endemic farming communities, along with controlling livestock slaughter practices. Cat exposure and litter boxes (option A) relate to toxoplasmosis. Thorough pork cooking (option C) prevents Taenia solium and trichinosis. Avoiding freshwater swimming (option D) relates to schistosomiasis or Naegleria. Counseling about the dog-sheep cycle and offal disposal directly targets the transmission route for cystic echinococcosis.
stages.hint: Break the dog-sheep cycle: don't feed dogs raw offal, and deworm them.

cases.diagnosis: Hepatic hydatid cyst (cystic echinococcosis, Echinococcus granulosus)
pearls: Suspect hydatid disease with a liver cyst containing daughter cysts and wall calcification in a patient from an endemic sheep/dog region; serology and eosinophilia support it.
Cyst rupture or careless aspiration can cause anaphylaxis and peritoneal seeding (secondary echinococcosis) from antigenic protoscolex-laden fluid.

Manage active cysts with albendazole plus a definitive procedure (surgery or PAIR), staged by the WHO classification; PAIR uses a scolicidal agent (hypertonic saline/ethanol) and avoids biliary-communicating cysts.
Prevention targets the dog-sheep cycle: don't feed dogs raw offal, deworm dogs, and practice good hygiene in endemic areas.

cases.title: Intermittent Yellow Eyes in a Young Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Episodic yellowing of the eyes noticed during a recent illness.
cases.history: A 23-year-old college student presents after his roommate noticed his eyes looked yellow for the past two days. He recently had a viral upper respiratory illness with poor appetite and skipped several meals while studying for finals. He recalls a similar transient episode last year during a fasting religious observance that resolved on its own. He denies abdominal pain, dark urine, pale stools, pruritus, fevers, or weight loss. He drinks alcohol only socially (2-3 drinks per month), uses no IV drugs, has no tattoos, and has had no recent travel or new sexual partners. He takes no prescription medications, no acetaminophen, and no herbal supplements. He has no allergies. He has never been hospitalized and has no history of blood transfusion. Family history is notable for a maternal uncle who 'turns yellow when he gets sick' but was never told he had liver disease. Review of systems is otherwise negative. He feels well aside from mild fatigue attributed to his recent cold and exam stress.
cases.exam: Well-appearing young man in no distress. Scleral icterus is present without overt cutaneous jaundice. Skin shows no spider angiomata, palmar erythema, or excoriations. The abdomen is soft, non-tender, and non-distended; the liver edge is non-palpable and the spleen is not enlarged. There is no asterixis and no shifting dullness. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal. No lymphadenopathy. Neurologic exam is intact with no signs of encephalopathy. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial laboratory test to characterize this patient's jaundice?
options: Fractionated bilirubin with a CBC, reticulocyte count,
Hepatitis B surface antigen and hepatitis C antibody only
Antimitochondrial antibody and ANA panel as the first step today
Abdominal ultrasound with Doppler as the first step today
stages.explanation: The first step in any jaundiced patient is to fractionate the bilirubin to determine whether hyperbilirubinemia is predominantly unconjugated or conjugated, paired with a CBC, reticulocyte count, and aminotransferases to separate hemolysis from hepatocellular or cholestatic disease. This cheaply directs the entire workup. An abdominal ultrasound is most useful when conjugated hyperbilirubinemia or biliary obstruction is suspected, not as the very first test in a well young man. Antimitochondrial antibody/ANA screen for autoimmune cholestatic disease, which is unlikely here. Viral hepatitis serologies are reasonable later but are premature before fractionation; isolated viral hepatitis would typically also raise the aminotransferases, which characterization will reveal.

stages.label: INITIAL LABS
stages.content:
Total bilirubin 3.1 mg/dL
Direct (conjugated) bilirubin 0.3 mg/dL
Indirect (unconjugated) bilirubin 2.8 mg/dL
AST 22 U/L
ALT 25 U/L
Alkaline phosphatase 68 U/L
Hemoglobin 15.1 g/dL
Reticulocyte count 1.1%
LDH 160 U/L
Haptoglobin 110 mg/dL
Peripheral smear: normal, no schistocytes or spherocytes

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given isolated unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia with normal liver enzymes and no evidence of hemolysis, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Acute viral hepatitis A
Hereditary spherocytosis
Crigler-Najjar syndrome type I
Gilbert syndrome
stages.explanation: Mild, fluctuating unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia (typically <4-5 mg/dL) precipitated by fasting, intercurrent illness, or stress, with completely normal aminotransferases and no hemolysis, is the classic presentation of Gilbert syndrome, caused by reduced UGT1A1 activity. The autosomal recessive family pattern fits. Hereditary spherocytosis produces hemolysis with reticulocytosis, low haptoglobin, elevated LDH, and spherocytes on smear—all absent here. Crigler-Najjar type I causes severe unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia presenting in neonates with kernicterus, incompatible with a healthy 23-year-old. Acute hepatitis A would elevate the aminotransferases markedly and produce a mixed/conjugated pattern, not isolated unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia with normal enzymes.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management for this patient?
options: Refer for liver biopsy to confirm the diagnosis as initial therapy
Start phenobarbital to induce UGT1A1 and lower bilirubin
Begin ursodeoxycholic acid and recheck enzymes in 3 months
Reassurance and no further treatment
stages.explanation: Gilbert syndrome is a wholly benign condition requiring no treatment. The cornerstone of management is reassurance plus counseling that jaundice may recur with fasting, dehydration, illness, or stress, none of which cause harm. Phenobarbital can lower bilirubin by inducing UGT1A1 but is unnecessary and carries sedation risk; it is reserved for cosmetically distressing cases or diagnostic confusion. Liver biopsy is invasive and not indicated when the clinical and laboratory picture is classic. Ursodeoxycholic acid treats cholestatic disease and has no role in an unconjugated, non-cholestatic hyperbilirubinemia. The diagnosis is clinical, supported by the lab pattern.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: The patient asks which medication consideration is most relevant to his condition in the future. Which is the best counseling point?
options: He may have reduced metabolism of irinotecan, increasing toxicity risk
He should never receive general anesthesia as the immediate next step
He requires lifelong iron chelation as the immediate next step
He must avoid all acetaminophen permanently as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: UGT1A1 also glucuronidates SN-38, the active metabolite of the chemotherapeutic irinotecan. Patients with Gilbert syndrome (reduced UGT1A1) clear SN-38 more slowly and are at increased risk of severe neutropenia and diarrhea, a clinically meaningful pharmacogenetic point. Routine acetaminophen at therapeutic doses is safe; there is no need to avoid it entirely. General anesthesia is not contraindicated. Iron chelation is irrelevant—Gilbert syndrome is not an iron-overload disorder and does not cause hemolysis requiring transfusion. This counseling reinforces the otherwise benign nature while flagging the one genuinely relevant drug interaction.

pearls: Gilbert syndrome causes mild, fluctuating unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia from reduced UGT1A1 activity, classically unmasked by fasting, illness, dehydration, or stress.
Diagnosis is clinical: isolated indirect hyperbilirubinemia (usually <4-5 mg/dL) with normal liver enzymes and no hemolysis—no biopsy or genetic testing needed.
It is entirely benign and requires only reassurance; phenobarbital can lower bilirubin but is rarely indicated.
Reduced UGT1A1 increases toxicity risk from irinotecan (impaired SN-38 glucuronidation)—the one drug interaction worth knowing.
