cases.title: Yellow Skin and Weight Loss in an Older Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive yellowing of the skin and unintentional weight loss over two months.
cases.history: A 68-year-old man presents with two months of progressive jaundice, dark urine, and pale, greasy stools. His wife noticed his eyes and skin turning yellow. He reports generalized itching and has lost roughly 8 kg (18 lb) without trying. He describes a vague, dull, gnawing discomfort in the mid-epigastrium that sometimes radiates to his back, but no severe pain. Appetite is poor and he feels fatigued. He denies fevers, rigors, or vomiting. PMH includes type 2 diabetes mellitus diagnosed only 4 months ago and hypertension. Medications: metformin 1000 mg twice daily, lisinopril 10 mg daily. No allergies. He has a 40-pack-year smoking history and drinks 1-2 beers weekly. He worked as a chemical plant operator. Family history: father had 'stomach cancer.' Review of systems is positive for new-onset depression and the recent diabetes diagnosis; he denies hematochezia, melena, or chest pain. He has had no prior abdominal surgeries and no history of gallstones or pancreatitis.
cases.exam: Cachectic, jaundiced man with marked scleral icterus and diffuse skin excoriations from scratching. The abdomen is soft with mild epigastric fullness. A non-tender, smooth, distended structure is palpable in the right upper quadrant below the liver edge consistent with a palpable gallbladder. There is no rebound or guarding, no shifting dullness, and the spleen is not enlarged. No periumbilical or left supraclavicular lymphadenopathy is appreciated on this exam. Stool on rectal exam is acholic (clay-colored). No asterixis. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A non-tender, palpable, distended gallbladder in a jaundiced patient (Courvoisier sign) most strongly suggests which underlying process?
options: Malignant obstruction of the distal common bile duct rather than gallstone disease
Acute cholecystitis from an impacted cystic duct stone as the first step today
Hemolytic anemia causing pigment overload as the first step today
Cirrhosis with portal hypertension as the first step today
stages.explanation: Courvoisier's law states that a palpable, non-tender, distended gallbladder in the setting of jaundice is more likely from malignant distal biliary obstruction (e.g., pancreatic head adenocarcinoma or cholangiocarcinoma) than from gallstones. Chronic stone disease produces a fibrotic, scarred gallbladder that cannot distend, whereas slowly progressive malignant obstruction allows painless dilation. Acute cholecystitis produces a tender gallbladder (positive Murphy sign), not a painless one. Cirrhosis causes hepatocellular jaundice and a small nodular liver, not a distended palpable gallbladder. Hemolysis causes unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia without obstructive features such as pale stools, dark urine, and pruritus seen here.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Total bilirubin 12.4 mg/dL
Direct bilirubin 9.8 mg/dL
Alkaline phosphatase 540 U/L
GGT 610 U/L
AST 78 U/L
ALT 92 U/L
Lipase 60 U/L (normal)
Hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL
HbA1c 8.1%
CA 19-9 1240 U/mL (normal <37)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The cholestatic lab pattern and elevated CA 19-9 support obstruction. What is the most appropriate imaging study to characterize the lesion and assess resectability?
options: Pancreatic-protocol CT of the abdomen
HIDA scan as the primary diagnosis
Plain abdominal radiograph in this clinical context
Right upper quadrant ultrasound alone
stages.explanation: A pancreatic-protocol multiphase contrast-enhanced CT is the standard test to characterize a suspected pancreatic head mass, define ductal dilation, and—critically—assess vascular involvement (SMA, SMV, celiac, portal vein) that determines surgical resectability. Ultrasound is a reasonable first screen and may show biliary dilation but is operator-dependent and cannot adequately stage the tumor or assess vessels. A plain radiograph has no role in evaluating a soft-tissue pancreatic mass. A HIDA scan evaluates cystic-duct patency for acute cholecystitis and is irrelevant to staging a malignancy. CA 19-9 supports the diagnosis but is not specific; imaging is required for staging and tissue planning.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Pancreatic-protocol CT: 3.2 cm hypoattenuating mass in the pancreatic head
Double-duct sign: dilated common bile duct (14 mm) and dilated main pancreatic duct
Mass abuts <180° of the superior mesenteric vein; no SMA encasement
No hepatic or peritoneal metastases; no ascites
Regional lymph nodes not pathologically enlarged

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The tumor abuts <180° of the SMV without SMA encasement and no metastases. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Repeat CT in 6 months to monitor growth as initial therapy
Immediate palliative biliary stenting and hospice referral as initial therapy
Empiric chemotherapy without tissue diagnosis as initial therapy
Multidisciplinary evaluation for resectable/borderline-resectable disease, with EUS-guided biopsy
stages.explanation: This is borderline-resectable/resectable pancreatic head cancer (limited SMV contact, no SMA encasement, no metastases). The correct pathway is multidisciplinary tumor board evaluation, tissue confirmation by EUS-guided fine-needle biopsy, and a plan for neoadjuvant chemotherapy followed by pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple). Palliation/hospice is inappropriate when potentially curative surgery is on the table. Chemotherapy is increasingly given neoadjuvantly but should follow tissue diagnosis and staging, not be started empirically. Watchful waiting for a malignant, obstructing tumor is unacceptable and forfeits the only curative window. Surgical resection offers the only chance of cure in pancreatic adenocarcinoma.

stages.label: RISK FACTORS
stages.question: Which feature of this patient's presentation is a recognized clue that should raise suspicion for pancreatic adenocarcinoma?
options: His long-standing hypertension as the immediate next step
New-onset diabetes mellitus in an older adult preceding the diagnosis
His occasional beer consumption as the immediate next step
His use of lisinopril as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: New-onset diabetes in an adult over 50, particularly when accompanied by weight loss, is an established paraneoplastic clue to occult pancreatic adenocarcinoma; the tumor can induce insulin resistance and beta-cell dysfunction months before diagnosis. This patient's diabetes appeared just 4 months ago. Cigarette smoking (his 40-pack-year history) and chronic pancreatitis are the major modifiable risk factors, not hypertension. Light alcohol use is not an independent risk factor for pancreatic cancer. Lisinopril and metformin are not implicated; in fact metformin is generally protective. Recognizing new-onset diabetes plus weight loss as a red flag enables earlier evaluation.

pearls: Courvoisier sign—a palpable, non-tender, distended gallbladder with jaundice—points to malignant distal biliary obstruction rather than gallstones.
The 'double-duct sign' (dilated CBD and pancreatic duct) on imaging is highly suggestive of a pancreatic head mass.
New-onset diabetes plus weight loss in an older adult is a paraneoplastic red flag for pancreatic adenocarcinoma; smoking is the leading modifiable risk factor.
Resectability hinges on vascular involvement (SMA/SMV/celiac); pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple) is the only curative option and CA 19-9 supports but does not diagnose.

cases.title: Persistent Belly Pain Weeks After a Hospital Stay
cases.chiefComplaint: Ongoing upper abdominal pain and early satiety five weeks after a pancreatitis admission.
cases.history: A 50-year-old man is admitted to the ward with persistent epigastric pain, nausea, and early satiety. Five weeks ago he was hospitalized for acute alcoholic pancreatitis (lipase >2000 U/L at the time) and discharged after symptoms improved and he stopped drinking. Over the past two weeks his epigastric pain has returned as a steady ache, and he feels full after only a few bites and has had several episodes of non-bloody vomiting. He reports a low-grade sense of abdominal fullness but no high fevers, no jaundice, and no hematemesis. PMH: alcohol use disorder (abstinent 5 weeks), hypertension. Medications: pantoprazole 40 mg daily, amlodipine 5 mg daily, as-needed acetaminophen. No allergies. Social: previously drank a pint of vodka daily for years, now abstinent; smokes half a pack daily. Family history non-contributory. ROS positive for mild weight loss from poor intake; denies diarrhea, melena, or chest pain.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable but non-toxic man. Abdomen is mildly distended with tender fullness in the epigastrium; a vague, ill-defined mass effect is appreciated in the upper abdomen. There is no rebound, no rigidity, and no Murphy sign. Bowel sounds are present. There is no jaundice, no flank ecchymosis, and no palpable hepatosplenomegaly. No peripheral edema. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given persistent pain and a palpable upper-abdominal fullness weeks after acute pancreatitis, what is the most appropriate initial imaging?
options: MRCP without contrast
Tagged red blood cell scan
Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen
Plain upright abdominal radiograph
stages.explanation: Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen is the best initial study to evaluate complications of acute pancreatitis, including a fluid collection, pseudocyst, walled-off necrosis, or abscess, and to assess the surrounding anatomy and vasculature. It readily characterizes the size, location, and wall maturity of a peripancreatic collection. MRCP/MRI better delineates solid debris versus pure fluid and ductal anatomy and is useful as a follow-up, but CT is the standard first test. A plain radiograph cannot assess pancreatic collections. A tagged RBC scan localizes active GI bleeding, which is not the clinical question here. The timing (weeks after pancreatitis) and symptoms strongly suggest an evolving fluid collection.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
Contrast CT: well-circumscribed 7 cm fluid collection in the lesser sac with a mature non-epithelialized wall, abutting the posterior stomach
No solid necrotic debris within the collection
No gas/air-fluid level to suggest infection
Main pancreatic duct mildly dilated; no pseudoaneurysm
Lipase 180 U/L
WBC 8.9 ×10³/µL
Amylase 210 U/L

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the imaging—a mature-walled fluid collection without solid debris >4 weeks after pancreatitis—what is the diagnosis?
options: Pancreatic pseudocyst
Walled-off pancreatic necrosis
Pancreatic abscess
Acute peripancreatic fluid collection
stages.explanation: A pancreatic pseudocyst is a well-circumscribed peripancreatic fluid collection with a mature, non-epithelialized fibrous wall that develops typically >4 weeks after acute pancreatitis and contains fluid without significant solid necrotic debris—exactly this picture at 5 weeks. An acute peripancreatic fluid collection occurs in the first <4 weeks and lacks a defined wall. Walled-off necrosis also matures after 4 weeks but contains solid necrotic material, which is absent here. A pancreatic abscess would show gas, an air-fluid level, leukocytosis, and systemic infection signs, none present. The mature wall, timing, and absence of debris define a pseudocyst.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The pseudocyst is 7 cm and causing persistent pain, early satiety, and gastric outlet compression. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Endoscopic ultrasound-guided cystgastrostomy
Continued observation with no drainage regardless of symptoms
Percutaneous needle aspiration alone, then discharge
Immediate open surgical excision of the pseudocyst as monotherapy
stages.explanation: Symptomatic pseudocysts—those causing persistent pain, gastric outlet or biliary obstruction, or that become infected—warrant drainage. The preferred modern approach for a mature pseudocyst abutting the stomach is endoscopic ultrasound-guided transmural drainage (cystgastrostomy), which is effective and less morbid than surgery. Open surgical drainage is reserved for cases not amenable to endoscopic or percutaneous approaches. Simple percutaneous needle aspiration alone has high recurrence because it does not establish durable drainage. Continued observation is appropriate only for asymptomatic pseudocysts, which often resolve spontaneously; this patient is clearly symptomatic with obstructive features, so intervention is indicated.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Before drainage, which complication of a pancreatic pseudocyst is most important to exclude on imaging because it can be life-threatening?
options: Pancreatic ascites as the leading concern
Cyst infection as the leading concern
Splenic vein thrombosis with gastric varices
Pseudoaneurysm of a peripancreatic artery
stages.explanation: A pseudoaneurysm—most often of the splenic artery—forms when pancreatic enzymes erode an adjacent vessel wall and can rupture into the pseudocyst or GI tract, causing catastrophic hemorrhage. It must be excluded (and embolized) before any drainage, since puncturing a pseudoaneurysm is fatal. Splenic vein thrombosis with gastric varices is a real complication but is generally less immediately lethal and is managed differently (splenectomy if bleeding). Pancreatic ascites from ductal leak is morbid but not acutely life-threatening in the same way. Infection converts the pseudocyst to an abscess requiring drainage but is not the single most dangerous pre-procedure consideration; missing a pseudoaneurysm is.

pearls: A pancreatic pseudocyst is a mature-walled (non-epithelialized) fluid collection forming >4 weeks after acute pancreatitis, without significant solid debris.
Asymptomatic pseudocysts are observed (many resolve); drainage is indicated for persistent pain, obstruction, or infection.
EUS-guided transmural drainage (cystgastrostomy) is the preferred modality for mature collections abutting the stomach.
Always exclude a peripancreatic pseudoaneurysm (often splenic artery) before drainage—rupture causes lethal hemorrhage.

cases.title: Episodic Confusion and Sweating in a Middle-Aged Woman
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent spells of confusion, sweating, and palpitations relieved by eating.
cases.history: A 45-year-old woman presents with several months of episodic confusion, blurred vision, tremor, sweating, and palpitations, typically occurring in the early morning before breakfast or when meals are delayed. On a few occasions her husband found her difficult to arouse and 'glassy-eyed'; symptoms resolve within minutes of drinking juice or eating. She has gained about 6 kg because she now snacks frequently to prevent episodes. She denies use of insulin or sulfonylureas and has no access to such medications; no one in the household is diabetic. PMH is unremarkable. Medications: only a daily multivitamin. No allergies. She does not drink alcohol and does not work in healthcare. Family history: a brother with kidney stones and a parent with a pituitary tumor. ROS notable for the spells described and weight gain; she denies headaches, diarrhea, flushing, or peptic ulcer symptoms, though these are screened given family history. She is otherwise healthy and physically active when not symptomatic.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, mildly overweight woman in no acute distress at the time of the visit (not currently symptomatic). Mental status is normal; no tremor or diaphoresis at rest. The abdomen is soft and non-tender without palpable masses or organomegaly. There is no skin flushing, no goiter, and no lymphadenopathy. Cardiovascular and pulmonary exams are normal. Neurologic exam is fully intact between episodes. No telangiectasias or hepatomegaly. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: To establish that her neuroglycopenic spells are due to true hypoglycemia, which approach best confirms the diagnosis?
options: Random fingerstick glucose at the clinic visit as the first step today
A supervised 72-hour fast with serial glucose, insulin, C-peptide, proinsulin,
Hemoglobin A1c as the first step today
Oral glucose tolerance test as the first step today
stages.explanation: Confirming endogenous hyperinsulinism requires demonstrating Whipple triad (symptoms of hypoglycemia, documented low plasma glucose, relief with glucose) under controlled conditions—classically a supervised 72-hour fast with simultaneous measurement of glucose, insulin, C-peptide, proinsulin, beta-hydroxybutyrate, and a sulfonylurea/meglitinide screen. This pattern distinguishes insulinoma from exogenous insulin and factitious causes. A random clinic glucose is usually normal between episodes and cannot capture the fasting event. HbA1c reflects chronic glycemia and is not diagnostic of intermittent hypoglycemia. An OGTT provokes hyperglycemia, not fasting hypoglycemia, and may even be misleading; it is the wrong physiologic challenge for suspected insulinoma.

stages.label: 72-HOUR FAST RESULTS
stages.content:
At symptom onset (hour 14 of fast):
Plasma glucose 38 mg/dL
Insulin 18 µU/mL (inappropriately elevated)
C-peptide 3.5 ng/mL (elevated)
Proinsulin 32 pmol/L (elevated)
Beta-hydroxybutyrate 1.2 mmol/L (low/suppressed)
Sulfonylurea/meglitinide screen: negative
Insulin antibodies: negative
Symptoms resolved with IV dextrose

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: How does the elevated C-peptide (with negative sulfonylurea screen) refine the diagnosis?
options: It indicates surreptitious exogenous insulin injection as the primary diagnosis
It points to an adrenal cause of hypoglycemia as the primary diagnosis
It confirms endogenous hyperinsulinism , since exogenous insulin would suppress C-peptide
It suggests non-islet-cell tumor hypoglycemia from IGF-2 as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Endogenous insulin is secreted with C-peptide in equimolar amounts, so high insulin AND high C-peptide together indicate the pancreas is overproducing insulin—endogenous hyperinsulinism, classically an insulinoma. Exogenous (injected) insulin contains no C-peptide and suppresses endogenous secretion, so insulin would be high while C-peptide is LOW—the opposite of this picture. The negative sulfonylurea screen excludes drug-induced insulin secretion, which would also raise C-peptide. Adrenal insufficiency causes hypoglycemia with LOW insulin (appropriate suppression). Non-islet-cell tumors cause hypoglycemia via IGF-2 with suppressed insulin and C-peptide. The combined elevations clinch endogenous hyperinsulinism.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Biochemical insulinoma is confirmed. What is the most appropriate next step to localize the tumor?
options: Bone scan as the immediate next step
Multiphase pancreatic-protocol CT/MRI, with endoscopic ultrasound if not localized
PET-FDG whole body scan as the immediate next step
Empiric distal pancreatectomy without localization as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: After biochemical confirmation, insulinomas are localized with cross-sectional imaging—multiphase pancreatic-protocol CT or MRI—because most are small, solitary, benign pancreatic lesions. Endoscopic ultrasound is highly sensitive for small tumors not seen on CT/MRI and can guide biopsy and surgical planning; somatostatin-receptor imaging (DOTATATE) is an adjunct. FDG-PET is insensitive for these typically low-grade, well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumors. A bone scan is irrelevant for primary localization. Blind/empiric resection without localization risks operating on the wrong region and missing the tumor; precise localization markedly improves surgical success. Insulinomas are distributed evenly throughout the pancreas, so targeted imaging is essential.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: A 1.5 cm solitary tumor is localized in the pancreatic tail. What is the definitive treatment?
options: Lifelong diazoxide therapy as initial therapy
Octreotide alone indefinitely as initial therapy
External beam radiation to the pancreas
Surgical resection
stages.explanation: Surgical resection—enucleation for small, accessible tumors or distal pancreatectomy for tail lesions—is the definitive, curative treatment for a benign solitary insulinoma. Diazoxide suppresses insulin secretion and controls hypoglycemia but is a temporizing/medical option reserved for poor surgical candidates or preoperative stabilization, not a cure. Radiation has no established role for a resectable benign insulinoma. Octreotide can help some neuroendocrine tumors but is unreliable for insulinomas (it may paradoxically worsen hypoglycemia by suppressing counterregulatory hormones) and is not definitive. Given a localized resectable lesion, surgery offers cure. Note her family history (parent pituitary tumor, brother kidney stones) raises concern for MEN1, warranting evaluation.

stages.label: ASSOCIATED SYNDROME
stages.question: Her family history (parent with pituitary tumor, brother with recurrent kidney stones) should prompt screening for which syndrome?
options: Neurofibromatosis type 1 in this setting
Von Hippel-Lindau disease in this setting
Multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1
Multiple endocrine neoplasia type 2A (MEN2A)
stages.explanation: MEN1 (the '3 Ps': Parathyroid hyperplasia, Pituitary adenomas, Pancreatic/enteric neuroendocrine tumors) fits perfectly—pancreatic NET (insulinoma) in the patient, a parent with a pituitary tumor, and a brother with kidney stones from primary hyperparathyroidism. She should be screened for hyperparathyroidism (calcium, PTH) and a pituitary lesion, and offered MEN1 genetic testing. MEN2A involves medullary thyroid carcinoma, pheochromocytoma, and parathyroid disease (RET mutation), not pituitary tumors or insulinomas. Von Hippel-Lindau causes hemangioblastomas, renal cell carcinoma, and pheochromocytoma. NF1 causes neurofibromas and café-au-lait macules. The constellation here is classic MEN1.

pearls: Whipple triad (symptoms + documented hypoglycemia + relief with glucose) under a supervised 72-hour fast confirms endogenous hyperinsulinism.
High insulin WITH high C-peptide and proinsulin (and negative sulfonylurea screen) indicates endogenous insulin overproduction; exogenous insulin suppresses C-peptide.
Localize with multiphase pancreatic CT/MRI and EUS; surgical enucleation or resection is curative for benign solitary insulinoma.
Insulinoma plus pituitary and parathyroid disease should prompt MEN1 evaluation (the 3 Ps).

cases.title: Facial Flushing and Chronic Diarrhea in a Woman
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent episodes of facial flushing, watery diarrhea, and wheezing for several months.
cases.history: A 57-year-old woman presents with months of episodic cutaneous flushing of the face and upper chest, often triggered by stress, alcohol, or certain foods (aged cheese, chocolate). The flushing lasts minutes and is sometimes accompanied by a sensation of warmth and palpitations. She also reports frequent watery, non-bloody diarrhea (up to 8 stools/day) and intermittent wheezing and shortness of breath, for which an inhaler gave little relief. She has lost a few kilograms. She denies heartburn, hematochezia, or recent travel. PMH: mild asthma diagnosed only this year, hypertension. Medications: albuterol inhaler PRN, amlodipine 5 mg daily. No allergies. Social: drinks wine occasionally (notes it triggers flushing), never smoked. Family history non-contributory. ROS notable for episodic flushing, diarrhea, and wheeze; she also reports mild exertional dyspnea and lower-extremity swelling more recently. She has had no abdominal surgeries and no known cardiac disease before this year.
cases.exam: Pleasant woman with transient facial erythema noted during the visit. Cardiac exam reveals a holosystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border that augments with inspiration, and a soft early diastolic murmur; jugular venous pressure is mildly elevated with a prominent v-wave and there is trace pedal edema. Lungs have scattered expiratory wheezes. The abdomen is soft with mild hepatomegaly; the liver edge is firm and palpable 3 cm below the costal margin. No splenomegaly. No telangiectasias of the lips. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given episodic flushing, secretory diarrhea, and wheezing, which initial biochemical test best screens for the suspected syndrome?
options: 24-hour urinary 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid
Plasma metanephrines as the first step today
Serum gastrin level as the first step today
Serum vasoactive intestinal peptide (VIP)
stages.explanation: The triad of flushing, secretory diarrhea, and bronchospasm suggests carcinoid syndrome from serotonin (and other vasoactive mediators) secreted by a neuroendocrine tumor. The best screening test is 24-hour urinary 5-HIAA, the major serotonin metabolite, which is highly specific when dietary serotonin-rich foods are avoided. Serum gastrin screens for gastrinoma (Zollinger-Ellison), which causes ulcers and diarrhea but not flushing/wheezing. Plasma metanephrines screen for pheochromocytoma, which causes episodic hypertension/palpitations but not secretory diarrhea or wheezing in this pattern. VIPoma causes profuse watery diarrhea (WDHA) without the characteristic flushing/wheeze and right-heart valvular disease. Chromogranin A is a useful adjunct marker.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
24-hour urinary 5-HIAA 95 mg/24h (normal <6)
Chromogranin A markedly elevated
NT-proBNP elevated
AST 60 U/L, ALT 55 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 220 U/L
Niacin (B3) level low-normal
Echocardiogram: thickened, retracted tricuspid leaflets with severe regurgitation and pulmonary valve involvement; preserved LV function

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The markedly elevated 5-HIAA confirms carcinoid syndrome. Why does this patient already have systemic (flushing, wheezing, diarrhea) symptoms and right-sided heart disease, given that the liver normally inactivates serotonin?
options: Serotonin is not metabolized by the liver as the primary diagnosis
The tumor is in the lungs, bypassing the liver as the primary diagnosis
Hepatic metastases release vasoactive mediators directly into the systemic circulation, bypassing first-pass hepatic clearance
She has primary biliary cholangitis impairing liver function as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: A midgut carcinoid tumor drains via the portal vein, so its serotonin is normally inactivated by the liver (first-pass metabolism)—which is why most localized GI carcinoids are asymptomatic. Carcinoid SYNDROME typically appears only once liver metastases secrete mediators directly into the hepatic veins/systemic circulation, escaping hepatic clearance. Her hepatomegaly, abnormal LFTs, and full syndrome indicate hepatic metastatic disease. Bronchial (lung) carcinoids can cause the syndrome without liver mets because they bypass the portal system, but her hepatomegaly points to liver mets. She has no evidence of PBC. Serotonin is metabolized by hepatic monoamine oxidase, so the liver normally does clear it.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: The echocardiogram shows tricuspid regurgitation and pulmonary valve disease. What is the pathophysiology of this carcinoid heart disease?
options: Left-sided valve fibrosis from systemic serotonin as the leading concern
Infective endocarditis of the tricuspid valve as the leading concern
Serotonin-induced plaque-like fibrosis of right-sided valves, with the lungs inactivating serotonin before it reaches the left heart
Rheumatic mitral stenosis as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Carcinoid heart disease (Hedinger syndrome) causes plaque-like fibrous deposits on the endocardium and valves, classically the RIGHT-sided tricuspid and pulmonary valves, producing tricuspid regurgitation and pulmonary stenosis/regurgitation. The pulmonary vascular bed inactivates serotonin (via lung monoamine oxidase), protecting the left heart—so left-sided disease is rare unless there is a right-to-left shunt or a bronchial primary. This is not infective endocarditis (no fever, vegetations, or organisms) and not rheumatic disease (which favors the mitral valve). Recognizing right-sided valvulopathy in a flushing/diarrhea patient is a classic clue to metastatic carcinoid.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate medical therapy to control her symptoms and inhibit tumor secretion?
options: Oral antihistamine alone as initial therapy
High-dose proton pump inhibitor as initial therapy
A long-acting somatostatin analog
Broad-spectrum antibiotics as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Long-acting somatostatin analogs (octreotide LAR, lanreotide) are the cornerstone of carcinoid syndrome management: they bind somatostatin receptors on the tumor, inhibiting release of serotonin and other vasoactive peptides, thereby controlling flushing and diarrhea, and they also have antiproliferative effects that slow tumor growth. They are additionally used to prevent carcinoid crisis perioperatively. A PPI treats acid hypersecretion (gastrinoma), not carcinoid. Antihistamines may blunt some flushing but do not address serotonin-driven diarrhea, the tumor, or valvular risk. Antibiotics have no role—this is neoplastic, not infectious. Additional measures include treating niacin deficiency (pellagra risk from tryptophan diversion) and liver-directed therapy for hepatic metastases.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which imaging is most useful to localize the primary tumor and stage somatostatin-receptor-expressing metastatic disease?
options: Coronary CT angiography in this setting
Mammography as the immediate next step
Somatostatin-receptor PET imaging
Plain chest radiograph only in this setting
stages.explanation: 68Ga-DOTATATE PET/CT (somatostatin-receptor imaging) is the most sensitive modality to localize well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumors and map metastatic disease, because these tumors overexpress somatostatin receptors. It guides both surgical planning and eligibility for peptide receptor radionuclide therapy (PRRT) and somatostatin-analog treatment. Coronary CT angiography evaluates coronary arteries, irrelevant to tumor localization. Mammography screens for breast cancer and is not indicated here. A plain chest radiograph might incidentally show a large bronchial carcinoid but is far too insensitive for staging a midgut primary and its hepatic metastases. DOTATATE imaging is the modern standard for NET localization and staging.

cases.diagnosis: Carcinoid syndrome with hepatic metastases and carcinoid heart disease
pearls: Carcinoid syndrome (flushing, secretory diarrhea, wheezing) usually requires liver metastases (or a bronchial primary) so vasoactive mediators bypass hepatic first-pass clearance.
Screen with 24-hour urinary 5-HIAA; chromogranin A is a useful adjunct; localize/stage with 68Ga-DOTATATE PET/CT.
Carcinoid heart disease causes RIGHT-sided valve fibrosis (tricuspid regurgitation, pulmonary stenosis) because the lungs inactivate serotonin before it reaches the left heart.
Somatostatin analogs (octreotide/lanreotide) control symptoms, slow tumor growth, and prevent carcinoid crisis; watch for niacin deficiency (pellagra).

cases.title: Painless Bloody Stools in a Young Boy
cases.chiefComplaint: Two episodes of painless bright-red blood mixed with stool over the past day.
cases.history: A 6-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department after his parents noticed a large amount of dark red and maroon blood mixed in his stool twice today. The bleeding is painless—he has had no crying spells, no abdominal pain, and continues to play normally between episodes. He has had no vomiting, no fever, and no recent diarrhea. There is no history of constipation, anal trauma, or hard stools, and parents deny seeing streaks of blood only on the toilet paper. He is otherwise healthy, fully immunized, and meeting developmental milestones. PMH is unremarkable with no prior surgeries. Medications: none; no recent NSAIDs or antibiotics. No allergies. Diet is age-appropriate; no recent travel or sick contacts. Family history is non-contributory with no inflammatory bowel disease or bleeding disorders. On further questioning the parents note he looked a bit pale today and was less energetic after the second episode of bleeding.
cases.exam: Alert but slightly pale child, mild tachycardia, no acute distress. The abdomen is soft, non-tender, and non-distended with normal bowel sounds; there is no palpable mass, no guarding, and no rebound. There is no abdominal wall erythema. Rectal exam reveals maroon, grossly bloody stool in the vault with no external fissures, hemorrhoids, or perianal lesions. There is no hepatosplenomegaly. Skin shows mild pallor of the conjunctivae but no petechiae, purpura, or rashes. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In a young, well-appearing child with painless, brisk lower GI bleeding, which diagnosis is most likely and should anchor the workup?
options: Meckel diverticulum with ectopic gastric mucosa
Intussusception as the first step today
Anal fissure as the first step today
Bacterial colitis as the first step today
stages.explanation: Painless, significant lower GI bleeding in a child under about 5-6 years is most commonly due to a Meckel diverticulum containing ectopic gastric mucosa, which secretes acid that ulcerates adjacent ileal mucosa and bleeds. It is the most common congenital GI anomaly and characteristically causes painless hematochezia. An anal fissure causes pain with defecation and bright streaks of blood on the surface/paper, not maroon blood mixed throughout, and the exam here shows no fissure. Intussusception produces colicky pain, vomiting, and 'currant-jelly' stool—this child is painless. Bacterial colitis causes fever, diarrhea, and crampy pain, also absent here. The painless brisk bleed points to Meckel.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 8.9 g/dL (baseline ~12)
MCV 74 fL (microcytic)
Platelets 320 ×10³/µL
WBC 8.1 ×10³/µL
PT/PTT/INR normal
Stool studies: negative for enteric pathogens; no fecal leukocytes

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which non-invasive imaging study is most appropriate to confirm a Meckel diverticulum in a hemodynamically stabilized child?
options: Barium enema as the primary diagnosis
Upper GI endoscopy
Technetium-99m pertechnetate scan
Abdominal ultrasound
stages.explanation: A technetium-99m pertechnetate scan (Meckel scan) is the test of choice: the radiotracer is taken up by gastric mucosa, including the ectopic gastric mucosa within a Meckel diverticulum, lighting up an abnormal focus in the right lower quadrant. It is non-invasive and well suited to children. A barium enema does not detect a Meckel diverticulum reliably and would obscure subsequent imaging. Abdominal ultrasound is useful for intussusception or appendicitis but is insensitive for Meckel. Upper GI endoscopy evaluates the esophagus, stomach, and duodenum—proximal to the distal ileal location of a Meckel and the bleeding source. Pretreatment with an H2 blocker or PPI can enhance scan sensitivity by reducing tracer washout.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Technetium-99m pertechnetate scan: focal abnormal tracer uptake in the right lower quadrant, separate from the stomach and bladder, consistent with ectopic gastric mucosa
No uptake elsewhere
Child remains hemodynamically stable after IV fluids; repeat Hgb 9.1 g/dL

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The Meckel scan is positive in a symptomatic, bleeding child. What is the definitive management?
options: Surgical resection of the diverticulum
Observation with iron supplementation only as initial therapy
Long-term proton pump inhibitor therapy alone as initial therapy
Colonoscopic clipping of the lesion as initial therapy
stages.explanation: A symptomatic, bleeding Meckel diverticulum is treated with surgical resection—either diverticulectomy or segmental small-bowel resection (especially if ectopic mucosa extends to the base or there is an inflamed/ulcerated segment). This removes the acid-secreting ectopic gastric tissue and the bleeding source definitively. A PPI may temporize bleeding but does not cure the anatomic lesion. Observation/iron alone is inadequate for ongoing significant hemorrhage and risks recurrent bleeds. Colonoscopic clipping cannot reach or definitively treat a distal ileal Meckel diverticulum and the ectopic gastric mucosa within it. Surgery is curative and is the standard of care for symptomatic disease.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: The 'rule of 2s' is a classic teaching aid for Meckel diverticulum. Which statement reflects it accurately?
options: Located ~2 feet from the ileocecal valve, ~2 inches long, ~2% prevalence, often symptomatic by age 2, 2 types of ectopic tissue
Always presents after age 20 with 2 distinct masses as the immediate next step
Affects 20% of the population and is usually 20 cm long as the immediate next step
Causes bleeding only in 2% of adults and never in children as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: The 'rule of 2s' summarizes Meckel diverticulum: it occurs in about 2% of the population, is located roughly 2 feet (within ~2 feet) proximal to the ileocecal valve on the antimesenteric border, is about 2 inches long, often becomes symptomatic by age 2 (though it can present later), is twice as common in males, and may contain 2 types of ectopic tissue—gastric (most common, causes bleeding) and pancreatic. It is a true diverticulum and a remnant of the omphalomesenteric (vitelline) duct. The other options misstate prevalence, location, and age of presentation. This mnemonic captures the high-yield epidemiology and anatomy.

pearls: Painless lower GI bleeding in a child under ~5 should raise suspicion for a Meckel diverticulum with ectopic gastric mucosa.
Technetium-99m pertechnetate (Meckel) scan detects ectopic gastric mucosa; H2 blocker/PPI pretreatment improves sensitivity.
Rule of 2s: ~2% prevalence, ~2 feet from ileocecal valve, ~2 inches long, often symptomatic by age 2, 2 tissue types (gastric and pancreatic).
Symptomatic (bleeding) Meckel diverticula are treated by surgical resection; it is the most common congenital GI anomaly (vitelline duct remnant).

cases.title: Inconsolable Crying Spells in a Toddler
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent episodes of sudden inconsolable crying with leg-drawing for several hours.
cases.history: An 18-month-old boy is brought in for several hours of intermittent, sudden episodes of inconsolable crying during which he draws his knees up to his chest and appears pale and sweaty. Each spell lasts a few minutes, after which he becomes limp and lethargic, then seems relatively normal before the next paroxysm. He has vomited twice (non-bilious initially) and refused feeds. Parents report he had a mild viral illness with a runny nose about a week ago. Most recently they noticed a stool that looked like dark red mucus mixed with blood. He has had no high fevers. PMH: full-term, healthy, immunizations up to date. Medications: none. No allergies. No prior abdominal surgery. Diet age-appropriate; no recent travel. Family history non-contributory. As the visit proceeds he becomes progressively more lethargic between episodes, prompting urgent evaluation. Parents deny known trauma and report no household sick contacts with diarrheal illness.
cases.exam: Lethargic but arousable toddler who becomes acutely distressed during a paroxysm, drawing up his legs. The abdomen is mildly distended; a sausage-shaped mass is palpable in the right upper quadrant, with an oddly empty-feeling right lower quadrant. There is mild diffuse tenderness without rigidity or rebound at this point. Rectal exam reveals a small amount of red, currant-jelly-like mucoid stool. Mucous membranes are slightly dry. There is no inguinal hernia. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable between episodes.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: An 18-month-old with paroxysmal colicky pain, a sausage-shaped RUQ mass, and currant-jelly stool most likely has which condition?
options: Volvulus from malrotation
Intussusception
Incarcerated inguinal hernia
Acute gastroenteritis
stages.explanation: The classic triad of intermittent colicky abdominal pain (with leg-drawing), a palpable sausage-shaped abdominal mass, and currant-jelly (blood-and-mucus) stool in a 6-month to 3-year-old child is intussusception—telescoping of one bowel segment into another, most commonly ileocolic. A preceding viral illness (lymphoid hyperplasia/Peyer patch hypertrophy acting as a lead point) is a common antecedent, as here. Gastroenteritis causes diffuse crampy pain with profuse diarrhea and fever, not a discrete mass or paroxysmal pattern. An incarcerated inguinal hernia would present with a tender groin/scrotal mass, absent here. Midgut volvulus typically presents earlier with bilious vomiting and is a different emergency. The mass and currant-jelly stool point to intussusception.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Abdominal ultrasound: 'target' (doughnut) sign in the right upper quadrant—concentric rings of bowel-within-bowel; on longitudinal view a 'pseudokidney' sign
No free intraperitoneal air
No evidence of bowel-wall pneumatosis or perforation
Mild surrounding free fluid

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Ultrasound shows the target sign confirming intussusception. In a hemodynamically stable child with no peritonitis or perforation, what is the appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Air or contrast enema for both diagnosis
Nasogastric decompression and discharge in this clinical context
Broad-spectrum antibiotics and observation
Immediate laparotomy with manual reduction
stages.explanation: In a stable child without signs of perforation or peritonitis, non-operative reduction with an air (pneumatic) enema—or, alternatively, a contrast/saline enema—under fluoroscopic or ultrasound guidance is both diagnostic and therapeutic and is the first-line treatment, successful in most ileocolic cases. Surgery is reserved for failed enema reduction, perforation, peritonitis, shock, or a pathologic lead point. NG decompression and discharge would miss the time-sensitive emergency and risk bowel ischemia. Antibiotics alone do not reduce the intussusception. Air enema avoids the messy peritoneal contamination of barium if perforation occurs and has high success rates, making it the preferred modality at many centers.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: During the air enema, the radiologist reports a sudden loss of resistance and free air appears under the diaphragm. What is the immediate next step?
options: Discharge home with oral antibiotics as the leading concern
Emergent surgical consultation
Schedule an outpatient CT in 24 hours as the leading concern
Repeat the air enema at higher pressure in this presentation
stages.explanation: Free air under the diaphragm during enema reduction signals bowel perforation, a recognized complication. The immediate response is to stop the procedure and obtain emergent surgical consultation for operative management—decompression, repair, and manual reduction or resection of any non-viable bowel. Increasing enema pressure after a perforation would worsen peritoneal contamination and is dangerous. Discharge with oral antibiotics is unacceptable for a perforated viscus, which is a surgical emergency. Delaying with an outpatient CT ignores an acute abdomen with free intraperitoneal air. Perforation, peritonitis, or hemodynamic instability are all indications to abandon non-operative reduction and proceed to surgery.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: If this child were older (e.g., 6 years) or had recurrent intussusception, what additional concern should be evaluated?
options: A pathologic lead point such as a Meckel diverticulum, polyp, or lymphoma
Lactose intolerance as the immediate next step
Routine viral gastroenteritis as the immediate next step
Functional constipation as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Most intussusception in toddlers is idiopathic (often after viral illness causing lymphoid hyperplasia). However, in older children, recurrent cases, or atypical presentations, a pathologic lead point should be sought—commonly a Meckel diverticulum, intestinal polyp, duplication cyst, hemangioma, or, importantly, lymphoma. Identifying a lead point may change management toward surgical exploration and resection. Viral gastroenteritis, lactose intolerance, and functional constipation do not cause intussusception and do not explain a telescoping bowel with a mass and currant-jelly stool. Recognizing the age-related shift toward a structural lead point is a key teaching point for the older or recurrent patient.

pearls: Classic triad: intermittent colicky pain with leg-drawing, a sausage-shaped abdominal mass, and currant-jelly stool in a 6-month to 3-year-old.
Ultrasound shows the 'target/doughnut' (transverse) and 'pseudokidney' (longitudinal) signs; it is the diagnostic test of choice.
Air (pneumatic) enema is first-line treatment in a stable child—both diagnostic and therapeutic; perforation or peritonitis mandates surgery.
Idiopathic in most toddlers (post-viral lead point); older or recurrent cases warrant a search for a pathologic lead point (Meckel, polyp, lymphoma).

cases.title: Forceful Vomiting in a One-Month-Old Infant
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive forceful vomiting after feeds for the past week.
cases.history: A 4-week-old boy, the firstborn of his family, is brought in for one week of progressively worsening vomiting after nearly every feed. The vomiting is non-bilious and has become forceful and projectile, sometimes shooting across the changing table. Notably, he appears hungry and eagerly feeds again immediately after vomiting. Parents report fewer wet diapers today and that he seems less vigorous. He was born full-term via uncomplicated vaginal delivery and was feeding and growing well until this week. There has been no fever, no diarrhea, and no bilious (green) emesis. No blood in the vomit. He was started on no new medications; mother took erythromycin briefly is denied. PMH otherwise unremarkable. No allergies. Family history notable for a maternal uncle who had 'stomach surgery as a baby.' On review, his weight today is below his prior well-child check, indicating poor weight gain over the past week.
cases.exam: Fussy, mildly dehydrated infant with a sunken anterior fontanelle and dry mucous membranes; capillary refill is ~3 seconds. The abdomen is soft and non-distended; after a test feed, visible peristaltic waves are seen moving across the upper abdomen, and a firm, mobile, olive-shaped mass is palpable in the epigastrium/right upper quadrant. There is no tenderness, guarding, or organomegaly otherwise. No inguinal hernia. Skin turgor is mildly decreased. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A 4-week-old with progressive non-bilious projectile vomiting, a hungry feeding pattern, and a palpable epigastric 'olive' most likely has which diagnosis?
options: Duodenal atresia
Hypertrophic pyloric stenosis
Gastroesophageal reflux
Midgut volvulus
stages.explanation: Hypertrophic pyloric stenosis (HPS) classically presents around 3-6 weeks of age with progressive, non-bilious, projectile vomiting in an infant who remains hungry ('hungry vomiter'), plus a palpable olive-shaped pyloric mass and visible gastric peristalsis. First-born males are at higher risk. Midgut volvulus causes BILIOUS vomiting and is a surgical emergency, but the vomiting here is non-bilious. Simple gastroesophageal reflux causes effortless spit-up, not forceful projectile vomiting with dehydration and an olive. Duodenal atresia presents in the first 1-2 days of life (often with bilious vomiting and a 'double bubble') and is associated with Down syndrome—not at 4 weeks with a previously feeding infant. The picture is classic HPS.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
Na 134 mEq/L
K 3.1 mEq/L
Cl 88 mEq/L
HCO3 34 mEq/L
pH 7.50
BUN 22 mg/dL
Urine: paradoxically acidic
Glucose 78 mg/dL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The labs show a hypochloremic, hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis with paradoxical aciduria. What is the most appropriate confirmatory test?
options: Upper GI contrast series
Esophageal pH monitoring
Abdominal ultrasound of the pylorus
Plain abdominal radiograph
stages.explanation: Abdominal ultrasound is the confirmatory test of choice for hypertrophic pyloric stenosis, demonstrating an elongated, thickened pyloric channel (commonly pyloric muscle thickness >3-4 mm and channel length >14-16 mm). It is non-invasive, radiation-free, and highly sensitive/specific. An upper GI contrast series can show the classic 'string sign' or 'shoulder sign' and is a second-line option if ultrasound is equivocal, but it involves contrast and radiation. A plain radiograph is non-specific. Esophageal pH monitoring evaluates reflux, not pyloric obstruction. The lab pattern (hypochloremic hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis from losing HCl in vomitus, with paradoxical aciduria) strongly supports HPS and should prompt ultrasound confirmation.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Ultrasound confirms pyloric muscle thickness of 5 mm and channel length of 18 mm. What must be done BEFORE definitive treatment?
options: Start oral feeds to maintain nutrition as initial therapy
Proceed immediately to surgery to relieve obstruction as monotherapy
Begin a proton pump inhibitor and discharge as initial therapy
Correct the dehydration
stages.explanation: Pyloric stenosis is a medical, not a surgical, emergency: the priority before the operation is to correct the dehydration and the hypochloremic, hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis with IV fluid resuscitation (normal saline with potassium added once urine output is established). Operating on an alkalotic, hypokalemic infant risks postoperative apnea and arrhythmia from the metabolic derangement and anesthesia. Rushing to surgery before resuscitation is unsafe. Oral feeds will provoke more vomiting and worsen the obstruction and electrolyte losses. A PPI does not relieve the mechanical obstruction. Once electrolytes and volume status normalize, definitive surgical correction is performed.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After the infant is rehydrated and electrolytes normalize, what is the definitive treatment?
options: Long-term thickened feeds and positioning
Pyloromyotomy (Ramstedt procedure)
Lifelong proton pump inhibitor therapy
Endoscopic balloon dilation
stages.explanation: The definitive treatment for hypertrophic pyloric stenosis is a pyloromyotomy (Ramstedt procedure), in which the hypertrophied pyloric muscle is incised down to the submucosa without entering the lumen, relieving the obstruction; it is curative with excellent outcomes and is commonly done laparoscopically. Thickened feeds and positioning manage simple reflux, not a mechanical pyloric obstruction. Endoscopic balloon dilation is not standard therapy for infantile HPS. A PPI treats acid-related disease and does nothing for the muscular obstruction. Feeds are typically resumed within hours after surgery, and most infants do very well with a low complication rate.

pearls: Classic HPS: non-bilious projectile vomiting at 3-6 weeks in a hungry infant (often first-born male), with a palpable olive and visible peristalsis.
Vomiting HCl produces a hypochloremic, hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis with paradoxical aciduria.
Ultrasound confirms the diagnosis (muscle thickness >3-4 mm, channel length >14-16 mm).
Correct fluids/electrolytes FIRST (it is a medical, not surgical, emergency); definitive treatment is pyloromyotomy (Ramstedt).

cases.title: A Newborn Who Has Not Passed Stool
cases.chiefComplaint: Failure to pass meconium with progressive abdominal distension since birth.
cases.history: A 3-day-old, full-term neonate on the postpartum ward has not passed meconium since delivery, now at roughly 72 hours of life. Nursing notes increasing abdominal distension and the infant has begun to have bilious-tinged emesis and feeds poorly. The pregnancy was uncomplicated and delivery was term via spontaneous vaginal birth; prenatal ultrasounds were reported as normal. There was no maternal diabetes and no magnesium exposure. The infant has otherwise been afebrile. Family history is notable for a sibling who required surgery for a 'bowel problem' in infancy and a parent with a relative who had Down syndrome. On further history, the parents recall that a digital rectal stimulation by the nurse earlier produced a forceful gush of gas and liquid stool, after which the abdomen temporarily softened. No prior abdominal surgery (newborn). No medications; standard newborn care only. The infant is being evaluated urgently for the delayed meconium passage and distension.
cases.exam: Irritable neonate with a markedly distended, tympanitic abdomen; bowel loops are visible. The abdomen is soft to mild palpation without a discrete mass; there is no overlying erythema. On digital rectal examination the rectal vault is tight and empty of stool, and withdrawal of the examining finger is followed by an explosive release of gas and stool ('squirt sign'). There is a normally positioned anus. No dysmorphic features are clearly noted, though the infant is being assessed for syndromic associations. Cardiopulmonary exam is unremarkable. The remainder of the exam is otherwise normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A full-term neonate with failure to pass meconium in the first 48 hours, distension, and an explosive 'squirt sign' on rectal exam most likely has which condition?
options: Meconium ileus
Hirschsprung disease
Anal atresia in this clinical context
Necrotizing enterocolitis
stages.explanation: Failure to pass meconium within the first 48 hours in a full-term infant, abdominal distension, and the explosive expulsion of gas/stool after rectal exam (the 'squirt sign') are classic for Hirschsprung disease—congenital aganglionosis of the distal colon causing a functional obstruction. Meconium ileus (often associated with cystic fibrosis) also delays meconium but classically shows inspissated meconium pellets and microcolon and lacks the squirt sign. Anal atresia is excluded by a normally positioned, patent anus on exam. Necrotizing enterocolitis typically affects premature infants and presents with bloody stools, pneumatosis intestinalis, and systemic toxicity, not an isolated meconium-passage failure with a tight empty rectum. The exam findings point to Hirschsprung.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Abdominal radiograph: dilated proximal bowel loops with a paucity of rectal gas
Contrast enema: a transition zone in the rectosigmoid—narrowed (aganglionic) distal segment with proximal dilation
Delayed films: retained contrast at 24 hours
No microcolon; no calcifications

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The contrast enema suggests a transition zone. What is the gold-standard test to confirm Hirschsprung disease?
options: Anorectal manometry alone as the primary diagnosis
Rectal suction biopsy demonstrating absence of ganglion cells
Serum electrolytes and CBC as the primary diagnosis
Stool sweat chloride test as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: The gold standard for diagnosing Hirschsprung disease is a rectal suction biopsy showing ABSENCE of ganglion cells in the submucosal (Meissner) and myenteric plexuses, typically with hypertrophied nerve trunks and increased acetylcholinesterase staining. This confirms the aganglionosis underlying the functional obstruction. Anorectal manometry (showing failure of the rectoanal inhibitory reflex—absent internal anal sphincter relaxation) is a useful screening adjunct but is not definitive, especially in neonates. The sweat chloride test diagnoses cystic fibrosis (relevant to meconium ileus, not Hirschsprung). Electrolytes and CBC assess the infant's status and complications but do not establish the diagnosis. Biopsy is required for definitive confirmation.

stages.label: BIOPSY
stages.content:
Rectal suction biopsy: absence of ganglion cells in the submucosal and myenteric plexuses
Hypertrophied nerve trunks present
Increased acetylcholinesterase activity on staining
Findings diagnostic of aganglionosis (Hirschsprung disease)

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The biopsy confirms aganglionosis. What is the definitive treatment for Hirschsprung disease?
options: Surgical resection of the aganglionic segment with a pull-through procedure
Botulinum toxin injection of the internal anal sphincter as initial therapy
Long-term total parenteral nutrition as initial therapy
Lifelong stool softeners and rectal irrigations as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Definitive treatment is surgical—resection of the aganglionic bowel segment and a 'pull-through' procedure (e.g., Swenson, Soave, or Duhamel) that brings normally innervated, ganglionic bowel down to the anus. Initial management may include rectal irrigations/decompression to stabilize before surgery, but these are temporizing, not definitive. Botulinum toxin can transiently relax the internal sphincter in selected refractory situations but does not cure the aganglionosis. Total parenteral nutrition is supportive and not a treatment for the structural disease. The goal is to remove the non-functioning aganglionic segment so that peristalsis can resume across innervated bowel.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which life-threatening complication of Hirschsprung disease must be recognized urgently, both pre- and post-operatively?
options: Inguinal hernia in this presentation
Gastroesophageal reflux
Hirschsprung-associated enterocolitis
Gilbert syndrome in this presentation
stages.explanation: Hirschsprung-associated enterocolitis (HAEC) is the most dangerous complication—presenting with fever, explosive foul-smelling diarrhea, abdominal distension, and potentially sepsis and toxic megacolon; it can be fatal if not promptly recognized and treated with rectal irrigations/decompression, IV fluids, broad-spectrum antibiotics, and bowel rest. It can occur before or after surgery. Gilbert syndrome (benign unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia) is unrelated and harmless. Gastroesophageal reflux is common in infants but not a life-threatening complication of Hirschsprung. An inguinal hernia is a separate surgical issue, not a feared sequela of aganglionosis. Recognizing and aggressively treating HAEC is a key safety point in these patients.

stages.label: ASSOCIATED CONDITION
stages.question: Hirschsprung disease has well-recognized genetic and syndromic associations. Which is most strongly associated?
options: Down syndrome and RET proto-oncogene mutations
Fragile X syndrome as the immediate next step
Marfan syndrome as the immediate next step
Turner syndrome as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Hirschsprung disease is most strongly associated with Down syndrome (trisomy 21), which accounts for a substantial fraction of cases, and with mutations in the RET proto-oncogene, the most commonly implicated gene in the failed craniocaudal migration of neural crest-derived ganglion cells. Other associations include Waardenburg syndrome and MEN2A (also RET-related). Turner syndrome (45,X) is linked to coarctation and horseshoe kidney, not aganglionosis. Marfan syndrome (FBN1) causes aortic root dilation and ectopia lentis, unrelated to enteric ganglia. Fragile X causes intellectual disability and macroorchidism, not Hirschsprung. Knowing the Down syndrome and RET associations is high-yield, and a positive family history (as in this infant's sibling) should heighten suspicion.

pearls: Suspect Hirschsprung disease when a full-term neonate fails to pass meconium in 48 hours with distension and an explosive 'squirt sign' on rectal exam.
Contrast enema shows a transition zone; rectal suction biopsy showing ABSENT ganglion cells (with hypertrophied nerve trunks/increased acetylcholinesterase) is the gold standard.
Definitive treatment is surgical pull-through (Swenson/Soave/Duhamel) resecting the aganglionic segment; associated with Down syndrome and RET mutations.
Hirschsprung-associated enterocolitis (fever, explosive diarrhea, distension, sepsis) is the life-threatening complication requiring urgent decompression, fluids, and antibiotics.

cases.title: Black Stools and a Stomach Wall Mass in an Older Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Fatigue and intermittent black stools with a mass found on endoscopy.
cases.history: A 60-year-old man presents with several weeks of fatigue, mild light-headedness, and intermittent dark, tarry stools (melena). He reports vague early satiety and mild upper-abdominal fullness but no severe pain. He has lost a few kilograms. He denies hematemesis, NSAID overuse, or heavy alcohol use. PMH: hypertension, hyperlipidemia. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily, atorvastatin 20 mg daily; no anticoagulants or chronic NSAIDs. No allergies. Social: never smoked, occasional wine. Family history non-contributory; no known familial tumor syndromes. He was referred after an outpatient upper endoscopy performed for the melena revealed a smooth, rounded submucosal bulge in the gastric body with a central ulceration that had been the bleeding source. Biopsies of the overlying mucosa were non-diagnostic. He is otherwise well and hemodynamically stable in clinic, now being evaluated for the nature of the gastric mass and definitive management.
cases.exam: Pale-appearing but comfortable man in no distress. Conjunctivae are pale. The abdomen is soft with mild epigastric fullness; no discrete mass is palpable externally and there is no tenderness, guarding, or rebound. There is no hepatomegaly, splenomegaly, or ascites. No peripheral lymphadenopathy. Rectal exam reveals melenic (black, tarry) stool. Cardiopulmonary exam is normal aside from mild tachycardia. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A smooth submucosal gastric mass with intact overlying mucosa (and non-diagnostic mucosal biopsies) and GI bleeding most suggests which tumor type?
options: Gastric adenocarcinoma as the first step today
MALT lymphoma as the first step today
Gastric carcinoid (type 1) as the first step today
A mesenchymal tumor such as a gastrointestinal stromal tumor
stages.explanation: A smooth, rounded, submucosal mass with intact overlying mucosa—so that standard mucosal forceps biopsies are non-diagnostic—is characteristic of a mesenchymal tumor, most commonly a gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST) arising from the interstitial cells of Cajal in the muscularis propria. Central ulceration causing bleeding is a classic GIST presentation. Gastric adenocarcinoma arises from mucosa and is usually diagnosable by mucosal biopsy as an ulcerated/infiltrative mucosal lesion, not a submucosal bulge. MALT lymphoma is mucosa-associated, often diffuse, and biopsy-diagnosable, frequently linked to H. pylori. Gastric carcinoids are typically smaller mucosal/submucosal nodules associated with hypergastrinemia. The submucosal location with negative mucosal biopsy points to GIST.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.content:
Endoscopic ultrasound: 4.5 cm hypoechoic mass arising from the muscularis propria (4th layer), with central ulceration
EUS-guided fine-needle biopsy obtained
Hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL, MCV 76 fL (iron-deficiency pattern)
CT abdomen/pelvis: gastric body mass, no hepatic or peritoneal metastases
Immunohistochemistry: spindle-cell tumor, KIT (CD117) positive, DOG1 positive

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The biopsy shows a spindle-cell tumor that is KIT (CD117) and DOG1 positive. What does this immunophenotype confirm?
options: Leiomyosarcoma
Gastric adenocarcinoma
Schwannoma in this clinical context
Gastrointestinal stromal tumor
stages.explanation: Positivity for KIT (CD117) and DOG1 in a spindle-cell mesenchymal gastric tumor confirms a gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST); most GISTs harbor activating mutations in the KIT (or PDGFRA) receptor tyrosine kinase, and CD117/DOG1 are the defining immunohistochemical markers. Leiomyosarcoma is desmin/smooth-muscle-actin positive and KIT negative. Schwannoma is S-100 positive and KIT negative. Gastric adenocarcinoma is an epithelial (cytokeratin-positive) tumor, not a KIT-positive spindle-cell mesenchymal lesion. The KIT/DOG1 profile is essentially pathognomonic for GIST and also explains its responsiveness to tyrosine kinase inhibitors.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this 4.5 cm localized gastric GIST without metastases, what is the primary treatment?
options: Surgical resection with negative margins
Definitive chemoradiation as initial therapy
Endoscopic mucosal resection as initial therapy
Wide gastrectomy with extensive lymph node dissection
stages.explanation: The mainstay of treatment for a localized, resectable GIST is complete surgical resection with negative (R0) margins. Because GISTs rarely metastasize to lymph nodes, an extensive lymphadenectomy is NOT routinely required (unlike adenocarcinoma)—a key distinction. Wide gastrectomy with extensive nodal dissection is therefore unnecessary and adds morbidity. GISTs are relatively resistant to conventional cytotoxic chemotherapy and radiation, so definitive chemoradiation is not standard. Endoscopic mucosal resection is inadequate for a 4.5 cm tumor arising from the muscularis propria and risks incomplete resection and perforation. Function-preserving wedge resection with clear margins is the goal for gastric GISTs.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: The tumor is high-risk (size and mitotic rate). Which targeted adjuvant therapy is indicated?
options: Imatinib
Bevacizumab in this setting
Trastuzumab in this setting
Rituximab as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Imatinib, a tyrosine kinase inhibitor targeting KIT and PDGFRA, is the targeted therapy for GIST. It is used as adjuvant therapy after resection of intermediate/high-risk tumors (based on size, mitotic index, and location) to reduce recurrence, as neoadjuvant therapy to downsize tumors, and as primary therapy for unresectable/metastatic disease; mutational analysis guides dosing and response. Trastuzumab targets HER2 (breast/gastric adenocarcinoma). Rituximab targets CD20 on B-cell lymphomas. Bevacizumab is an anti-VEGF agent used in several carcinomas but not standard for GIST. Imatinib's efficacy directly reflects the KIT-driven biology confirmed by the CD117-positive immunophenotype, linking the molecular diagnosis to treatment.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: Which two features most strongly determine the malignant/recurrence risk of a GIST?
options: Presence of H. pylori infection
Patient age and tumor color
Serum CEA and CA 19-9 levels
Tumor size and mitotic rate
stages.explanation: GIST malignant potential and recurrence risk are stratified primarily by tumor SIZE and MITOTIC RATE, with anatomic LOCATION as an additional modifier (gastric GISTs generally behave better than small-bowel ones of equal size/mitoses). These parameters drive risk-stratification schemes that determine the need for adjuvant imatinib and surveillance intensity. Patient age and gross color are not prognostic. CEA and CA 19-9 are tumor markers for epithelial GI cancers, not GISTs. H. pylori is linked to gastric adenocarcinoma and MALT lymphoma, not GIST pathogenesis. Knowing that size and mitotic count drive prognosis is essential for management decisions after resection.

pearls: A smooth submucosal gastric mass with intact mucosa and non-diagnostic forceps biopsy suggests a mesenchymal tumor—GIST arises from interstitial cells of Cajal in the muscularis propria.
GIST is defined by KIT (CD117)/DOG1 positivity and KIT or PDGFRA activating mutations; EUS with FNA characterizes it.
Localized GIST is treated by complete resection with negative margins; lymphadenectomy is usually unnecessary because nodal spread is rare.
Imatinib (KIT/PDGFRA TKI) is used as neoadjuvant, adjuvant (intermediate/high risk), and metastatic therapy; risk is driven by tumor size and mitotic rate.
